topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Thermodynamics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermodynamics** is the branch of physical science that deals with the relationships among **heat**, **work**, **temperature**, and the properties of matter. It governs the transformation of energy from one form to another and predicts the direction in which physical and chemical processes will spontaneously proceed.

In the context of **Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics**, the focus is on applying these principles to systems involving chemical reactions, phase equilibria, and the design of separation equipment such as distillation columns, reactors, compressors, and heat exchangers.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of thermodynamics as the **"accounting department"** of the universe. Just as an accountant tracks every rupee entering, leaving, and stored in a business, a thermodynamicist tracks every unit of energy. You cannot create energy from nothing, you cannot destroy it, and you cannot get something for nothing — the **Laws of Thermodynamics** are the rules of this energy bookkeeping.

## 1.2 The Thermodynamic System and Its Surroundings

The most fundamental step in any thermodynamic analysis is to define the **system** — the precisely specified region of the universe in which we are interested.

> [!NOTE]
> **System:** A precisely defined quantity of matter or a region in space chosen for thermodynamic study.
> **Surroundings:** Everything external to the system that can interact with it.
> **Boundary:** The real or imaginary surface that separates the system from its surroundings.

### Real-World Analogy

Imagine a pressure cooker on a stove:
- **System:** The contents (water, steam, food) inside the cooker.
- **Boundary:** The metallic wall of the cooker (which is rigid but allows heat transfer).
- **Surroundings:** The stove, kitchen air, and the rest of the universe.

## 1.3 Types of Thermodynamic Systems

| Type | Mass Transfer | Energy Transfer | Example |
|------|---------------|-----------------|---------|
| **Closed System (Control Mass)** | ❌ Not allowed | ✅ Allowed (Heat & Work) | Piston-cylinder device, pressure cooker |
| **Open System (Control Volume)** | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | Turbine, compressor, nozzle, pipe flow |
| **Isolated System** | ❌ Not allowed | ❌ Not allowed | Thermos flask (ideal), Universe |

> [!TIP]
> A **batch reactor** in a chemical plant is treated as a **closed system** because reactants are charged at the start, reactions occur, and products are removed at the end — but no mass crosses the boundary during the reaction.
>
> A **continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR)** is an **open system** because reactants continuously enter and products continuously leave.

## 1.4 Macroscopic vs. Microscopic Approach

Chemical engineering thermodynamics uses the **macroscopic approach** (also called **Classical Thermodynamics** or **Engineering Thermodynamics**), which:

- Treats matter as a **continuum** (no atomic/molecular structure).
- Uses measurable bulk properties: **P**, **V**, **T**, **n**, **ρ**.
- Requires no assumptions about molecular behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuum Assumption:** Even though matter is composed of discrete molecules, we assume properties vary continuously in space. This holds true when the smallest volume of interest contains an enormous number of molecules — typically valid down to about 10 µm scales.

## 1.5 Why Study Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Applications in Chemical Engineering:**
> - Design of **power cycles** and **refrigeration cycles**.
> - Prediction of **vapor-liquid equilibria (VLE)** for distillation column design.
> - Calculation of **equilibrium conversion** in chemical reactors.
> - Determination of **energy requirements** for compressors, pumps, and heat exchangers.
> - Estimation of **chemical reaction equilibrium** constants.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of a System

A **property** is any measurable characteristic of a system whose value depends only on the **state** of the system and not on how that state was reached.

### Classification of Properties

**(a) Intensive Properties**
- Independent of the mass of the system.
- Have the **same value at every point** within a homogeneous system.
- Two systems at the same state will have the **same value** of an intensive property.
- **Examples:** Temperature (**T**), Pressure (**P**), Density (**ρ**), Specific volume (**v**), Refractive index, Surface tension.

**(b) Extensive Properties**
- Depend on the **mass** (or extent) of the system.
- Additive in nature: if you divide the system into parts, the sum equals the original.
- **Examples:** Mass (**m**), Volume (**V**), Total internal energy (**U**), Total enthalpy (**H**), Total entropy (**S**), Heat capacity (**C**).

**(c) Specific Properties (Derived)**
- Obtained by dividing an extensive property by the mass.
- They become **intensive** properties.
- **Examples:** Specific volume $v = V/m$, Specific internal energy $u = U/m$, Specific enthalpy $h = H/m$, Specific entropy $s = S/m$.

### Why does this matter?

> A **mass flow rate** ($\dot{m}$) is extensive; a **mass flux** ($G$) is intensive. A student writing an energy balance on a reactor must clearly distinguish which property is being used — confusing intensive and extensive properties is a common reason for losing marks.

## 2.2 State and State Functions

> [!NOTE]
> **State of a System:** The condition completely described by its properties. For a single-component, single-phase simple compressible system, the state is fixed by **two independent intensive properties**.

**State Function (or Point Function):** A property whose value depends only on the **initial and final states**, not on the path followed. **Examples:** Internal energy (**U**), Enthalpy (**H**), Entropy (**S**), Pressure, Temperature, Volume.

**Path Function:** A quantity whose value depends on the **path** taken between two states. **Examples:** Work (**W**), Heat (**Q**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Consequence:** For any state function *Z*, $dZ$ is an **exact differential**, meaning:
> $$\oint dZ = 0$$
> For path functions like *W* and *Q*, the cyclic integral is generally **not zero**.

## 2.3 Processes and Cycles

- **Process:** A transformation of a system from one state to another.
- **Cycle:** A process (or series of processes) after which the system returns to its **initial state**.

### Important Process Types

| Process | Defining Condition | Constant Property |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Isothermal | Constant Temperature | **T** = constant |
| Isobaric (Isopiestic) | Constant Pressure | **P** = constant |
| Isochoric (Isometric) | Constant Volume | **V** = constant |
| Adiabatic | No Heat Transfer | **Q** = 0 |
| Polytropic | General PV relation | $PV^n$ = constant |
| Throttling | No work, no ΔKE, no ΔPE | **H** = constant |
| Reversible | Infinitely slow, no dissipation | Equilibrium throughout |
| Irreversible | Finite gradient, dissipation | Spontaneous & real |

## 2.4 Equilibrium

A system is in **equilibrium** when no property changes with time and no tendency exists for spontaneous change.

Four types of equilibrium must be satisfied simultaneously:
1. **Mechanical Equilibrium** — No unbalanced forces (uniform pressure).
2. **Thermal Equilibrium** — Uniform temperature throughout.
3. **Phase Equilibrium** — Mass of each phase remains constant.
4. **Chemical Equilibrium** — No net chemical reaction.

## 2.5 Temperature and the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zeroth Law:** If two bodies are each in thermal equilibrium with a third body, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.

This law is the basis for **temperature measurement** using thermometers. The "third body" is the thermometer itself.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Relation | Units (SI) |
|---|----------|---------------------|------------|
| 1 | Pressure | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | **Pa = N/m²** |
| 2 | Absolute Pressure | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Pa |
| 3 | Vacuum Pressure | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Pa |
| 4 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{V}{m} = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | m³/kg |
| 5 | Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | kg/m³ |
| 6 | Molar Volume | $V_m = \dfrac{V}{n} = \dfrac{MW}{\rho}$ | m³/mol |
| 7 | Universal Gas Constant | $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$ | J/(mol·K) |
| 8 | Work | $W = \int P\,dV$ | J |
| 9 | Temperature Conversion | $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$ | K |
| 10 | Pressure Conversion | $1\,\text{atm} = 101.325\,\text{kPa} = 1.01325\,\text{bar}$ | — |
| 11 | Degree of Freedom (Phase Rule) | $F = C - P + 2$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Continuum Limit | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{L} < 0.1$ | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> Memorise the conversion $1\,\text{atm} = 101.325\,\text{kPa} = 1.01325\,\text{bar} = 760\,\text{mmHg} = 14.696\,\text{psi}$. These conversions are tested in **nearly every KTU numerical**.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Measurement:** Understanding gauge vs. absolute pressure prevents catastrophic errors in vacuum systems (e.g., freeze-drying, vacuum distillation).
- **Specific Volume:** Used in steam tables — every entry is given per unit mass.
- **State vs. Path Functions:** Determining whether to integrate $\delta W$ or $dU$ is foundational to solving any energy balance problem in reactors and turbines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pressure Conversion Factors

**Given:**
$$1\,\text{atm} = 101325\,\text{Pa} = 101.325\,\text{kPa} = 1.01325\,\text{bar}$$

**Derivation of conversion from atm to mmHg:**

The standard atmospheric pressure supports a column of mercury of exactly **760 mm** in a barometer under standard gravity. Using the hydrostatic equation:

$$P = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

Substituting values:
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13595.1\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.80665\,\text{m/s}^2$
- $h = 0.760\,\text{m}$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= (13595.1)(9.80665)(0.760) \\
&= 101325\,\text{Pa} \\
&= 1\,\text{atm} \quad \text{(definition)}
\end{aligned}$$

Hence, **1 atm ≡ 760 mmHg**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Gauge-Absolute-Vacuum Relationship

Let us define each carefully using a numerical example.

**Problem:** A tire pressure gauge reads **35 psi**. The local atmospheric pressure is **14.7 psi**. Find the absolute pressure inside the tire.

**Step 1:** Identify the relationship.
$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values.
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{abs} &= 14.7\,\text{psi} + 35\,\text{psi} \\
&= 49.7\,\text{psi}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Result.
$$\boxed{P_{abs} = 49.7\,\text{psi}}$$

**Reverse Case:** A vacuum gauge reads **10 inHg vacuum** and atmospheric pressure is **29.9 inHg**. Find absolute pressure.

**Step 1:** Vacuum definition.
$$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange.
$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{abs} &= 29.9\,\text{inHg} - 10\,\text{inHg} \\
&= 19.9\,\text{inHg}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Intensive vs. Extensive Properties

The table below helps decide whether a property is intensive or extensive in a typical KTU problem.

| Property | Symbol | Classification | Why? | Per-Unit-Form |
|----------|--------|----------------|------|---------------|
| Mass | $m$ | Extensive | Adds with parts | — |
| Volume | $V$ | Extensive | Adds with parts | $v = V/m$ (intensive) |
| Pressure | $P$ | Intensive | Same in all sub-parts | — |
| Temperature | $T$ | Intensive | Same at equilibrium | — |
| Internal Energy | $U$ | Extensive | Adds with parts | $u = U/m$ |
| Enthalpy | $H$ | Extensive | Adds with parts | $h = H/m$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | Intensive | Same in all sub-parts | — |
| Specific Heat (per mass) | $c$ | Intensive | Normalised per unit mass | — |
| Total Heat Capacity | $C$ | Extensive | $\Sigma C_i = C_{total}$ | — |
| Molar Property (e.g., $V_m$) | $\overline{V}$ | Intensive | Per mole basis | — |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — State Postulate Application

**Problem:** A gas cylinder has a volume of **0.05 m³** and contains **2 kg of nitrogen** at **300 K**. Calculate:
1. Density of nitrogen.
2. Specific volume.
3. Molar volume.
4. Number of moles.

**Given:**
- $V = 0.05\,\text{m}^3$
- $m = 2\,\text{kg}$
- $T = 300\,\text{K}$
- $MW_{N_2} = 28\,\text{kg/kmol}$

**Step 1:** Density.
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{m}{V} \\
&= \frac{2\,\text{kg}}{0.05\,\text{m}^3} \\
&= 40\,\text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Specific volume.
$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{V}{m} \\
&= \frac{0.05}{2} \\
&= 0.025\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Number of moles.
$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{m}{MW} \\
&= \frac{2\,\text{kg}}{28\,\text{kg/kmol}} \\
&= 0.07143\,\text{kmol} = 71.43\,\text{mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Molar volume.
$$\begin{aligned}
V_m &= \frac{V}{n} \\
&= \frac{0.05\,\text{m}^3}{0.07143\,\text{kmol}} \\
&= 0.7000\,\text{m}^3/\text{kmol}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check using ideal gas law:** $V_m = RT/P$. Assuming $P = 1\,\text{atm} \approx 101.325\,\text{kPa}$:
> $V_m = \dfrac{(8.314)(300)}{101.325} = 24.6\,\text{m}^3/\text{kmol}$
> Our calculated $0.7\,\text{m}^3/\text{kmol}$ is **far lower**, confirming the cylinder is at high pressure (~35 atm), not 1 atm. This kind of sanity check is appreciated by KTU examiners.

## 3.5 Derivation of the Phase Rule (Gibbs Phase Rule)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gibbs Phase Rule** is a fundamental KTU syllabus item for Module 1.
> $$F = C - P + 2$$
> where $F$ = degrees of freedom, $C$ = number of components, $P$ = number of phases.

**Derivation Logic:**

For $C$ components and $P$ phases in a system at equilibrium:

**Step 1:** Total state variables to be specified.
$$\text{Total variables} = C \times P \quad \text{(composition of each phase)}$$
Plus temperature and pressure: $+2$.

**Step 2:** Total equations available from equilibrium.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Thermal equilibrium:} &\quad T^{\alpha} = T^{\beta} = \dots = T^{P} \Rightarrow (P-1)\text{ equations} \\
\text{Mechanical equilibrium:} &\quad P^{\alpha} = P^{\beta} = \dots = P^{P} \Rightarrow (P-1)\text{ equations} \\
\text{Chemical potential equality:} &\quad \mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta} = \dots \Rightarrow (P-1)C\text{ equations}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Total equations.
$$\text{Equations} = (P-1) + (P-1) + (P-1)C = (P-1)(C+2)$$

**Step 4:** Degrees of freedom.
$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \text{Total variables} - \text{Total equations} \\
&= CP + 2 - (P-1)(C+2) \\
&= CP + 2 - (PC + 2P - C - 2) \\
&= CP + 2 - PC - 2P + C + 2 \\
&= C - P + 2
\end{aligned}$$

Hence the Phase Rule:
$$\boxed{F = C - P + 2}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Thermodynamic Systems

This flowchart classifies the universe into a system of interest and its surroundings, with detailed sub-classifications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UNIVERSE] --> B[SYSTEM]
    A --> C[SURROUNDINGS]
    B --> D[CLOSED SYSTEM]
    B --> E[OPEN SYSTEM]
    B --> F[ISOLATED SYSTEM]
    D --> D1[NO MASS TRANSFER]
    D --> D2[HEAT AND WORK ALLOWED]
    D --> D3[EXAMPLE PISTON CYLINDER]
    E --> E1[MASS AND ENERGY TRANSFER]
    E --> E2[CONTROL VOLUME APPROACH]
    E --> E3[EXAMPLE TURBINE COMPRESSOR]
    F --> F1[NO MASS TRANSFER]
    F --> F2[NO ENERGY TRANSFER]
    F --> F3[EXAMPLE IDEAL THERMOS FLASK]
    D --> G1[ISOTHERMAL]
    D --> G2[ISOBARIC]
    D --> G3[ISOCHORIC]
    D --> G4[ADIABATIC]
    D --> G5[POLYTROPIC]
    E --> H1[STEADY STATE]
    E --> H2[UNSTEADY STATE TRANSIENT]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A Thermodynamic Analysis

This block diagram maps the logical workflow a chemical engineer follows when performing a thermodynamic analysis on any process equipment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[STEP 1 DEFINE SYSTEM AND BOUNDARY] --> S2[STEP 2 IDENTIFY ALL PROPERTIES]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3 CLASSIFY PROPERTIES INTENSIVE EXTENSIVE]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4 DETERMINE STATE VARIABLES]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5 APPLY EQUILIBRIUM CONDITIONS]
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6 CHOOSE OPEN OR CLOSED SYSTEM FORMULATION]
    S6 --> S7[STEP 7 APPLY FIRST LAW ENERGY BALANCE]
    S7 --> S8[STEP 8 APPLY SECOND LAW ENTROPY BALANCE]
    S8 --> S9[STEP 9 SOLVE FOR UNKNOWNS]
    S9 --> S10[STEP 10 INTERPRET AND VALIDATE RESULTS]
```

## 4.3 Pressure Reference Diagram — Gauge, Absolute, Vacuum

A conceptual pressure-reference schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[ABSOLUTE ZERO ZERO PRESSURE REFERENCE] --> A1[VACUUM REGION 0 TO P_ATM]
    A1 --> A2[ATMOSPHERIC PRESSURE P_ATM]
    A2 --> A3[GAUGE PRESSURE REGION P_ATM AND ABOVE]
    A3 --> A4[HIGH PRESSURE INDUSTRIAL SCALE]
    A2 -.-> B1[GAUGE READS ZERO AT THIS POINT]
    A0 -.-> B2[ABSOLUTE READS ZERO AT THIS POINT]
```

## 4.4 State Postulate Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[P PROPERTY 1] --> ST[STATE OF SYSTEM]
    P2[P PROPERTY 2] --> ST
    P3[EQUILIBRIUM] --> ST
    ST --> R1[ALL OTHER PROPERTIES ARE FIXED]
    ST --> R2[INDEPENDENT VARIABLES DECREASE TO TWO]
    ST --> R3[MATHEMATICAL CONSEQUENCE EXACT DIFFERENTIALS]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a closed system and an open system. Give one chemical engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **closed system** (control mass) is a fixed quantity of matter across whose boundary no mass transfer occurs; only energy in the form of heat and work can cross the boundary. **Example:** The contents of a sealed batch reactor during a chemical reaction.

An **open system** (control volume) is a region in space across whose boundary both mass and energy can transfer. **Example:** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger through which a process stream continuously flows.

(1 mark for each definition + 0.5 mark for each example = 3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Distinguish between intensive and extensive properties. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Intensive | Extensive |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Mass dependence | Independent of mass | Depends on mass |
| Sub-system value | Same in every part | Sum of parts |
| Examples | Temperature, Pressure | Volume, Total Energy |

(1 mark for distinction + 2 marks for examples = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define the following terms and explain with neat sketches: **(7 marks)**
1. Thermodynamic system, surroundings, and boundary
2. Closed, open, and isolated systems

**(b)** A gas cylinder of volume **0.025 m³** contains **3.5 kg of oxygen** at **25 °C**. Calculate the molar volume, density, specific volume, and the pressure using the ideal gas law. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Definitions and Diagrams **(7 marks)**

**System:** A quantity of matter or a region in space chosen for thermodynamic study. **(1 mark)**

**Surroundings:** Everything external to the system which can interact with it. **(1 mark)**

**Boundary:** The real or imaginary surface separating the system from its surroundings. **(1 mark)**

**Closed System:** Mass is fixed; only energy transfer (heat, work) occurs. *Example: Pressure cooker, sealed piston-cylinder.* **(1 mark)**

**Open System:** Both mass and energy cross the boundary. *Example: Compressor, turbine, nozzle.* **(1 mark)**

**Isolated System:** Neither mass nor energy crosses the boundary. *Example: Ideal thermos flask.* **(1 mark)**

A neat sketch differentiating the three system types (showing mass/energy arrows crossing the boundary) earns the final mark. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical on Oxygen Cylinder **(7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $V = 0.025\,\text{m}^3$
- $m = 3.5\,\text{kg}$
- $T = 25^{\circ}\text{C} = 298.15\,\text{K}$
- $MW_{O_2} = 32\,\text{kg/kmol}$
- $R = 8.314\,\text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$

**Step 1:** Number of moles. **(1 mark)**
$$n = \frac{m}{MW} = \frac{3.5}{32} = 0.109375\,\text{kmol}$$

**Step 2:** Molar volume. **(1 mark)**
$$V_m = \frac{V}{n} = \frac{0.025}{0.109375} = 0.2286\,\text{m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

**Step 3:** Density. **(1 mark)**
$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{3.5}{0.025} = 140\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Specific volume. **(1 mark)**
$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{0.025}{3.5} = 7.143 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 5:** Pressure using ideal gas law. **(2 marks)**
$$\begin{aligned}
PV &= nRT \\
P &= \frac{nRT}{V} \\
&= \frac{(0.109375)(8.314)(298.15)}{0.025} \\
&= \frac{271.05}{0.025} \\
&= 10842\,\text{kPa} \\
&\approx 10.84\,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{P \approx 10.84\,\text{MPa},\quad \rho = 140\,\text{kg/m}^3,\quad v = 7.143 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg},\quad V_m = 0.2286\,\text{m}^3/\text{kmol}}$$

**(1 mark for final boxed answer and units)**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** State and explain the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. How does it justify the concept of temperature and the use of a thermometer? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State the Gibbs Phase Rule and apply it to the following systems:
1. Pure water in liquid-vapour equilibrium. **(2 marks)**
2. A mixture of water and ethanol in vapour-liquid equilibrium. **(2 marks)**
3. A gaseous mixture of N₂, H₂, and NH₃ at chemical equilibrium. **(3 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Zeroth Law **(7 marks)**

**Statement:** *"If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."* **(2 marks)**

**Explanation:** This law establishes the transitive nature of thermal equilibrium. By identifying a property (temperature) that is equal for all systems in mutual equilibrium, the law provides a basis for temperature measurement. **(2 marks)**

**Connection to Thermometers:** When a thermometer (System C) is placed in contact with a hot body (System A), they reach thermal equilibrium. The thermometer is then placed in contact with another body (System B). If B is also in equilibrium with C, then by the Zeroth Law, A and B are at the same temperature. **(2 marks)**

**Significance:** Without the Zeroth Law, temperature measurement would have no logical foundation. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Part (b) — Phase Rule Applications **(7 marks)**

**Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2$ **(1 mark)**

**Case 1: Pure water in L-V equilibrium** **(2 marks)**
- $C = 1$ (one component: H₂O)
- $P = 2$ (liquid + vapour)
- $F = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$

> One degree of freedom: fixing T automatically fixes P (and vice versa) — the saturation curve.

**Case 2: Water + Ethanol in VLE** **(2 marks)**
- $C = 2$ (water, ethanol)
- $P = 2$ (liquid + vapour)
- $F = 2 - 2 + 2 = 2$

> Two degrees of freedom: e.g., T and one composition — used in distillation column design.

**Case 3: N₂ + H₂ + NH₃ gaseous mixture at chemical equilibrium** **(3 marks)**
- $C = 3$ (three chemical species)
- $P = 1$ (single gas phase)
- $F = 3 - 1 + 2 = 4$

> Four degrees of freedom: typically T, P, and two independent mole fractions chosen; the third mole fraction and equilibrium composition are then fixed by the equilibrium constant.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Converting between °C and K incorrectly. **Always** convert °C to K (add 273.15) before substituting into gas law equations.
> 2. **Intensive vs. Extensive Confusion:** Writing "Volume" instead of "Specific volume" loses 0.5–1 mark per instance. Express per-unit-mass quantities as $v$, $u$, $h$, $s$.
> 3. **Phase Rule Pitfall:** Forgetting to count all phases. If solid ice + liquid water + vapour coexist for pure water: $P = 3$, so $F = 1 - 3 + 2 = 0$ — this is the **triple point**, a fixed invariant point.
> 4. **Path Function vs. State Function:** Students often write $\Delta Q$ or $\Delta W$. The correct notation is **Q** and **W** (no $\Delta$, because they are not state functions).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Introduction**

- [x] **Thermodynamics** = science of energy, heat, work, and matter interactions.
- [x] **System** = the region we study; **Surroundings** = everything else; **Boundary** = interface.
- [x] Three system types: **Closed** (control mass), **Open** (control volume), **Isolated**.
- [x] **Intensive properties** are mass-independent (T, P, ρ); **Extensive** depend on mass (V, U, H, S).
- [x] **State Postulate:** Two independent intensive properties fix the state of a pure simple compressible system.
- [x] **State functions** (U, H, S) → exact differentials. **Path functions** (W, Q) → inexact.
- [x] **Zeroth Law** → justifies temperature measurement and thermometers.
- [x] **Pressure relationships:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$; $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$.
- [x] **Conversions to memorise:** 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 14.696 psi.
- [x] **Gibbs Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2$ → cornerstone for phase equilibrium calculations.
- [x] **Continuum Assumption:** $Kn < 0.1$ for macroscopic thermodynamics to apply.
- [x] **Equilibrium types:** Mechanical, Thermal, Phase, Chemical — all must hold simultaneously for true equilibrium.
- [x] **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** — universal gas constant.
- [x] **Specific properties** ($v, u, h, s$) are obtained by dividing the total by mass — converting extensive → intensive.
- [x] Chemical engineering applications: VLE prediction, reactor equilibrium, energy balances on process equipment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: First Law of Thermodynamics – Energy Balance for Closed and Open Systems – Application of First Law to Analyse Flow Through Horizontal Pipe and Nozzle – Limitations of First Law – Second Law of Thermodynamics – Definition of Entropy – Calculation of Entropy Changes in Processes Involving Ideal Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** First Law of Thermodynamics – Energy Balance for Closed and Open Systems – Application of First Law to Analyse Flow Through Horizontal Pipe and Nozzle – Limitations of First Law – Second Law of Thermodynamics – Definition of Entropy – Calculation of Entropy Changes in Processes Involving Ideal Gases --- # 📘 MODULE 1 — FUNDAMENTALS OF ENERGY AND ENTROPY > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** This module forms the **backbone** of all subsequent modules in Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics. Mastery of the energy balance for both closed and open systems is non-negotiable, as questions carrying **14 marks** in End Semester Examinations (ESE) are routinely drawn from this topic. --- ## 1.1 The First Law of Thermodynamics — Core Definition ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > **The First Law of Thermodynamics** states that *energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another.* For any thermodynamic system undergoing a cyclic process, the net energy exchange with the surroundings is zero. Mathematically, for a **cyclic process**: $$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$ For a **process between two states (1 → 2)**: $$Q - W = \Delta U$$ where: - $Q$ = Heat transferred **into** the system (J) - $W$ = Work done **by** the system (J) - $\Delta U = U_2 - U_1$ = Change in internal energy (J) - The universal gas constant is **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** - The standard reference temperature is **T₀ = 298.15 K (25 °C)** ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Think of the First Law like a **bank account**: - **Deposit (Q)** = heat added to your account. - **Withdrawal (W)** = work done BY you (you spend energy). - **Net Balance Change (ΔU)** = the difference between deposit and withdrawal. Just like a bank will not accept a transaction that increases or decreases total money beyond what was deposited/withdrawn, **energy is strictly conserved**. You cannot get more energy out than you put in. > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** > - $Q > 0$ → Heat added **to** the system > - $W > 0$ → Work done **by** the system > - $\Delta U > 0$ → Internal energy of the system **increases** --- ## 1.2 Closed System (Non-Flow Process) Energy Balance A **closed system** (also called *control mass*) has a fixed amount of mass; **no mass crosses the boundary**, but energy (heat and work) may cross. **General First Law statement:** $$Q - W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$ For most engineering processes where macroscopic kinetic and potential energies are negligible: $$\boxed{Q - W = \Delta U}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Property for Ideal Gases:** Internal energy $U$ of an ideal gas is a function of **temperature only**: > $$\Delta U = m \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T = n \cdot C_v \cdot \Delta T$$ --- ## 1.3 Open System (Control Volume) Energy Balance A **control volume** has fixed geometry in space, but mass **may flow across** its boundary (inlet and outlet streams). **General Energy Balance (First Law for Open System):** $$\frac{dU_{cv}}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{out}$$ For **steady-state, single inlet, single outlet**, this simplifies to: $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}\left[\Delta h + \frac{\Delta V^2}{2} + g\Delta z\right]$$ Dividing by mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ gives the **specific form**: $$\boxed{q - w_s = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta V^2}{2} + g\Delta z}$$ where $h = u + Pv$ is the **specific enthalpy** (J/kg or kJ/kg). --- ## 1.4 The Second Law of Thermodynamics — Core Definition ### Formal Definition > **The Second Law of Thermodynamics** asserts that natural processes have a **preferred direction**: heat flows spontaneously from a higher temperature to a lower temperature, and it is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body. Two classical statements: | Statement | Verbatim | |-----------|----------| | **Kelvin–Planck** | It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than extracting heat from a single reservoir and converting it entirely into work. | | **Clausius** | It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body. | ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Imagine you spill a cup of coffee on the floor. The coffee **naturally** spreads out and cools down. You have **never** seen the spilled coffee spontaneously gather itself back into the cup and heat up. The Second Law tells us **why** this asymmetry exists — it introduces a new state property called **entropy**. --- ## 1.5 Entropy — The State Property ### Formal Definition > **Entropy (S)** is an **extensive thermodynamic property** that quantifies the degree of molecular disorder or the unavailability of energy for useful work. It is a state function, so its change between two equilibrium states is path-independent. Mathematically: $$dS = \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$$ For a finite reversible process between states 1 and 2: $$\Delta S = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$ **Units:** J/K or kJ/K (extensive); J/(kg·K) or kJ/(kmol·K) (specific). > [!NOTE] > The unit of specific entropy is most commonly **kJ/(kg·K)** in engineering practice, while molar entropy uses **kJ/(kmol·K)**. --- # 📐 SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET --- ## 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown ### 🔹 Step 1: Establish the System Boundary Before writing any energy equation, you must **identify** the system: 1. **Closed system** → no mass flow, only $Q$ and $W$ matter. 2. **Open system** → mass crosses the boundary, carry terms ($h$, $V^2/2$, $gz$) must be included. ### 🔹 Step 2: Apply the Correct Form of First Law | System Type | First Law Equation | When to Use | |-------------|--------------------|--------------| | Closed, no ΔKE, ΔPE | $Q - W = \Delta U$ | Batch reactors, piston-cylinder | | Open, steady state | $q - w_s = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta V^2}{2} + g\Delta z$ | Turbines, compressors, nozzles, pipes, heat exchangers | | Open, steady, adiabatic, no work, horizontal | $h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$ | Nozzles, throttling valves | ### 🔹 Step 3: Apply Ideal Gas Relations For an ideal gas: - $h = h(T)$ only → $\Delta h = c_p \Delta T$ - $u = u(T)$ only → $\Delta u = c_v \Delta T$ - $Pv = RT$ → ideal gas equation of state > [!IMPORTANT] > The Mayer relation connects $c_p$ and $c_v$: > $$c_p - c_v = R$$ > This is essential for solving ideal gas problems where one specific heat is given and the other is needed. ### 🔹 Step 4: Combine with Continuity (for Open Systems) $$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} \quad \text{(steady-state)}$$ --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table | # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes | |---|---------|----------|---------------| | 1 | First Law (closed, sign convention I) | $Q - W = \Delta U$ | J or kJ | | 2 | First Law (open, specific) | $q - w_s = \Delta h + \dfrac{\Delta V^2}{2} + g\Delta z$ | J/kg | | 3 | Enthalpy definition | $H = U + PV$ | J or kJ | | 4 | Specific enthalpy | $h = u + Pv$ | J/kg | | 5 | Closed system, ideal gas | $\Delta U = m c_v \Delta T$ | $c_v$ in J/(kg·K) | | 6 | Open system, ideal gas | $\Delta H = m c_p \Delta T$ | $c_p$ in J/(kg·K) | | 7 | Mayer's relation | $c_p - c_v = R$ | R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | | 8 | Work, isothermal reversible, ideal gas | $W = nRT \ln \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$ | Closed system | | 9 | Ideal gas law | $Pv = RT$ (specific) or $PV = nRT$ (molar) | Pa·m³ = J | | 10 | Steady-flow energy equation (mass form) | $\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}(\Delta h + \dfrac{\Delta V^2}{2} + g\Delta z)$ | W or J/s | | 11 | Horizontal pipe (incompressible) | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2}$ | No heat, no work | | 12 | Nozzle (adiabatic, no work) | $h_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2}$ | KE converted to/from h | | 13 | Nozzle with ideal gas | $c_p T_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} = c_p T_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2}$ | Apply $c_p$ in J/kg·K | | 14 | Entropy definition (reversible) | $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | J/K | | 15 | Entropy change, ideal gas | $\Delta s = c_p \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - R \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | J/(kg·K) | | 16 | Entropy change, ideal gas (T–V form) | $\Delta s = c_v \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} + R \ln \dfrac{v_2}{v_1}$ | J/(kg·K) | | 17 | Entropy change, isothermal ideal gas | $\Delta s = -R \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = R \ln \dfrac{v_2}{v_1}$ | J/(kg·K) | | 18 | Clausius inequality | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | Equality for reversible | | 19 | Entropy generation (open system) | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}(s_2 - s_1) - \sum \dfrac{\dot{Q}_i}{T_i}$ | W/K | | 20 | Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k}$ | $k = c_p/c_v$ | > [!TIP] > **Tip for KTU exams:** Always show **two** separate steps when applying the energy equation to a nozzle/pipe: > (i) Write the **general** SFEE > (ii) **Simplify** by listing each assumption (adiabatic, no work, no $\Delta z$, etc.) > This earns full method marks even if your arithmetic is slightly off. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Turbines & Compressors:** The First Law SFEE directly yields shaft work output/input. - **Rocket Nozzles:** Converting high-pressure gas enthalpy into kinetic energy. - **Pipelines:** Understanding pressure drop and energy losses in long-distance fluid transport. - **Refrigeration Cycles:** Entropy analysis pinpoints irreversibilities, guiding efficiency improvements. - **Distillation Columns:** Heat exchanger design uses SFEE; minimum work of separation uses Second Law. --- # 🧮 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX --- ## 3.1 Derivation: First Law for a Closed System Starting from the **conservation of energy** for a closed system of fixed mass: $$\text{Energy IN} - \text{Energy OUT} = \text{Energy Accumulated}$$ For a closed system, only heat and work can cross the boundary: $$Q_{in} - W_{out} = U_2 - U_1$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{Q - W = \Delta U}$$ If we include kinetic and potential energy changes: $$Q - W = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$ **For an ideal gas undergoing a constant-volume process** ($W = 0$): $$Q_v = \Delta U = m c_v (T_2 - T_1)$$ **For an ideal gas undergoing an isothermal process** ($\Delta U = 0$): $$Q = W = \int_1^2 P \, dV = \int_1^2 \frac{nRT}{V} dV = nRT \ln \frac{V_2}{V_1}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) Consider a control volume with one inlet (1) and one outlet (2). Apply the **general energy balance**: $$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{out}$$ For **steady-state**: $\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$, and continuity gives $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}$. Therefore: $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$ Dividing by $\dot{m}$ to get the **specific** form: $$\boxed{q - w_s = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta V^2}{2} + g\Delta z}$$ > [!NOTE] > The work term $W_s$ is the **shaft work** only. It excludes the **flow work** $Pv$ which is already bundled into the enthalpy $h$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Flow Through a Horizontal Pipe (Incompressible Fluid) **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state 2. No shaft work ($w_s = 0$) 3. No elevation change ($\Delta z = 0$) 4. Adiabatic OR negligible heat loss ($q \approx 0$) 5. Incompressible fluid ($\rho =$ constant) Starting from SFEE: $$0 - 0 = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta V^2}{2} + 0$$ For incompressible flow, $\Delta h = \Delta u + \Delta(Pv) = c_v \Delta T + \frac{\Delta P}{\rho}$. If temperature change is negligible, $\Delta h \approx \frac{\Delta P}{\rho}$: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$ Or equivalently: $$\boxed{P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho}{2}\left(V_2^2 - V_1^2\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the well-known **Bernoulli equation** (without the gravitational head). For a pipe with constant cross-section, $V_1 = V_2$, hence $P_1 = P_2$, meaning **no useful pressure drop occurs unless velocity changes** (e.g., due to constriction) or unless friction is considered. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Flow Through a Nozzle **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state 2. Adiabatic ($q = 0$) 3. No shaft work ($w_s = 0$) 4. No elevation change ($\Delta z = 0$) 5. Negligible KE change at inlet: $V_1 \approx 0$ (stagnation inlet) SFEE simplifies to: $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$ For an **ideal gas** with constant $c_p$: $$c_p T_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = c_p T_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$ Solving for the exit velocity: $$\boxed{V_2 = \sqrt{V_1^2 + 2 c_p (T_1 - T_2)}}$$ This is the **fundamental nozzle equation** — used to design rocket engines, steam turbines, and jet engines. --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas (General) Starting from the combined First and Second Law for a closed system: $$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev} = T \, dS - P \, dV$$ Since $H = U + PV$ → $dH = dU + PdV + VdP$: $$dH = T \, dS + V \, dP$$ Dividing both by mass to get specific forms: $$T \, ds = dh - v \, dP$$ For an ideal gas: $dh = c_p \, dT$ and $Pv = RT$ → $v = \dfrac{RT}{P}$: $$T \, ds = c_p \, dT - \frac{RT}{P} \, dP$$ Dividing by $T$: $$ds = c_p \frac{dT}{T} - R \frac{dP}{P}$$ Integrating from state 1 to state 2 (assuming constant $c_p$): $$\boxed{\Delta s = c_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)}$$ **Alternative form** using $Pv = RT$ to eliminate $P$: $$\Delta s = c_v \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$$ **Special cases:** | Process | Condition | $\Delta s$ | |---------|-----------|------------| | Isothermal | $T_2 = T_1$ | $\Delta s = -R \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | | Isobaric | $P_2 = P_1$ | $\Delta s = c_p \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | | Isochoric | $v_2 = v_1$ | $\Delta s = c_v \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | | Isentropic | $\Delta s = 0$ | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} = \left(\dfrac{v_1}{v_2}\right)^{(k-1)}$ | --- ## 3.6 Limitations of the First Law > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Believing the First Law is "complete." It is not — it only says *energy is conserved*, not *which processes can occur* or *how efficient* they can be. 1. **Direction of processes:** First Law does not predict whether a process is *spontaneous*. Example: heat flowing from cold to cold body does not violate energy conservation but is impossible by the Second Law. 2. **Feasibility:** Cannot judge whether a proposed cycle or process is *possible*. 3. **Quality of energy:** Does not distinguish between 1 kJ of work and 1 kJ of heat at low temperature (the latter is less "useful"). 4. **Equilibrium:** Cannot predict the final equilibrium state of an isolated system. 5. **Irreversibility:** Does not quantify the *degree* of irreversibility or lost work. > [!NOTE] > The Second Law (and the concept of entropy) was developed precisely to address these limitations. --- # 🗺️ SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS --- ## 4.1 System Classification Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Thermodynamic System] --> B[Closed System
Control Mass] A --> C[Open System
Control Volume] A --> D[Isolated System] B --> B1[Energy in Q, W
No mass crosses boundary] C --> C1[Mass, heat, work
cross boundary] C --> C2[Steady State
dE/dt = 0] C --> C3[Unsteady State
dE/dt is non zero] D --> D1[No mass
No heat
No work] B1 --> E[First Law Q minus W equals delta U] C2 --> F[SFEE q minus ws equals delta h plus delta V squared over 2 plus g delta z] C3 --> G[General unsteady
energy balance] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.2 SFEE Application Flow (Nozzle & Horizontal Pipe) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start Problem]) --> Q1{System type?} Q1 -->|Closed| C[Use Q minus W equals delta U] Q1 -->|Open| D[Use SFEE] D --> Q2{Device type?} Q2 -->|Nozzle| N1[Apply Assumptions:
Adiabatic q equals 0
No shaft work ws equals 0
No elevation delta z equals 0] N1 --> N2[h1 plus V1 squared over 2 equals h2 plus V2 squared over 2] N2 --> N3[For ideal gas:
cp T1 plus V1 squared over 2 equals cp T2 plus V2 squared over 2] N3 --> N4[Solve for V2 exit velocity] Q2 -->|Horizontal Pipe| P1[Apply Assumptions:
No shaft work
No elevation change
Incompressible fluid] P1 --> P2[P1 over rho plus V1 squared over 2 equals P2 over rho plus V2 squared over 2] P2 --> P3[Solve for pressure drop or velocity change] Q2 -->|Turbine or Compressor| T1[Apply Assumptions:
Adiabatic or known Q
No KE PE change] T1 --> T2[ws equals minus delta h] T2 --> T3[Calculate shaft work] style Start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style N2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style P2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style T2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style N4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style P3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: First Law → Second Law ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LAW1 [First Law Domain] A1[Energy Conservation] A2[Quantity of Energy] A3[Closed System: Q minus W equals delta U] A4[Open System: SFEE] A1 --> A2 --> A3 A1 --> A2 --> A4 end subgraph GAP [Limitations Identified] B1[Does not predict direction] B2[Does not quantify quality] B3[No irreversibility measure] end subgraph LAW2 [Second Law Domain] C1[Entropy State Function] C2[Clausius Inequality] C3[Direction of spontaneous processes] C4[Reversible versus Irreversible] C1 --> C2 C3 --> C4 C1 --> C4 end A3 --> GAP A4 --> GAP GAP --> LAW2 style LAW1 fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px style GAP fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style LAW2 fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` --- # 🎯 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. State the First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system and write its mathematical form.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The First Law of Thermodynamics states that **energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another.** For a closed system undergoing a process between states 1 and 2: $$Q - W = \Delta U = U_2 - U_1$$ where $Q$ is the heat added to the system, $W$ is the work done by the system, and $\Delta U$ is the change in internal energy. The First Law is essentially the law of **conservation of energy** applied to thermodynamic systems. --- ### **Q2. Define entropy and state the Clausius inequality.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Entropy (S)** is an extensive thermodynamic property that measures the degree of molecular disorder or the unavailability of energy for useful work in a system. Mathematically, for a reversible process: $$dS = \left(\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}\right)$$ The **Clausius inequality** states that for any real (irreversible) cycle: $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$ Equality holds for a completely reversible cycle, while strict inequality indicates irreversibility. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** For Part B questions, examiners strictly enforce: > 1. **Write the GENERAL equation first** (e.g., full SFEE), then list **each assumption** as a separate line. > 2. Always carry **units** through calculations; missing units = **1–2 mark deduction**. > 3. State the **reference state** (e.g., for ideal gas, $h$ depends only on $T$, so $\Delta h = c_p \Delta T$). > 4. Draw a simple **schematic** of the nozzle/pipe; missing diagram = **up to 1 mark lost** in presentation. --- ### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose either A or B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Air at 300 °C and 1.5 bar enters a horizontal nozzle with negligible velocity and leaves at 1.05 bar with velocity 250 m/s. Assuming air behaves as an ideal gas with $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), determine:** **(a)** The exit temperature of the air. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The entropy change per kg of air during the process. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION **Given:** - Inlet: $T_1 = 300 + 273 = 573$ K, $P_1 = 1.5$ bar, $V_1 \approx 0$ - Outlet: $P_2 = 1.05$ bar, $V_2 = 250$ m/s - $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) = 1005 J/(kg·K) - $R$ for air = 8.314/29 = **0.287 kJ/(kg·K)** = 287 J/(kg·K) --- ### 📝 Part (a) — Exit Temperature [7 marks] **Step 1:** Write the general SFEE for the nozzle. $$q - w_s = \Delta h + \frac{\Delta V^2}{2} + g\Delta z$$ **Step 2:** List assumptions explicitly: - Adiabatic → $q = 0$ - No shaft work → $w_s = 0$ - Horizontal → $\Delta z = 0$ - $V_1 \approx 0$ (negligible inlet velocity) **Step 3:** Simplified SFEE reduces to: $$0 = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute $h_2 - h_1 = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ and $V_1 = 0$: $$0 = c_p (T_2 - T_1) + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$ **Step 5:** Solve for $T_2$: $$T_2 = T_1 - \frac{V_2^2}{2 c_p}$$ $$T_2 = 573 - \frac{(250)^2}{2 \times 1005}$$ $$T_2 = 573 - \frac{62500}{2010}$$ $$T_2 = 573 - 31.09$$ $$\boxed{T_2 = 541.91 \; \text{K} \approx 268.91 \; °\text{C}}$$ --- ### 📝 Part (b) — Entropy Change [7 marks] **Step 1:** For an ideal gas: $$\Delta s = c_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values: $$\Delta s = 1005 \ln\left(\frac{541.91}{573}\right) - 287 \ln\left(\frac{1.05}{1.5}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the logarithms: $$\ln\left(\frac{541.91}{573}\right) = \ln(0.9459) = -0.05565$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{1.05}{1.5}\right) = \ln(0.7) = -0.35667$$ **Step 4:** Compute each term: $$1005 \times (-0.05565) = -55.93 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}$$ $$-287 \times (-0.35667) = +102.36 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}$$ **Step 5:** Final entropy change: $$\boxed{\Delta s = -55.93 + 102.36 = 46.43 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** Since $\Delta s > 0$, the process is **irreversible** (consistent with an adiabatic nozzle — friction and turbulence produce entropy). --- ### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Steam flows steadily through a horizontal pipe. At the inlet, the conditions are: pressure 3 bar, temperature 200 °C, velocity 30 m/s. At the outlet, the velocity is 60 m/s and the pressure is 2.7 bar. Heat loss to the surroundings is 25 kJ/kg. Assuming steam behaves as an ideal gas with $c_p = 1.872$ kJ/(kg·K) and $R = 0.4615$ kJ/(kg·K), determine:** **(a)** The temperature at the outlet of the pipe. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The entropy change per kg of steam during the process. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION **Given:** - $T_1 = 200 + 273 = 473$ K, $P_1 = 3$ bar, $V_1 = 30$ m/s - $V_2 = 60$ m/s, $P_2 = 2.7$ bar - $q = -25$ kJ/kg (heat loss to surroundings) - $w_s = 0$ (no shaft work), $\Delta z = 0$ (horizontal) --- ### 📝 Part (a) — Outlet Temperature [7 marks] **Step 1:** Apply SFEE per kg of steam: $$q - w_s = c_p (T_2 - T_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g\Delta z$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values ($w_s = 0$, $\Delta z = 0$): $$-25 \times 1000 = 1.872 \times 1000 \times (T_2 - 473) + \frac{60^2 - 30^2}{2}$$ $$-25000 = 1872 (T_2 - 473) + \frac{3600 - 900}{2}$$ $$-25000 = 1872 (T_2 - 473) + 1350$$ **Step 3:** Rearrange: $$1872 (T_2 - 473) = -25000 - 1350 = -26350$$ $$T_2 - 473 = \frac{-26350}{1872} = -14.075$$ $$\boxed{T_2 = 458.93 \; \text{K} \approx 185.93 \; °\text{C}}$$ --- ### 📝 Part (b) — Entropy Change [7 marks] **Step 1:** Apply ideal gas entropy equation: $$\Delta s = c_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute ($c_p = 1.872$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.4615$ kJ/kg·K): $$\Delta s = 1.872 \ln\left(\frac{458.93}{473}\right) - 0.4615 \ln\left(\frac{2.7}{3}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate: $$\ln\left(\frac{458.93}{473}\right) = \ln(0.9703) = -0.03022$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{2.7}{3}\right) = \ln(0.9) = -0.10536$$ **Step 4:** Compute: $$\Delta s = 1.872 \times (-0.03022) - 0.4615 \times (-0.10536)$$ $$\Delta s = -0.0566 + 0.0486$$ $$\boxed{\Delta s = -0.0080 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)} = -8.0 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}}$$ --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - ✅ **First Law (closed):** $Q - W = \Delta U$ — memorize sign convention. - ✅ **First Law (open, steady):** $q - w_s = \Delta h + \dfrac{\Delta V^2}{2} + g\Delta z$ — the workhorse of all flow problems. - ✅ **Enthalpy for ideal gas** is a function of $T$ alone — always use $\Delta h = c_p \Delta T$. - ✅ **Nozzle assumption package:** adiabatic ($q=0$), no work ($w_s=0$), horizontal ($\Delta z = 0$). - ✅ **Horizontal pipe assumption package:** incompressible fluid, $\Delta z = 0$, often $V_1 = V_2$ ⇒ no pressure drop in ideal frictionless pipe. - ✅ **Mayer's relation:** $c_p - c_v = R$ — required when only one specific heat is given. - ✅ **Limitations of First Law:** No directional info, no quality of energy, no equilibrium prediction. - ✅ **Second Law:** Introduces entropy; Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements; defines reversible vs irreversible. - ✅ **Entropy for ideal gas:** $\Delta s = c_p \ln \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - R \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ — the most-tested formula in entropy problems. - ✅ **Isentropic shortcut:** $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k}$ — derived by setting $\Delta s = 0$. - ✅ **Clausius inequality:** $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ — equality is reversible, inequality is irreversible. - ✅ **Always write units** in every step; examiners penalize missing units heavily. - ✅ **Draw a schematic** of the device (nozzle/pipe) before starting the solution.

topics.title: Third Law of Thermodynamics and Fundamental Property Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Third Law of Thermodynamics and Fundamental Property Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Third Law of Thermodynamics

### Formal Definition
The **Third Law of Thermodynamics** establishes the reference point for the absolute entropy of a pure substance. It is most rigorously expressed in two complementary statements:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nernst's Heat Theorem (1906):** *The entropy change for any isothermal reversible process approaches zero as the temperature approaches absolute zero.*
>
> $$\lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S_{T} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Planck's Statement (1912):** *The entropy of a perfectly ordered, pure crystalline substance is exactly zero at the absolute zero of temperature (0 K).*
>
> $$\lim_{T \to 0} S = 0 \quad \text{(for a perfect crystal)}$$

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Shuffle of Disorder"
Imagine a brand-new, perfectly organized deck of cards (the *perfect crystal* at 0 K). Every card has a designated position — no randomness, no missing pieces, no jokers. The deck is in a state of **absolute order**, and we assign this state an entropy value of **zero**.

If we add a tiny bit of thermal energy (heat) to the deck, cards begin to slightly vibrate in their slots — order is lost, and entropy increases. This is exactly what happens in a real crystal as we move from **0 K** upward. The Third Law simply gives us the "starting point" of this entropy journey.

### Why It Matters
- Permits the calculation of **absolute entropy** $S(T, P)$ of a substance from calorimetric data.
- Confirms that **absolute zero is unattainable** in a finite number of thermodynamic steps.
- Provides closure to the three laws: First Law (energy balance), Second Law (entropy generation), Third Law (entropy reference).

### Physical Constants & Reference Metrics
- **Absolute Zero:** $T = 0 \text{ K} = -273.15 \, ^\circ\text{C}$
- **Triple Point of Water:** $T = 273.16 \text{ K}$, $P = 611.657 \text{ Pa}$
- Standard reference state for entropy: $S^0_{298.15 \text{ K}}$ in $\text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$

## 1.2 Fundamental Property Relations

### Formal Definition
The **Fundamental Property Relations** are the four differential equations that unite the four most important thermodynamic potentials ($U, H, A, G$) with the canonical variables that describe them naturally. They are derived by combining the **First Law** and the **Second Law** in differential form.

$$\boxed{dU = T\,dS - P\,dV}$$
$$\boxed{dH = T\,dS + V\,dP}$$
$$\boxed{dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV}$$
$$\boxed{dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP}$$

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Four Faces of Energy"
Think of thermodynamics as a **Swiss Army knife**. The same thermodynamic information is accessible through four different "blades" (potentials), each optimized for a particular set of conditions:
- $U(S, V)$ — useful for constant-volume, isolated systems
- $H(S, P)$ — useful for constant-pressure, flow processes
- $A(T, V)$ — useful for constant-temperature, constant-volume
- $G(T, P)$ — useful for constant-temperature, constant-pressure (chemistry & phase equilibria)

### Importance
> [!IMPORTANT]
> These relations form the **mathematical backbone** of classical thermodynamics. Every Maxwell relation, every Gibbs–Helmholtz equation, and every thermodynamic identity stems from these four equations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Third Law — Detailed Logical Breakdown

### Core Premises
1. **Thermal equilibrium condition** at $T \to 0$ implies no further disorder can be thermally induced.
2. **Quantum mechanical justification:** The ground state of a perfect crystal is non-degenerate; hence $W = 1$ and $S = k_B \ln W = 0$.
3. **Statistical interpretation:** Entropy is a measure of the number of accessible microstates. A perfect crystal at 0 K has only **one** accessible microstate.

### Consequences of the Third Law

| # | Consequence | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific heat at constant pressure vanishes | $\lim_{T \to 0} C_P = 0$ |
| 2 | Coefficient of thermal expansion vanishes | $\lim_{T \to 0} \alpha = 0$ |
| 3 | Absolute entropy is finite and computable | $S = \int_0^T \dfrac{C_P}{T}\,dT$ |
| 4 | Two isotherms of an ideal gas coincide below any finite $T$ | $S(T, P_1) = S(T, P_2)$ as $T \to 0$ |
| 5 | Unattainability of absolute zero | $T > 0$ for any finite process |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Cryogenics:** Liquefaction of helium, hydrogen, and nitrogen relies on knowledge of residual entropy near 0 K.
- **Materials Science:** Prediction of low-temperature behavior of superconductors and superfluids.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Standard absolute entropy tables ($S^\circ$) for reaction equilibria calculations.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic of Fundamental Property Relations

**Step 1 — Start from the First Law for a closed system:**
$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W = \delta Q_{\text{rev}} - P\,dV$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Second Law for a reversible process:**
$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{\text{rev}}}{T} \implies \delta Q_{\text{rev}} = T\,dS$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** to obtain the first fundamental relation:
$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

**Step 4 — Define $H = U + PV$** and differentiate:
$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Step 5 — Define $A = U - TS$** and differentiate:
$$dA = dU - T\,dS - S\,dT = -S\,dT - P\,dV$$

**Step 6 — Define $G = H - TS$** and differentiate:
$$dG = dH - T\,dS - S\,dT = -S\,dT + V\,dP$$

## 2.3 Maxwell Relations — The Big Picture

By the **Euler reciprocity relation** (exact differential criterion), if $dz = M\,dx + N\,dy$ is exact, then:
$$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$

Applying this to each fundamental relation yields the four **Maxwell relations**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize These for KTU Exams**

### Core Maxwell Relations

| Relation | Derived From | Key Form |
|---|---|---|
| Maxwell-1 | $dU$ exactness | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$ |
| Maxwell-2 | $dH$ exactness | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$ |
| Maxwell-3 | $dA$ exactness | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ |
| Maxwell-4 | $dG$ exactness | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ |

### Useful Derived Identities

| Quantity | Identity |
|---|---|
| $C_P - C_V$ | $C_P - C_V = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ |
| Internal pressure $\pi_T$ | $\pi_T = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$ |
| Joule–Thomson coefficient | $\mu_{JT} = -\dfrac{1}{C_P}\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = \dfrac{1}{C_P}\!\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$ |
| Entropy of an ideal gas | $dS = C_P \dfrac{dT}{T} - R\dfrac{dP}{P}$ |
| Absolute entropy | $S(T) = \int_0^T \dfrac{C_P}{T}\,dT + \sum \Delta H_{\text{trans}}/T$ |

> **Mnemonic (Thermodynamic Square):** $T, S$ on the sides; $P, V$ on the top/bottom. The four Maxwell relations connect opposite corners.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Maxwell relations** allow us to compute **non-measurable partial derivatives** (e.g., $\partial S/\partial P$ at constant $T$) from **measurable quantities** (e.g., $PVT$ data).
- Critical in calculating **departure functions** for real gases and liquids.
- Essential for **chemical reaction equilibria** via $\Delta G^\circ$ and temperature dependence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Third Law Absolute Entropy Equation

### Starting Point
Heat supplied to a substance from 0 K to temperature $T$ (at constant pressure):
$$\delta Q_{\text{rev}} = C_P \, dT$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The entropy change from $0$ K to $T$ is:
$$S(T) - S(0) = \int_0^T \frac{\delta Q_{\text{rev}}}{T} = \int_0^T \frac{C_P}{T}\,dT$$

By the Third Law (Planck's statement), $S(0) = 0$, so:
$$S(T) = \int_0^T \frac{C_P}{T}\,dT$$

For real substances undergoing phase transitions, the absolute entropy includes contributions from each phase:
$$S(T) = \int_0^{T_f} \frac{C_{P,s}}{T}\,dT + \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T_f} + \int_{T_f}^{T_b} \frac{C_{P,l}}{T}\,dT + \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T_b} + \int_{T_b}^{T} \frac{C_{P,g}}{T}\,dT$$

**Textual explanation:** We heat the solid from 0 K to its fusion point $T_f$, melt it (absorbing latent heat $\Delta H_{fus}$), heat the liquid to the boiling point $T_b$, vaporize it, and finally heat the vapor to $T$. The integral of $C_P/T$ over each segment, plus the discrete latent-heat contributions divided by their respective temperatures, gives the **absolute entropy**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of All Four Maxwell Relations

### Derivation 1 — From $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$

This is an exact differential in canonical variables $(S, V)$. Therefore:
$$U = U(S, V)$$
$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V dS + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S dV$$

Comparing term by term:
$$T = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V \qquad \text{and} \qquad -P = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S$$

Apply **Euler reciprocity** (mixed second partial derivatives of $U$ are equal):
$$\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial V \,\partial S} = \frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial S \,\partial V}$$

Compute both sides:
$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial V}\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V\right]_S = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial S}\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S\right]_V$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial (-P)}{\partial S}\right)_V$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V} \quad \text{(Maxwell-1)}$$

### Derivation 2 — From $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$

$H = H(S, P)$, exact in $(S, P)$:
$$dH = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P dS + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S dP$$

Identify:
$$T = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P \qquad \text{and} \qquad V = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S$$

Euler reciprocity:
$$\frac{\partial^2 H}{\partial P \,\partial S} = \frac{\partial^2 H}{\partial S \,\partial P}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P} \quad \text{(Maxwell-2)}$$

### Derivation 3 — From $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$

$A = A(T, V)$, exact in $(T, V)$:
$$dA = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

Identify:
$$-S = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V \qquad \text{and} \qquad -P = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T$$

Euler reciprocity:
$$\frac{\partial^2 A}{\partial V \,\partial T} = \frac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T \,\partial V}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial (-S)}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial (-P)}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V} \quad \text{(Maxwell-3)}$$

### Derivation 4 — From $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$

$G = G(T, P)$, exact in $(T, P)$:
$$dG = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

Identify:
$$-S = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P \qquad \text{and} \qquad V = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

Euler reciprocity:
$$\frac{\partial^2 G}{\partial P \,\partial T} = \frac{\partial^2 G}{\partial T \,\partial P}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial (-S)}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P} \quad \text{(Maxwell-4)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Derivation: $C_P - C_V$ Identity

### Starting Equations
- From $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$, at constant $P$:
$$dH = T\,dS \quad \text{(constant P)}$$
- From $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$, at constant $V$:
$$dU = T\,dS \quad \text{(constant V)}$$

### Derivation Steps
**Step 1:** Express $S$ as $S(T, P)$:
$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 2:** At constant $P$, $dP = 0$:
$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into $dH$ expression:
$$dH = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT = C_P\, dT \quad \text{(definition)}$$

So:
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_P}{T}$$

**Step 4:** Similarly, at constant $V$, express $S = S(T, V)$:
$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 5:** Apply Maxwell-3 to convert the second term:
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:
$$dS = \frac{C_V}{T} dT + \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV$$

**Step 7:** Equate the two expressions for $dS$ and introduce the chain rule. Let $V = V(T, P)$, so $dV = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$. At constant $P$:
$$dV = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dT$$

**Step 8:** So:
$$\frac{C_P}{T}\,dT = \frac{C_V}{T}\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dT$$

**Step 9:** Simplify:
$$C_P - C_V = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

$$\boxed{C_P - C_V = T \cdot \frac{\alpha^2 V}{\kappa_T}}$$

where $\alpha = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ is the **coefficient of thermal expansion** and $\kappa_T = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$ is the **isothermal compressibility**.

> **Verification for ideal gas:** $PV = RT \Rightarrow \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = R/V$ and $\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = R/P$. Then $C_P - C_V = T \cdot (R/V)(R/P) = R$. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Thermodynamic Square (TDSquare) Mnemonic

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Square:** The four sides are $T, S$ (intensive thermal pair) and $P, V$ (intensive mechanical pair). Diagonally opposite vertices are connected by Maxwell relations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["S"] --- B["T"]
    B --- C["P"]
    C --- D["V"]
    D --- A
    B -.Maxwell 1.-> D
    C -.Maxwell 2.-> A
    A -.Maxwell 3.-> C
    D -.Maxwell 4.-> B
    M1["(dT/dV)S = -(dP/dS)V"]:::mx
    M2["(dT/dP)S = (dV/dS)P"]:::mx
    M3["(dS/dV)T = (dP/dT)V"]:::mx
    M4["(dS/dP)T = -(dV/dT)P"]:::mx
    classDef mx fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4a017,color:#000,stroke-width:2px;
```

**How to read it:** Pick two adjacent vertices. The variable you move *along* is the natural derivative direction. The opposite vertex gives the conjugate variable. The diagonal relation becomes a Maxwell identity.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of the Third Law and Entropy Calculation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Substance at T = 0 K, P = P0"] --> B["Heat solid to Tf"]
    B["Heat solid 0 to Tf"] --> C["Melt at Tf: absorb DH_fus"]
    C["Melt at Tf"] --> D["Heat liquid Tf to Tb"]
    D["Heat liquid Tf to Tb"] --> E["Vaporize at Tb: absorb DH_vap"]
    E["Vaporize at Tb"] --> F["Heat vapor Tb to T"]
    F["Heat vapor Tb to T"] --> G["Integrate Cp/T over all segments"]
    G["Sum entropy contributions"] --> H["Absolute Entropy S at T and P"]
    H["S value from Third Law"] --> I["Use in DG = DH - TDS for equilibria"]
    I["Use in DG"] --> J["Phase and Reaction Equilibria"]
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow: From Fundamental Relations to Maxwell Relations

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["First Law: dU = dQ - dW"] --> L2["Add Second Law: dS = dQ/T"]
    L2["Combine First and Second Law"] --> L3["dU = TdS - PdV"]
    L3["Combine First and Second Law"] --> L4["Define H, A, G potentials"]
    L4["Define H, A, G potentials"] --> L5["Four fundamental relations"]
    L5["Four fundamental relations"] --> L6["Apply Euler reciprocity"]
    L6["Apply Euler reciprocity"] --> L7["Four Maxwell relations"]
    L7["Four Maxwell relations"] --> L8["Compute non-measurable derivatives"]
    L8["Compute non-measurable derivatives"] --> L9["Residual properties, fugacity, equilibria"]
```

## 4.4 Consequence Map of the Third Law

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1["Third Law: S perfect crystal = 0 at 0 K"] --> C1["Cp approaches 0 as T approaches 0"]
    T1["Third Law: S perfect crystal = 0 at 0 K"] --> C2["Thermal expansion coeff approaches 0"]
    T1["Third Law: S perfect crystal = 0 at 0 K"] --> C3["Absolute entropy computable from Cp data"]
    T1["Third Law: S perfect crystal = 0 at 0 K"] --> C4["Absolute zero unattainable in finite steps"]
    C3["Cp approaches 0 as T approaches 0"] --> A1["Enables standard entropy tables"]
    A1["Enables standard entropy tables"] --> A2["Use in chemical reaction equilibria"]
    A2["Use in chemical reaction equilibria"] --> A3["Design of reactors, separators"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. State the Third Law of Thermodynamics. Mention any two consequences. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** The Third Law states that the entropy of a perfectly ordered, pure crystalline substance is zero at the absolute zero of temperature (Planck's statement). Equivalently, the entropy change for any isothermal process approaches zero as $T \to 0$ (Nernst's Heat Theorem).

$$\lim_{T \to 0} S = 0 \quad \text{(perfect crystal)} \quad ; \quad \lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S_T = 0$$

**Two Consequences:**

1. **Specific heat vanishes:** $\lim_{T \to 0} C_P = 0$, which means $\int_0^{T} (C_P/T)\,dT$ converges.
2. **Absolute entropy is computable:** We can compute $S(T) = \int_0^{T} (C_P/T)\,dT$ from purely calorimetric data, enabling absolute entropy tables.

*(A third point students may add: absolute zero is unattainable in a finite number of steps.)*

---

### Q2. Define the Helmholtz free energy $A$ and the Gibbs free energy $G$. Write their total differentials. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definitions:**
- Helmholtz free energy: $A = U - TS$ — useful for closed systems at constant $T$ and $V$.
- Gibbs free energy: $G = H - TS = U + PV - TS$ — useful for open/flow systems at constant $T$ and $P$.

**Total differentials (Fundamental Property Relations):**

$$dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$$
$$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$$

These are exact differentials, and applying Euler reciprocity to them yields the Maxwell relations Maxwell-3 and Maxwell-4 respectively.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `CHOICE 1`

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): State and explain the Third Law of Thermodynamics. Discuss its important consequences. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement (2 marks):**
The Third Law, in its most accepted form (Planck), states:
> "The entropy of a perfectly ordered, pure crystalline substance is zero at the absolute zero of temperature (0 K)."

Mathematically: $S_{0\,K} = 0$ for a perfect crystal.

**Step 2 — Nernst's Heat Theorem (2 marks):**
Nernst's more general statement: as $T \to 0$, the entropy change for any isothermal process between two states of internal equilibrium tends to zero:
$$\lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S_T = 0$$

This implies the two isotherms of a substance at $T \to 0$ coincide — i.e., $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T \to 0$ and $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T \to 0$ as $T \to 0$.

**Step 3 — Important Consequences (3 marks):**
1. **Vanishing $C_P$:** From $dS = (C_P/T)\,dT$ at constant $P$, the convergence of $S$ as $T \to 0$ requires $C_P \to 0$.
2. **Vanishing coefficient of thermal expansion:** $\alpha = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P \to 0$.
3. **Absolute entropy:** $S(T) = \int_0^{T} (C_P/T)\,dT + \sum \Delta H_{\text{trans}}/T$ — enables tabulation.
4. **Unattainability of 0 K:** No finite sequence of thermodynamic operations can cool a system to exactly 0 K.

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Derive all four Maxwell relations from the fundamental property relations. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:** *(See Section 3.2 for full derivations. Here is the exam-ready summary.)*

Starting from the four exact differentials:

**Maxwell-1** from $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ (exact in $S, V$):
$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$$

**Maxwell-2** from $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ (exact in $S, P$):
$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$$

**Maxwell-3** from $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$ (exact in $T, V$):
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**Maxwell-4** from $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ (exact in $T, P$):
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Method for each (general recipe, write this in exam):**
1. Identify the natural variables of the potential.
2. Write $d(\text{potential}) = M\,d(\text{var}_1) + N\,d(\text{var}_2)$ where $M$ and $N$ are partial derivatives.
3. Apply Euler reciprocity: $\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial \text{var}_2}\right)_{\text{var}_1} = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial \text{var}_1}\right)_{\text{var}_2}$.
4. Simplify to a Maxwell identity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `CHOICE 2`

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Explain the Thermodynamic Square (Mnemonic) and use it to derive two Maxwell relations. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Square (3 marks):**
The thermodynamic square arranges $S$ and $T$ on the vertical sides (thermal) and $P$ and $V$ on the horizontal sides (mechanical). A potential's **natural variables** are the two sides **adjacent** to its corner:

| Corner | Potential | Natural Variables |
|---|---|---|
| $U$ at $(S, V)$ | Internal energy | $S, V$ |
| $H$ at $(S, P)$ | Enthalpy | $S, P$ |
| $A$ at $(T, V)$ | Helmholtz energy | $T, V$ |
| $G$ at $(T, P)$ | Gibbs energy | $T, P$ |

The differential of each potential is read off from the arrows in the square.

**Step 2 — Deriving Maxwell-3 (from $A$ at $T,V$) (2 marks):**
- Move from $A$ to $T$: derivative is $-S$.
- Move from $A$ to $V$: derivative is $-P$.
- Therefore: $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$.
- Euler reciprocity: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$.

**Step 3 — Deriving Maxwell-4 (from $G$ at $T,P$) (2 marks):**
- Move from $G$ to $T$: derivative is $-S$.
- Move from $G$ to $P$: derivative is $V$.
- Therefore: $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$.
- Euler reciprocity: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$.

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): The molar volume of a solid varies with temperature as $V = V_0 + aT^2$, where $V_0$ and $a$ are constants. Find the entropy change $\Delta S$ when the solid is heated from $T_1$ to $T_2$ at constant pressure. Assume $C_P = bT$, where $b$ is a constant. Also, show that $C_P \to 0$ as $T \to 0$, consistent with the Third Law. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = V_0 + aT^2$, where $V_0$, $a$ are constants
- $C_P = bT$, where $b$ is a constant
- Process: constant pressure, $T_1 \to T_2$

**Step 1 — Express entropy change at constant $P$ (2 marks):**
At constant pressure, the reversible heat is $dQ_{\text{rev}} = C_P\,dT$, so:
$$dS = \frac{C_P\,dT}{T} = \frac{bT\,dT}{T} = b\,dT$$

**Step 2 — Integrate (2 marks):**
$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} b\,dT = b(T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 3 — Use Maxwell-4 to find $dS$ at constant $T$ (1 mark):**
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\frac{\partial}{\partial T}(V_0 + aT^2) = -2aT$$

**Step 4 — Verify Third Law consistency (2 marks):**
As $T \to 0$:
$$C_P = bT \to 0 \quad \checkmark$$
$$\alpha = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{2aT}{V_0 + aT^2} \to 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Both $C_P$ and $\alpha$ vanish as $T \to 0$, which is fully consistent with the Third Law of Thermodynamics.

**Final Answers:**
$$\Delta S = b(T_2 - T_1) \quad ; \quad C_P \to 0 \text{ as } T \to 0$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign errors in Maxwell-4:** The most common error is missing the **negative sign** in $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$. Always re-derive from scratch.
> 2. **Mixing up which variable is held constant:** Board examiners explicitly check that the subscript on the partial derivative matches the natural variable of the potential. Wrong subscript = 1–2 marks lost.
> 3. **Forgetting Euler reciprocity:** If you write the four fundamental relations but skip the criterion for an exact differential, you lose the **logical connection** to Maxwell relations.
> 4. **Confusing $C_P$ with $C_V$ in entropy integral:** Always remember: at constant $P$, $dS = C_P\,dT/T$; at constant $V$, $dS = C_V\,dT/T$.
> 5. **Third Law — omitting "perfect crystal":** Planck's statement requires the substance to be in a *perfectly ordered* state. If the crystal has frozen-in disorder (e.g., CO, N$_2$O), the residual entropy is **non-zero** even at 0 K.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep**

- **Third Law (Planck):** $S_{\text{perfect crystal, 0 K}} = 0$.
- **Third Law (Nernst):** $\lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S_T = 0$ for isothermal processes.
- **Consequence 1:** $C_P \to 0$ as $T \to 0$ → integral $S = \int_0^T C_P/T \, dT$ converges.
- **Consequence 2:** Coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha \to 0$ as $T \to 0$.
- **Consequence 3:** Absolute zero is **unattainable** in finite steps.
- **Absolute entropy equation:** $S(T) = \int_0^{T_f} \frac{C_{P,s}}{T}\,dT + \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T_f} + \int_{T_f}^{T_b} \frac{C_{P,l}}{T}\,dT + \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T_b} + \int_{T_b}^{T} \frac{C_{P,g}}{T}\,dT$.
- **Four fundamental relations (memorize in pairs):**
  - $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ — natural $(S, V)$
  - $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ — natural $(S, P)$
  - $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$ — natural $(T, V)$
  - $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ — natural $(T, P)$
- **Four Maxwell relations (memorize via Thermodynamic Square):**
  - M1: $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$
  - M2: $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$
