  - M3: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$
  - M4: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$
- **Key identity:** $C_P - C_V = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{T \alpha^2 V}{\kappa_T}$.
- **Joule–Thomson:** $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$.
- **Euler reciprocity** is the universal tool: if $dz = M\,dx + N\,dy$ is exact, then $\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$.
- **For ideal gases:** $dS = C_P \frac{dT}{T} - R\frac{dP}{P}$ — special-case application of M4.
- **For incompressible liquids:** $\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = V\alpha$, so M4 gives $dS = C_P \frac{dT}{T} - \alpha V\,dP$.

> **One-line takeaway for the exam:**
> The Third Law fixes the **zero** of entropy; the fundamental relations fix the **derivatives**; Maxwell relations fix the **connections** between them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maxwell's Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Maxwell's Equations # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Maxwell's Equations (also called Maxwell Relations)** are a set of four exact thermodynamic identities derived from the **exactness (path-independence) of the fundamental thermodynamic potentials**: Internal Energy ($U$), Enthalpy ($H$), Helmholtz Free Energy ($A$), and Gibbs Free Energy ($G$). They relate partial derivatives of one thermodynamic variable with respect to another to partial derivatives involving more easily measurable properties (such as pressure $P$, volume $V$, and temperature $T$). In the formal language of the **KTU 2024 syllabus**, Maxwell's Equations are introduced as a *mathematical consequence* of Euler's criterion for exact differentials (the **reciprocity theorem** of mixed partial derivatives). If a property $Z = Z(x, y)$ has an exact differential $dZ = M\,dx + N\,dy$, then: $$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$ Applying this theorem to each of the four fundamental potentials yields the **four Maxwell Relations**, which are indispensable tools for **converting non-measurable entropy-based derivatives into measurable PVT derivatives**. ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Thermodynamic Cheat Code"** > > Imagine you are in a chemical plant. Your boss asks: *"How does the entropy of this fluid change when you compress it at constant temperature?"* — a quantity like $(\partial S / \partial P)_T$ is **almost impossible to measure directly**, because entropy has no instrument! > > Maxwell's Equations act like a **currency exchange counter at an airport**. You have "entropy dollars" (which you cannot count physically), but you want "pressure-volume-temperature euros" (which you *can* measure with gauges and thermometers). The Maxwell relation is the **exchange rate** that lets you swap one for the other. > > So the boss's question becomes: *"What is $-(\partial V/\partial T)_P$?"* — and suddenly, you just need a **dilatometer** and a **pressure gauge**. ## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted) - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$ - **Standard Temperature:** $T^{\circ} = 298.15 \; \text{K}$ ($25^{\circ}\text{C}$) - **Standard Pressure:** $P^{\circ} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$ (**1 atm**) - **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion:** $\alpha = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ (units: $\text{K}^{-1}$) - **Isothermal Compressibility:** $\beta_T = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$ (units: $\text{Pa}^{-1}$) - **Heat Capacity at Constant Pressure:** $C_P = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$ (units: $\text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$) ## KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights > [!IMPORTANT] > As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCHT303)** Module 1 syllabus, the student is expected to: > - Derive the Maxwell relations from the four fundamental property relations. > - Apply the Maxwell relations to obtain relationships among measurable PVT quantities and entropy. > - Use the relations $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{\alpha}{\beta_T}$ and $\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\alpha}{\beta_T}\cdot R$ to perform **cyclic and non-cyclic derivations** in PVT systems. > - Apply these identities in real chemical engineering contexts (fugacity, residual properties, Joule-Thomson coefficient). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Exact Differentials The **entire derivation** of Maxwell's Equations rests on one elegant principle of calculus: **the equality of mixed second partial derivatives** for any continuous, well-behaved function. This is also called the **Schwarz-Clairaut theorem** or the **reciprocity condition**. For any state function $Z(x,y)$: $$dZ = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\right)_y dx + \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x dy = M\,dx + N\,dy$$ The condition that $dZ$ is an **exact differential** is: $$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter?** Because all four fundamental potentials ($U$, $H$, $A$, $G$) are **state functions** — their differentials are exact. Hence the reciprocity condition *must* hold, and this is what gives rise to the Maxwell relations. ## 2.2 The Four Fundamental Property Relations (Starting Point) These four equations are the **axioms** from which Maxwell's Equations are derived. Each is obtained by combining the **First Law** with the **Second Law** of Thermodynamics. | # | Thermodynamic Potential | Exact Differential Form | Natural Variables | |:--:|:--|:--|:--| | 1 | **Internal Energy** $U$ | $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ | $S, V$ | | 2 | **Enthalpy** $H$ | $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ | $S, P$ | | 3 | **Helmholtz Free Energy** $A$ | $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$ | $T, V$ | | 4 | **Gibbs Free Energy** $G$ | $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ | $T, P$ | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **THE MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize This for KTU Board Exams** | Source Potential | Maxwell Relation | Physical Significance | |:--|:--|:--| | From $U(S,V)$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$ | Slope of isentrope vs. entropy-pressure slope | | From $H(S,P)$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$ | Slope of isentrope vs. entropy-volume slope | | From $A(T,V)$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | **Most frequently used** — converts entropy to PVT | | From $G(T,P)$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | **Most frequently used** — converts entropy to PVT | ## 2.4 Derived Useful Identities (High-Yield) Using the definitions of **thermal expansion coefficient** $\alpha$ and **isothermal compressibility** $\beta_T$, we get the **bridge formulas**: $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{\alpha}{\beta_T}$$ $$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \alpha \cdot V$$ $$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\beta_T \cdot V$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility of These Bridge Formulas:** > 1. **Equations of State Development:** They let us generate $C_P - C_V$ relations, Joule-Thomson coefficients, and fugacity expressions without ever touching entropy. > 2. **Design of Compressors/Expanders:** The isentropic exponent and work integrals depend on $(\partial P/\partial V)_S$, which can be computed using $C_P / C_V$ and $\alpha, \beta_T$. > 3. **Refrigeration & Liquefaction Cycles:** Joule-Thomson inversion temperature is determined by $(\partial T/\partial P)_H$, which reduces (via Maxwell) to measurable PVT derivatives. > 4. **Residual Property Calculations:** Departure functions for $H^{R}$ and $S^{R}$ require these Maxwell-derived PVT integrals. ## 2.5 The Triple Product Rule (Auxiliary Tool) When three variables $x, y, z$ are related by $f(x,y,z) = 0$, the cyclic product of their partial derivatives equals **unity**: $$\left(\frac{\partial x}{\partial y}\right)_z \left(\frac{\partial y}{\partial z}\right)_x \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)_y = -1$$ This is a workhorse identity in deriving thermodynamic relations and is **frequently tested** in KTU exams as a 3-mark short answer. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Maxwell Relation #1 (from Internal Energy $U$) **Starting Equation:** The combined First and Second Law gives $$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$ Since $U = U(S, V)$, we can also write $$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V dS + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S dV$$ **Step 1 — Compare Coefficients of $dS$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V = T$$ **Step 2 — Compare Coefficients of $dV$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S = -P$$ **Step 3 — Apply Schwarz Reciprocity:** Since $U(S, V)$ has an exact differential, the mixed second partial derivatives must be equal: $$\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial V \, \partial S} = \frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial S \, \partial V}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate $T$ with respect to $V$ at constant $S$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = \frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial V \, \partial S}$$ **Step 5 — Differentiate $-P$ with respect to $S$ at constant $V$:** $$-\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V = \frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial S \, \partial V}$$ **Step 6 — Equate the two mixed derivatives:** $$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This relation is rarely used in practice because both sides involve entropy, which is hard to measure. However, it is the **first Maxwell relation**, and KTU examiners expect you to derive all four. ## 3.2 Derivation of Maxwell Relation #2 (from Enthalpy $H$) **Starting Equation:** $$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$ with $H = H(S, P)$. **Step 1 — Coefficient of $dS$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P = T$$ **Step 2 — Coefficient of $dP$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S = V$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate $T$ with respect to $P$ at constant $S$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \frac{\partial^2 H}{\partial P \, \partial S}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate $V$ with respect to $S$ at constant $P$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P = \frac{\partial^2 H}{\partial S \, \partial P}$$ **Step 5 — Apply reciprocity:** $$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Maxwell Relation #3 (from Helmholtz Free Energy $A$) **Starting Equation:** $$dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$$ with $A = A(T, V)$. **Step 1 — Coefficient of $dT$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V = -S$$ **Step 2 — Coefficient of $dV$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T = -P$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate $-S$ with respect to $V$ at constant $T$:** $$-\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \frac{\partial^2 A}{\partial V \, \partial T}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate $-P$ with respect to $T$ at constant $V$:** $$-\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T \, \partial V}$$ **Step 5 — Apply reciprocity:** $$-\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$ **Step 6 — Cancel the negative signs:** $$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the most-used Maxwell relation** because both derivatives involve *easily measurable* properties. $(\partial P / \partial T)_V$ can be obtained from any equation of state (e.g., van der Waals, Redlich-Kwong, virial), giving us direct access to entropy-volume relations. ## 3.4 Derivation of Maxwell Relation #4 (from Gibbs Free Energy $G$) **Starting Equation:** $$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$$ with $G = G(T, P)$. **Step 1 — Coefficient of $dT$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$$ **Step 2 — Coefficient of $dP$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T = V$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate $-S$ with respect to $P$ at constant $T$:** $$-\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = \frac{\partial^2 G}{\partial P \, \partial T}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate $V$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\partial^2 G}{\partial T \, \partial P}$$ **Step 5 — Apply reciprocity:** $$-\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$ **Step 6 — Rearrange:** $$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the OTHER most-used Maxwell relation** because $(\partial V / \partial T)_P$ is directly accessible from any cubic equation of state or from PVT experimental data. This is the workhorse identity for calculating **entropy changes in isothermal processes**, **fugacity**, and **departure functions**. ## 3.5 Master Summary Matrix — All Four Relations | Potential | Differential Form | Mixed Partial Identity | Final Maxwell Relation | |:--|:--|:--|:--| | $U$ | $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial V}\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial S}\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$ | | $H$ | $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial P}\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial S}\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$ | | $A$ | $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$ | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial V}\left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\dfrac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | | $G$ | $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial P}\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | ## 3.6 Worked Example — Applying Maxwell to Derive $\left(\partial P / \partial T\right)_V$ A common KTU 3-mark question asks to prove: $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{\alpha}{\beta_T}$$ **Step 1 — Start with Maxwell's 3rd relation:** $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the definition of $\alpha$:** $$\alpha = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$ **Step 3 — Apply the triple product rule to $(\partial V / \partial T)_P$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T \cdot \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange:** $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = -\frac{\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P}{\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T}$$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of $\alpha$ and $\beta_T$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = -\frac{\alpha V}{-\beta_T V} = \frac{\alpha}{\beta_T}$$ **Step 6 — Final Result:** $$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{\alpha}{\beta_T}\;}$$ This is **highly tested** in KTU exams. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Thermodynamic Potential Network This block diagram shows how the **four Maxwell relations** emerge from the **four fundamental potentials**, with their **natural variables** at the corners. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph FUNDAMENTAL_POTENTIALS["FUNDAMENTAL THERMODYNAMIC POTENTIALS"] direction TB N1["INTERNAL ENERGY U(S,V)
dU = T dS - P dV"] N2["ENTHALPY H(S,P)
dH = T dS + V dP"] N3["HELMHOLTZ FREE ENERGY A(T,V)
dA = -S dT - P dV"] N4["GIBBS FREE ENERGY G(T,P)
dG = -S dT + V dP"] end subgraph MAXWELL_RELATIONS["MAXWELL RELATIONS DERIVED"] direction TB M1["MR1: (dT/dV)_S = -(dP/dS)_V"] M2["MR2: (dT/dP)_S = (dV/dS)_P"] M3["MR3: (dS/dV)_T = (dP/dT)_V"] M4["MR4: (dS/dP)_T = -(dV/dT)_P"] end subgraph BRIDGE["BRIDGE TO PVT MEASURABLES"] direction TB B1["THERMAL EXPANSION alpha"] B2["ISOTHERMAL COMPRESSIBILITY beta_T"] B3["EQUATION OF STATE P(V,T)"] B4["C_P AND C_V RELATIONS"] end subgraph APPLICATIONS["CHEMICAL ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS"] direction TB A1["FUGACITY CALCULATION"] A2["ENTROPY DEPARTURE FUNCTIONS"] A3["JOULE THOMSON COEFFICIENT"] A4["ISENTROPIC PROCESS DESIGN"] end N1 --> M1 N2 --> M2 N3 --> M3 N4 --> M4 M3 --> B3 M4 --> B3 M3 --> B1 M4 --> B2 B1 --> A2 B2 --> A2 B3 --> A1 B4 --> A3 B4 --> A4 style N1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD style N2 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD style N3 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD style N4 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD style M1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style M2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style M3 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style M4 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style B1 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F style B2 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F style B3 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F style B4 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F style A1 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457 style A2 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457 style A3 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457 style A4 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Logical Derivation Pathway This diagram outlines the **logical flow** from the First and Second Laws to the final Maxwell relations. ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["FIRST LAW
dU = dQ - dW"] --> S2["SECOND LAW
dQ_rev = T dS"] S2 --> S3["COMBINED LAW
dU = T dS - P dV"] S3 --> S4["DEFINE H, A, G
via Legendre Transforms"] S4 --> S5["FOUR EXACT DIFFERENTIALS
for U, H, A, G"] S5 --> S6["APPLY SCHWARZ RECIPROCITY
Mixed second partials equal"] S6 --> S7["FOUR MAXWELL RELATIONS
MR1, MR2, MR3, MR4"] S7 --> S8["INTRODUCE alpha, beta_T
THERMAL EXPANSION AND COMPRESSIBILITY"] S8 --> S9["BRIDGE FORMULAS
Convert S derivatives to PVT"] S9 --> S10["ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS
Fugacity, Joule-Thomson, Departure Functions"] style S1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style S2 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style S3 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17 style S4 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17 style S5 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17 style S6 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style S7 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style S8 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style S9 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style S10 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State and derive the Maxwell relation obtained from the Helmholtz free energy $A(T, V)$.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Helmholtz free energy is defined as $A = U - TS$, with natural variables $T$ and $V$. Its exact differential is: $$dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$$ Since $dA$ is an exact differential, the reciprocity condition requires: $$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$ This is the Maxwell relation derived from $A$. **(1 mark)** for stating the relation, **(1 mark)** for starting equation, **(1 mark)** for showing the derivation. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Using a Maxwell relation, express $\left(\partial S / \partial V\right)_T$ in terms of measurable PVT properties.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** From the Maxwell relation derived from Helmholtz free energy: $$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$ Now using the definitions of thermal expansion coefficient $\alpha$ and isothermal compressibility $\beta_T$, we can write: $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{\alpha}{\beta_T}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \frac{\alpha}{\beta_T}\;}$$ **(1 mark)** for stating Maxwell relation, **(1 mark)** for the bridge formula, **(1 mark)** for the final boxed expression. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module-level ESE questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Each sub-part is typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Solutions must show full derivation steps and final boxed results. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive all four Maxwell relations starting from the exact differentials of $U$, $H$, $A$, and $G$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a gas obeying the van der Waals equation $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$, show using a Maxwell relation that the entropy change in an **isothermal expansion** from $V_1$ to $V_2$ is given by: $$\Delta S = R \ln\left(\frac{V_2 - b}{V_1 - b}\right)$$ **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A #### Part (a) — Derivation of All Four Maxwell Relations (7 Marks) **For $U(S, V)$:** $$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$ This gives $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V = T$ and $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S = -P$. Applying reciprocity: $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$ **(1.5 marks)** **For $H(S, P)$:** $$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$ This gives $\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P = T$ and $\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S = V$. Applying reciprocity: $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$ **(1.5 marks)** **For $A(T, V)$:** $$dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$$ This gives $\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V = -S$ and $\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T = -P$. Applying reciprocity: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ **(2 marks)** **For $G(T, P)$:** $$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$$ This gives $\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$ and $\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T = V$. Applying reciprocity: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ **(2 marks)** --- #### Part (b) — Entropy Change for van der Waals Gas (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Write the Maxwell relation we need:** $$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$ **Step 2 — Express $P$ from the van der Waals equation:** $$P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate $P$ with respect to $T$ at constant $V$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{R}{V - b} - 0 = \frac{R}{V - b}$$ (Note: The term $-a/V^2$ is independent of $T$, so it drops out.) **Step 4 — Apply the Maxwell relation:** $$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \frac{R}{V - b}$$ **Step 5 — Integrate from $V_1$ to $V_2$ at constant $T$:** $$\Delta S = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{R}{V - b}\,dV = R \ln(V - b)\Big|_{V_1}^{V_2}$$ **Step 6 — Final Result:** $$\boxed{\;\Delta S = R \ln\left(\frac{V_2 - b}{V_1 - b}\right)\;}$$ **(1 mark)** for Maxwell relation, **(2 marks)** for differentiation, **(2 marks)** for integration setup, **(2 marks)** for final result and conclusion. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation: $$\left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\frac{H}{T^2}$$ using the relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$ and the definition $G = H - TS$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A real gas follows the relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \dfrac{R}{V - b}$ (Redlich-Kwong-like). If the molar volume is $V = 0.005 \; \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$ and $b = 0.0001 \; \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$ at $T = 400 \; \text{K}$, calculate the entropy change per mole when the gas is **isothermally expanded** to $V = 0.010 \; \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$. Take $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B #### Part (a) — Derivation of the Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Start with the two definitions:** $$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S \quad \text{and} \quad G = H - TS$$ **Step 2 — Solve the second equation for $S$:** $$S = \frac{H - G}{T}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the first equation:** $$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\frac{H - G}{T}$$ **Step 4 — Cross-multiply by $T$:** $$T\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -(H - G) = G - H$$ **Step 5 — Divide both sides by $T^2$:** $$\frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{G}{T^2} - \frac{H}{T^2}$$ **Step 6 — Recognize the left side as a derivative of $G/T$:** $$\frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P \cdot \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\bigg|_P \cdot \frac{T}{T} = \left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P$$ (Mathematically, $\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T} = \frac{1}{T}\frac{\partial G}{\partial T} - \frac{G}{T^2}$... so we need to recombine.) **Step 7 — Correct derivation using quotient rule:** $$\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T} = \frac{T \cdot (\partial G/\partial T)_P - G}{T^2} = \frac{T \cdot (-S) - G}{T^2} = \frac{-TS - G}{T^2}$$ **Step 8 — Substitute $G = H - TS$:** $$= \frac{-TS - (H - TS)}{T^2} = \frac{-TS - H + TS}{T^2} = \frac{-H}{T^2}$$ **Step 9 — Final Result:** $$\boxed{\;\left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\frac{H}{T^2}\;}$$ **(2 marks)** for setting up the definitions, **(2 marks)** for quotient rule application, **(2 marks)** for substitution and final result, **(1 mark)** for proper notation and box. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Entropy Calculation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Apply the Maxwell relation:** $$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{R}{V - b}$$ **Step 2 — Set up the integral:** $$\Delta S = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{R}{V - b}\,dV = R \ln\left(\frac{V_2 - b}{V_1 - b}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:** - $V_1 = 0.005 \; \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$ - $V_2 = 0.010 \; \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$ - $b = 0.0001 \; \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$ - $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$ **Step 4 — Compute $V_1 - b$ and $V_2 - b$:** $$V_1 - b = 0.005 - 0.0001 = 0.0049 \; \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$ $$V_2 - b = 0.010 - 0.0001 = 0.0099 \; \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the ratio:** $$\frac{V_2 - b}{V_1 - b} = \frac{0.0099}{0.0049} = 2.0204$$ **Step 6 — Compute $\ln(2.0204) \approx 0.7031$:** $$\Delta S = 8.314 \times 0.7031 = 5.846 \; \text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$$ **Step 7 — Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\;\Delta S \approx 5.85 \; \text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}\;}$$ **(1 mark)** for Maxwell relation, **(1 mark)** for integral setup, **(2 marks)** for correct substitution, **(2 marks)** for arithmetic, **(1 mark)** for boxed result with units. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Board Exams** > > 1. **Sign Errors (Most Common — ~2 marks lost per question):** The Maxwell relations have very specific signs. MR1 has a negative sign on the right; MR4 has a negative sign on the right. MR2 and MR3 are positive. Students often flip these. **Tip:** Always re-derive from the potential's differential form — never memorize blind. > > 2. **Forgetting the Reciprocity Step:** Students sometimes write the coefficients directly without explicitly invoking the equality of mixed second partial derivatives. Examiners **require this step** for full marks. > > 3. **Notation Confusion:** $A$ (Helmholtz) vs $a$ (van der Waals constant) — these are entirely different! Be careful when both appear in a question. > > 4. **Missing Constant-of-Integration Context:** When integrating Maxwell-derived expressions (like Part (b) of Question A), students often forget that integration assumes **constant temperature** (isothermal process). Always state the constraint. > > 5. **Not Boxing the Final Result:** KTU examiners *do* deduct 0.5 mark if the final answer is not enclosed in a box ($\boxed{\,}$ or similar). Always box. > > 6. **Incorrect Units in Numerical Problems:** $(\partial P/\partial T)_V$ has units of $\text{Pa/K}$. Students sometimes write $\text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}$ (which is the entropy unit). Keep units consistent throughout. > > 7. **Missing the Cyclic/Triple Product Rule Application:** Many 7-mark questions in KTU require use of the triple product rule $(\partial x/\partial y)_z (\partial y/\partial z)_x (\partial z/\partial x)_y = -1$ as an intermediate step. Skipping this leads to 2-mark penalty. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Maxwell's Equations** > > - **4 Fundamental Potentials:** $U(S,V)$, $H(S,P)$, $A(T,V)$, $G(T,P)$ — memorize their differential forms. > - **4 Maxwell Relations:** Derived using Schwarz reciprocity (mixed second partials are equal). > - **MR1:** $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$ (from $U$) > - **MR2:** $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$ (from $H$) > - **MR3:** $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ (from $A$) — **Most Used** > - **MR4:** $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ (from $G$) — **Most Used** > - **Bridge Formulas:** > - $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{\alpha}{\beta_T}$ > - $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \frac{\alpha}{\beta_T}$ > - $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\alpha V$ > - **Triple Product Rule:** $\left(\frac{\partial x}{\partial y}\right)_z \left(\frac{\partial y}{\partial z}\right)_x \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)_y = -1$ > - **Key Definitions:** > - $\alpha = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ (thermal expansion coefficient) > - $\beta_T = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$ (isothermal compressibility) > - **Sign Convention Memory Trick:** Natural variables $(T, V)$ always pair to give the same sign in the Maxwell relation. Natural variables $(T, P)$ always pair to give **opposite** signs. This is because the potentials $U, H, A, G$ alternate in sign conventions. > - **Engineering Application Power Zones:** > - Entropy change in isothermal process: $\Delta S = \int (\partial S/\partial V)_T dV$ (uses MR3) > - Entropy change in isobaric process: $\Delta S = \int (\partial S/\partial P)_T dP$ (uses MR4) > - Fugacity coefficient derivation: depends on MR3 + virial equation > - Joule-Thomson coefficient: $\mu_{JT} = (V/C_P)(T\alpha - 1)$, derived using MR4 > - **Always state the reciprocity theorem explicitly** in derivations — this is a 1-mark line item. > - **Box every final answer** in KTU exams. > - **Memorize the chain rule of partial derivatives** as a backup tool for problems involving multiple Maxwell relations.

topics.title: Clausius-Clapeyron Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Clausius-Clapeyron Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation** is a fundamental thermodynamic relation that quantitatively describes the slope of the **phase coexistence boundary** (the curve separating two phases of a pure substance) on a Pressure–Temperature (P–T) diagram. It relates the change in saturation (vapor) pressure with temperature to the latent heat of phase change and the molar volume change between the two phases.

Mathematically, the **differential (Clapeyron) form** is:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}$$

where $\Delta H_{vap}$ is the molar enthalpy of vaporization, $T$ is the absolute temperature, and $\Delta V$ is the difference in molar volumes between the vapor and liquid phases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** This equation is a high-weightage topic linking the First Law, PVT behavior, and pure component phase equilibria. It is frequently tested in both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) of the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a kettle of water boiling on a stove. At sea level (**P = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa**), water boils at **100 °C (373.15 K)**. If you climb a mountain, the atmospheric pressure drops, and you will notice the water boils at a much lower temperature — perhaps **90 °C** — and your tea takes longer to brew because the kinetic energy available for cooking is reduced.

The Clausius-Clapeyron equation is essentially the **mathematical translator of this everyday experience**. It tells us *exactly* how much the boiling temperature must change for a given change in pressure, using the *molecular information* stored in the latent heat ($\Delta H_{vap}$) and the molar volume difference ($\Delta V$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants You Must Memorize:**
> - Universal Gas Constant: $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**
> - Normal Boiling Point of Water: $T_b = 373.15$ **K** at $P = 101.325$ **kPa**
> - For water at 100 °C: $\Delta H_{vap} \approx 40.656$ **kJ/mol**

### Engineering Significance

The Clausius-Clapeyron equation is the **workhorse** of chemical engineering design for:

- **Distillation column design** (predicting component volatility)
- **Boiler and evaporator design**
- **Refrigeration cycle analysis** (predicting refrigerant saturation pressures)
- **Meteorology and weather prediction** (water vapor in atmosphere)
- **Process safety** (predicting flash points and vapor cloud behavior)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Thermodynamic Foundations

The derivation rests on three core principles of pure-component phase equilibrium:

1. **Equality of Chemical Potentials** at the phase boundary: $\mu^{\alpha}(T, P) = \mu^{\beta}(T, P)$
2. **Equality of Molar Gibbs Free Energies** at coexistence: $G^{\alpha} = G^{\beta}$
3. **Clapeyron's Identity**: $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$

Because two phases coexist along a single curve, the system has only **one degree of freedom** (Gibbs Phase Rule for 1 component and 2 phases: $F = C - P + 2 = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$).

### Logical Steps of the Derivation Logic

- **Step 1:** Begin with the equality of molar Gibbs energies at the coexistence curve: $G^{\alpha} = G^{\beta}$
- **Step 2:** Take the total differential of both sides along the curve. Since they remain equal at the new point, the differentials must be equal: $dG^{\alpha} = dG^{\beta}$
- **Step 3:** Substitute $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ for each phase and equate.
- **Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate the slope $\dfrac{dP}{dT}$.
- **Step 5:** Recognize that the entropy change is related to the latent heat via $\Delta S = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T}$ (reversible phase change at constant T and P).
- **Step 6:** Substitute to obtain the **Clausius-Clapeyron differential form**.
- **Step 7:** Apply the **ideal gas assumption** for the vapor phase and neglect liquid molar volume relative to vapor (since $V_{vap} \gg V_{liq}$).
- **Step 8:** Integrate to get the **integrated form** used for engineering calculations.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation / Form** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Application / When to Use** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clapeyron Equation (General) | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}$ | Exact; for any pure-component phase transition | P in Pa, T in K, V in m³/mol, H in J/mol |
| Clausius-Clapeyron (Ideal Gas, $\Delta V \approx V_{vap}$) | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta H_{vap}}{R \cdot T^{2}}$ | When vapor behaves as ideal gas; liquid V is negligible | Same as above |
| Separated Differential Form | $\dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{dT}{T^{2}}$ | Ready for integration | Dimensionless form |
| **Integrated Form (constant $\Delta H_{vap}$)** | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$ | **Most frequently tested in KTU exams** | P in same units, T in K |
| Linear Form (Y = mX + C) | $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + C$ | Plotting $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ gives a straight line; slope $= -\Delta H_{vap}/R$ | — |
| Trouton's Rule (estimate) | $\Delta H_{vap} \approx 88 \cdot T_b$ (J/mol·K) | Rough estimate of latent heat from normal boiling point | T in K |
| Latent Heat from Clausius-Clapeyron | $\Delta H_{vap} = -R \cdot (\text{slope of } \ln P \text{ vs } 1/T)$ | Experimental determination of $\Delta H_{vap}$ | J/mol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The slope $\dfrac{dP}{dT}$ is **always positive** along the vaporization and fusion curves (since both $\Delta H_{vap} > 0$ and $\Delta V > 0$). Only along the **sublimation curve** does it become negative for substances like water (anomalous expansion on freezing).

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **petrochemical refinery**, the Clausius-Clapeyron equation is used to predict how the bubble point of a crude oil mixture will shift in a distillation column as operating pressure changes. By integrating the equation, engineers can determine at what temperature naphtha, kerosene, and diesel will condense at a given tray pressure, allowing them to design column internals and reflux ratios accurately.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: The General Clapeyron Equation

**Starting Premise:** At equilibrium between phase $\alpha$ and phase $\beta$, the molar Gibbs free energies are equal:

$$G^{\alpha}(T, P) = G^{\beta}(T, P)$$

**Step 1 — Total Differential of Both Sides:**
Taking the total differential along the coexistence curve:

$$dG^{\alpha} = dG^{\beta}$$

**Step 2 — Substituting $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$:**

$$-S^{\alpha}\,dT + V^{\alpha}\,dP = -S^{\beta}\,dT + V^{\beta}\,dP$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging (grouping dT and dP terms):**

$$(V^{\beta} - V^{\alpha})\,dP = (S^{\beta} - S^{\alpha})\,dT$$

$$\Delta V\,dP = \Delta S\,dT$$

**Step 4 — Isolating the Slope $dP/dT$:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta V}$$

**Step 5 — Using the Reversible Phase Change Identity:**

For a reversible phase change at constant T and P, the entropy change is:

$$\Delta S = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T}$$

**Step 6 — Final General Clapeyron Equation:**

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This form is exact. It does not assume ideal gas behavior. The negative sign convention is avoided because $\Delta H_{vap}$ and $\Delta V$ for vaporization are both positive, giving a positive slope — exactly what we observe on P–T diagrams.

---

### Derivation 2: The Clausius-Clapeyron Equation (Ideal Gas + Negligible Liquid Volume)

**Assumption 1:** The vapor phase behaves as an ideal gas:

$$V_{vap} = \frac{RT}{P}$$

**Assumption 2:** The molar volume of the liquid is negligible compared to vapor:

$$\Delta V = V_{vap} - V_{liq} \approx V_{vap} = \frac{RT}{P}$$

**Step 1 — Substituting into the Clapeyron Equation:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \left(\dfrac{RT}{P}\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Simplifying the Right-Hand Side:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap} \cdot P}{R \cdot T^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Separating Variables:**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Integrating Between States 1 and 2 (assuming $\Delta H_{vap}$ is constant over the temperature range):**

$$\int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluating the Integrals:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Final Integrated Form:**

$$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **most heavily tested form in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions**. Given any 3 of the 4 variables (P₁, P₂, T₁, T₂), the fourth can be computed. The temperature **must be in Kelvin**.

---

### Derivation 3: The Antoine-Type Linear Form (Y = mX + C)

**Step 1 — Rewrite the integrated form with P₂ = P and T₂ = T:**

$$\ln P = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C$$

**Step 2 — Identify Slope and Intercept:**

$$\text{Slope } (m) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}$$

$$\text{Intercept } (C) = \ln P_{1} + \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R \cdot T_{1}}$$

**Step 3 — Experimental Determination of $\Delta H_{vap}$:**

$$\Delta H_{vap} = -R \cdot m$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This linear form is the foundation of **Antoine Equation** extensions used industrially. By measuring vapor pressure at multiple temperatures and plotting $\ln P$ versus $1/T$, engineers extract the latent heat directly from the slope.

---

### Worked Example: KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** The vapor pressure of water is 101.325 kPa at 100 °C. Calculate the vapor pressure at 110 °C. Given: $\Delta H_{vap} = 40.656$ kJ/mol. (Use $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Units:**

$$T_1 = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 110 + 273.15 = 383.15 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta H_{vap} = 40.656 \text{ kJ/mol} = 40656 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Integrated Clausius-Clapeyron Equation:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Reciprocal Difference:**

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{383.15} - \frac{1}{373.15}$$

$$= 0.0026102 - 0.0026800 = -6.978 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{40656}{8.314} \times (-6.978 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$= 4889.9 \times 6.978 \times 10^{-5} = 0.3413$$

**Step 5 — Solve for P₂:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = e^{0.3413} = 1.4069$$

$$P_2 = 1.4069 \times 101.325 = 142.55 \text{ kPa}$$

> **Answer:** The vapor pressure of water at 110 °C is approximately **142.55 kPa**, which matches the steam table value of 143.38 kPa within engineering accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Derivation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram traces the logical flow from the foundational thermodynamic postulate (chemical potential equality) to the final integrated form used in KTU exam calculations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START Phase Coexistence Condition"] --> B["Molar Gibbs Energy Equality: G alpha equals G beta"]
    B --> C["Take Total Differential: dG alpha equals dG beta"]
    C --> D["Substitute dG equals minus S dT plus V dP for Both Phases"]
    D --> E["Rearrange to: Delta V dP equals Delta S dT"]
    E --> F["Isolate Slope: dP by dT equals Delta S by Delta V"]
    F --> G["Apply Reversible Phase Change: Delta S equals Delta H vap by T"]
    G --> H["General Clapeyron Equation: dP by dT equals Delta H vap by T times Delta V"]
    H --> I["Apply Ideal Gas Assumption: V vap equals RT by P"]
    I --> J["Neglect Liquid Molar Volume: Delta V approx V vap"]
    J --> K["Clausius Form: dP by dT equals P times Delta H vap by R T squared"]
    K --> L["Separate Variables: dP by P equals Delta H vap by R times dT by T squared"]
    L --> M["Integrate from State 1 to State 2 assuming constant Delta H vap"]
    M --> N["Final Integrated Form: ln of P2 by P1 equals minus Delta H vap by R times one by T2 minus one by T1"]
    N --> O["Linear Form: ln P equals minus Delta H vap by R times one by T plus C"]
    O --> P["END KTU Exam Ready Formulas"]
```

### P–T Phase Diagram Schematic (Text Representation)

```
    P (kPa)
    ^
    |              *  (Critical Point)
    |             /|
    |            / |
    |           /  |
    |          /   |
    |         /    |
    |        /     |  <-- Vaporization curve (dP/dT > 0)
    |       /      |      (Clausius-Clapeyron applies)
    |      /       |
    |     /        |
    |    /         |
    |   /          |
    |  /           |
    | /            |
    |/_____________*_______> T (K)
    |   Triple     \
    |    Point      \
    |                \  <-- Sublimation curve
    |                 \    (dP/dT < 0 for water)
    v
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each boundary curve (solid, dashed, and dotted) is mathematically described by the Clapeyron equation. The slope of the vaporization curve is what Clausius-Clapeyron specifically helps you compute, given $\Delta H_{vap}$ and $\Delta V$ (or the ideal gas simplification).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Clapeyron equation and explain the significance of each term involved.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Clapeyron equation gives the slope of the phase coexistence curve on a P–T diagram:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}$$

- $dP/dT$: Slope of the phase boundary on the P–T diagram
- $\Delta H_{vap}$: Molar latent heat of phase transition (J/mol)
- $T$: Absolute temperature at coexistence (K)
- $\Delta V$: Difference in molar volumes between the two coexisting phases (m³/mol)

**Significance:** It enables quantitative prediction of how saturation pressure changes with temperature — essential for designing distillation columns, evaporators, and boilers. **(1 mark for equation, 1 mark for terms, 1 mark for significance)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**How is the Clausius-Clapeyron equation different from the Clapeyron equation? List the assumptions made in deriving the former.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Clapeyron Equation** | **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation** |
|---|---|
| Exact; applies to all phase transitions | Approximate; specific to vaporization with ideal gas vapor |
| Uses actual $\Delta V$ | Approximates $\Delta V \approx V_{vap} = RT/P$ |

**Assumptions:**
1. The vapor phase behaves as an **ideal gas**.
2. The molar volume of the **liquid phase is negligible** compared to the vapor phase ($\Delta V \approx V_{vap}$).
3. The **latent heat of vaporization $\Delta H_{vap}$ is constant** over the temperature range of interest.

**(1 mark for difference, 2 marks for assumptions)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the Clausius-Clapeyron equation in its integrated form starting from the condition of phase equilibrium. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The vapor pressure of benzene is 100 kPa at 80.1 °C. Calculate its vapor pressure at 50 °C. The enthalpy of vaporization of benzene is 30.8 kJ/mol. ($R = 8.314$ J/mol·K) **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The detailed step-by-step derivation is shown in **Section 3, Derivation 1 and 2** above. The key marks allocation is:

- **(2 marks)** Starting from $G^{\alpha} = G^{\beta}$, taking the differential, and arriving at $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta S}{\Delta V}$.
- **(2 marks)** Substituting $\Delta S = \Delta H_{vap}/T$ to get the general Clapeyron equation.
- **(2 marks)** Applying ideal gas and $V_{liq} \ll V_{vap}$ assumptions, then integrating to obtain $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$.
- **(1 mark)** Stating all three assumptions clearly.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert temperatures to Kelvin.

$$T_1 = 80.1 + 273.15 = 353.25 \text{ K}$$
$$T_2 = 50 + 273.15 = 323.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Convert $\Delta H_{vap}$ to J/mol.

$$\Delta H_{vap} = 30.8 \times 1000 = 30800 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 3:** Apply integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{100}\right) = -\frac{30800}{8.314} \left(\frac{1}{323.15} - \frac{1}{353.25}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Compute the bracket.

$$\frac{1}{323.15} - \frac{1}{353.25} = 0.003095 - 0.002831 = 2.640 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate RHS.

$$\text{RHS} = -3704.6 \times 2.640 \times 10^{-4} = -0.9780$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $P_2$.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{100}\right) = -0.9780 \implies \frac{P_2}{100} = e^{-0.9780} = 0.3761$$

$$P_2 = 37.61 \text{ kPa}$$

> **Final Answer:** The vapor pressure of benzene at 50 °C is **≈ 37.61 kPa**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Starting from the equality of chemical potentials, derive the Clapeyron equation for a pure substance undergoing a phase transition. Explain why $dP/dT$ is positive along the vaporization curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The normal boiling point of a liquid is 350 K and its enthalpy of vaporization is 30 kJ/mol. Estimate the boiling point of the liquid at a pressure of 50 kPa using the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. Given: Normal pressure $P_1 = 101.325$ kPa, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** Equality of chemical potentials at phase coexistence: $\mu^{\alpha} = \mu^{\beta}$ (since $\mu = G_m$ for pure substances).
- **(2 marks)** Total differential: $d\mu^{\alpha} = d\mu^{\beta}$, and using $d\mu = -S_m dT + V_m dP$, we get $-S_m^{\alpha} dT + V_m^{\alpha} dP = -S_m^{\beta} dT + V_m^{\beta} dP$.
- **(2 marks)** Rearranging: $\Delta V_m\, dP = \Delta S_m\, dT \implies \dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta S_{vap}}{\Delta V_{vap}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{vap}}$.
- **(1 mark)** The slope is positive because both $\Delta H_{vap} > 0$ (heat absorbed) and $\Delta V_{vap} > 0$ (vapor occupies more volume than liquid) for a normal substance.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Given data.

$$T_1 = 350 \text{ K}, \quad P_1 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}, \quad P_2 = 50 \text{ kPa}$$
$$\Delta H_{vap} = 30000 \text{ J/mol}, \quad R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply integrated Clausius-Clapeyron.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{50}{101.325}\right) = -\frac{30000}{8.314} \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{350}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Compute LHS.

$$\ln(0.4935) = -0.7062$$

**Step 4:** Compute coefficient.

$$-\frac{30000}{8.314} = -3608.13$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the bracket.

$$-0.7062 = -3608.13 \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{350}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{350} = \frac{-0.7062}{-3608.13} = 1.9574 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $T_2$.

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 1.9574 \times 10^{-4} + 0.002857 = 0.003053$$

$$T_2 = 327.5 \text{ K} \approx 54.4 \text{ °C}$$

> **Final Answer:** The boiling point of the liquid at 50 kPa is **T₂ ≈ 327.5 K (54.4 °C)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (-2 marks):** Students frequently use °C instead of K in the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. **Always convert to Kelvin first.**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $\Delta H$ to J/mol (-1 mark):** If the latent heat is given in kJ/mol, convert it before plugging into the equation.
> 3. **Sign Error in the Bracket (-2 marks):** Note the equation uses $\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ with a negative sign outside. Common error is writing $\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$.
> 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions (-1 mark):** The three assumptions (ideal gas, $V_{liq} \approx 0$, constant $\Delta H_{vap}$) must be explicitly written for full marks in derivation questions.
> 5. **Using ln vs log₁₀ (-0.5 mark):** The natural logarithm (ln, base e) is used because R = 8.314 J/mol·K is in natural units.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Clapeyron equation** is the **exact** form: $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}$
- **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** is the **integrated, ideal-gas form** — the most tested in KTU exams.
- **Integrated form:** $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$
- **Linear form:** $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + C$ — slope gives $-\Delta H_{vap}/R$
- **Three assumptions:** Ideal gas vapor, $V_{liq} \approx 0$, $\Delta H_{vap} \approx$ constant over the T range
- **Always use Kelvin** for temperature in the equation
- **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** is the standard value used
- **The slope $dP/dT$ is positive** for vaporization (since $\Delta H_{vap} > 0$ and $\Delta V > 0$)
- **Anomaly for water:** $\Delta V_{fusion} < 0$ (ice floats on water), so fusion curve has a **negative slope** — an important KTU viva question
- **Trouton's Rule:** $\Delta H_{vap} \approx 88 \cdot T_b$ J/(mol·K) is a quick estimate
- The equation is the **mathematical foundation** of the Antoine equation used in process simulators like Aspen Plus and HYSYS
- For a 14-mark derivation question, marks are split as: **(2 marks) starting point + (2 marks) differential step + (2 marks) Clapeyron form + (2 marks) assumptions + (2 marks) integration + (1 mark) final form + (3 marks) numerical part** — keep this allocation in mind when practicing

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entropy – Heat Capacity Relationships – Equations for Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Entropy – Heat Capacity Relationships – Equations for Entropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Entropy — The Rigorous Thermodynamic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entropy (S)** is an extensive thermodynamic property (units: **J·K⁻¹** or **kJ·K⁻¹**) that quantifies the degree of molecular disorder, randomness, or the number of microstates accessible to a macroscopic system. It is a **point function (state function)**, meaning its change between two equilibrium states is path-independent.

The defining mathematical relation for entropy, introduced through the **Second Law of Thermodynamics (Clausius Inequality / Equality)**, is given by:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}\right)_{sys}$$

For a finite, reversible change between two equilibrium states **State 1** and **State 2**:

$$\Delta S = \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constraint:** The integration must be carried out along a **reversible path** connecting the initial and final states. Because S is a state function, $\Delta S$ computed along this hypothetical reversible path is identical to the actual process entropy change, regardless of whether the real process is reversible or irreversible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Coffee Cup" View)

Imagine pouring a hot cup of coffee on a desk in a perfectly insulated (adiabatic) room.

* **Initially:** The coffee molecules are highly energetic and organized in a localized hot cluster.
* **Eventually:** Energy disperses throughout the entire room, the coffee cools, and the room slightly warms. We can never spontaneously reverse this — the room will not suddenly re-heat the coffee.

This **one-way spreading of energy** is entropy. Think of entropy as the universe's measure of *where energy is hiding*. High entropy = energy is smeared out and useless for doing work. Low entropy = energy is concentrated and available. The **Second Law** simply states the universe's entropy can never decrease spontaneously in an isolated system.

## 1.3 Heat Capacity — The Engine Driving Entropy

Heat capacity is the bridge between **energy (Q)** and **temperature (T)**, and therefore the direct link to entropy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Capacity (C)** is the amount of heat (Q) required to raise the temperature of a system by **1 Kelvin (K)**.

Two principal forms are used universally in chemical engineering:

| Form | Symbol | Definition | Typical Units |
|------|--------|------------|---------------|
| Heat Capacity at Constant Volume | $C_v$ | $\left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{dT}\right)_V = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | **kJ·kmol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| Heat Capacity at Constant Pressure | $C_p$ | $\left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{dT}\right)_P = \left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | **kJ·kmol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |

For a closed, non-flow system, the molar heat capacities are commonly modelled using empirical polynomial correlations of the form:

$$\frac{C_p}{R} = a + bT + cT^{2} + dT^{-2}$$

where **a, b, c, d** are gas-specific empirical constants (tabulated in standard thermodynamic data references) and **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** is the universal gas constant.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Chemical Engineering

In KTU's Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics framework, this topic forms the **computational backbone** for:
* Compressors, turbines, expanders (isentropic design)
* Refrigeration and liquefaction cycles
* Reactor heat management (entropy generation analysis)
* Vapor-compression and absorption cycles
* Exergy (availability) calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Building Blocks

### 2.1.1 The Maxwell Relations (Recap)
The four Maxwell relations, derived from the exact differentials of U, H, A, and G, are the engine that converts "hard" derivatives into "easy" measurable ones:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

### 2.1.2 The Combined First and Second Law (Tds Equations)
Combining the First Law ($dU = \delta Q - \delta W$) with the Second Law ($dS = \delta Q_{rev}/T$) for a closed, reversible system ($\delta W = PdV$) yields:

$$T\,dS = dU + P\,dV$$

Since enthalpy is defined as $H = U + PV$ (so $dH = dU + PdV + VdP$), substitution gives:

$$T\,dS = dH - V\,dP$$

These are the famous **"Tds Equations"** — the master relations from which all entropy expressions are derived.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Reference Table** — Memorize these for guaranteed marks in the KTU board exam.

| # | Equation | Conditions | Purpose |
|---|----------|------------|---------|
| 1 | $C_p - C_v = T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | General (any pure substance) | Relates $C_p$ and $C_v$ generally |
| 2 | $C_p - C_v = R$ | Ideal gas only | Most-used relation in board problems |
| 3 | $C_p - C_v = \dfrac{TV\beta^{2}}{\kappa_T}$ | Compressible liquid/solid | Real-fluid relation |
| 4 | $T\,dS = C_v\,dT + T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV$ | Closed system, general | Tds in terms of $(T,V)$ |
| 5 | $T\,dS = C_p\,dT - T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$ | Closed system, general | Tds in terms of $(T,P)$ |
| 6 | $dS = C_p\dfrac{dT}{T} - R\dfrac{dP}{P}$ | Ideal gas, closed system | Primary ideal-gas entropy relation |
| 7 | $dS = C_v\dfrac{dT}{T} + R\dfrac{dV}{V}$ | Ideal gas, closed system | Volume-based ideal-gas relation |
| 8 | $\Delta S = C_p\ln\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - R\ln\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Ideal gas, $C_p$ = constant | **Most-asked KTU formula** |
| 9 | $\Delta S = C_v\ln\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} + R\ln\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$ | Ideal gas, $C_p$ = constant | Volume-based analog |
| 10 | $\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\!\dfrac{C_p}{T}\,dT - R\ln\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Ideal gas, $C_p = f(T)$ | Variable heat capacity |
| 11 | $P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$ | Reversible adiabatic (isentropic), ideal gas | Process relation |
| 12 | $T V^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$ | Reversible adiabatic, ideal gas | Temperature–Volume |
| 13 | $T P^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma} = \text{constant}$ | Reversible adiabatic, ideal gas | Temperature–Pressure |
| 14 | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1}$ | Isentropic, ideal gas | End-state temperature |

**Where:** $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ is the **specific heat ratio** (dimensionless; ~1.4 for air, ~1.3 for CO₂, ~1.67 for monatomic gases).

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications

* **Compressor/Turbine Design:** Isentropic efficiency is benchmarked against Equations 11–14.
* **Nozzle Flow:** Rocket and jet nozzles are designed assuming isentropic expansion.
* **Reactor Safety:** Entropy generation indicates irreversibility and lost work potential.
* **HVAC & Refrigeration:** Coefficient of Performance (COP) calculations require entropy balances.
* **Cryogenics:** Liquefaction cycles (Linde-Hampson, Claude) use Tds equations extensively.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The General Tds Equations

### Step 1: Start with the First Law for a Closed, Reversible System
The First Law in differential form for a closed, reversible system is:
$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev} = \delta Q_{rev} - P\,dV$$

### Step 2: Apply the Second Law (Clausius Definition of Entropy)
The differential definition of entropy is:
$$\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$$

### Step 3: Combine Both Laws
Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:
$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$
Rearranging:
$$T\,dS = dU + P\,dV \quad \text{...(Equation A — Tds-1)}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Enthalpy Form
The enthalpy is defined as:
$$H = U + PV \quad \Rightarrow \quad dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

Therefore:
$$dU + P\,dV = dH - V\,dP$$

### Step 5: Substitute Into Equation A
$$T\,dS = dH - V\,dP \quad \text{...(Equation B — Tds-2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These two **Tds equations** are the starting point for *every* subsequent entropy derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — General $C_p - C_v$ Relation

### Step 1: Express U(T,V) Total Differential
Treating internal energy as a function of T and V:
$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

### Step 2: Identify the Coefficients
By definition: $C_v = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$

So:
$$dU = C_v\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

### Step 3: Substitute into Tds-1
$$T\,dS = C_v\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV + P\,dV$$

$$T\,dS = C_v\,dT + \left[\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T + P\right] dV$$

### Step 4: Use a Maxwell Relation to Eliminate the Unknown
From the Helmholtz function exact differential, the Maxwell relation gives:
$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back
$$T\,dS = C_v\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P + P\right] dV$$

$$\boxed{T\,dS = C_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV}$$

Dividing both sides by T:
$$dS = \frac{C_v}{T}\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV$$

### Step 6: Apply to Ideal Gas
For an ideal gas: $PV = RT \Rightarrow \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \dfrac{R}{V}$

$$T\,dS = C_v\,dT + T\cdot\frac{R}{V}\,dV$$

Dividing by T:
$$dS = C_v\frac{dT}{T} + R\frac{dV}{V}$$

This is **Equation 7** in the Formula Sheet.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The $C_p - C_v = R$ Identity for Ideal Gas

### Step 1: Repeat the Derivation Using Enthalpy
Express H(T,P):
$$dH = C_p\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

### Step 2: Maxwell Relation for H
$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = V - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

### Step 3: Substitute into Tds-2
$$T\,dS = C_p\,dT + \left[V - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP - V\,dP$$

$$T\,dS = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

Dividing by T:
$$dS = \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

### Step 4: Apply to Ideal Gas
$PV = RT \Rightarrow \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{R}{P}$

$$\boxed{dS = C_p\frac{dT}{T} - R\frac{dP}{P}}$$

This is **Equation 6** — the most-tested relation in KTU.

### Step 5: Integrate for a Finite Change
$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p\frac{dT}{T} - \int_{P_1}^{P_2} R\frac{dP}{P}$$

For $C_p$ = constant (the standard KTU assumption):
$$\boxed{\Delta S = C_p\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} - R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}}$$

This is **Equation 8**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The General $C_p - C_v$ Equation

Starting from the Tds-1 form: $T\,dS = C_v\,dT + T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV$

Rearrange to solve for $dS$:
$$dS = \frac{C_v}{T}\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV$$

Now consider entropy as $S(T,P)$:
$$dS = \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

Equating the two expressions at constant V (dV = 0):
$$\frac{C_v}{T}\,dT = \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

Rearranging:
$$(C_p - C_v)\frac{dT}{T} = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{C_p - C_v}{T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P}$$

Using the cyclic rule and the compressibility definitions $\beta = \dfrac{1}{V}\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ and $\kappa_T = -\dfrac{1}{V}\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$:

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = \frac{TV\beta^{2}}{\kappa_T}}$$

This is **Equation 3** — used for solids and liquids.

For an **ideal gas**: $\beta = 1/T$, $\kappa_T = 1/P$, and $PV = RT$:
$$C_p - C_v = \frac{T \cdot (RT/P) \cdot (1/T^{2})}{1/P} = R$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic) Relations for Ideal Gas

### Step 1: Set Conditions
For a **reversible adiabatic** process:
$$\delta Q_{rev} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad dS = 0 \quad \text{(isentropic)}$$

### Step 2: From $dS = C_p\dfrac{dT}{T} - R\dfrac{dP}{P} = 0$

$$C_p\frac{dT}{T} = R\frac{dP}{P}$$

For constant $C_p$:
$$C_p \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} = R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $C_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$ (where $\gamma = C_p / C_v$)

$$\frac{\gamma R}{\gamma-1}\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} = R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

$$\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

Exponentiating:
$$\boxed{\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$

### Step 4: Derive $PV^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$
Using the ideal gas law: $P_1V_1/T_1 = P_2V_2/T_2$, substitute to eliminate T:

$$\frac{P_2V_2}{P_1V_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

$$\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{-1/\gamma}$$

Rearranging:
$$P_2 V_2^{\gamma} = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{PV^{\gamma} = \text{constant}}$$

## 3.6 Summary Matrix of Special Process Entropy Changes (Ideal Gas)

| Process | Condition | Entropy Change Equation |
|---------|-----------|------------------------|
| Isothermal | $T_2 = T_1$ | $\Delta S = -R\ln(P_2/P_1) = R\ln(V_2/V_1)$ |
| Isobaric | $P_2 = P_1$ | $\Delta S = C_p\ln(T_2/T_1)$ |
| Isochoric | $V_2 = V_1$ | $\Delta S = C_v\ln(T_2/T_1)$ |
| Isentropic | $\Delta S = 0$ | $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = (V_1/V_2)^{\gamma-1}$ |
| Polytropic | $PV^n = \text{const}$ | $\Delta S = C_v\ln(T_2/T_1) + R\ln(V_2/V_1)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart — Deriving Entropy Equations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["First Law dU = dQ - P dV"] --> nodeB["Second Law dS = dQ_rev divided by T"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Combine to get Tds = dU + P dV"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Enthalpy definition H = U + PV"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["dH = dU + P dV + V dP"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Substitute to get Tds = dH - V dP"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG["Express U as U T V"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Apply Maxwell relation for dU by dV at constant T"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Tds = Cv dT + T dP by dT at constant V times dV"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Express H as H T P"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Apply Maxwell relation for dH by dP at constant T"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Tds = Cp dT - T dV by dT at constant P times dP"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["Apply Ideal Gas PV = RT"]
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN["Final Ideal Gas Equations dS = Cp dT by T minus R dP by P and dS = Cv dT by T plus R dV by V"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Integrate with Cp constant to get Delta S = Cp ln T2 by T1 minus R ln P2 by P1"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["Set dS = 0 to obtain isentropic relations T2 by T1 equals P2 by P1 to the power gamma minus 1 by gamma"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Entropy Analysis of a Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["System Defined by T1 P1 State 1"] --> nodeB["Process Path Reversible or Irreversible"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Heat Transfer delta Q at Temperature T"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Work Transfer delta W"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Entropy Balance dS = dQ rev by T plus S gen"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["State 2 T2 P2"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Compute delta S using Ideal Gas Eqn 8"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Compute delta S using Tabulated Cp Polynomial"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Compute delta S for Incompressible Solid Liquid using Cp ln T2 by T1"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Output: delta S total in kJ per K"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Use in Second Law Check: delta S universe greater than or equal to 0"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Entropy Formula to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Given Process"]) --> q1{"Is the substance an ideal gas?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Is Cp constant or temperature dependent?"}
    q1 -- No --> q3["Incompressible liquid or solid? Use Delta S equals integral of Cp by T dT"]
    q3 --> q4{"Is Cp constant?"}
    q4 -- Yes --> q5["Delta S equals Cp ln T2 by T1"]
    q4 -- No --> q6["Delta S equals integral of Cp by T dT from T1 to T2"]
    q2 -- Constant Cp --> q7["Use Eqn 8: Delta S equals Cp ln T2 by T1 minus R ln P2 by P1"]
    q2 -- Cp equals f of T --> q8["Use Eqn 10: integral plus R ln P1 by P2"]
    q7 --> done(["Compute Numerical Value"])
    q8 --> done
    q5 --> done
    q6 --> done
```
