<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the Clausius definition of entropy. Why is entropy called a point function?**

**Model Answer:**
The Clausius definition of entropy is mathematically expressed as:
$$dS = \left(\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}\right)_{system}$$

For a finite reversible process:
$$\Delta S = \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

**Entropy is a point function** because:
1. It is defined through an exact differential (ds).
2. Its value depends only on the **state** of the system (defined by T, P, V, composition, etc.), not on the **path** taken to reach that state.
3. A cyclic integral is zero: $\oint dS = 0$.

The integrating factor **1/T** converts the inexact differential $\delta Q_{rev}$ into the exact differential dS.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Reasoning (1.5) = **3 marks**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Derive the relationship $C_p - C_v = R$ for an ideal gas.**

**Model Answer:**

For an ideal gas, the Tds equations reduce to:

$$dS = C_p \frac{dT}{T} - R \frac{dP}{P} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$dS = C_v \frac{dT}{T} + R \frac{dV}{V} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Equating (i) and (ii):
$$C_p \frac{dT}{T} - R \frac{dP}{P} = C_v \frac{dT}{T} + R \frac{dV}{V}$$

$$(C_p - C_v) \frac{dT}{T} = R\left(\frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dP}{P}\right)$$

Differentiating $PV = RT$ logarithmically: $\dfrac{dP}{P} + \dfrac{dV}{V} = \dfrac{dT}{T}$

$$(C_p - C_v) \frac{dT}{T} = R \frac{dT}{T}$$

$$\boxed{C_p - C_v = R}$$

**Marks Distribution:** Tds equations setup (1.5) + Final derivation (1.5) = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**a) (7 marks)** Starting from the combined first and second law, derive the two fundamental Tds equations:
(i) $T\,dS = dU + P\,dV$
(ii) $T\,dS = dH - V\,dP$

**b) (7 marks)** Air (assumed ideal gas) enters a compressor at **300 K, 100 kPa** and leaves at **600 K, 800 kPa**. For air, $C_p = 29.1$ kJ/kmol·K and $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate the entropy change of air per kmol.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The First Law for a closed, reversible system:
$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev} = \delta Q_{rev} - P\,dV$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The Second Law (Clausius definition):
$$\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substituting Step 2 into Step 1:
$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$
$$\Rightarrow T\,dS = dU + P\,dV \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Enthalpy definition: $H = U + PV$
Differentiating: $dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** So $dU + P\,dV = dH - V\,dP$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Substituting in equation (i):
$$\boxed{T\,dS = dH - V\,dP \quad \text{...(ii)}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
* $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 100$ kPa
* $T_2 = 600$ K, $P_2 = 800$ kPa
* $C_p = 29.1$ kJ/kmol·K
* $\gamma = 1.4$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the relevant equation (ideal gas, constant Cp):
$$\Delta S = C_p\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} - R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Calculate $R$ for air: $R = C_p - C_v = C_p(1 - 1/\gamma) = 29.1(1 - 1/1.4) = 29.1 \times 0.2857 = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K ✓

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substitute values:
$$\Delta S = 29.1 \ln\left(\frac{600}{300}\right) - 8.314 \ln\left(\frac{800}{100}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Evaluate logarithms:
$$\ln(2) = 0.6931 \quad \ln(8) = 2.0794$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final calculation:
$$\Delta S = 29.1 \times 0.6931 - 8.314 \times 2.0794$$
$$\Delta S = 20.169 - 17.288$$
$$\boxed{\Delta S = +2.881 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion: Entropy increases, confirming the **compressor is irreversible** (as expected for real compression processes).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a) (7 marks)** Derive the equation $\Delta S = C_p\ln(T_2/T_1) - R\ln(P_2/P_1)$ for an ideal gas undergoing any process, assuming $C_p$ is constant.

**b) (7 marks)** Two kilograms of air (ideal gas) is heated reversibly at constant pressure from **300 K to 500 K**. Given $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, calculate:
(i) The change in entropy
(ii) The change in internal energy
(iii) The change in enthalpy

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Start with the Tds-2 equation:
$$T\,dS = C_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For ideal gas: $V = RT/P \Rightarrow \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{R}{P}$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute:
$$T\,dS = C_p\,dT - T\cdot\frac{R}{P}\,dP \quad \Rightarrow \quad dS = C_p\frac{dT}{T} - R\frac{dP}{P}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Integrate from State 1 to State 2 (with $C_p$ = constant):
$$\Delta S = C_p \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{dT}{T} - R\int_{P_1}^{P_2}\frac{dP}{P}$$
$$\boxed{\Delta S = C_p\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} - R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** m = 2 kg, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 500$ K, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, constant pressure (so $P_2 = P_1$).

**Step 1 — Calculate $C_v$ (1 mark):**
$$C_v = C_p - R = 1.005 - 0.287 = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**(i) Entropy Change (2 marks):**
For isobaric process ($P_2 = P_1$):
$$\Delta S = m C_p \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} = 2 \times 1.005 \times \ln\frac{500}{300}$$
$$\Delta S = 2.010 \times \ln(1.6667) = 2.010 \times 0.5108$$
$$\boxed{\Delta S = 1.027 \text{ kJ/K}}$$

**(ii) Change in Internal Energy (2 marks):**
$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = 2 \times 0.718 \times (500 - 300)$$
$$\Delta U = 2 \times 0.718 \times 200$$
$$\boxed{\Delta U = 287.2 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(iii) Change in Enthalpy (2 marks):**
$$\Delta H = m C_p (T_2 - T_1) = 2 \times 1.005 \times 200$$
$$\boxed{\Delta H = 402.0 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Verification:** $\Delta H - \Delta U = 402.0 - 287.2 = 114.8$ kJ = $mR\Delta T = 2 \times 0.287 \times 200 = 114.8$ kJ ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Kerala University Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units of R:** Use $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K OR $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K — never mix. If $C_p$ is in kJ/kmol·K, R must match.
>
> 2. **Mixing Cv and Cp in the wrong term:** $C_p$ multiplies $\ln(T_2/T_1)$; $C_v$ multiplies $\ln(T_2/T_1)$ only if you use the **volume** form of the equation.
>
> 3. **Sign Convention on Ln(P₂/P₁):** When pressure **decreases** (e.g., expansion), $\ln(P_2/P_1) < 0$ and the term **adds** to entropy. Students often wrongly compute this as a loss.
>
> 4. **Assuming $C_p = C_v$ for incompressible substances:** For liquids and solids, $C_p \approx C_v \approx C$, and the entropy change is simply $\int C\,dT/T$.
>
> 5. **Reversible vs. Irreversible path confusion:** Even for an irreversible process, the **system entropy change** is computed along a hypothetical reversible path between the same two states.
>
> 6. **Polytropic process misidentification:** A process described as "adiabatic" is **not** isentropic unless explicitly stated as **reversible adiabatic**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Entropy & Heat Capacity**

- [x] **Entropy (S)** is an **extensive, state function** with units of J/K (or kJ/K).
- [x] **Clausius Definition:** $dS = \delta Q_{rev} / T$
- [x] **Cyclic Integral:** $\oint dS = 0$ (zero around any cycle)
- [x] **Heat Capacity at constant V:** $C_v = (\partial U / \partial T)_V$
- [x] **Heat Capacity at constant P:** $C_p = (\partial H / \partial T)_P$
- [x] **General Relation:** $C_p - C_v = T V \beta^{2} / \kappa_T$
- [x] **Ideal Gas Identity:** $C_p - C_v = R$
- [x] **Tds-1:** $T\,dS = dU + P\,dV$
- [x] **Tds-2:** $T\,dS = dH - V\,dP$
- [x] **Ideal Gas T-V form:** $dS = C_v\,dT/T + R\,dV/V$
- [x] **Ideal Gas T-P form:** $dS = C_p\,dT/T - R\,dP/P$
- [x] **★ Master Formula:** $\Delta S = C_p\ln(T_2/T_1) - R\ln(P_2/P_1)$
- [x] **Isentropic (Rev. Adiabatic):** $PV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$, $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$
- [x] **Specific Heat Ratio:** $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ (1.4 for air, 1.67 for monatomic gases)
- [x] **Incompressible Solid/Liquid:** $\Delta S \approx C \ln(T_2/T_1)$ (since $V\,dP$ term is negligible)
- [x] **Isobaric process:** $\Delta S = C_p \ln(T_2/T_1)$
- [x] **Isothermal process:** $\Delta S = -R \ln(P_2/P_1) = R \ln(V_2/V_1)$
- [x] **Isochoric process:** $\Delta S = C_v \ln(T_2/T_1)$
- [x] **Universe Entropy (2nd Law):** $\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$
- [x] **Polytropic process:** $PV^n = \text{constant}$, where $n \neq \gamma$ for non-isentropic
- [x] **For variable Cp:** $\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (C_p/T)\,dT - R\ln(P_2/P_1)$
- [x] **For ideal gas mixture:** $\Delta S_{mix} = -R \sum y_i \ln(y_i)$ (always positive)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Energy and Enthalpy in Terms of Measurable Quantities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Internal Energy and Enthalpy in Terms of Measurable Quantities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In classical thermodynamics, the **internal energy (U)** and **enthalpy (H)** of a pure substance are fundamental state functions. Although they cannot be measured absolutely, their *changes* can be expressed entirely in terms of **measurable macroscopic properties** — temperature (T), pressure (P), volume (V), and specific heats (C_V, C_P).

Mathematically, treating U as a continuous, differentiable function of T and V:

$$U = U(T, V)$$

And H as a function of T and P:

$$H = H(T, P)$$

The partial derivatives of these state functions with respect to their natural variables yield relationships that connect them to experimentally determinable quantities via the **Maxwell relations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is foundational for Module 1 because every subsequent module (Phase Equilibria, Solution Thermodynamics, Reaction Equilibria) uses the partial derivatives (∂U/∂V)_T and (∂H/∂P)_T. Mastery of these is non-negotiable for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hidden Thermometer" Intuition

Imagine you have a sealed metal canister of gas in a dark room. You cannot directly *see* the internal energy — it is a hidden, microscopic bookkeeping quantity. However, you *can* measure:

- The **temperature** of the gas (via thermometer).
- The **pressure** it exerts (via gauge).
- The **volume** it occupies (via ruler).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of U and H as the "bank account balance" of a thermodynamic system. You cannot directly count every microscopic molecular vibration and translation (like counting every rupee in every wallet in India). But you *can* measure the *net change* in the balance by looking at observable "transactions" — temperature rise (heat absorbed), volume expansion (work done), and pressure change. The Maxwell relations are essentially the **audit rules** that tell you how these observable transactions translate to invisible balance changes.

### 1.3 Measurable Quantities — The Engineering Toolbox

The **measurable quantities** in this topic are:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit (SI) | How Measured |
|--------|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| T | Absolute Temperature | **K (Kelvin)** | Thermocouple / RTD |
| P | Absolute Pressure | **Pa (Pascal)** | Bourdon gauge / Transducer |
| V | Molar Volume | **m³/mol** | Piston displacement |
| C_V | Heat Capacity @ constant V | **J/(mol·K)** | Calorimeter |
| C_P | Heat Capacity @ constant P | **J/(mol·K)** | Flow calorimeter |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Reference:** The universal gas constant **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** is the bridge between microscopic (statistical mechanics) and macroscopic (calorimetric) worlds. Always carry it to four significant figures in KTU numericals.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" — Logical Decomposition

The derivation logic flows through four sequential pillars:

**Pillar 1: Choice of Natural Variables**
- U is naturally a function of (S, V) from the First Law: dU = TdS − PdV.
- However, S is **not directly measurable**. Hence we switch variables using Legendre transforms.
- Result: U is re-expressed in (T, V), and H is re-expressed in (T, P).

**Pillar 2: Total Differentials (Exact Differentials)**
- Because U and H are state functions, their differentials are **exact**.
- An exact differential dZ = M dx + N dy satisfies: (∂M/∂y)_x = (∂N/∂x)_y (Euler's reciprocity).

**Pillar 3: Introduction of Measurable Heat Capacities**
- At constant volume: (∂U/∂T)_V = C_V (measurable in a bomb calorimeter).
- At constant pressure: (∂H/∂T)_P = C_P (measurable in a flow calorimeter).

**Pillar 4: Maxwell Relations — The Game Changer**
- These four identities arise from the equality of mixed second partial derivatives of U, H, A (Helmholtz), and G (Gibbs).
- They allow us to **replace unmeasurable entropy derivatives** (like (∂S/∂P)_T) with **measurable PVT derivatives** (like −(∂V/∂T)_P).

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the second column (Final Working Equation) — these are the only forms accepted in KTU board exams. Memorize the third column (Maxwell Origin) for conceptual sub-questions.

| # | Quantity | Final Working Equation (PVT/C form) | Maxwell / Thermodynamic Origin |
|---|----------|------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | dU (general) | dU = C_V dT + [T(∂P/∂T)_V − P] dV | First Law + Exactness |
| 2 | dH (general) | dH = C_P dT + [V − T(∂V/∂T)_P] dP | dH = dU + d(PV) |
| 3 | (∂U/∂V)_T | = T(∂P/∂T)_V − P ≡ π_T (Internal Pressure) | From dU = TdS − PdV |
| 4 | (∂H/∂P)_T | = V − T(∂V/∂T)_P | From dH = TdS + VdP |
| 5 | (∂S/∂V)_T | = (∂P/∂T)_V | Maxwell from dA |
| 6 | (∂S/∂P)_T | = −(∂V/∂T)_P | Maxwell from dG |
| 7 | (∂U/∂T)_V | = C_V | Definition |
| 8 | (∂H/∂T)_P | = C_P | Definition |
| 9 | μ_JT (Joule-Thomson) | = (1/C_P)[T(∂V/∂T)_P − V] | From (∂H/∂P)_T |
| 10 | dS | = (C_V/T) dT + (∂P/∂T)_V dV | Fundamental Relation |
| 11 | dS | = (C_P/T) dT − (∂V/∂T)_P dP | Fundamental Relation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Encoding Note:** In the table above, all mathematical "divider bars" (such as those in ∂P/∂T) are rendered as forward slashes (/) to preserve table integrity. Do not use the vertical pipe character inside table cells — it breaks Markdown parsing.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Liquefaction of Gases (Linde Process):** Uses μ_JT (Joule-Thomson coefficient) to predict inversion temperatures.
- **Compressor Design:** Uses (∂U/∂V)_T to compute non-ideal work corrections in real-gas compression.
- **Reactor Safety:** The expression dU = C_V dT + π_T dV is used in adiabatic reactor temperature-rise calculations.
- **Distillation Columns:** Enthalpy departures from ideality are integrated using (∂H/∂P)_T correlations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Expression for dU in Terms of Measurable Quantities

**Starting Point — The Combined First & Second Law:**

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

**Step 1: Express dS in terms of measurable variables.**
Since S = S(T, V), the total differential is:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 2: Evaluate (∂S/∂T)_V.**
From the definition of C_V:

$$C_V \equiv \left(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial T}\right)_V = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V \implies \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{C_V}{T}$$

**Step 3: Apply Maxwell Relation to (∂S/∂V)_T.**
Starting from A = U − TS, so dA = −S dT − P dV. By exactness:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

This is the **first Maxwell relation.**

**Step 4: Substitute everything into the energy equation.**

$$dU = T\left[\frac{C_V}{T} dT + \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV\right] - P\,dV$$

**Step 5: Collect dT and dV terms.**

$$dU = C_V\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P\right] dV$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Master Equation (Equation 3.1):** This is the KTU board-exam gold-standard result. The coefficient of dV is the **internal pressure**, denoted π_T.

$$\pi_T \equiv \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$$

**Step 6: Verification for Ideal Gas.**
For an ideal gas: PV = nRT, so P = nRT/V, hence:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{nR}{V}$$

Substituting:

$$\pi_T^{IG} = T \cdot \frac{nR}{V} - P = \frac{nRT}{V} - P = P - P = 0$$

This confirms **Joule's Law**: U is a function of T alone for an ideal gas. ✔

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Expression for dH in Terms of Measurable Quantities

**Starting Point — Definition of Enthalpy:**

$$H \equiv U + PV \implies dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 1: Substitute dU from Equation 3.1.**

$$dH = C_V\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P\right] dV + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 2: The P dV terms cancel.**

$$dH = C_V\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 3: Switch natural variable from V to P.**
We need dV in terms of dP. Since V = V(T, P):

$$dV = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 4: Substitute dV.**

$$dH = C_V\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V\left[\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T dP\right] + V\,dP$$

**Step 5: Use the cyclic relation identity.**

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \cdot \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\partial(P,V)}{\partial(T,T)}$$

A standard triple-product cyclic identity gives:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

To keep the algebra transparent, we adopt a cleaner approach using Maxwell relations directly on H.

**Step 5 (Alternative — Maxwell Approach):**

From H = U + PV and dH = TdS + VdP. Since H = H(T, P):

$$dH = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

By definition: (∂H/∂T)_P = C_P. By exactness (cross-partial equality):

$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T + V$$

**Step 6: Apply the Maxwell relation (∂S/∂P)_T = −(∂V/∂T)_P.**

$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = V - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Master Equation (Equation 3.2):** This is the KTU board-exam second gold-standard result.

$$dH = C_P\,dT + \left[V - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP$$

**Step 7: Verification for Ideal Gas.**
For ideal gas: V = nRT/P, hence (∂V/∂T)_P = nR/P. Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T^{IG} = \frac{nRT}{P} - T\cdot\frac{nR}{P} = 0$$

This confirms **Joule-Thomson's finding** for an ideal gas: H depends only on T. ✔

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Joule-Thomson Coefficient (μ_JT)

The Joule-Thomson coefficient is defined experimentally as:

$$\mu_{JT} \equiv \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$$

We invert this:

$$\mu_{JT} = -\frac{1}{\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T \big/ \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P} = -\frac{1}{C_P}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

Substituting Equation 3.6:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Master Equation (Equation 3.3):** Joule-Thomson Coefficient in measurable form.

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$$

The **inversion temperature** T_inv occurs when μ_JT = 0, i.e., when:

$$T_{inv}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = V$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: The Four Maxwell Relations (Derivation from Exactness)

We summarize the full Maxwell quartet, derived from the exactness of dU, dH, dA, dG.

**From dU = T dS − P dV (Natural: S, V):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$$

**From dH = T dS + V dP (Natural: S, P):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$$

**From dA = −S dT − P dV (Natural: T, V):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**From dG = −S dT + V dP (Natural: T, P):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid ("Good Physicists Have Studied Under Very Small Tents"):**
> **G** → dG = −S dT + V dP → (∂S/∂P)_T = −(∂V/∂T)_P
> **P** → dH = T dS + V dP → (∂T/∂P)_S = (∂V/∂S)_P
> **H** → dU = T dS − P dV → (∂T/∂V)_S = −(∂P/∂S)_V
> **S** → dA = −S dT − P dV → (∂S/∂V)_T = (∂P/∂T)_V
> **U** → dU equation
> **V** → ... mnemonic completed.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — General Equations vs. Ideal-Gas Specializations

| General Real-Fluid Equation | Ideal-Gas Limit | Implication |
|----------------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| dU = C_V dT + [T(∂P/∂T)_V − P] dV | dU = C_V dT | U = U(T) only |
| dH = C_P dT + [V − T(∂V/∂T)_P] dP | dH = C_P dT | H = H(T) only |
| μ_JT = (1/C_P)[T(∂V/∂T)_P − V] | μ_JT = 0 | No cooling on throttling |
| (∂S/∂V)_T = (∂P/∂T)_V | = nR/V | Ideal gas entropy rule |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Logical Chain from First Law to Measurable dU and dH

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[First Law: dU = TdS minus PdV] --> B[Express S as S of T,V]
    B --> C[Compute partial derivative of S wrt T at constant V]
    C --> D[Result: partial S over partial T sub V equals C sub V over T]
    A --> E[Apply Exactness to dU: Cross partial equality]
    E --> F[Maxwell Relation 1: partial S over partial V sub T equals partial P over partial T sub V]
    F --> G[Substitute back into dU]
    G --> H[Master Eq 3.1: dU equals C sub V dT plus pi sub T dV]
    I[Definition: H equals U plus PV] --> J[dH equals dU plus P dV plus V dP]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Switch variable: dV to dT dP form]
    K --> L[Apply Maxwell Relation 2: partial S over partial P sub T]
    L --> M[Master Eq 3.2: dH equals C sub P dT plus bracket V minus T partial V over partial T sub P bracket dP]
    M --> N[Define mu JT equals partial T over partial P sub H]
    N --> O[Use chain rule inversion]
    O --> P[Master Eq 3.3: mu JT equals 1 over C sub P times bracket T partial V over partial T sub P minus V bracket]
    P --> Q[Engineering Application: Linde Process Gas Liquefaction]
    H --> Q
    M --> Q
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Measurable Thermodynamic Derivatives

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs[Measurable Macroscopic Inputs]
        direction LR
        T[Temperature T in K]
        P[Pressure P in Pa]
        V[Volume V in m3]
        CV[Heat Capacity C sub V]
        CP[Heat Capacity C sub P]
    end
    subgraph MaxwellLayer[Maxwell Relations Bridge]
        direction TB
        M1[partial S over partial V sub T equals partial P over partial T sub V]
        M2[partial S over partial P sub T equals minus partial V over partial T sub P]
    end
    subgraph Unmeasurable[Hidden State Functions]
        direction TB
        U[Internal Energy U]
        H[Enthalpy H]
        S[Entropy S]
    end
    subgraph Outputs[Final Engineering Equations]
        direction TB
        OE1[dU equals C sub V dT plus pi sub T dV]
        OE2[dH equals C sub P dT plus bracket V minus T partial V over partial T sub P bracket dP]
        OE3[mu JT equals 1 over C sub P times bracket T partial V over partial T sub P minus V bracket]
    end
    T --> M1
    P --> M1
    T --> M2
    V --> M2
    CV --> OE1
    M1 --> OE1
    M1 --> U
    M2 --> H
    CP --> OE2
    M2 --> OE2
    CP --> OE3
    M2 --> OE3
    U --> OE1
    H --> OE2
    H --> OE3
```

### 4.3 Schematic Concept Map — Natural Variables vs. Measurable Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Natural[Natural Variables Unmeasurable]
        direction TB
        S_N[Entropy S]
        V_N[Volume V for U]
        P_N[Pressure P for H]
    end
    subgraph Measurable[Measurable Variables]
        direction TB
        T_M[Temperature T]
        V_M[Volume V for state eq]
        P_M[Pressure P for state eq]
        CV_M[C sub V]
        CP_M[C sub P]
    end
    subgraph StateFns[Thermodynamic Potentials]
        direction TB
        U_SF[U of S V]
        H_SF[H of S P]
        A_SF[A of T V]
        G_SF[G of T P]
    end
    S_N --> U_SF
    V_N --> U_SF
    S_N --> H_SF
    P_N --> H_SF
    T_M --> A_SF
    V_M --> A_SF
    T_M --> G_SF
    P_M --> G_SF
    U_SF -- Legendre Transform to T V --> T_M
    H_SF -- Legendre Transform to T P --> T_M
    A_SF -- Maxwell Bridge --> Measurable
    G_SF -- Maxwell Bridge --> Measurable
    Measurable --> FinalEq[Final Measurable Equations for dU and dH]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The "Natural Variables" of each potential (U, H, A, G) are mathematically elegant but not directly observable. The Maxwell relations act as the **translator layer** that converts entropy-based partial derivatives (unmeasurable) into PVT-based partial derivatives (measurable). The block diagrams above explicitly trace this translation pathway.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define internal pressure. Show that for an ideal gas, the internal pressure is zero.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 2):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Internal pressure is defined as the rate of change of internal energy with respect to volume at constant temperature:

$$\pi_T \equiv \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T$$

**Derivation for Ideal Gas (2 Marks):** From the thermodynamic identity:

$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$$

For an ideal gas, PV = nRT, so P = nRT/V. Holding V constant:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{nR}{V}$$

Substituting:

$$\pi_T = T \cdot \frac{nR}{V} - P = \frac{nRT}{V} - \frac{nRT}{V} = 0$$

Hence, for an ideal gas, internal energy is independent of volume. **(2 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
State any two Maxwell relations and explain their significance in expressing entropy derivatives in measurable form.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1):**

**Maxwell Relation 1 (1 Mark):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**Maxwell Relation 2 (1 Mark):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Significance (1 Mark):** The left-hand side involves the entropy S, which cannot be measured directly. The right-hand side involves only the **measurable PVT properties** (P, V, T) of the substance. Thus, Maxwell relations act as a bridge, converting unmeasurable entropy derivatives into experimentally obtainable PVT derivatives.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** As per the 2024 NEP-aligned ESE pattern, every Part B (14-mark) question in Module 1 offers an internal choice between two 7+7 sub-structures. Below, Question A and Question B are mutually exclusive alternatives on the same module topic.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Starting from the combined first and second law (dU = TdS − PdV), derive the expression for dU in terms of C_V and the internal pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A real gas follows the van der Waals equation of state:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$$

For this gas, compute:
- (i) The internal pressure π_T at 300 K and V = 0.025 m³/mol. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) The change in internal energy when the gas expands isothermally from V₁ = 0.020 m³/mol to V₂ = 0.040 m³/mol at 300 K. **(4 Marks)**

**Given:** a = 0.365 Pa·m⁶/mol², R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), assume 'a' and 'b' are constants.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Write the First Law combined with Second Law:

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Express entropy as a function of natural measurable variables (T, V):

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Evaluate (∂S/∂T)_V using the definition of C_V:

$$C_V = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V \implies \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{C_V}{T}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Apply Maxwell relation to (∂S/∂V)_T. Since dA = −S dT − P dV is exact:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Substitute back into dU:

$$dU = T\left[\frac{C_V}{T} dT + \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V dV\right] - P\,dV$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Simplify to final form:

$$dU = C_V\,dT + \left[T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P\right] dV$$

The coefficient of dV is the **internal pressure** π_T. ✔

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute (∂P/∂T)_V for van der Waals gas (1 Mark):**

$$P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$

Holding V constant and differentiating with respect to T:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{R}{V-b}$$

**Step 2 — Compute π_T (1 Mark):**

$$\pi_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - P$$

But from the EOS, RT/(V−b) = P + a/V². Therefore:

$$\pi_T = P + \frac{a}{V^2} - P = \frac{a}{V^2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at T = 300 K, V = 0.025 m³/mol (1 Mark):**

$$\pi_T = \frac{0.365}{(0.025)^2} = \frac{0.365}{6.25 \times 10^{-4}} = 584 \text{ Pa}$$

**(i) Answer: π_T = 584 Pa**

**Step 4 — Set up isothermal integration (1 Mark):**

For an isothermal process, dT = 0, so:

$$dU = \pi_T\,dV = \frac{a}{V^2}\,dV$$

**Step 5 — Integrate (2 Marks):**

$$\Delta U = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{a}{V^2}\,dV = a\left[-\frac{1}{V}\right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = a\left(\frac{1}{V_1} - \frac{1}{V_2}\right)$$

$$\Delta U = 0.365 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.020} - \frac{1}{0.040}\right) = 0.365 \times (50 - 25) = 0.365 \times 25$$

**(ii) Answer: ΔU = 9.125 J/mol**

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation (1 Mark):** The positive ΔU indicates that the gas absorbs internal energy during expansion against van der Waals attractive forces, which is characteristic of real-gas behavior. ✔

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for dH in terms of C_P, V, T, and (∂V/∂T)_P. Show clearly the use of the relevant Maxwell relation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a gas obeying the virial equation of state truncated after the second term:

$$Z = 1 + \frac{B(T)}{V}$$

where B(T) = −0.00005 m³/mol at the operating temperature. Determine:
- (i) The expression for (∂H/∂P)_T at 300 K. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) The Joule-Thomson coefficient μ_JT at 300 K and P = 10 bar, assuming C_P = 29.1 J/(mol·K). **(4 Marks)**

**Given:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), T = 300 K, P = 10⁶ Pa.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Start with the combined law for enthalpy:

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Recognize H as function of (T, P):

$$dH = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Identify (∂H/∂T)_P = C_P by definition.

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Apply exactness to (∂H/∂P)_T:

$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T + V$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Apply the Maxwell relation from dG:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Substitute to obtain final expression:

$$dH = C_P\,dT + \left[V - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP$$

✔

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify EOS (1 Mark):**

$$Z = \frac{PV}{RT} = 1 + \frac{B(T)}{V} \implies V = \frac{RT}{P}\left(1 + \frac{B}{V}\right)$$

For small B/V (truncated virial, dilute limit), V ≈ RT/P. Better approximation: V ≈ (RT/P)(1 + BP/RT). For this problem, treat B as a small perturbation.

**Step 2 — Compute (∂V/∂T)_P (1 Mark):**

To leading order: V ≈ RT/P + B. Differentiating at constant P:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$

(Note: B depends on T, but since B is given as a constant value at the operating T, we treat it as approximately constant for this calculation.)

**Step 3 — Compute (∂H/∂P)_T (1 Mark):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = V - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{RT}{P} + B - T \cdot \frac{R}{P} = B$$

**(i) Answer: (∂H/∂P)_T = B = −0.00005 m³/mol**

**Step 4 — Apply μ_JT formula (1 Mark):**

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate bracket (1 Mark):**

$$T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V = T \cdot \frac{R}{P} - \left(\frac{RT}{P} + B\right) = -B$$

**Step 6 — Substitute values (1 Mark):**

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{-B}{C_P} = \frac{-(-0.00005)}{29.1} = \frac{0.00005}{29.1}$$

**(ii) Answer: μ_JT ≈ 1.72 × 10⁻⁶ K/Pa**

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation (1 Mark):** The positive μ_JT indicates that the gas cools upon isenthalpic throttling at 300 K and 10 bar, making it suitable for Joule-Thomson-based cooling cycles. ✔

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Examiner's Eye-View (KTU Board):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to cancel P dV terms (loss: 2 marks):** Many students substitute dU into dH but forget that the +P dV from d(PV) cancels with the −P dV inside dU. Always cancel explicitly.
> 
> 2. **Confusing (∂U/∂V)_T with (∂U/∂T)_V (loss: 1 mark):** Subscript confusion is the single most common error. Mnemonic: "**V**olume goes with **U**; **P**ressure goes with **H**."
> 
> 3. **Using the wrong Maxwell relation (loss: 2 marks):** For (∂H/∂P)_T, you MUST use the dG-based Maxwell relation, not the dA-based one. Verify by checking the cross-partial of the correct potential.
> 
> 4. **Dropping the units in the final answer (loss: 0.5 mark):** KTU examiners explicitly deduct for unit-less final numerical answers. Always append **Pa**, **J/mol**, **K/Pa**, etc.
> 
> 5. **Not verifying the ideal-gas limit (loss: 1 mark):** Always test your final expression against PV = nRT to confirm it reduces to the known ideal-gas form. This shows the examiner "depth of understanding" and can earn partial credit even if the main derivation has minor slips.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Internal Energy & Enthalpy in Measurable Form**

- ✅ **Master Equation 1 (dU):** dU = C_V dT + [T(∂P/∂T)_V − P] dV
- ✅ **Master Equation 2 (dH):** dH = C_P dT + [V − T(∂V/∂T)_P] dP
- ✅ **Internal Pressure Identity:** π_T = (∂U/∂V)_T = T(∂P/∂T)_V − P
- ✅ **Joule-Thomson Coefficient:** μ_JT = (1/C_P)[T(∂V/∂T)_P − V]
- ✅ **Four Maxwell Relations:** Always derivable via the **exactness (cross-partial equality)** of dU, dH, dA, dG.
- ✅ **Ideal-Gas Limits:** U = U(T) only, H = H(T) only, π_T = 0, μ_JT = 0.
- ✅ **Engineering Constants:** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), C_P − C_V = R (per mole of ideal gas).
- ✅ **Mnemonic for Maxwell:** "Good Physicists Have Studied Under Very Small Tents" (G, P, H, S, U, V).
- ✅ **Legendre Transform Intuition:** Switches unmeasurable variables (S) to measurable ones (T) by adding/subtracting conjugate products (TS, PV).
- ✅ **Experimental Signatures:** Bomb calorimeter → C_V; Flow calorimeter → C_P; Joule-Thomson throttling → μ_JT.
- ✅ **Board-Exam Pattern:** Part A always has 3-mark direct definitions; Part B almost always tests the dU or dH derivation in the (a) sub-part (7 marks) and applies it numerically in (b) (7 marks).
- ✅ **Inversion Temperature:** μ_JT = 0 → T_inv (∂V/∂T)_P = V → separates cooling from heating regions.
- ✅ **Real-Gas Signatures:** van der Waals → π_T = a/V²; Virial → π_T = RT²/V² · (dB/dT).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "express dU in terms of measurable quantities," the examiner is asking for the equation containing C_V, T, P, V, and PVT partial derivatives ONLY. Never leave S in your final answer — that defeats the entire purpose of the derivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joule-Thomson Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Joule-Thomson Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Joule-Thomson Coefficient** (denoted $\mu_{JT}$ or $\mu$) is defined as the rate of change of temperature with respect to pressure at **constant enthalpy** during an adiabatic throttling (isenthalpic) process. Mathematically:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H}$$

It quantifies how much a real gas **cools or warms** when it is forced through a porous plug, valve, or any throttling device under adiabatic, steady-flow conditions where no shaft work is extracted and kinetic/potential energy changes are negligible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "The Joule-Thomson coefficient is the partial derivative of temperature with respect to pressure at constant enthalpy. It represents the temperature change per unit pressure drop experienced by a fluid during a throttling process."

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Aerosol Can Effect" 💨

Imagine spraying an **aerosol deodorant can** continuously for a few seconds. Touch the nozzle immediately after — it feels **icy cold**! 

**Why does this happen?**
- The pressurized gas inside rushes out through a tiny nozzle (a throttling device).
- The expansion is rapid and adiabatic (no time for heat exchange with surroundings).
- The gas does **internal work** to overcome intermolecular attractive forces as molecules move apart.
- This internal energy "debt" is paid by the **kinetic energy of the molecules** → average molecular velocity drops → **temperature falls**.

**Mathematical Connection:**
- The pressure *decreases* ($\Delta P < 0$).
- For most gases at room temperature, temperature also *decreases* ($\Delta T < 0$).
- Therefore, the ratio $\frac{\Delta T}{\Delta P} > 0$ → $\mu_{JT} > 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal Gas Behavior:** For a perfect/ideal gas, there are **no intermolecular forces**, so $\mu_{JT} = 0$. The gas neither cools nor warms on throttling. This is one of the classic experimental proofs that real gases deviate from ideality.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Sign Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **0.08206** | L·atm/(mol·K) |
| Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101,325** | Pa |
| STP Temperature | $T_{STP}$ | **273.15** | K |
| Inversion $T$ for N₂ | $T_{i,N_2}$ | **621** | K |
| Inversion $T$ for H₂ | $T_{i,H_2}$ | **220** | K |
| Inversion $T$ for He | $T_{i,He}$ | **~50** | K |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick:** "**C-O-L-D-H**" → **C**ooling **O**ccurs at **L**ow temperature, **D**uring **H**igh pressure throttling. Above the inversion temperature, the gas *heats up* on expansion instead!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Throttling Process — Conceptual Foundation

A **throttling process** is a steady-flow process through a restriction (porous plug, valve, capillary tube) characterized by:

1. **Adiabatic** → $Q = 0$
2. **No shaft work** → $W_s = 0$
3. **Negligible $\Delta KE$ and $\Delta PE$**
4. **Steady-state** → $H_{in} = H_{out}$ (isenthalpic)

Applying the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE):

$$H_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gZ_1 + Q = H_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gZ_2 + W_s$$

All extraneous terms vanish → $\boxed{H_1 = H_2}$

## 2.2 The Three Logical Steps to Obtain $\mu_{JT}$

**Step 1 — Start from the fundamental property relation for enthalpy:**

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Step 2 — Apply the isenthalpic constraint** ($dH = 0$):

$$T\,dS + V\,dP = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T\,dS = -V\,dP$$

**Step 3 — Express $S$ as a function of $(T, P)$** and use **Maxwell's relations** to eliminate entropy:

Since $S = S(T, P)$, we have:
$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

Substituting and using:
- $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{C_P}{T}$
- $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ (Maxwell relation)

We obtain the final expression.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` instead of `|` in table cells to avoid markdown breaking.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables Explained | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Definition** | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$ | $T$ = Temp, $P$ = Pressure, $H$ = Enthalpy | Direct measure of cooling/heating effect |
| 2 | **In Terms of PVT** | $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$ | $C_P$ = Heat capacity at const $P$, $V$ = Volume | Used for experimental determination |
| 3 | **In Terms of $\alpha$** (thermal expansion coeff.) | $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{V}{C_P}\left(\alpha T - 1\right)$ | $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{V}\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Compact form, easy sign analysis |
| 4 | **Ideal Gas Limit** | $\mu_{JT}^{ideal} = 0$ | Always zero by Joule's law | Sanity check for derivations |
| 5 | **Inversion Condition** | $\mu_{JT} = 0 \Rightarrow T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = V$ | At the inversion point | Determines inversion curve |
| 6 | **van der Waals Inversion Temp** | $T_i = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$ | $a$ = attraction param, $b$ = covolume | Quick estimate of inversion $T$ |
| 7 | **Entropy Change (real, irreversible)** | $\Delta S = - \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{V}{T}\,dP \Big\vert_H$ | At constant $H$ | Used in exergy/lost-work analysis |
| 8 | **Maxwell Relation Used** | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | From $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ | Key identity in derivation |
| 9 | **Identity Relation** | $\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = V - T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Often called the thermodynamic "pressure-coefficient" | Connects $\mu_{JT}$ to enthalpy variation |
| 10 | **Cooling Condition** | $\mu_{JT} > 0 \Leftrightarrow T < T_i$ | Below inversion temperature | Used in Linde liquefaction cycle |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Joule-Thomson Effect Matters

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| 🏭 **Air Separation Plants** | Liquefaction of air to separate O₂, N₂, Ar |
| ❄️ **Cryogenics** | Production of liquid N₂, H₂, He |
| 🚢 **LNG Processing** | Natural gas liquefaction for transport |
| 🧊 **Refrigeration (JT cycle)** | Domestic refrigerators using N₂/H₂ mixtures |
| ⛽ **Gas Processing** | Dew-point control, hydrocarbon recovery |
| 🔬 **Lab Scale** | Gas chromatography carrier gas regulation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Linde Process Works:** The Linde-Hampson cycle exploits the fact that when a real gas below its inversion temperature is throttled, it cools. This cooled gas is then used to pre-cool the incoming high-pressure gas through a counter-flow heat exchanger, eventually reaching liquefaction temperatures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of $\mu_{JT}$ from First Principles

### 🎯 Goal: Derive $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$

**Step 1:** Begin with the combined first and second laws for a closed system:

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

**Step 2:** By definition of enthalpy, $H = U + PV$. Differentiating:

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ into the expression for $dH$:

$$dH = (T\,dS - P\,dV) + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

The $P\,dV$ terms cancel exactly, leaving:

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $H$ is naturally a function of $(S, P)$. Therefore, we can write:

$$dH = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P dS + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S dP = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

This is consistent — $T = \left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P$ and $V = \left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S$.

**Step 5:** For a throttling process, $dH = 0$ (isenthalpic). Hence:

$$0 = T\,dS + V\,dP \quad \Rightarrow \quad T\,dS = -V\,dP$$

This gives an important auxiliary result:

$$\Delta S = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{V}{T}\,dP \quad \text{(at constant } H\text{)}$$

**Step 6:** Since $S = S(T, P)$, the total differential is:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 7:** We now need to evaluate each partial derivative:

**Identity A:** From the heat capacity definition at constant pressure:
$$C_P = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_P}{T}$$

**Identity B (Maxwell Relation):** From the Gibbs free energy $G = H - TS$ and $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$:

$$-S = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P, \quad V = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

By equality of mixed partial derivatives (Clairaut's theorem):

$$\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial P}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T\right)_P$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial (-S)}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute both identities into the $dS$ expression:

$$dS = \frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

**Step 9:** Multiply through by $T$:

$$T\,dS = C_P\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

**Step 10:** Apply the isenthalpic condition from Step 5: $T\,dS = -V\,dP$:

$$-V\,dP = C_P\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

**Step 11:** Group the $dT$ and $dP$ terms:

$$C_P\,dT = T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP - V\,dP$$

$$C_P\,dT = \left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right] dP$$

**Step 12:** Divide by $C_P\,dP$:

$$\frac{dT}{dP}\bigg\vert_H = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$$

**Step 13:** Recognize the left-hand side as $\mu_{JT}$:

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]}$$

✅ **Derivation complete.**

## 3.2 Proof that $\mu_{JT} = 0$ for an Ideal Gas

For an ideal gas, $PV = nRT$, so $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$. Differentiating at constant $P$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{nR}{P}$$

Substituting into the $\mu_{JT}$ expression:

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T \cdot \frac{nR}{P} - \frac{nRT}{P}\right] = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[\frac{nRT}{P} - \frac{nRT}{P}\right] = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Inversion Temperature for van der Waals Gas

The van der Waals equation of state is:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$$

Solving for $P$:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{V_m^2}$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant $V_m$ (constant $V$):

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{R}{V_m - b}$$

The inversion condition is $T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = V$, which is equivalent to $\mu_{JT} = 0$.

For a van der Waals gas, in the limit of moderate pressures, one can show:

$$T_i = \frac{2a}{Rb}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows that gases with **strong intermolecular attractions** (large $a$, e.g., NH₃, CO₂) have **high inversion temperatures**, while gases with **weak attractions** (small $a$, e.g., H₂, He) have **low inversion temperatures**. This is why hydrogen and helium must be **pre-cooled** (e.g., with liquid nitrogen) before JT expansion can liquefy them.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sign of $\mu_{JT}$ for Different Gases

| Gas | $a$ (L²·atm/mol²) | $b$ (L/mol) | $T_i = 2a/(Rb)$ (K) | Room $T$ Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Helium (He) | 0.0341 | 0.0237 | **~50** | Heats up (below 50K required for cooling) |
| Hydrogen (H₂) | 0.244 | 0.0266 | **~220** | Heats up at 298 K |
| Nitrogen (N₂) | 1.39 | 0.0391 | **~850** | Cools (room $T$ < $T_i$) |
| Oxygen (O₂) | 1.36 | 0.0318 | **~1040** | Cools |
| CO₂ | 3.59 | 0.0427 | **~2040** | Strongly cools |
| NH₃ | 4.17 | 0.0371 | **~2730** | Strongly cools |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "H₂ cannot be liquefied by JT expansion." This is **false**. Hydrogen *can* be liquefied by JT expansion, but only after **pre-cooling below 220 K** (e.g., using liquid N₂ at 77 K). The same applies to helium.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Joule-Thomson Derivation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: dH = TdS + VdP"] --> B["Apply Throttling Constraint: dH = 0"]
    B --> C["Result: TdS = -VdP"]
    C --> D["Express dS as function of T and P"]
    D --> E["dS = C_P/T dT + partialSdP dP"]
    E --> F["Apply Maxwell Relation: partialSbyPartialP_T = -partialVbyPartialT_P"]
    F --> G["Substitute C_P/T and Maxwell into dS"]
    G --> H["Obtain: C_P dT - T partialVbyPartialT_P dP = -V dP"]
    H --> I["Rearrange: C_P dT = T partialVbyPartialT_P dP - V dP"]
    I --> J["Solve for dT/dP at constant H"]
    J --> K["Final Result: mu_JT = 1 by C_P times T partialVbyPartialT_P minus V"]
    K --> L["Special Case 1: Ideal Gas -> mu_JT = 0"]
    K --> M["Special Case 2: van der Waals -> T_i = 2a by Rb"]
    L --> N["Physical Interpretation: No cooling or heating for ideal gas"]
    M --> O["Physical Interpretation: Inversion curve separates cooling and heating regions"]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style K fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style L fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style M fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Joule-Thomson Throttling Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Inlet: High Pressure P1, Temperature T1, Enthalpy H1"] -->|Steady Flow| V["Throttling Valve Porous Plug or Restriction"]
    V -->|Adiabatic Q=0| P2["Outlet: Low Pressure P2, Temperature T2, Enthalpy H2"]
    P2 --> O["Observation: Temperature change deltaT measured"]
    O --> CALC["Compute mu_JT approx deltaT by deltaP at constant H"]

    P1 -.->|Constant Enthalpy| P2

    CALC --> REG1["If mu_JT greater than 0: Gas COOLS on expansion"]
    CALC --> REG2["If mu_JT less than 0: Gas WARMS on expansion"]
    CALC --> REG3["If mu_JT equals 0: At inversion point or ideal gas"]

    style P1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style V fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style CALC fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style REG1 fill:#17becf,color:#fff
    style REG2 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style REG3 fill:#bcbd22,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Inversion Curve & Behavior Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Real Gas at State T, P"] --> CHECK{"Compare T with T_inversion"}
    CHECK -->|T less than T_i| COOL["Cooling Zone: mu_JT greater than 0"]
    CHECK -->|T equals T_i| INV["Inversion Curve: mu_JT equals 0"]
    CHECK -->|T greater than T_i| HEAT["Heating Zone: mu_JT less than 0"]
    COOL --> APP1["Application: Liquefaction, Linde process, Cryogenic cooling"]
    HEAT --> APP2["Application: Pre-cooling required before JT expansion, Joule heating effect"]
    INV --> APP3["Application: Boundary condition, used in cycle design"]

    style START fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff

    style COOL fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style HEAT fill:#d62728,color:#fff

```

## 4.4 Schematic — Functional Architecture of Linde Liquefaction Process (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COMP["Compressor: Pressurizes gas to high P1"] -->|High P, T rises| HEX["Counter-flow Heat Exchanger: Pre-cools incoming gas using cold return gas"]
    HEX -->|Cooled, High P| JTV["Joule-Thomson Valve: Throttling causes further cooling"]
    JTV -->|Cold low P gas| SEP["Separator: Liquefied portion collected, vapor returned"]
    SEP -->|Cold vapor| HEX
    SEP -->|Liquid Product| PROD["Liquid Product: Stored in cryogenic tank"]

    style COMP fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style HEX fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style JTV fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style SEP fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style PROD fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```
