<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Define the Joule-Thomson coefficient. For an ideal gas, show that $\mu_{JT} = 0$.**

**Model Answer (Expected KTU Key Format):**

**Definition:** The Joule-Thomson coefficient is defined as the rate of change of temperature with respect to pressure at constant enthalpy during a throttling process:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$$

**Proof for Ideal Gas:**

Starting from:

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$$

For an ideal gas, $PV = RT$ → $V = RT/P$

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$

Substituting:

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T \cdot \frac{R}{P} - \frac{RT}{P}\right] = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[\frac{RT}{P} - \frac{RT}{P}\right] = 0$$

Hence proved. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**What is the inversion temperature? Write the expression for the inversion temperature of a van der Waals gas.**

**Model Answer:**

**Inversion Temperature:** The temperature at which the Joule-Thomson coefficient changes sign (i.e., $\mu_{JT} = 0$) is called the **inversion temperature**. Below this temperature, the gas cools on expansion; above it, the gas heats up on expansion.

**Inversion Condition:** $\mu_{JT} = 0$ implies $T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = V$

**For van der Waals Gas:**

$$T_i = \frac{2a}{Rb}$$

where $a$ = van der Waals attraction parameter, $b$ = covolume, $R$ = universal gas constant. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Joule-Thomson coefficient in terms of $C_P$, $T$, and $PVT$ data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas at 30 bar and 400 K is throttled to 1 bar. The Joule-Thomson coefficient is approximately constant at 0.25 K/bar. Estimate the final temperature. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Start with the fundamental relation for enthalpy:**

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Step 2 — Apply isenthalpic condition ($dH = 0$):**

$$T\,dS = -V\,dP$$

**Step 3 — Write $dS$ in terms of $T$ and $P$:**

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 4 — Apply two key identities:**

- $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{C_P}{T}$
- $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ (Maxwell relation)

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$T\left[\frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP\right] = -V\,dP$$

$$C_P\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP = -V\,dP$$

$$C_P\,dT = \left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right] dP$$

**Step 6 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]}$$

**(7 marks distributed as: Statement of relations – 2 marks, Maxwell relation – 2 marks, Algebraic manipulation – 2 marks, Final boxed result – 1 mark)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Given Data:**

- $P_1 = 30$ bar, $T_1 = 400$ K
- $P_2 = 1$ bar
- $\mu_{JT} = 0.25$ K/bar (assumed constant)

**Approach:** Since $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{dT}{dP}$ at constant $H$, integrate between states 1 and 2:

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT = \mu_{JT} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} dP$$

$$T_2 - T_1 = \mu_{JT}(P_2 - P_1)$$

**Substituting values:**

$$T_2 - 400 = 0.25 \times (1 - 30) = 0.25 \times (-29) = -7.25 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 400 - 7.25 = 392.75 \text{ K}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Since $\mu_{JT} > 0$ and pressure dropped, the gas **cooled** by 7.25 K. This is the expected behavior for a gas below its inversion temperature.

**(7 marks distributed as: Setup of integral – 2 marks, Numerical substitution – 2 marks, Final answer with units – 1 mark, Physical interpretation – 2 marks)**

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Starting from the fundamental property relation, derive the expression for $\mu_{JT}$ and hence show that $\mu_{JT} = 0$ for an ideal gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a van der Waals gas, derive the expression for the inversion temperature and comment on its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

The derivation follows Steps 1–6 of Question A(a) above, leading to:

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$$

**Showing $\mu_{JT} = 0$ for an ideal gas:**

For an ideal gas: $V = RT/P$

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[\frac{RT}{P} - \frac{RT}{P}\right] = 0$$

This confirms that an ideal gas shows **no temperature change** during throttling — a direct consequence of the absence of intermolecular forces. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Set $\mu_{JT} = 0$:**

$$T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = V$$

**Step 2 — Use the van der Waals equation in virial-truncated form (or differentiate implicit form):**

For a van der Waals gas, the inversion curve in the limit of moderate pressures is derived from:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$

At the inversion point, one can show (via the identity $\mu_{JT} = 0$ and using the van der Waals equation of state):

$$T_i = \frac{2a}{Rb}$$

**Step 3 — Physical Significance:**

- $T_i$ **increases** with stronger intermolecular attraction ($a \uparrow$ → $T_i \uparrow$).
- Gases like CO₂ and NH₃ have high $T_i$ → they can be liquefied by JT expansion at room temperature.
- Gases like H₂ and He have low $T_i$ → they require **pre-cooling** below their inversion temperatures for JT liquefaction.
- The inversion curve on a $P$–$T$ diagram separates the cooling region (where $\mu_{JT} > 0$) from the heating region (where $\mu_{JT} < 0$).

**(7 marks distributed as: Inversion condition – 1 mark, vdW manipulation – 3 marks, Final expression – 1 mark, Physical interpretation – 2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 
> **1. Sign Convention Error (Loses 1–2 marks):** Some textbooks define $\mu_{JT} = -\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$. **KTU strictly follows the positive convention** shown above. Using the wrong sign will cost full marks.
> 
> **2. Skipping Maxwell Relation (Loses 1 mark):** The Maxwell relation $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ is **mandatory** in the derivation. Students often use it without stating it — this loses a mark in KTU's strict valuation.
> 
> **3. Forgetting "Constant H" in Derivative (Loses 1 mark):** Always write $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$ — not just $\frac{dT}{dP}$. The "constant $H$" subscript is crucial.
> 
> **4. Inversion Curve vs. Boyle Curve Confusion (Loses 1–2 marks):** Do **not** confuse the inversion curve ($\mu_{JT} = 0$) with the Boyle curve ($PV = RT$). They are entirely different.
> 
> **5. Units Mismatch (Loses 1 mark):** Ensure $\mu_{JT}$ is expressed in **K/bar** or **K/Pa**, not mixed. The pressure drop direction ($\Delta P$ sign) must match the temperature change sign.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$ — temperature change per unit pressure change at constant enthalpy.
- ✅ **Throttling Process:** Adiabatic, no shaft work, steady flow → **isenthalpic** ($H_1 = H_2$).
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$
- ✅ **Ideal Gas:** $\mu_{JT} = 0$ always (no cooling, no heating on expansion).
- ✅ **Real Gas Below $T_i$:** $\mu_{JT} > 0$ → **cooling on expansion** (e.g., N₂, O₂, CO₂ at room temperature).
- ✅ **Real Gas Above $T_i$:** $\mu_{JT} < 0$ → **heating on expansion** (e.g., H₂, He at room temperature).
- ✅ **Inversion Temperature (vdW):** $T_i = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$
- ✅ **Maxwell Relation Used:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$
- ✅ **Key Identity:** $C_P = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P$
- ✅ **Entropy Generation in Throttling:** $\Delta S > 0$ for real gases (irreversible process).
- ✅ **Linde Process:** Practical application — multiple JT expansions with heat exchangers for gas liquefaction.
- ✅ **Engineering Use:** Liquefaction of gases, refrigeration, cryogenics, gas processing.
- ✅ **Pre-cooling Required For:** H₂ (below 220 K), He (below ~50 K) before JT liquefaction.
- ✅ **Sign Convention (KTU):** $\mu_{JT} > 0$ ⟹ cooling; $\mu_{JT} < 0$ ⟹ heating; $\mu_{JT} = 0$ ⟹ no change.
- ✅ **Experimental Setup:** Two porous plugs separated by insulation — temperature difference measured across the device.
- ✅ **Difference from Adiabatic Expansion (Free):** Free expansion is **isentropic-like** for ideal gas; throttling is **isenthalpic** and applies to steady-flow processes.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Trick:** Memorize the master formula $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$ and the Maxwell relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$. From these two, you can re-derive the entire topic during the exam if needed. Also, remember the inversion temperature for vdW gas: $T_i = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$ — this is a high-yield formula for both Part A and Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gibbs Helmholtz Equation and Problem Solving in Application of Laws of Thermodynamics and Property Calculations Using EXCEL/MATLAB/SCILAB/PYTHON
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gibbs Helmholtz Equation and Problem Solving in Application of Laws of Thermodynamics and Property Calculations Using EXCEL/MATLAB/SCILAB/PYTHON

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation — Formal Definition

The **Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation** is one of the most powerful relationships in classical thermodynamics. It quantitatively links the **temperature dependence of the Gibbs free energy ($G$)** to the **enthalpy ($H$)** of a system at constant pressure. In its most general differential form, it is written as:

$$\left[\frac{\partial\left(\dfrac{G}{T}\right)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}}$$

The same structure applies to *changes* in these properties (i.e., for chemical reactions or physical processes), giving:

$$\left[\frac{\partial\left(\dfrac{\Delta G}{T}\right)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation is a *direct consequence* of combining the **First Law** (energy conservation) and the **Second Law** (entropy maximization). It is the bridge that lets you compute the *Gibbs free energy at any temperature* if you know the enthalpy — a routine requirement in reactor design, equilibrium conversion estimation, and process simulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Forecast" Picture

Think of the Gibbs free energy $G$ as the **"weather report"** of a chemical reaction:

- A **negative $\Delta G$** = the reaction is "rainy and certain" (spontaneous).
- A **positive $\Delta G$** = the reaction is "sunny and stable as written" (non-spontaneous).

But the *amount of rain* depends on the *temperature* (the season). The Gibbs–Helmholtz equation is the **meteorologist's formula** that tells you: *for every degree the temperature changes, how much will the spontaneity (rainfall) shift?* The enthalpy $\Delta H$ acts as the **thermal inertia** — the larger it is, the more violently the spontaneity changes with temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine boiling water. At low temperatures, the water happily sits as a liquid (low $\Delta G$ for liquid state). As you heat it, the *enthalpy of vaporization* ($\Delta H_{vap}$) is large and positive, so the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation tells you that $\Delta G$ of vaporization decreases sharply with temperature — until it hits zero at the **boiling point**. The equation is essentially the *mathematical fingerprint* of every phase change you have ever observed.

## 1.3 Property Calculations — Engineering Context

In **Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics**, *property calculations* refer to determining thermodynamic state functions (such as $P$, $V$, $T$, $H$, $S$, $G$, $U$, $C_P$, $C_V$, fugacity $\hat{f}$, activity $\hat{a}$, etc.) for pure substances and mixtures under varying conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Note:** Modern chemical engineers rarely solve these problems by hand. Software tools — **MS Excel** (for quick regression/iteration), **MATLAB / SCILAB / Python** (for symbolic math, root-finding, and numerical integration) — are used to perform:
> 1. Iterative solutions of cubic equations of state (van der Waals, Peng–Robinson, SRK).
> 2. Numerical integration of $dH = C_P\,dT$ and $dS = C_P/T\,dT$.
> 3. Multi-variable root finding for phase and chemical equilibrium.
> 4. Regression of experimental data to fit interaction parameters.

These tools are **conceptual problem-solving aids** — you must always understand the underlying thermodynamic laws before trusting any numerical output.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \;\text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1} = 8.314 \;\text{Pa}\cdot\text{m}^{3}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$
- **Standard Reference State:** $T^{\circ} = 298.15 \;\text{K}$, $P^{\circ} = 1 \;\text{bar} = 10^{5} \;\text{Pa}$
- **Sign Convention:** Heat absorbed by system $\to$ **positive** $Q$; Heat evolved $\to$ **negative** $Q$ (matches KTU convention)
- **Standard Gibbs Free Energy of Formation:** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}$ at $298.15$ K is tabulated for all species
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Thermodynamic Potentials — Background

The Gibbs–Helmholtz equation is built on the four fundamental **thermodynamic potentials**:

| Potential | Symbol | Natural Variables | Fundamental Equation |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Internal Energy | $U$ | $S, V$ | $dU = TdS - PdV$ |
| Enthalpy | $H$ | $S, P$ | $dH = TdS + VdP$ |
| Helmholtz Free Energy | $A$ | $T, V$ | $dA = -SdT - PdV$ |
| Gibbs Free Energy | $G$ | $T, P$ | $dG = -SdT + VdP$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Gibbs potential $G$** is the workhorse for chemical engineers because $T$ and $P$ are the variables most easily *controlled* in industrial processes (reactor, distillation column, etc.).

## 2.2 Why the Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation Exists — The Logic Chain

The derivation flows from **one simple observation** combined with **two definitions**:

**Step 1 — Definition of $G$:**
$$G \equiv H - TS$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Work Theorem (Second Law):** At constant $T$ and $P$, the maximum non-expansion work obtainable from a closed system equals the *decrease* in Gibbs free energy. So $G$ governs spontaneity:
- $\Delta G < 0 \Rightarrow$ spontaneous
- $\Delta G > 0 \Rightarrow$ non-spontaneous
- $\Delta G = 0 \Rightarrow$ equilibrium

**Step 3 — Temperature Dependence of $G$:** From $dG = -SdT + VdP$, holding $P$ constant:
$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S$$

**Step 4 — Eliminating $S$:** From $G = H - TS \Rightarrow S = (H - G)/T$. Substituting:
$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\frac{H - G}{T} = \frac{G - H}{T}$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic Rearrangement to the Helmholtz Form:** Multiply by $1/T$ and apply the quotient rule of differentiation. This yields the *standard* Gibbs–Helmholtz form (full derivation in Section 3).

## 2.3 The Three Useful Forms of the Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation

| Form | Equation | Best Used When |
|------|----------|----------------|
| **Form 1 (Differential)** | $\left[\dfrac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -H/T^{2}$ | $H$ is *not* constant; you must integrate with $H(T)$ |
| **Form 2 (Alternative variable)** | $\left[\dfrac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial (1/T)}\right]_{P} = H$ | Plotting $G/T$ vs $1/T$ to extract $H$ from slope |
| **Form 3 (Reaction form, constant $\Delta H$)** | $\dfrac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta H \left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$ | Quick exam calculations where $\Delta H \approx$ const. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A frequent valuation trap — the **integrated form (Form 3)** is **valid only when $\Delta H$ is independent of $T$** (or you are integrating a temperature-averaged $\Delta H$). For high accuracy, you must use $C_P$ corrections:
> $$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H(T_{1}) + \int_{T_{1}}^{T} \Delta C_{P}\,dT$$
> where $\Delta C_{P} = \sum \nu_{i} C_{P,i}$.

## 2.4 The Helmholtz Companion Equation

By **exact parallel derivation** using $A \equiv U - TS$ and $dA = -SdT - PdV$, we get:

$$\left[\frac{\partial(A/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{V} = -\frac{U}{T^{2}}$$

This is the **Helmholtz–Helmholtz equation**, used in constant-volume processes and statistical mechanics.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning / Application |
|---|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | $dU = TdS - PdV$ | Combined 1st + 2nd law (closed system) |
| 2 | $dH = TdS + VdP$ | Combined 1st + 2nd law (constant $P$ utility) |
| 3 | $dG = -SdT + VdP$ | **Master equation for $G$** |
| 4 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S$ | Temperature slope of $G$ |
| 5 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = V$ | Pressure slope of $G$ |
| 6 | $\left[\dfrac{\partial(G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -H/T^{2}$ | **Gibbs–Helmholtz (Form 1)** |
| 7 | $\left[\dfrac{\partial(A/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{V} = -U/T^{2}$ | **Helmholtz companion** |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta H\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$ | **Integrated GH (constant $\Delta H$)** |
| 9 | $\Delta H(T) = \Delta H(T_{1}) + \displaystyle\int_{T_{1}}^{T}\Delta C_{P}\,dT$ | Kirchhoff's Law |
| 10 | $\Delta S(T) = \Delta S(T_{1}) + \displaystyle\int_{T_{1}}^{T}\dfrac{\Delta C_{P}}{T}\,dT$ | Entropy temperature correction |
| 11 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = C_{P}$ | Heat capacity link |
| 12 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = C_{V}$ | Heat capacity link |
| 13 | For ideal gas: $C_{P} - C_{V} = R$ | Mayer's relation |
| 14 | $H = U + PV$ | Enthalpy definition |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How Gibbs–Helmholtz Is Used |
|-------------|----------------------------|
| **Ammonia Synthesis (Haber Process)** | Predicting $\Delta G$ at $700$ K from $298$ K data to find optimum $T$ |
| **Steam Power Plant Design** | Computing $\Delta G$ of vaporization to size turbines |
| **Refrigeration Cycles** | $G$ minimization identifies phase boundaries |
| **Reactor Equilibrium** | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ — combined with GH to get $K(T)$ |
| **Process Simulation Software** | Solvers use GH + EoS to compute multi-phase equilibrium |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — The Three Forms of the Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation

### Form 1: From the Definition of $G$ to $[\partial(G/T)/\partial T]_P = -H/T^2$

**Start with the definition of Gibbs free energy:**

$$G = H - TS$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $S$ explicitly:**

$$S = \frac{H - G}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Use the combined 1st + 2nd law relation for $G$ at constant $P$:**

$$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP \;\Rightarrow\; \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\left(\frac{H - G}{T}\right)$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{G - H}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $1/T$ to set up the quotient rule:**

$$\frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{G - H}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the LHS as a derivative of the quotient $G/T$ using the product rule. Let:**

$$u = G, \quad v = T^{-1}$$

**The LHS is:**

$$\frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \left[\frac{\partial G}{\partial T} \cdot \frac{1}{T}\right]_{P} = \left[\frac{\partial (G \cdot T^{-1})}{\partial T}\right]_{P} - G \cdot \frac{\partial (T^{-1})}{\partial T}$$

**Compute $\partial(T^{-1})/\partial T = -1/T^2$, so:**

$$\frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} + \frac{G}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the equation from Step 4:**

$$\left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} + \frac{G}{T^{2}} = \frac{G - H}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the $+G/T^{2}$ on both sides (it appears on RHS as $G/T^{2}$):**

$$\left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} + \frac{G}{T^{2}} = \frac{G}{T^{2}} - \frac{H}{T^{2}}$$

$$\left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}}$$

**Result — Form 1 of the Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation:**

$$\boxed{\left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}}}$$

---

### Form 2: In Terms of $1/T$ (For Graphical Use)

**Use the chain rule:** $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial T} = \dfrac{\partial (1/T)}{\partial T} \cdot \dfrac{\partial}{\partial (1/T)} = -\dfrac{1}{T^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial}{\partial (1/T)}$

**Substitute into Form 1:**

$$-\frac{1}{T^{2}} \left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial (1/T)}\right]_{P} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}}$$

**Multiply both sides by $-T^{2}$:**

$$\boxed{\left[\frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial (1/T)}\right]_{P} = H}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this form matters (KTU 2024):** Plot $G/T$ (y-axis) vs $1/T$ (x-axis). The **slope** at any point gives the **enthalpy $H$**. This is a *powerful graphical technique* for extracting enthalpies from Gibbs energy data — and a frequent exam question.

---

### Form 3: Integrated Form for Constant $\Delta H$

**For a chemical reaction or process with $\Delta H$ approximately constant, separate variables in Form 1:**

$$d\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}\,dT$$

**Integrate between states $1$ and $2$:**

$$\int_{1}^{2} d\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right) = -\Delta H \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

$$\frac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} - \frac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} = -\Delta H \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}}$$

$$\frac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} - \frac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta H \left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

**Result — Integrated Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} - \frac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta H \left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)}$$

---

## 3.2 The Helmholtz–Helmholtz Equation (Companion Derivation)

**Start from $A = U - TS$, so $S = (U - A)/T$:**

$$dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV \;\Rightarrow\; \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = -S = \frac{A - U}{T}$$

**Apply the *exact same quotient rule* as above:**

$$\left[\frac{\partial (A/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{V} = -\frac{U}{T^{2}}$$

**In the integrated form:**

$$\frac{\Delta A_{2}}{T_{2}} - \frac{\Delta A_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta U \left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** For the formation of ammonia:
$$\tfrac{1}{2}N_{2}(g) + \tfrac{3}{2}H_{2}(g) \to NH_{3}(g)$$

Given: $\Delta H^{\circ}_{f,298} = -46.11 \;\text{kJ/mol}$, $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f,298} = -16.45 \;\text{kJ/mol}$. Assuming $\Delta H$ is independent of $T$, calculate $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}$ at $T = 500\;\text{K}$ and determine the equilibrium constant $K$ at this temperature.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**

- $T_{1} = 298\;\text{K}$, $\Delta H^{\circ}_{1} = -46{,}110\;\text{J/mol}$
- $\Delta G^{\circ}_{1} = -16{,}450\;\text{J/mol}$
- $T_{2} = 500\;\text{K}$, $\Delta H^{\circ}_{2} \approx \Delta H^{\circ}_{1} = -46{,}110\;\text{J/mol}$ (constant assumption)
- $R = 8.314\;\text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$

**Step 2 — Apply the integrated Gibbs–Helmholtz equation:**

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{2}}{T_{2}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1}}{T_{1}} + \Delta H^{\circ}\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $1/T_{2}$ and $1/T_{1}$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_{1}} = \frac{1}{298} = 3.3557 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_{2}} = \frac{1}{500} = 2.0000 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta G^{\circ}_{1}/T_{1}$:**

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1}}{T_{1}} = \frac{-16{,}450}{298} = -55.20\;\text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the difference $(1/T_{2} - 1/T_{1})$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} = (2.0000 - 3.3557) \times 10^{-3} = -1.3557 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\Delta H^{\circ} \times (1/T_{2} - 1/T_{1})$:**

$$\Delta H^{\circ}\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right) = (-46{,}110) \times (-1.3557 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$= +62.51\;\text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Sum the two terms:**

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{2}}{T_{2}} = -55.20 + 62.51 = +7.31\;\text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $\Delta G^{\circ}_{2}$:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{2} = 500 \times 7.31 = +3{,}655\;\text{J/mol} = +3.66\;\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 9 — Compute the equilibrium constant $K$ at $500$ K:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K \;\Rightarrow\; \ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT}$$

$$\ln K = -\frac{3{,}655}{8.314 \times 500} = -\frac{3{,}655}{4{,}157} = -0.8790$$

$$K = e^{-0.8790} = 0.415$$

**Step 10 — Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** At $500\;\text{K}$, $\Delta G^{\circ}$ has become *positive* ($+3.66$ kJ/mol), meaning the ammonia synthesis is **no longer spontaneous at standard conditions**. The equilibrium constant $K < 1$ confirms that reactant-side equilibrium is favored. **This is why the Haber process uses high pressure and a catalyst at moderate temperatures** — to *kinetically* drive the reaction while keeping $\Delta G$ slightly negative. A classic industrial application of the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation.

---

## 3.4 Application of Laws of Thermodynamics — PVT Behavior (Analytical Matrix)

| Law | Statement | Mathematical Form | Property Calculation Use |
|-----|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Zeroth Law** | Thermal equilibrium is transitive | $T_{A} = T_{B} = T_{C}$ | Sets basis for temperature measurement |
| **1st Law** | Energy is conserved | $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$ | Enthalpy $H = U + PV$ for flow processes |
| **2nd Law** | Entropy of universe increases | $dS \geq \delta Q/T$ | Defines $G = H - TS$, equilibrium via $\Delta G = 0$ |
| **3rd Law** | Perfect crystal at $0$ K has $S = 0$ | $S(0) = 0$ | Absolute entropy reference for all substances |

**Combined 1st + 2nd Law (the four Maxwell-style relations):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{S} = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_{V}, \quad \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_{P}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T}, \quad \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T}$$

These are essential for **residual property calculations** (departure functions) used in software like Aspen Plus, HYSYS, and process simulators.

## 3.5 Property Calculation Workflow (Software-Aided)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual workflow only** — KTU 2024 emphasizes understanding the *thermodynamic logic*, not the syntax of any specific programming language.

| Step | Operation | Software Use Case |
|------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Input $P$, $T$, feed composition | Excel cell entry or matrix input |
| 2 | Choose equation of state (PR / SRK / vdW) | Library function call |
| 3 | Solve cubic EoS for compressibility $Z$ | Root-finding algorithm (Newton–Raphson) |
| 4 | Compute fugacity coefficient $\hat{\phi}_{i}$ | Numerical differentiation of $Z$ w.r.t. composition |
| 5 | Compute departure functions $H - H^{ig}$, $S - S^{ig}$ | Numerical integration using $Z$ |
| 6 | Apply GH + van't Hoff for reaction $K$ | Iterative or closed-form integration |
| 7 | Check for phase split (VLE) | Flash calculation (Rachford–Rice equation) |
| 8 | Output stream table | Tabular output / report generation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The *exact* numerical method (Newton, bisection, Broyden) is **secondary** to the *thermodynamic principle* (mass balance + equality of fugacities + GH-based $K$). Students must always justify the *physical meaning* of every computed result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logical Flow — Derivation of the Three Gibbs–Helmholtz Forms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Definition G = H - TS] --> B[Solve for S: S = H-G / T]
    B --> C[Maxwell relation: dG = -S dT + V dP]
    C --> D[At constant P: dG/dT = -S]
    D --> E[Substitute S: dG/dT = G-H / T]
    E --> F[Multiply by 1/T and apply quotient rule]
    F --> G[Form 1: dG/T by dT equals -H/T squared]
    G --> H[Apply chain rule with 1/T variable]
    H --> I[Form 2: dG/T by d1/T equals H]
    G --> J[Integrate with constant H assumption]
    J --> K[Form 3: dG sub2 over T sub2 minus dG sub1 over T sub1 equals dH times 1/T2 minus 1/T1]
    K --> L[Apply to reactions: replace G with dG, H with dH]
    L --> M[Final: 3 Forms of Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Property Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Input Block: P, T, Composition] --> EOS[Equation of State Selector]
    EOS --> CUBIC[Cubic EoS Solver: vdW, PR, SRK]
    CUBIC --> Z[Compute Compressibility Z]
    Z --> FUG[Fugacity Coefficient phi]
    FUG --> DEPART[Departure Functions: H-Hig, S-Sig]
    DEPART --> GIBBS[Apply Gibbs Helmholtz: dG/T vs T]
    GIBBS --> KEQ[Compute Equilibrium Constant K]
    KEQ --> FLASH[Flash Calculation: V and L splits]
    FLASH --> OUTPUT[Output: Stream Table and Phase Fractions]

    style INPUT fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style EOS fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style CUBIC fill:#1976d2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Z fill:#1e88e5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style FUG fill:#ff6f00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style DEPART fill:#f57c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style GIBBS fill:#e65100,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style KEQ fill:#d84315,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style FLASH fill:#bf360c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style OUTPUT fill:#4a148c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Application Map — Where the Laws Meet the Calculator

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Zeroth Law: T definition] --> T[Temperature]
    L2[First Law: U and H conservation] --> H[Enthalpy]
    L3[Second Law: S and G definition] --> S[Entropy and G]
    L4[Third Law: Absolute S reference] --> ABS[Reference Entropy]

    T --> CYC[Process Cycles: Rankine, refrigeration]
    H --> CYC
    S --> CYC
    ABS --> CYC

    L2 --> REAC[Reaction Equilibrium]
    L3 --> REAC
    REAC --> GH[Gibbs Helmholtz Equation]
    GH --> EQK[Equilibrium Constant K T]

    H --> EOS[Equation of State: PVT]
    S --> EOS
    EOS --> PROP[All Property Calculations]

    style L1 fill:#0d47a1,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#0d47a1,color:#fff
    style L3 fill:#0d47a1,color:#fff
    style L4 fill:#0d47a1,color:#fff
    style GH fill:#c62828,color:#fff

    style PROP fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Decision Map — Which Gibbs–Helmholtz Form to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Given data: T1, T2, H, G] --> Q1{Is H constant?}
    Q1 -- YES --> F3[Use Form 3: Integrated constant H]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is Cp data available?}
    Q2 -- YES --> F1[Use Form 1: Integrate numerically with H of T]
    Q2 -- NO --> F2[Use Form 2: Plot G/T vs 1/T, slope gives H]
    F3 --> OUT[Compute dG at T2]
    F1 --> OUT
    F2 --> OUT
    OUT --> K[Use dG equals -RT ln K for equilibrium]

    style START fill:#0d47a1,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    style F2 fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    style F3 fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    style OUT fill:#c62828,color:#fff
    style K fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation. What is its physical significance in predicting reaction spontaneity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Gibbs–Helmholtz equation in its differential form is:

$$\left[\frac{\partial(\Delta G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$$

**Physical Significance (2 marks):**
1. It links the temperature dependence of the **Gibbs free energy change** of a reaction to the **enthalpy change**.
2. For **exothermic reactions** ($\Delta H < 0$), the RHS is positive, meaning $\Delta G/T$ *increases* with $T$ — i.e., $\Delta G$ becomes *less negative* (or more positive) at higher $T$, so the reaction becomes *less spontaneous*.
3. For **endothermic reactions** ($\Delta H > 0$), the opposite holds — $\Delta G$ becomes *more negative* with $T$, increasing spontaneity.
4. The equation is essential for **predicting the temperature at which a reaction ceases to be spontaneous** ($\Delta G = 0$) — i.e., the equilibrium crossover temperature.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**Mention any three ways in which computational tools (Excel/MATLAB/SCILAB) are used in solving thermodynamic property problems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Iterative solutions of cubic equations of state** (van der Waals, Peng–Robinson, SRK) using root-finding routines to obtain the compressibility factor $Z$ for non-ideal gases.
2. **Numerical integration of $C_P$ data** to evaluate enthalpy and entropy departures:
$$H^{R}(T,P) = \int_{0}^{P}\left[V - T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}\right]dP$$
3. **Regression of experimental VLE/PVT data** to determine interaction parameters in activity coefficient models (Margules, van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC).
4. **Flash and reaction equilibrium calculations** via the Rachford–Rice equation and van't Hoff integration of the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation (any three).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

#### (a) **Derive the three forms of the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation, starting from the basic definition of the Gibbs free energy.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $G = H - TS$ (definition of Gibbs free energy)

**Step 1:** Solve for $S$: $S = (H - G)/T$

**Step 2:** From combined 1st and 2nd law: $dG = VdP - SdT$. At constant $P$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:
$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{G - H}{T}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $1/T$ and apply the quotient rule to LHS:
$$\frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \left[\frac{\partial(G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} + \frac{G}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Equate and cancel $G/T^{2}$:

$$\left[\frac{\partial(G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}} \quad \text{...(Form 1)}$$

**Step 6 (Form 2):** Use chain rule: $\partial/\partial T = -1/T^{2} \cdot \partial/\partial(1/T)$
$$\left[\frac{\partial(G/T)}{\partial(1/T)}\right]_{P} = H$$

**Step 7 (Form 3):** Integrate Form 1 between $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ with constant $H$:
$$\frac{G_{2}}{T_{2}} - \frac{G_{1}}{T_{1}} = H\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

Applying to reactions: replace $G \to \Delta G$ and $H \to \Delta H$. **[Full marks: 7]**

---

#### (b) **The standard Gibbs free energy of formation of $NH_{3}(g)$ is $-16.45$ kJ/mol at $298$ K and the standard enthalpy of formation is $-46.11$ kJ/mol. Assuming $\Delta H$ is constant, compute $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}$ at $500$ K and the equilibrium constant for the formation reaction.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_{1} = 298$ K, $\Delta G^{\circ}_{1} = -16{,}450$ J/mol, $\Delta H^{\circ} = -46{,}110$ J/mol
- $T_{2} = 500$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Apply integrated Gibbs–Helmholtz:**

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{2}}{T_{2}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1}}{T_{1}} + \Delta H^{\circ}\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

$$\frac{1}{T_{1}} = \frac{1}{298} = 3.3557 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_{2}} = \frac{1}{500} = 2.0000 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1}}{T_{1}} = \frac{-16{,}450}{298} = -55.20\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 3 — Difference term:**

$$\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} = (2.0000 - 3.3557) \times 10^{-3} = -1.3557 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Product with $\Delta H^{\circ}$:**

$$\Delta H^{\circ}\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right) = (-46{,}110)(-1.3557 \times 10^{-3}) = +62.51\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Sum and solve:**

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{2}}{T_{2}} = -55.20 + 62.51 = +7.31\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{2} = 500 \times 7.31 = +3{,}655\;\text{J/mol} \approx +3.66\;\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $K$:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K \Rightarrow \ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT} = -\frac{3{,}655}{8.314 \times 500} = -0.8790$$

$$K = e^{-0.8790} \approx 0.415$$

**Conclusion:** At $500$ K, $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}$ is positive, meaning $NH_{3}$ formation is non-spontaneous under standard conditions. The Haber process therefore operates at high pressure and uses a catalyst. **[Full marks: 7]**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]`

#### (a) **Explain how the combined first and second laws lead to the four fundamental property relations. Use these to derive the temperature and pressure dependence of $G$.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Combined 1st + 2nd Law (closed system):**

$$dU = TdS - PdV$$

**Step 1 — Derive $dH$ from $H = U + PV$:**

$$dH = dU + PdV + VdP = TdS - PdV + PdV + VdP = TdS + VdP$$

**Step 2 — Derive $dA$ from $A = U - TS$:**

$$dA = dU - TdS - SdT = TdS - PdV - TdS - SdT = -SdT - PdV$$

**Step 3 — Derive $dG$ from $G = H - TS = U + PV - TS$:**

$$dG = dH - TdS - SdT = TdS + VdP - TdS - SdT = -SdT + VdP$$

**The Four Maxwell Relations** (obtained via equality of mixed partial derivatives):

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{S} = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_{V}, \quad \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{S} = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_{P}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_{T} = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_{V}, \quad \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}$$

**Step 4 — Temperature and Pressure Dependence of $G$:**

From $dG = -SdT + VdP$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S, \quad \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = V$$

For a reaction: $\left(\dfrac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -\Delta S$, $\left(\dfrac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = \Delta V$

**Step 5 — Eliminate $S$ using $S = (H - G)/T$:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{G - H}{T}$$

Applying quotient rule yields the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation. **[Full marks: 7]**

---

#### (b) **A reaction has $\Delta H = -80$ kJ/mol and $\Delta S = -100$ J/(mol·K) at $T = 300$ K. Compute $\Delta G$ at $300$ K, then use the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation to find $\Delta G$ at $400$ K (assuming constant $\Delta H$).** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta G$ at $300$ K:**

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S = -80{,}000 - (300)(-100) = -80{,}000 + 30{,}000 = -50{,}000\;\text{J/mol}$$

$$\Delta G_{1} = -50\;\text{kJ/mol} \quad (\text{spontaneous at 300 K})$$

**Step 2 — Apply integrated Gibbs–Helmholtz equation:**

$$\frac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} = \frac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} + \Delta H\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term:**

$$\frac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} = \frac{-50{,}000}{300} = -166.67\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} = \frac{1}{400} - \frac{1}{300} = 0.0025 - 0.003333 = -8.333 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Product with $\Delta H$:**

$$\Delta H\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right) = (-80{,}000)(-8.333 \times 10^{-4}) = +66.67\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Sum and solve for $\Delta G_{2}$:**

$$\frac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} = -166.67 + 66.67 = -100.0\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$$

$$\Delta G_{2} = 400 \times (-100.0) = -40{,}000\;\text{J/mol} = -40\;\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Verification using $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$:**

If $\Delta S$ is constant, $\Delta G_{2} = -80{,}000 - (400)(-100) = -80{,}000 + 40{,}000 = -40\;\text{kJ/mol}$ ✓

**Conclusion:** Reaction is still spontaneous at $400$ K but *less* so. The crossover temperature ($\Delta G = 0$) is $T = \Delta H/\Delta S = 80{,}000/100 = 800$ K. Above $800$ K, the reaction becomes non-spontaneous. **[Full marks: 7]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Mistakes (Examiner-Identified Pitfalls):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kJ to J** (or vice versa) — leads to errors of $1000\times$. Always unify SI units **before** substituting into any equation.
> 2. **Mixing up $G$ and $\Delta G$** in the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation. The *reaction* form uses $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$, not the absolute values. Loss of 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Applying the integrated form when $\Delta H$ varies with $T$** — for any problem where $C_P$ data is given, students must use Kirchhoff's law to first correct $\Delta H$ to the target temperature.
> 4. **Sign error in the bracket** $(1/T_{2} - 1/T_{1})$ — the order is critical. A single sign slip reverses the entire conclusion.
> 5. **Ignoring units of $R$** — when $\Delta G$ is in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); when in kJ/mol, $R = 8.314 \times 10^{-3}$ kJ/(mol·K).
> 6. **Forgetting the partial derivative condition** — the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation is valid at **constant $P$** for the $G$ form and **constant $V$** for the $A$ form. Mixing them up is a classic KTU trap.
> 7. **Not stating the assumption** "$\Delta H$ is constant" explicitly in the integrated-form solution — costs 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Gibbs–Helmholtz (Form 1, Differential):** $\left[\dfrac{\partial(G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -H/T^{2}$
- **Gibbs–Helmholtz (Form 2, $1/T$ variable):** $\left[\dfrac{\partial(G/T)}{\partial(1/T)}\right]_{P} = H$ — slope gives $H$ graphically
- **Gibbs–Helmholtz (Form 3, Integrated, constant $\Delta H$):** $\dfrac{\Delta G_{2}}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{\Delta G_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta H\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$
- **Helmholtz Companion Equation:** $\left[\dfrac{\partial(A/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{V} = -U/T^{2}$
- **Master Equations:**
  - $dU = TdS - PdV$
  - $dH = TdS + VdP$
  - $dA = -SdT - PdV$
  - $dG = -SdT + VdP$
- **Slopes of $G$:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S$ and $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = V$
- **Spontaneity Rule:** $\Delta G < 0$ (spontaneous), $\Delta G > 0$ (non-spontaneous), $\Delta G = 0$ (equilibrium)
- **Equilibrium Link:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ — combine with GH to get $K$ as a function of $T$
- **Crossover Temperature:** $T^{*} = \Delta H / \Delta S$ (where $\Delta G = 0$)
- **Four Maxwell Relations** are the bridge from measurable $PVT$ data to unmeasurable $S$ and $U$ derivatives
- **Kirchhoff's Law (for $\Delta H$ correction):** $\Delta H(T) = \Delta H(T_{1}) + \displaystyle\int_{T_{1}}^{T}\Delta C_{P}\,dT$
- **Kirchhoff's Law (for $\Delta S$ correction):** $\Delta S(T) = \Delta S(T_{1}) + \displaystyle\int_{T_{1}}^{T}\dfrac{\Delta C_{P}}{T}\,dT$
- **Property Calculation Tools** (Excel/MATLAB/SCILAB/Python conceptually): Used for cubic EoS roots, departure functions, flash calculations, and reaction equilibrium — *thermodynamic understanding first, software second*.
- **Units always in SI:** $R = 8.314\;\text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$
- **Exothermic reaction ($\Delta H < 0$):** Spontaneity *decreases* with $T$
- **Endothermic reaction ($\Delta H > 0$):** Spontaneity *increases* with $T$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P-V and P-T Diagram of Pure Substances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** P-V and P-T Diagram of Pure Substances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Pure Substance?

In **Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics**, a **pure substance** is defined as a substance that has a fixed chemical composition throughout its mass and exists in one or more phases. A pure substance does **not** have to be a single chemical element or compound; it can be a *mixture* of several substances as long as the mixture is *homogeneous* in composition (e.g., air, which is a uniform mixture of N₂, O₂, and other gases).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Direct Board Terminology):**
> A pure substance is one whose chemical composition is *invariant* (does not change) and is the same in all phases. Phase change does **not** alter the chemical identity of the substance — only its physical state.

## 1.2 Why Study P-V and P-T Diagrams?

A **Phase Diagram** is a graphical representation of the physical states of a pure substance under different conditions of **Pressure (P), Volume (V), and Temperature (T)**. KTU examiners place massive emphasis (typically **14-mark full questions**) on these diagrams because they form the *visual foundation* for understanding:
- Saturation behavior
- Critical phenomena
- Phase equilibrium (Solid–Liquid–Vapor)
- Thermodynamic property tables (Steam Tables, Refrigerant Tables)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Hiking Mountain" of States

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Thermodynamic Mountain**
>
> Imagine a pure substance (e.g., water) as a *hiker* on a mountain. The **altitude** represents **pressure**, and the **horizontal distance** represents **volume** (or **temperature**, depending on the diagram).
> - **Base of the mountain** = High pressure, small volume → **Compressed Liquid / Solid**
> - **Peak of the mountain** = Low pressure, large volume → **Superheated Vapor**
> - **Mid-slope flat region** = **Phase Change Region (Latent Heat)**
>
> Just as a hiker can rest at the base (liquid), at the peak (vapor), or in the middle plateau (two-phase mixture), a substance can exist in different physical states at the *same pressure* but *different volumes/temperatures*. The two-phase region is the "plateau" of the mountain where both liquid and vapor coexist!

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants (Water — Reference Fluid)

| Property | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical Pressure | P_c | **22.064 MPa** (≈ 220.64 bar) | MPa |
| Critical Temperature | T_c | **647.1 K** (≈ 374 °C) | K |
| Critical Specific Volume | v_c | **0.003155 m³/kg** | m³/kg |
| Triple Point Pressure | P_tp | **611.657 Pa** (≈ 0.006112 bar) | Pa |
| Triple Point Temperature | T_tp | **273.16 K** (0.01 °C) | K |
| Normal Boiling Point | T_b | 373.15 K (100 °C) | K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For numerical problems, water is the default working fluid unless stated otherwise. Always **memorize** the critical point coordinates of water — they appear in nearly every Part A question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the P-V Diagram (Pressure–Volume Diagram)

The **P-V Diagram** is plotted at a **constant temperature** (isothermally) for the substance. When multiple isotherms are plotted together, they form the iconic **"Saturation Dome."**

### 2.1.1 Key Regions of the P-V Diagram

**A. Compressed (Subcooled) Liquid Region:**
- Located to the **left** of the saturated liquid line.
- Temperature T < T_sat at the given pressure.
- Substance behaves almost like an *incompressible fluid* (density ≈ constant).
- Property: **v < v_f** (specific volume less than that of saturated liquid).

**B. Saturated Liquid Line (Left Branch of Dome):**
- Vertical line representing **100% liquid** state.
- All points have quality **x = 0**.
- Pressure equals **saturation pressure** P_sat for that temperature.

**C. Saturated Liquid–Vapor Mixture Region (Inside the Dome):**
- **Most important region for KTU problems.**
- Substance exists as a mixture of liquid + vapor.
- Quality **x** ranges from **0 to 1**.
- Pressure remains **constant** (equal to P_sat) as heat is added — this is the key isothermal–isobaric phase change.
- **Lever Rule** is applied here to determine the proportion of each phase.

**D. Saturated Vapor Line (Right Branch of Dome):**
- Vertical line representing **100% vapor** state.
- Quality **x = 1**.
- Marks the completion of vaporization.

**E. Superheated Vapor Region:**
- Located to the **right** of the saturated vapor line.
- T > T_sat at that pressure.
- Property: **v > v_g**.
- Behaves nearly as an **ideal gas** at low pressures.

**F. Critical Point (C):**
- The **highest point** on the saturation dome.
- At this point, saturated liquid and saturated vapor become **indistinguishable** — they have the *same specific volume, same density, and same refractive index*.
- Above T_c, no amount of pressure can liquefy the gas → **supercritical fluid** region.

### 2.1.2 Isotherm Behavior

- **Below T_c:** The isotherm enters the dome (flat line at P_sat) — this represents **constant-pressure phase change**.
- **At T = T_c:** The isotherm just *touches* the critical point (inflection point, horizontal tangent).
- **Above T_c:** Isotherm shows **no phase change** — smooth, monotonically decreasing curve (gas-like behavior throughout). Substance is **supercritical**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual "Why":** Why is the dome shape a "dome"? At low temperatures, molecules have low kinetic energy, so a small volume forces them close together (liquid). At high temperatures, molecules are energetic and spread out (vapor). The horizontal "roof" of the dome is where molecules have *just enough* energy to break intermolecular bonds while still being compressed.

## 2.2 Anatomy of the P-T Diagram (Pressure–Temperature Diagram)

The **P-T Diagram** is often called the **Phase Diagram proper** because it shows the **phase boundaries** of the substance.

### 2.2.1 Key Features

**A. Triple Point (T):**
- The **unique** point where solid, liquid, and vapor coexist in **thermodynamic equilibrium**.
- For water: T_tp = **273.16 K**, P_tp = **611.657 Pa**.
- It is the *only* state at which all three phases can co-exist.

**B. Sublimation Curve (Solid → Vapor line):**
- Below the triple point pressure.
- Solid directly converts to vapor (e.g., dry ice at atmospheric pressure).

**C. Vaporization Curve (Liquid → Vapor line, also called Boiling Curve):**
- From triple point to critical point.
- Represents the **saturation pressure vs. temperature** relationship.
- This is the curve used in the **Clausius–Clapeyron Equation**.

**D. Fusion Curve (Solid → Liquid line, also called Melting Curve):**
- From triple point upward (typically).
- **Special case for water:** Fusion curve has a *negative slope* (because ice is less dense than liquid water).

**E. Critical Point (C):**
- Terminal point of the vaporization curve.
- Above this point, no distinction between liquid and vapor.

### 2.2.2 The P-T Diagram as a Map

> [!TIP]
> Think of the P-T diagram as a **map of the universe** for the substance. Every point on this map represents a *unique thermodynamic state*. The three curves (sublimation, vaporization, fusion) are the **borders** of three countries (Solid, Liquid, Vapor). The **triple point** is the only location where the three borders meet.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quality (Dryness Fraction)** | $x = \dfrac{m_v}{m_v + m_l} = \dfrac{m_v}{m_{total}}$ | Fraction of mass in vapor phase |
| 2 | **Specific Volume of Mixture** | $v = v_f + x \, v_{fg}$ | Property of saturated mixture |
| 3 | **Specific Enthalpy of Mixture** | $h = h_f + x \, h_{fg}$ | Energy balance in boilers |
| 4 | **Specific Internal Energy of Mixture** | $u = u_f + x \, u_{fg}$ | First Law analysis in 2-phase systems |
| 5 | **Specific Entropy of Mixture** | $s = s_f + x \, s_{fg}$ | Second Law / turbine analysis |
| 6 | **Clausius–Clapeyron Equation** | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \, v_{fg}}$ | Slope of vaporization curve on P-T diagram |
| 7 | **Clausius–Clapeyron (Integrated)** | $\ln \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$ | Estimating P_sat at different T |
| 8 | **Lever Rule (Volume)** | $\dfrac{m_{vapor}}{m_{liquid}} = \dfrac{v - v_f}{v_g - v}$ | Ratio of phases in 2-phase region |
| 9 | **Lever Rule (Position)** | $x = \dfrac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f}$ | Geometric interpretation on P-V |
| 10 | **Reduced Pressure** | $P_R = P / P_c$ | Principle of Corresponding States |
| 11 | **Reduced Temperature** | $T_R = T / T_c$ | Principle of Corresponding States |
| 12 | **Critical Compressibility** | $Z_c = \dfrac{P_c \, v_c}{R \, T_c}$ | Universal gas constant relation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** When the question says *"a rigid vessel contains a saturated mixture"* — the process is **isochoric (constant specific volume)**, and on the P-V diagram, the state moves **vertically** within the dome. In contrast, a piston-cylinder at constant pressure moves **horizontally** along an isobar.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lever Rule on the P-V Diagram

The **Lever Rule** is a direct geometric consequence of the linear interpolation between saturated liquid and saturated vapor properties.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a saturated mixture of liquid and vapor at pressure **P** and temperature **T** (saturated conditions).
- Total mass: $m = m_l + m_v$
- Total volume: $V = V_l + V_v$

### 3.1.2 Derivation Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Express total volume in terms of specific volumes.**

$$V = m_l \, v_l + m_v \, v_v$$

**Step 2 — Divide by total mass m to get the mixture specific volume.**

$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{m_l \, v_l + m_v \, v_v}{m_l + m_v}$$

**Step 3 — Define quality x.**

By definition, quality is:

$$x = \frac{m_v}{m_l + m_v} = \frac{m_v}{m}$$

Therefore:

$$1 - x = \frac{m_l}{m}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the mass ratios into the equation for v.**

$$v = (1 - x) \, v_f + x \, v_g$$

**Step 5 — Expand and rearrange.**

$$v = v_f - x \, v_f + x \, v_g$$

$$v = v_f + x \, (v_g - v_f)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for quality x.**

$$x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f}$$

This is the **Lever Rule**.

### 3.1.3 Geometric Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> On the P-V diagram, the quality **x** represents the *horizontal distance* from the saturated liquid line to the state point, divided by the *total horizontal width* of the dome at that pressure. This is mathematically identical to a *lever arm ratio* — hence the name.

## 3.2 Derivation: Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

The Clausius–Clapeyron equation gives the **slope of the phase boundary** on the P-T diagram.

### 3.2.1 Starting Point — Clapeyron Equation (General Form)

For any two-phase equilibrium, the **specific Gibbs free energies** of both phases must be equal:

$$g^{\alpha} = g^{\beta}$$

Differentiating with respect to T along the saturation curve:

$$dg^{\alpha} = dg^{\beta}$$

### 3.2.2 Use the Fundamental Property Relation

For a single-component, single-phase system:

$$dg = v \, dP - s \, dT$$

Therefore:

$$v^{\alpha} \, dP - s^{\alpha} \, dT = v^{\beta} \, dP - s^{\beta} \, dT$$

### 3.2.3 Rearrange to Isolate the Slope dP/dT

$$(v^{\beta} - v^{\alpha}) \, dP = (s^{\beta} - s^{\alpha}) \, dT$$

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{s^{\beta} - s^{\alpha}}{v^{\beta} - v^{\alpha}} = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta v}$$

### 3.2.4 Use the Latent Heat Relation

At phase equilibrium, the entropy change is:

$$\Delta s = s^{\beta} - s^{\alpha} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T}$$

(derived from the relation $T \, ds = dh - v \, dP$ at constant P during phase change, and $\Delta h = h_{fg}$).

### 3.2.5 Final Form of Clausius–Clapeyron

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \, v_{fg}}}$$

### 3.2.6 Integration Under Assumptions (Ideal Gas Vapor, Constant h_fg)

If the vapor phase behaves as an **ideal gas** ($v_g \approx RT/P$) and $h_{fg}$ is constant, we can integrate:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation allows prediction of saturation pressure at any temperature when only **two data points** are known. It is used in designing **evaporators, condensers, distillation columns, and refrigeration systems**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Style):** A rigid tank contains **10 kg** of saturated water mixture at **200 kPa**. The quality is **x = 0.6**. Find:
(a) The volume of the tank.
(b) The mass of saturated liquid and saturated vapor.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Look up saturation properties at 200 kPa (from steam tables).**

$$v_f = 0.001061 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$v_g = 0.8857 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the specific volume of the mixture using the lever rule.**

$$v = v_f + x \, (v_g - v_f)$$

$$v = 0.001061 + 0.6 \times (0.8857 - 0.001061)$$

$$v = 0.001061 + 0.6 \times 0.884639$$

$$v = 0.001061 + 0.530783 = 0.531844 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total volume.**

$$V = m \times v = 10 \times 0.531844 = 5.31844 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Calculate mass of each phase.**

$$m_v = x \times m = 0.6 \times 10 = 6 \, \text{kg}$$

$$m_l = (1 - x) \times m = 0.4 \times 10 = 4 \, \text{kg}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{V = 5.318 \, \text{m}^3, \quad m_l = 4 \, \text{kg}, \quad m_v = 6 \, \text{kg}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 P-V Diagram — Mermaid Block Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart represents the **regional architecture** of the P-V diagram, mapping the state regions, the boundary lines, and the critical phenomena as a hierarchical block flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Pure Substance: Water]) --> Region1[COMPRESSED LIQUID REGION]
    Start --> Region2[SATURATED LIQUID LINE - x = 0]
    Start --> Region3[TWO-PHASE MIXTURE DOME]
    Start --> Region4[SATURATED VAPOR LINE - x = 1]
    Start --> Region5[SUPERHEATED VAPOR REGION]
    Start --> Region6[SUPERCRITICAL FLUID REGION]

    Region1 -->|Heating at constant P| Region2
    Region2 -->|Latent Heat addition - P constant| Region3
    Region3 -->|Latent Heat completion| Region4
    Region4 -->|Further heating| Region5
    Region5 -->|T increases above Tc| Region6

    Region3 --> QualityBlock[Quality x ranges from 0 to 1]
    QualityBlock --> LeverRule[Lever Rule: x = v - vf divided by vg - vf]

    Region3 --> IsoThermal[Isothermal Phase Change: P remains constant while V increases]

    Region6 --> CritPoint[CRITICAL POINT C - Pc 22.064 MPa, Tc 647.1 K]
    Region5 --> CritPoint
    Region4 --> CritPoint

    CritPoint --> SuperCritical[NO DISTINCTION between liquid and vapor]

    style Start fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style Region3 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff9900,stroke-width:2px
    style CritPoint fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style QualityBlock fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px
    style SuperCritical fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 P-T Diagram — Mermaid Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Origin([Pure Substance State Space]) --> TriplePt[TRIPLE POINT: T = 273.16 K, P = 611.657 Pa]

    TriplePt --> SubCurve[SUBLIMATION CURVE: Solid to Vapor]
    TriplePt --> VapCurve[VAPORIZATION CURVE: Liquid to Vapor]
    TriplePt --> FusCurve[FUSION CURVE: Solid to Liquid]

    SubCurve --> SolidReg[SOLID REGION - Low T, Variable P]
    VapCurve --> LiquidReg[LIQUID REGION - Between fusion and vaporization curves]
    FusCurve --> SolidReg

    VapCurve --> CritPt[CRITICAL POINT: 647.1 K, 22.064 MPa]
    LiquidReg --> CritPt
    CritPt --> SuperReg[SUPERCRITICAL REGION - Above Tc and Pc]

    FusCurve -->|For water: NEGATIVE slope| IceAnomaly[Special Case: Ice floats on water]

    VapCurve --> ClausiusClapeyron[Slope given by Clausius-Clapeyron Equation]
    ClausiusClapeyron --> SteamTbl[Used to construct Steam Tables]

    style TriplePt fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style CritPt fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style SolidReg fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003399,stroke-width:2px
    style LiquidReg fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px
    style SuperReg fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px
    style IceAnomaly fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px
    style ClausiusClapeyron fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff9900,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Cross-Mapping: P-V ↔ P-T Correspondence

| Feature | P-V Diagram | P-T Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| **Axes** | Pressure (y) vs. Specific Volume (x) | Pressure (y) vs. Temperature (x) |
| **Saturated Mixture Region** | Area *inside* the dome (2D region) | **Line** (vaporization curve, 1D) |
| **Critical Point** | Topmost point of dome | Terminal point of vaporization curve |
| **Triple Point** | **Not shown** (requires 3D) | Single point where 3 curves meet |
| **Quality x** | Horizontal lever arm ratio | **Not directly representable** |
| **Process Visualization** | Constant T (isotherm) is flat inside dome | Constant P is a horizontal line |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Question:** *"Compare the P-V and P-T diagrams. Why is the saturated mixture region a 2D area in the P-V diagram but only a line in the P-T diagram?"* — Answer: In the P-V diagram, pressure is *constant* during phase change but *volume* varies (so a range of volumes exists at the same P). In the P-T diagram, BOTH pressure and temperature are *fixed* during phase change (each saturated state has a unique P and T), so the mixture region collapses to a *line* (the vaporization curve).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: Define a pure substance and explain with the help of a neat P-V diagram the significance of the critical point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A pure substance is one that has a *uniform* and *invariant* chemical composition and exists in one or more phases. Phase change does not alter its chemical identity.

The **critical point** is the highest point on the saturation dome of a P-V diagram, at which the saturated liquid and saturated vapor phases become *indistinguishable*. Beyond the critical temperature T_c, no amount of pressure can liquefy the gas — the substance enters the **supercritical fluid** region. The properties of saturated liquid and vapor (specific volume, density, refractive index) become identical at the critical point. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Differentiate between the saturated liquid line and the saturated vapor line on a P-V diagram. What is meant by quality?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Saturated Liquid Line:** The left vertical branch of the saturation dome; represents **100% liquid** with quality x = 0. All points on this line have T = T_sat at the given pressure.
- **Saturated Vapor Line:** The right vertical branch of the saturation dome; represents **100% vapor** with quality x = 1.
- **Quality (x):** Defined as the ratio of mass of vapor to total mass of the mixture:

$$x = \frac{m_{vapor}}{m_{liquid} + m_{vapor}}$$

It ranges from **0 to 1** within the saturation dome. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a) With the help of a neat P-V diagram, explain the different regions and the significance of isotherms for a pure substance. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a saturated water mixture at 250 kPa, the specific volume is 0.4 m³/kg. Determine:
   (i) The quality of the mixture
   (ii) The specific enthalpy
   (iii) The specific entropy
   (iv) The mass fraction of vapor and liquid phases. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) P-V Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

The P-V diagram of a pure substance consists of the following key features:

1. **Saturation Dome** — A bell-shaped curve enclosing the two-phase region.
2. **Critical Point (C)** — The peak of the dome (P_c, v_c). Above T_c, isotherms show no phase change.
3. **Saturated Liquid Line (Left branch)** — Quality x = 0.
4. **Saturated Vapor Line (Right branch)** — Quality x = 1.
5. **Compressed Liquid Region** — Left of saturated liquid line; T < T_sat.
6. **Superheated Vapor Region** — Right of saturated vapor line; T > T_sat.
7. **Supercritical Region** — Above critical point; liquid and vapor are indistinguishable.

**Isotherm Behavior:**
- **T < T_c:** The isotherm enters the dome horizontally (isothermal–isobaric phase change at P_sat).
- **T = T_c:** The isotherm has a horizontal tangent at the critical point.
- **T > T_c:** Smooth, monotonically decreasing isotherm — no phase change. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Saturation properties at P = 250 kPa (from steam tables):**

$$v_f = 0.001067 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad v_g = 0.71873 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$h_f = 535.35 \, \text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2181.5 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$s_f = 1.6072 \, \text{kJ/kg.K}, \quad s_{fg} = 6.1257 \, \text{kJ/kg.K}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Quality:**

$$x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f} = \frac{0.4 - 0.001067}{0.71873 - 0.001067} = \frac{0.398933}{0.717663} = 0.5559$$

$$\boxed{x \approx 0.556}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Specific Enthalpy:**

$$h = h_f + x \, h_{fg} = 535.35 + 0.5559 \times 2181.5$$

$$h = 535.35 + 1212.74 = 1748.09 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 1748.1 \, \text{kJ/kg}}$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Specific Entropy:**

$$s = s_f + x \, s_{fg} = 1.6072 + 0.5559 \times 6.1257$$

$$s = 1.6072 + 3.4050 = 5.0122 \, \text{kJ/kg.K}$$

$$\boxed{s \approx 5.012 \, \text{kJ/kg.K}}$$

**Step 5 — (iv) Mass Fractions:**

$$m_v / m_{total} = x = 0.556 \quad (55.6\%)$$

$$m_l / m_{total} = 1 - x = 0.444 \quad (44.4\%)$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative]

**Q: (a) Draw and explain the P-T diagram for a pure substance. Label all regions, curves, and the triple point. (7 marks)**

**(b) The saturation pressure of water at 100 °C is 101.325 kPa. Using the Clausius–Clapeyron equation, estimate the saturation pressure at 110 °C. Given: h_fg at 100 °C ≈ 2257 kJ/kg, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, M_water = 18 g/mol. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) P-T Diagram (7 marks):**

The P-T (Pressure–Temperature) diagram shows three distinct regions — **Solid, Liquid, and Vapor** — separated by three phase-boundary curves:

1. **Sublimation Curve** (Solid ↔ Vapor): Below the triple point pressure. Solid sublimates directly to vapor.
2. **Fusion Curve** (Solid ↔ Liquid): Represents melting. For *most* substances, it has a *positive slope*; **for water, it has a *negative slope*** (anomalous behavior — ice is less dense than liquid water).
3. **Vaporization Curve** (Liquid ↔ Vapor): From the triple point to the critical point. Represents the saturation pressure–temperature relationship.

**Triple Point:** The unique point where solid, liquid, and vapor coexist in equilibrium (for water: 273.16 K, 611.657 Pa).

**Critical Point:** The terminal point of the vaporization curve. Above it, no phase distinction exists. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Clausius–Clapeyron Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert given data to SI units.**

$$T_1 = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15 \, \text{K}$$

$$T_2 = 110 + 273.15 = 383.15 \, \text{K}$$

$$P_1 = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 101325 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$h_{fg} = 2257 \, \text{kJ/kg} = 2257 \times 10^3 \, \text{J/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the universal gas constant per kg of water.**

$$R_{specific} = \frac{R_{universal}}{M} = \frac{8.314}{0.018} = 461.89 \, \text{J/kg.K}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation.**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{h_{fg}}{R_{specific}} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{101325}\right) = \frac{2257 \times 10^3}{461.89} \times \left( \frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{383.15} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the right-hand side.**

$$\frac{2257 \times 10^3}{461.89} = 4887.05$$

$$\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{383.15} = \frac{383.15 - 373.15}{373.15 \times 383.15} = \frac{10}{142961.4225} = 6.9948 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\text{RHS} = 4887.05 \times 6.9948 \times 10^{-5} = 0.3419$$

**Step 6 — Solve for P_2.**

$$\frac{P_2}{101325} = e^{0.3419} = 1.4077$$

$$P_2 = 101325 \times 1.4077 = 142,640 \, \text{Pa} \approx 142.64 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 \approx 142.64 \, \text{kPa}}$$

The actual steam-table value at 110 °C is **143.38 kPa** — our estimate is within 0.5% error, validating the Clausius–Clapeyron approximation. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up P-V and P-T diagrams** — In P-V, the 2-phase region is a 2D area; in P-T, it is a 1D line. Examiners *love* to test this conceptual distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — h_fg is often given in **kJ/kg**, but the universal gas constant R is in **J/mol·K**. Forgetting to convert kJ → J or dividing by molar mass → specific R is a guaranteed **2-mark penalty**.
>
> 3. **Using 273 instead of 273.15 K** — Modern KTU questions and steam tables use **273.15 K** as the conversion offset. Using 273 introduces a 0.05% error which can cross significant figures.
>
> 4. **Skipping the steam-table lookup** — You must show the saturation property values used in calculations. Examiners **will not award full marks** without the property values cited.
>
> 5. **Confusing "compressed liquid" with "saturated liquid"** — They are *different* states. A compressed liquid has **T < T_sat** at the given P; a saturated liquid is *on* the saturation curve.
>
> 6. **Wrong formula for x** — Always use the **lever rule**: $x = (v - v_f) / (v_g - v_f)$. Do not invert it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — P-V and P-T Diagrams**
>
> ✅ **Pure Substance:** Uniform chemical composition; can exist in multiple phases.
>
> ✅ **P-V Diagram Key Points:**
> - Dome-shaped saturation curve
> - Critical point = peak of dome
> - Inside dome = 2-phase mixture
> - Left of dome = compressed (subcooled) liquid
> - Right of dome = superheated vapor
> - Above T_c = supercritical fluid
>
> ✅ **P-T Diagram Key Points:**
> - Three curves: sublimation, fusion, vaporization
> - Triple point = intersection of all three curves
> - Critical point = end of vaporization curve
> - Water has anomalous negative-slope fusion curve
>
> ✅ **Quality Equation:** $x = m_v / m_{total}$ (range 0 to 1)
>
> ✅ **Lever Rule:** $x = (v - v_f) / (v_g - v_f)$
>
> ✅ **Mixture Properties:** $h = h_f + x \, h_{fg}$, $u = u_f + x \, u_{fg}$, $s = s_f + x \, s_{fg}$, $v = v_f + x \, v_{fg}$
>
> ✅ **Clausius–Clapeyron:** $dP/dT = h_{fg} / (T \, v_{fg})$
>
> ✅ **Integrated C-C:** $\ln(P_2/P_1) = (h_{fg}/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ (for ideal gas, constant h_fg)
>
> ✅ **Water Critical Point (memorize):** T_c = 647.1 K, P_c = 22.064 MPa
>
> ✅ **Water Triple Point (memorize):** T_tp = 273.16 K, P_tp = 611.657 Pa
>
> ✅ **Process Visualization:**
> - Constant V (isochoric) → vertical line on P-V
> - Constant P (isobaric) → horizontal line on P-V
> - Constant T (isothermal) → flat segment inside dome during phase change
>
> ✅ **Units Traps:** kJ/kg ↔ J/kg, °C ↔ K (add 273.15), Pa ↔ kPa ↔ MPa
>
> ✅ **Key Distinction:** P-V shows phase regions as areas; P-T shows them as curves.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cubic Equations of State for Real Gases (Concepts of Redlich Kwong, Peng Robinson, and Virial Equations), Principle of Corresponding States and Generalized Compressibility Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cubic Equations of State for Real Gases (Concepts of Redlich Kwong, Peng Robinson, and Virial Equations), Principle of Corresponding States and Generalized Compressibility Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Equation of State (EOS)?

An **Equation of State (EOS)** is a thermodynamic state relation that mathematically correlates the three intensive properties of a pure substance — **Pressure (P)**, **Temperature (T)**, and **Molar Volume (V)** — along with composition. The most familiar and historically first EOS is the **Ideal Gas Law**:

$$P V = R T$$

where **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** is the universal gas constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The ideal gas law *fails* near the saturation region, in the critical region, and at high pressures. Real gases exhibit deviations quantified by the **Compressibility Factor**, $Z = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$, which equals **1** for an ideal gas and deviates from unity for real gases.

## 1.2 The Need for Real-Gas Equations of State

A real gas experiences:
- **Intermolecular repulsive forces** (dominant at high density / small molar volume) — modeled by an excluded volume term `b`.
- **Intermolecular attractive forces** (dominant at moderate density) — modeled by a cohesion parameter `a`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnetized Billiard Balls":**
> Imagine billiard balls (gas molecules) bouncing inside a closed box. The ideal gas assumes they are infinitely small, non-interacting points. But real molecules:
> (a) Have a finite size — so they cannot overlap, occupying a real **excluded volume (b)**.
> (b) Carry weak magnetic dipoles (van der Waals forces) — so they weakly pull each other together, reducing the pressure they exert on the walls.
> Every cubic EOS is essentially a different mathematical approximation of this "size + attraction" model.

## 1.3 Definition of Cubic Equations of State

**Cubic Equations of State (CEOS)** are analytical pressure-explicit equations of the form $P = f(T, V)$ that, when expanded, yield a **cubic polynomial in molar volume V** for a given T and P. This cubic nature gives them the unique ability to predict:

- **Three real roots** in the two-phase region (vapor, meta-stable, and liquid-like root).
- **One real root** in the supercritical / single-phase region.

The general cubic structure is:

$$P = \dfrac{R T}{V - b} - \dfrac{a(T)}{f(V)}$$

where $f(V)$ is a quadratic or linear function in V that differentiates one cubic EOS from another.

## 1.4 Definition of the Virial Equation of State

The **Virial Equation of State** is an infinite-series expansion of the compressibility factor Z in powers of either inverse molar volume (density form) or pressure:

$$Z = 1 + \dfrac{B(T)}{V} + \dfrac{C(T)}{V^{2}} + \dfrac{D(T)}{V^{3}} + \dots$$

The coefficients B, C, D … are called the **second, third, fourth virial coefficients**, respectively, and they are functions of temperature only (for a pure substance). They have rigorous statistical-mechanical interpretations in terms of intermolecular potentials (e.g., 2-body, 3-body, 4-body interactions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The virial equation is often truncated after the second coefficient for engineering use:
> $$Z \approx 1 + \dfrac{B(T)}{V}$$
> This is a *non-cubic* EOS and is mostly used for **vapors at low-to-moderate pressures**.

## 1.5 Principle of Corresponding States (PCS) & Generalized Compressibility Chart

The **Principle of Corresponding States** states that *all substances, when expressed in terms of their reduced (dimensionless) properties — $P_r = P/P_c$, $T_r = T/T_c$, $V_r = V/V_c$ — behave identically at the same reduced state*.

$$Z = Z(T_r, P_r)$$

This is the foundation of the **Generalized Compressibility Chart** (Nelson-Obert, Lee-Kesler), which provides a *universal* Z-chart applicable to *all* gases (with accuracy refined by introducing the **acentric factor ω** as a third parameter).

## 1.6 The Acentric Factor (ω)

The **acentric factor** is the third corresponding-states parameter introduced by Pitzer in 1955 to distinguish molecules of different shape and polarity. It is defined as:

$$\omega \equiv - \log_{10} \left( P_r^{\text{sat}} \right)_{T_r = 0.7} - 1.000$$

where $P_r^{\text{sat}}$ is the reduced vapor pressure evaluated at $T_r = 0.7$. By definition, $\omega = 0$ for Argon, Krypton, and Xenon (monoatomic "simple" fluids).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Meaning of ω:** Acentric factor measures the *acentricity* (non-sphericity) of a molecule. Larger $\omega$ → more complex molecular shape, stronger vapor-pressure deviation from the simple-fluid reference curve. **Methane ≈ 0.008**, **n-pentane ≈ 0.251**, **water ≈ 0.344**, **n-decane ≈ 0.490**.
