# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Evolution of Cubic Equations of State

The progression of cubic EOS is best understood as successive refinements of the "size + attraction" model:

### 2.1.1 Van der Waals (vdW) Equation — The First Cubic EOS

$$P = \dfrac{R T}{V - b} - \dfrac{a}{V^{2}}$$

with constants derived from the **critical-point conditions**:

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_{T_c} = 0, \quad \left( \dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial V^{2}} \right)_{T_c} = 0$$

This gives:

$$a = \dfrac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c}, \quad b = \dfrac{R T_c}{8 P_c}, \quad Z_c = 0.375$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The vdW equation predicts a *universal* $Z_c = 0.375$, but real substances exhibit $Z_c$ ranging from 0.23 (acetic acid) to 0.31 (helium, hydrogen). Hence, vdW is qualitatively correct but quantitatively poor.

### 2.1.2 Redlich-Kwong (RK) Equation — 1949

$$P = \dfrac{R T}{V - b} - \dfrac{a}{T^{0.5} V (V + b)}$$

with:

$$a = \dfrac{0.42748 \, R^{2} T_c^{2.5}}{P_c}, \quad b = \dfrac{0.08664 \, R T_c}{P_c}, \quad Z_c = 0.333$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RK improved vdW:**
> 1. The attractive term denominator $V(V+b)$ replaces $V^2$, giving better liquid-volume predictions.
> 2. The temperature-dependence $T^{-0.5}$ in the `a` term captures the observation that cohesive forces weaken with temperature.

**Advantages:** Excellent for vapor-phase calculations up to ~10 atm. **Disadvantages:** Predicts only fair liquid densities, fails in the critical region.

### 2.1.3 Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK) Equation — 1972

$$P = \dfrac{R T}{V - b} - \dfrac{a(T)}{V (V + b)}$$

with temperature-dependent `a`:

$$a(T) = a_c \cdot \alpha(T_r, \omega) = 0.42748 \, \dfrac{R^{2} T_c^{2}}{P_c} \cdot \alpha(T_r, \omega)$$

$$b = 0.08664 \, \dfrac{R T_c}{P_c}$$

$$\alpha^{0.5} = 1 + m \left( 1 - T_r^{0.5} \right), \quad m = 0.480 + 1.574 \omega - 0.176 \omega^{2}$$

SRK matches the experimental vapor-pressure curve through the `α` function and Pitzer's acentric factor. It is widely used in **petroleum-refinery and natural-gas VLE calculations**.

### 2.1.4 Peng-Robinson (PR) Equation — 1976

$$P = \dfrac{R T}{V - b} - \dfrac{a(T)}{V (V + b) + b (V - b)}$$

with:

$$a(T) = 0.45724 \, \dfrac{R^{2} T_c^{2}}{P_c} \cdot \alpha(T_r, \omega)$$

$$b = 0.07780 \, \dfrac{R T_c}{P_c}$$

$$\alpha^{0.5} = 1 + \kappa \left( 1 - T_r^{0.5} \right), \quad \kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226 \omega - 0.26992 \omega^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison:**
> - PR's denominator $V(V+b) + b(V-b) = V^2 + 2bV - b^2$ produces a slightly stronger repulsion than RK/SRK.
> - PR predicts $Z_c = 0.307$ (much closer to real values 0.27–0.31).
> - PR gives **better liquid-density predictions** than SRK, hence its dominance in modern process simulators (Aspen, HYSYS, ChemCAD).

## 2.2 Virial Equation — Detailed Form

The virial equation exists in three equivalent forms:

### 2.2.1 Density (Berlin) Form

$$Z = 1 + \dfrac{B}{V} + \dfrac{C}{V^{2}} + \dfrac{D}{V^{3}} + \dots$$

### 2.2.2 Pressure (Leiden) Form

$$Z = 1 + B^{\prime} P + C^{\prime} P^{2} + D^{\prime} P^{3} + \dots$$

### 2.2.3 Truncated Two-Parameter Forms

$$Z = 1 + \dfrac{B P}{R T} \quad \text{(pressure form, low-P)}$$

$$Z = 1 + \dfrac{B}{V} \quad \text{(density form)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Statistical-Mechanical Meaning:**
> - **B** = 2nd virial coefficient → represents **pairwise (2-body) intermolecular interactions**.
> - **C** = 3rd virial coefficient → represents **three-body interactions**.
> - **D** = 4th virial coefficient → represents **four-body interactions**, etc.

The Pitzer correlations for the second virial coefficient (KTU-favorite):

$$\dfrac{B P_c}{R T_c} = B^{(0)} + \omega B^{(1)}$$

$$B^{(0)} = 0.083 - \dfrac{0.422}{T_r^{1.6}}, \quad B^{(1)} = 0.139 - \dfrac{0.172}{T_r^{4.2}}$$

These are valid for $0.3 \le T_r \le 5.0$.

## 2.3 Principle of Corresponding States — Two & Three Parameter

### 2.3.1 Two-Parameter PCS (van der Waals, 1873)

$$Z = Z(T_r, P_r)$$

Assumes substances of *similar size, shape, and polarity* behave identically. Accurate for noble gases, N₂, O₂, CO, CH₄. **Fails** for polar (water, ammonia) and large-chain (long hydrocarbons) molecules.

### 2.3.2 Three-Parameter PCS (Pitzer, 1955)

$$Z = Z(T_r, P_r, \omega)$$

The acentric factor `ω` captures the missing shape/polarity information. Used in:
- **Generalized Compressibility Charts** (Nelson-Obert with ω-correction).
- **Lee-Kesler corresponding-states tables** (most accurate; covers liquids and vapors).
- **Pitzer acentric-factor correlations** (gives $B, C, V, H^{R}, S^{R}$ directly from `ω`).

## 2.4 Generalized Compressibility Chart — Lee-Kesler Approach

The Lee-Kesler tables provide $Z^{(0)}$ (simple fluid, ω = 0) and $Z^{(1)}$ (acentric reference, ω ≈ 0.398 for n-octane) such that:

$$Z = Z^{(0)} + \dfrac{\omega}{\omega^{(1)}} \left( Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)} \right) = Z^{(0)} + \omega \dfrac{Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)}}{0.3978}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Newton's Correction for Pressures Above 1 atm:** Because the charts plot Z vs $P_r$ for discrete $T_r$ values, we use Newton's linear interpolation:
> $$Z(T_r, P_r) = Z^{(0)} + \omega \left( Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)} \right)$$
> Computed at the *target* $T_r$ and $P_r$ directly (no interpolation between isotherms needed because both $Z^{(0)}$ and $Z^{(1)}$ are themselves tabulated as functions of $T_r$ and $P_r$).

## 2.5 Fugacity from a Generalized Chart

For real gases:

$$\ln \dfrac{f}{P} = \int_{0}^{P} \dfrac{Z - 1}{P} \, dP \quad \text{at constant } T$$

Substituting $Z = Z(T_r, P_r, \omega)$ and changing variables:

$$\ln \phi = \ln \dfrac{f}{P} = \int_{0}^{P_r} (Z - 1) \, \dfrac{dP_r}{P_r} \quad \text{at constant } T_r$$

From Lee-Kesler, two helper fugacity-coefficient correlations are tabulated: $\phi^{(0)}$ and $\phi^{(1)}$:

$$\ln \phi = \ln \phi^{(0)} + \omega \ln \phi^{(1)} \quad \text{(sometimes written as } \ln \phi^{(1)} - \ln \phi^{(0)} \text{ difference)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always use *in* of $\phi$ (or $\phi^{(0)}, \phi^{(1)}$) when applying Lee-Kesler — they are tabulated as natural logarithms, not $\log_{10}$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Constants / Limits | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **vdW EOS** | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V - b} - \dfrac{a}{V^{2}}$ | $a = 27 R^{2} T_c^{2} / 64 P_c$, $b = R T_c / 8 P_c$ | Qualitative, vapor-phase |
| 2 | **Redlich-Kwong (RK)** | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V - b} - \dfrac{a}{T^{0.5} V (V + b)}$ | $a = 0.42748 R^{2} T_c^{2.5} / P_c$, $b = 0.08664 R T_c / P_c$ | Vapors, $P < 10$ atm |
| 3 | **SRK** | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V - b} - \dfrac{a(T)}{V (V + b)}$ | $\alpha^{0.5} = 1 + m(1 - T_r^{0.5})$, $m = 0.480 + 1.574\omega - 0.176\omega^{2}$ | Hydrocarbon VLE |
| 4 | **Peng-Robinson (PR)** | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V - b} - \dfrac{a(T)}{V(V + b) + b(V - b)}$ | $\kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^{2}$ | Process simulation, both phases |
| 5 | **Virial (Density form)** | $Z = 1 + B/V + C/V^{2} + \dots$ | B = f(T), C = f(T) | Vapors at low–moderate P |
| 6 | **Virial (Pressure form)** | $Z = 1 + B'P + C'P^{2} + \dots$ | B' = f(T), C' = f(T) | Low-pressure gases |
| 7 | **Pitzer B correlation** | $B P_c / R T_c = B^{(0)} + \omega B^{(1)}$ | $B^{(0)} = 0.083 - 0.422/T_r^{1.6}$, $B^{(1)} = 0.139 - 0.172/T_r^{4.2}$ | Valid $0.3 \le T_r \le 5.0$ |
| 8 | **Two-Param PCS** | $Z = Z(T_r, P_r)$ | $T_r = T/T_c$, $P_r = P/P_c$ | Noble gases, N₂, O₂ |
| 9 | **Three-Param PCS** | $Z = Z(T_r, P_r, \omega)$ | $\omega = -\log_{10} P_r^{\text{sat}}\vert_{T_r = 0.7} - 1$ | Polar, large, asymmetric mols |
| 10 | **Lee-Kesler Z** | $Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega \dfrac{Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)}}{0.3978}$ | $Z^{(0)} = $ simple fluid, $Z^{(1)} = $ n-octane | All regions |
| 11 | **Fugacity Coefficient** | $\ln \phi = \ln \phi^{(0)} + \omega \ln \phi^{(1)}$ | tabulated in Smith-Van Ness, Kyle | VLE & K-value calcs |
| 12 | **Acentric factor** | $\omega = -\log_{10} P_r^{\text{sat}}\vert_{T_r = 0.7} - 1$ | Reference fluid: Ar, Kr, Xe ($\omega = 0$) | Third PCS parameter |
| 13 | **Compressibility factor** | $Z = PV/RT$ | $Z = 1$ for ideal gas | Deviation from ideality |
| 14 | **Reduced volume** | $V_r = V / V_c$ | vdW: $V_c = 3b$ | PCS formulation |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Simulators (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, ProMax, OLI):** PR and SRK are the *workhorse* EOS for hydrocarbon, natural gas, refinery, and CO₂-capture processes.
- **Compressor / Pump Design:** $Z$ from a generalized chart is used to size compressors handling real gases (e.g., natural gas pipelines).
- **LNG / LPG Storage & Transport:** Accurate PVT data via cubic EOS determine tank pressures, liquefaction loads, and boil-off rates.
- **Refrigeration (NH₃, CO₂, HFC cycles):** PR EOS gives accurate vapor-pressure and liquid-density data needed for cycle optimization.
- **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE):** PR/SRK model supercritical CO₂ behavior used in decaffeination of coffee and essential-oil extraction.
- **Geothermal & Reservoir Engineering:** Cubic EOS model CH₄, CO₂, and H₂O mixtures in deep-earth reservoirs.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of vdW Constants from Critical-Point Conditions

**Starting Equation:**

$$P = \dfrac{R T}{V - b} - \dfrac{a}{V^{2}}$$

**Step 1: First partial derivative of P with respect to V at constant T.**

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_{T} = - \dfrac{R T}{(V - b)^{2}} + \dfrac{2 a}{V^{3}}$$

**Step 2: Set equal to zero at the critical point (inflection point).**

$$- \dfrac{R T_c}{(V_c - b)^{2}} + \dfrac{2 a}{V_c^{3}} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{R T_c}{(V_c - b)^{2}} = \dfrac{2 a}{V_c^{3}} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3: Second partial derivative of P with respect to V at constant T.**

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial V^{2}} \right)_{T} = \dfrac{2 R T}{(V - b)^{3}} - \dfrac{6 a}{V^{4}}$$

**Step 4: Set second derivative to zero at the critical point.**

$$\dfrac{2 R T_c}{(V_c - b)^{3}} = \dfrac{6 a}{V_c^{4}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{R T_c}{(V_c - b)^{3}} = \dfrac{3 a}{V_c^{4}} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5: Divide equation (i) by equation (ii) to eliminate $a$ and $T_c$.**

$$\dfrac{R T_c / (V_c - b)^{2}}{R T_c / (V_c - b)^{3}} = \dfrac{2 a / V_c^{3}}{3 a / V_c^{4}}$$

$$(V_c - b) = \dfrac{2}{3} V_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 V_c - 3 b = 2 V_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_c = 3 b$$

**Step 6: Substitute $V_c = 3b$ back into equation (i).**

$$\dfrac{R T_c}{(3 b - b)^{2}} = \dfrac{2 a}{(3 b)^{3}} = \dfrac{2 a}{27 b^{3}}$$

$$\dfrac{R T_c}{4 b^{2}} = \dfrac{2 a}{27 b^{3}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = \dfrac{8 a}{27 R T_c} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 7: Equate $b$ from (iii) and $b = V_c / 3$.**

$$\dfrac{V_c}{3} = \dfrac{8 a}{27 R T_c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_c = \dfrac{8 a}{9 R T_c}$$

**Step 8: Substitute $V_c = 3b$ into the original EOS at the critical point.**

$$P_c = \dfrac{R T_c}{3 b - b} - \dfrac{a}{(3 b)^{2}} = \dfrac{R T_c}{2 b} - \dfrac{a}{9 b^{2}}$$

**Step 9: Substitute $b = R T_c / 8 P_c$ (obtained by re-arranging) into the above.**

$$P_c = \dfrac{R T_c}{2 \cdot R T_c / 8 P_c} - \dfrac{a}{9 \cdot (R T_c / 8 P_c)^{2}}$$

$$P_c = 4 P_c - \dfrac{64 a P_c^{2}}{9 R^{2} T_c^{2}}$$

**Step 10: Solve for $a$.**

$$3 P_c = \dfrac{64 a P_c^{2}}{9 R^{2} T_c^{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \dfrac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\, a = \dfrac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c}, \quad b = \dfrac{R T_c}{8 P_c} \,}$$

**Critical compressibility factor:**

$$Z_c = \dfrac{P_c V_c}{R T_c} = \dfrac{P_c \cdot 3 b}{R T_c} = \dfrac{P_c \cdot 3 \cdot R T_c / 8 P_c}{R T_c} = \dfrac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Redlich-Kwong Constants

**Starting Equation:**

$$P = \dfrac{R T}{V - b} - \dfrac{a}{T^{0.5} V (V + b)}$$

**Step 1: First partial derivative w.r.t. V at constant T.**

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_{T} = - \dfrac{R T}{(V - b)^{2}} + \dfrac{a \cdot (2 V + b)}{T^{0.5} V^{2} (V + b)^{2}}$$

**Step 2: Set to zero at $T_c, P_c, V_c$.**

$$\dfrac{R T_c}{(V_c - b)^{2}} = \dfrac{a (2 V_c + b)}{T_c^{0.5} V_c^{2} (V_c + b)^{2}} \quad \text{...(I)}$$

**Step 3: Second partial derivative w.r.t. V at constant T.**

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial V^{2}} \right)_{T} = \dfrac{2 R T}{(V - b)^{3}} - \dfrac{2 a}{T^{0.5} V^{3}} + \dfrac{2 a}{T^{0.5} V^{2} (V + b)} - \dfrac{2 a (2 V + b)}{T^{0.5} V^{3} (V + b)} + \dfrac{2 a (2 V + b)^{2}}{T^{0.5} V^{4} (V + b)^{2}}$$

The algebraic simplification (using a symbolic engine) yields:

$$0 = \dfrac{2 R T_c}{(V_c - b)^{3}} - \dfrac{2 a (2 V_c^{2} + 2 b V_c - b^{2})}{T_c^{0.5} V_c^{3} (V_c + b)^{3}} \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 4: Combine (I) and (II) by dividing (II) by (I).**

$$2 (V_c + b) = \dfrac{2 V_c^{2} + 2 b V_c - b^{2}}{V_c (2 V_c + b)} \cdot (V_c - b)$$

After algebraic reduction:

$$2 V_c^{3} - 3 b V_c^{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_c (2 V_c - 3 b) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_c = \dfrac{3 b}{2}$$

**Step 5: Substitute $V_c = 3b/2$ into original RK EOS at critical point.**

$$P_c = \dfrac{R T_c}{3 b/2 - b} - \dfrac{a}{T_c^{0.5} \cdot (3 b/2) \cdot (3 b/2 + b)}$$

$$P_c = \dfrac{2 R T_c}{b} - \dfrac{4 a}{9 b^{2} T_c^{0.5}}$$

**Step 6: Substitute $P_c$ back into (I) and solve for $a$.**

After algebra:

$$\boxed{\, a = \dfrac{0.42748 \, R^{2} T_c^{2.5}}{P_c}, \quad b = \dfrac{0.08664 \, R T_c}{P_c} \,}$$

**Step 7: Critical compressibility factor.**

$$Z_c = \dfrac{P_c V_c}{R T_c} = \dfrac{P_c \cdot 3b/2}{R T_c} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot 0.08664 = 0.333$$

> [!NOTE]
> RK improves on vdW by reducing $Z_c$ from 0.375 → 0.333, closer to real values (0.27–0.31).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Truncated Virial EOS and Its Pressure-Form Equivalent

**Step 1: Start with density-form virial equation truncated at the second coefficient.**

$$Z = 1 + \dfrac{B}{V}$$

**Step 2: Solve for V in terms of P using the definition of Z.**

$$Z = \dfrac{P V}{R T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \dfrac{Z R T}{P}$$

**Step 3: Substitute V into the virial equation.**

$$Z = 1 + \dfrac{B \cdot P}{Z R T}$$

**Step 4: Multiply both sides by $Z R T$.**

$$Z^{2} R T = Z R T + B P \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z^{2} R T - Z R T = B P$$

**Step 5: Factor out $Z R T$.**

$$Z R T (Z - 1) = B P$$

**Step 6: Substitute $Z = PV/RT$ and solve for Z.**

$$\dfrac{P V}{R T} \cdot R T \cdot \left( \dfrac{P V}{R T} - 1 \right) = B P$$

$$P V \left( \dfrac{P V}{R T} - 1 \right) = B P \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{P^{2} V^{2}}{R T} - P V = B P$$

**Step 7: Divide by P.**

$$\dfrac{P V^{2}}{R T} - V = B$$

This implicit relation is rarely used; instead, we recognize that for the pressure form:

$$Z = 1 + B' P, \quad \text{where} \quad B' = \dfrac{B}{R T}$$

**Step 8: Verify the relationship between the two virial forms.**

Setting $Z = 1 + B/V = 1 + BP/(ZRT)$:

$$Z = 1 + \dfrac{B P}{Z R T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z^{2} = Z + \dfrac{B P}{R T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 4 B P / R T}}{2}$$

The series expansion of this expression gives the *pressure form*:

$$Z = 1 + \dfrac{B P}{R T} + \left( \dfrac{B P}{R T} \right)^{2} + \dots$$

Thus, $B' = B / R T$ (to first order), and $C' = (B/RT)^{2} - C/(RT)^{2}$ (matching higher-order virial coefficients between the two forms).

## 3.4 Derivation of the Acentric Factor

**Step 1: Start with the Clausius-Clapeyron equation at low pressures (where vapor behaves as an ideal gas).**

$$\ln P^{\text{sat}} = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$$

**Step 2: Introduce the reduced form $P_r^{\text{sat}} = P^{\text{sat}} / P_c$.**

$$\ln P_r^{\text{sat}} = \ln P^{\text{sat}} - \ln P_c = A - \dfrac{B}{T} - \ln P_c$$

**Step 3: Apply the two-parameter corresponding states principle.** For a *simple fluid* (argon, krypton, xenon), the reduced vapor pressure is a *universal* function of $T_r$ only:

$$\ln P_r^{\text{sat, (0)}} = f^{(0)}(T_r)$$

**Step 4: Pitzer's empirical observation** showed that $\log_{10} P_r^{\text{sat}}$ is *almost linear* in $T_r^{-1}$ for simple fluids, and the deviation from linearity correlates with the acentric factor. Define:

$$\log_{10} P_r^{\text{sat, (0)}} = \alpha_{0} - \beta_{0} / T_r$$

**Step 5: Evaluate at $T_r = 0.7$ for a simple fluid (where $P_r^{\text{sat}} = 0.1$ for Ar, Kr, Xe experimentally).**

For Ar at $T_r = 0.7$: $\log_{10}(0.1) = -1$. For real fluids, $P_r^{\text{sat}} \ne 0.1$ at $T_r = 0.7$. Define:

$$\omega = \log_{10} P_r^{\text{sat, (0)}}\big\vert_{T_r = 0.7} - \log_{10} P_r^{\text{sat}}\big\vert_{T_r = 0.7}$$

Since $\log_{10} P_r^{\text{sat, (0)}} \big\vert_{T_r = 0.7} = -1.000$ for simple fluids:

$$\boxed{\, \omega = -1.000 - \log_{10} P_r^{\text{sat}}\big\vert_{T_r = 0.7} = - \log_{10} P_r^{\text{sat}}\big\vert_{T_r = 0.7} - 1.000 \,}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of Lee-Kesler Z Interpolation

**Step 1: Pitzer's three-parameter corresponding states postulate.**

$$Z(T_r, P_r, \omega) = Z^{(0)}(T_r, P_r) + \omega \, Z^{(1)}(T_r, P_r)$$

where $Z^{(0)}$ is for a *simple fluid* (ω = 0, e.g., Ar, Kr, Xe averaged) and $Z^{(1)}$ is for a *reference acentric fluid* (n-octane, with $\omega^{(R)} = 0.3978$).

**Step 2: Lee-Kesler (1975) refinement.** Instead of using the difference $Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)}$ directly, they tabulated both $Z^{(0)}$ and $Z^{(1)}$ as functions of $T_r$ and $P_r$ and introduced the normalized interpolation:

$$Z = Z^{(0)} + \dfrac{\omega}{\omega^{(R)}} \left( Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)} \right)$$

**Step 3: Substituting $\omega^{(R)} = 0.3978$ for n-octane:**

$$Z = Z^{(0)} + \dfrac{\omega}{0.3978} \left( Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)} \right) = Z^{(0)} + \omega \cdot \dfrac{Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)}}{0.3978}$$

This is the form most commonly used in KTU textbooks (e.g., Smith, Van Ness, Abbott).

## 3.6 Fugacity from a Generalized Chart — Derivation

**Step 1: Definition of the fugacity coefficient for a pure gas.**

$$\ln \phi = \ln \dfrac{f}{P} = \int_{0}^{P} (Z - 1) \dfrac{dP}{P} \quad \text{at constant } T$$

**Step 2: Change variables to reduced form, $P = P_r P_c$, $T = T_r T_c$.**

$$\ln \phi = \int_{0}^{P_r} (Z - 1) \dfrac{dP_r}{P_r} \quad \text{at constant } T_r$$

**Step 3: Substitute $Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega (Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)}) / 0.3978$.**

$$\ln \phi = \int_{0}^{P_r} (Z^{(0)} - 1) \dfrac{dP_r}{P_r} + \dfrac{\omega}{0.3978} \int_{0}^{P_r} (Z^{(1)} - 1) \dfrac{dP_r}{P_r}$$

**Step 4: Define the tabulated functions.**

$$\ln \phi^{(0)} \equiv \int_{0}^{P_r} (Z^{(0)} - 1) \dfrac{dP_r}{P_r}, \quad \ln \phi^{(1)} \equiv \int_{0}^{P_r} (Z^{(1)} - 1) \dfrac{dP_r}{P_r}$$

**Step 5: Final interpolation formula.**

$$\boxed{\, \ln \phi = \ln \phi^{(0)} + \dfrac{\omega}{0.3978} \left( \ln \phi^{(1)} - \ln \phi^{(0)} \right) \,}$$

In some textbooks (e.g., Smith-Van Ness, Appendix B), $\ln \phi^{(1)}$ is tabulated directly, simplifying the formula.

## 3.7 Derivation of the Compressibility Factor from a Cubic EOS (Example: PR)

**Step 1: Rewrite PR EOS in terms of Z.**

PR EOS:

$$P = \dfrac{R T}{V - b} - \dfrac{a(T)}{V (V + b) + b (V - b)}$$

Divide both sides by $RT/V$:

$$Z = \dfrac{V}{V - b} - \dfrac{a(T) / (R T)}{V (V + b) + b (V - b)} \cdot \dfrac{V}{R T / P}$$

Let $A = a P / R^{2} T^{2}$ and $B = b P / R T$. Then $a / (R T) = A R T / P$ and $V = Z R T / P$. Substituting:

$$Z = \dfrac{Z}{Z - B} - \dfrac{A}{Z + 2 B - B^{2}/Z} \cdot Z$$

After algebra:

$$Z^{3} + (2 B - 1) Z^{2} + (A - 3 B^{2} - 2 B) Z - (A B - B^{2} - B^{3}) = 0$$

This is the **cubic-in-Z form** of the Peng-Robinson equation. For SRK:

$$Z^{3} + B Z^{2} + (A - 3 B^{2} - 2 B) Z - (A B - B^{2} - B^{3}) = 0$$

For RK, the cubic is:

$$Z^{3} - Z^{2} + (A - B - B^{2}) Z - A B = 0, \quad A = a P / R^{2} T^{2.5}, \quad B = b P / R T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Solved-Problem Strategy:**
> 1. Compute $T_r = T/T_c$, $P_r = P/P_c$.
> 2. Compute $A$ and $B$ using the appropriate (RK, SRK, or PR) formulas.
> 3. Solve the cubic in Z using either the **analytical Cardano's method** or **Newton-Raphson iteration**.
> 4. Choose the root: **largest Z** for vapor, **smallest positive Z** for liquid.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evolution of Cubic Equations of State — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ideal Gas Law PV equals RT] --> B[Van der Waals 1873]
    B --> C[Redlich Kwong 1949]
    C --> D[Soave RK 1972]
    D --> E[Peng Robinson 1976]
    A1[Compressibility Factor Z equals PV over RT] --> B1[vdW Zc equals 0.375]
    C1[RK Zc equals 0.333] --> D1[SRK matches vapor pressure curve]
    E1[PR Zc equals 0.307 accurate liquid density]
    B --> B1
    C --> C1
    D --> D1
    E --> E1
    F[Attractive term a] --> F1[vdW a over V squared]
    F1 --> F2[RK a over T half V V plus b]
    F2 --> F3[SRK aT over V V plus b]
    F3 --> F4[PR aT over V V plus b plus b V minus b]
    F --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4
    G[Repulsive term b] --> G1[Excluded volume constant b]
    G1 --> G2[Temperature dependent in modified forms]
    G --> G1
    G1 --> G2
```

## 4.2 Architecture of the Virial Equation of State

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Virial Equation of State] --> B[Density Form Z equals 1 plus B over V plus C over V squared plus ...]
    A --> C[Pressure Form Z equals 1 plus B prime P plus C prime P squared plus ...]
    B --> D[B Second Virial Coefficient - Two body interactions]
    B --> E[C Third Virial Coefficient - Three body interactions]
    B --> F[D Fourth Virial Coefficient - Four body interactions]
    C --> G[B prime equals B over RT]
    C --> H[C prime equals C minus B squared over RT squared]
    D --> I[Pitzer Correlation B Pc over RTc equals B0 plus omega B1]
    I --> J[B0 equals 0.083 minus 0.422 over Tr to the 1.6]
    I --> K[B1 equals 0.139 minus 0.172 over Tr to the 4.2]
    L[Engineering Use] --> M[Low to moderate pressure vapors]
    L --> N[Fugacity coefficient calculation]
    L --> O[Truncate at 2nd coefficient for B level accuracy]
```

## 4.3 Principle of Corresponding States — Conceptual Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corresponding States Principle] --> B[Two Parameter PCS]
    A --> C[Three Parameter PCS]
    B --> B1[Reference Properties Tc and Pc only]
    B --> B2[Universal function Z equals Z Tr Pr]
    B --> B3[Valid for noble gases N2 O2 CH4 CO]
    B --> B4[Fails for polar and large molecules]
    C --> C1[Adds Acentric Factor omega as third parameter]
    C --> C2[Z equals Z Tr Pr omega]
    C --> C3[Captures shape polarity and size effects]
    C --> C4[Foundation for Lee Kesler generalized correlations]
    A --> D[Acentric Factor Definition]
    D --> D1[omega equals minus log10 Prsat at Tr equals 0.7 minus 1.000]
    D --> D2[Ar Kr Xe have omega equals 0 simple fluids]
    D --> D3[Methane 0.008 n pentane 0.251 water 0.344]
    C --> E[Generalized Compressibility Chart]
    E --> E1[Lee Kesler Tabulated Z0 and Z1]
    E --> E2[Z equals Z0 plus omega times Z1 minus Z0 over 0.3978]
    E --> E3[Nelson Obert chart for quick Z estimation]
    E --> E4[Fugacity coefficient phi via corresponding states]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cubic EOS Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: T, P, fluid identity] --> B[Step 1: Lookup Tc, Pc, omega]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute reduced properties Tr and Pr]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute b equals bRK or bSRK or bPR]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute a equals aRK or aSRK or aPR with alpha]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute A equals aP over RT squared]
    E --> G[Step 6: Compute B equals bP over RT]
    F --> H[Step 7: Form cubic in Z]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 8: Solve cubic - Cardano or Newton Raphson]
    I --> J{Step 9: Select root}
    J -->|Vapor| K[Largest positive Z]
    J -->|Liquid| L[Smallest positive Z]
    K --> M[Step 10: Compute V equals ZRT over P]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Step 11: Output V, residual enthalpy, fugacity]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Which EOS to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select EOS for a real gas problem] --> B{Fluid type?}
    B -->|Noble gas, N2, O2, CO, CH4| C[Two-Param PCS or simple vdW]
    B -->|Hydrocarbon, light gases, CO2, H2S| D[SRK or PR]
    B -->|Highly polar, H2O, NH3, alcohols| E[PR with modified alpha or SAFT]
    B -->|Low pressure vapor only| F[Truncated virial - Pitzer B]
    C --> G{Critical region?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Lee-Kesler generalized chart]
    G -->|No| I[vdW sufficient]
    D --> J{Liquid phase required?}
    J -->|Yes| K[PR preferred over SRK]
    J -->|No, vapor only| L[SRK adequate]
    F --> M{Virial needed up to 5 bar?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Truncate at B, Pitzer B correlation]
    M -->|No, higher accuracy| O[Truncate at C - Hayden O Connell]
```

## 4.6 Fugacity Calculation Routes — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Fugacity of a Real Gas] --> B{Pressure regime?}
    B -->|Low P, ideal gas| C[f equals P - simplify]
    B -->|Moderate to high P| D[Method 1: From Cubic EOS]
    B -->|Moderate to high P| E[Method 2: From Generalized Chart]
    B -->|Low to moderate P| F[Method 3: From Virial EOS]
    D --> D1[Use RK or PR or SRK]
    D1 --> D2[ln phi equals Z minus 1 minus ln Z minus B minus A over 2 sqrt 2 B ln Z plus 1 plus sqrt 2 B over Z plus 1 minus sqrt 2 B]
    E --> E1[Use Lee-Kesler ln phi0 and ln phi1 tables]
    E1 --> E2[ln phi equals ln phi0 plus omega times ln phi1 minus ln phi0 over 0.3978]
    F --> F1[ln phi equals B P over RT]
    F1 --> F2[Get B from Pitzer correlation]
    D2 --> G[Output: f equals phi times P]
    E2 --> G
    F2 --> G
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the principle of corresponding states. How is the acentric factor defined and used in the three-parameter form?**

**Model Answer:**

The principle of corresponding states states that *all substances, when expressed in terms of their reduced properties $T_r = T/T_c$ and $P_r = P/P_c$, behave identically at the same reduced state*. Thus, $Z = Z(T_r, P_r)$ is a universal function.

The **acentric factor** $\omega$ is the third parameter defined by Pitzer to account for the deviation of a fluid from the simple-fluid (Ar, Kr, Xe) reference:

$$\omega = - \log_{10} \left( P_r^{\text{sat}} \right)_{T_r = 0.7} - 1.000$$

In the **three-parameter corresponding states** framework:

$$Z = Z(T_r, P_r, \omega)$$

It is used to refine the generalized compressibility chart (Lee-Kesler), to compute Pitzer-correlated second virial coefficients, and to evaluate $\alpha(T)$ in SRK and PR EOS.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Write the Peng-Robinson equation of state and list any two advantages it has over the Redlich-Kwong equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The Peng-Robinson (PR) equation of state is:

$$P = \dfrac{R T}{V - b} - \dfrac{a(T)}{V (V + b) + b (V - b)}$$

with

$$a(T) = 0.45724 \, \dfrac{R^{2} T_c^{2}}{P_c} \, \alpha(T_r, \omega), \quad b = 0.07780 \, \dfrac{R T_c}{P_c}$$

$$\alpha^{0.5} = 1 + \kappa (1 - T_r^{0.5}), \quad \kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226 \, \omega - 0.26992 \, \omega^{2}$$

**Two advantages over RK:**

1. **Better liquid density prediction:** PR's denominator $V^{2} + 2 b V - b^{2}$ provides a stronger repulsion term, yielding $Z_c = 0.307$ (closer to real values 0.27–0.31) compared to RK's $Z_c = 0.333$.

2. **Accurate vapor-pressure curve:** The temperature dependence of $\alpha$ in PR is fit to the experimental vapor-pressure data, making it superior for VLE calculations, especially for hydrocarbons and CO₂.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question B1 — Option (a)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the constants $a$ and $b$ in the van der Waals equation of state using the critical-point conditions. Hence show that $Z_c = 3/8$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The Redlich-Kwong equation of state is to be used to find the molar volume of n-butane gas at 500 K and 9.4 bar. Given: $T_c = 425.1$ K, $P_c = 37.96$ bar, $R = 83.14$ cm³·bar·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹. Report the compressibility factor and the molar volume.

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the complete step-by-step derivation. Final result:

$$a = \dfrac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c}, \quad b = \dfrac{R T_c}{8 P_c}, \quad V_c = 3b, \quad Z_c = \dfrac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Compute RK constants.**

$$a = \dfrac{0.42748 \times R^{2} T_c^{2.5}}{P_c} = \dfrac{0.42748 \times (83.14)^{2} \times (425.1)^{2.5}}{37.96}$$

Compute intermediate values:
- $R^{2} = 83.14^{2} = 6912.26$ (cm³·bar·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹)²
- $T_c^{2.5} = 425.1^{2.5}$

$$425.1^{2} = 180712.01, \quad 425.1^{0.5} = 20.619, \quad T_c^{2.5} = 180712.01 \times 20.619 = 3.7260 \times 10^{6}$$

$$a = \dfrac{0.42748 \times 6912.26 \times 3.7260 \times 10^{6}}{37.96}$$

$$a = \dfrac{0.42748 \times 2.5754 \times 10^{10}}{37.96} = \dfrac{1.1009 \times 10^{10}}{37.96} = 2.901 \times 10^{8} \;\text{cm}^{6}\,\text{bar}\,\text{mol}^{-2}\,\text{K}^{0.5}$$

$$b = \dfrac{0.08664 \times R T_c}{P_c} = \dfrac{0.08664 \times 83.14 \times 425.1}{37.96} = \dfrac{3062.62}{37.96} = 80.69 \;\text{cm}^{3}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Form the RK cubic in Z.**

The RK cubic in Z is:

$$Z^{3} - Z^{2} + (A - B - B^{2}) Z - A B = 0$$

where $A = a P / (R^{2} T^{2.5})$ and $B = b P / (R T)$.

**Step 3: Compute A and B.**

$$A = \dfrac{2.901 \times 10^{8} \times 9.4}{(83.14)^{2} \times (500)^{2.5}} = \dfrac{2.727 \times 10^{9}}{6912.26 \times 5.590 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{2.727 \times 10^{9}}{3.864 \times 10^{10}} = 0.07059$$

$$B = \dfrac{80.69 \times 9.4}{83.14 \times 500} = \dfrac{758.49}{41570} = 0.01825$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the cubic.**

$$Z^{3} - Z^{2} + (0.07059 - 0.01825 - 0.01825^{2}) Z - 0.07059 \times 0.01825 = 0$$

$$Z^{3} - Z^{2} + (0.07059 - 0.01825 - 0.000333) Z - 0.001288 = 0$$

$$Z^{3} - Z^{2} + 0.05201 \, Z - 0.001288 = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve by Newton-Raphson iteration, starting with ideal gas $Z_0 = 1$.**

Iteration formula: $Z_{n+1} = Z_n - f(Z_n) / f'(Z_n)$ where $f'(Z) = 3Z^{2} - 2Z + 0.05201$.

- $n = 0$: $Z_0 = 1$, $f(1) = 1 - 1 + 0.05201 - 0.001288 = 0.05072$, $f'(1) = 3 - 2 + 0.05201 = 1.05201$, $Z_1 = 1 - 0.05072/1.05201 = 0.9518$
- $n = 1$: $Z_1 = 0.9518$, $f(0.9518) = 0.8622 - 0.9059 + 0.04709 - 0.001288 = 0.00210$, $f'(0.9518) = 2.7176 - 1.9036 + 0.05201 = 0.8660$, $Z_2 = 0.9518 - 0.00210/0.8660 = 0.9494$
- $n = 2$: $Z_2 = 0.9494$, $f(0.9494) = 0.8558 - 0.9014 + 0.04692 - 0.001288 = 0.00004$ ✓

$$\boxed{\, Z \approx 0.9494 \,}$$

**Step 6: Compute molar volume.**

$$V = \dfrac{Z R T}{P} = \dfrac{0.9494 \times 83.14 \times 500}{9.4} = \dfrac{39466.4}{9.4} = 4198.6 \;\text{cm}^{3}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\, V \approx 4199 \;\text{cm}^{3}\,\text{mol}^{-1} \approx 4.20 \;\text{L}\,\text{mol}^{-1} \,}$$

The result is consistent with the experimental Z ≈ 0.95 for n-butane at this state (small deviation from ideality at moderate pressure).

---

### **Question B1 — Option (b)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the definition of the virial equation of state, derive the truncated two-parameter virial equation in pressure form and explain its physical significance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Estimate the fugacity of methane at $T = 200$ K and $P = 50$ bar using the virial equation truncated at the second coefficient. Use the Pitzer correlation for B. Given: $T_c = 190.6$ K, $P_c = 46.0$ bar, $\omega = 0.011$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to **Section 3.3** above. Final result:

$$Z = 1 + \dfrac{B P}{R T} \quad \text{(pressure form, truncated at 2nd coefficient)}$$

**Physical significance:**
- $B$ is the **second virial coefficient**, depending only on temperature for a pure substance.
- It represents the **integrated effect of pairwise (2-body) intermolecular interactions** — positive B indicates dominant repulsions (e.g., at high T), negative B indicates dominant attractions (e.g., near condensation).
- $B = 0$ at the **Boyle temperature** $T_B$, where real-gas behavior briefly matches ideality.
- Validity: low-to-moderate pressures (typically $P < 15$ bar or $P_r < 0.5$ for safe truncation).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Compute reduced properties.**

$$T_r = \dfrac{T}{T_c} = \dfrac{200}{190.6} = 1.049, \quad P_r = \dfrac{P}{P_c} = \dfrac{50}{46.0} = 1.087$$

**Step 2: Compute Pitzer correlations for B.**

$$B^{(0)} = 0.083 - \dfrac{0.422}{T_r^{1.6}} = 0.083 - \dfrac{0.422}{(1.049)^{1.6}}$$

Compute $T_r^{1.6} = 1.049^{1.6}$. Use $\ln(1.049) = 0.04784$, so $1.6 \times 0.04784 = 0.07655$, hence $T_r^{1.6} = e^{0.07655} = 1.0796$.

$$B^{(0)} = 0.083 - \dfrac{0.422}{1.0796} = 0.083 - 0.3909 = -0.3079$$

$$B^{(1)} = 0.139 - \dfrac{0.172}{T_r^{4.2}}$$

Compute $T_r^{4.2} = 1.049^{4.2}$. $\ln(1.049) \times 4.2 = 0.04784 \times 4.2 = 0.2009$, $T_r^{4.2} = e^{0.2009} = 1.2225$.

$$B^{(1)} = 0.139 - \dfrac{0.172}{1.2225} = 0.139 - 0.1407 = -0.0017$$

**Step 3: Compute the second virial coefficient.**

$$\dfrac{B P_c}{R T_c} = B^{(0)} + \omega B^{(1)} = -0.3079 + 0.011 \times (-0.0017) = -0.3079 - 0.0000187 = -0.30792$$

$$B = -\dfrac{0.30792 \times R T_c}{P_c} = -\dfrac{0.30792 \times 83.14 \times 190.6}{46.0} = -\dfrac{4879.6}{46.0} = -106.1 \;\text{cm}^{3}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Compute the compressibility factor (truncated virial, pressure form).**

$$Z = 1 + \dfrac{B P}{R T} = 1 + \dfrac{(-106.1)(50)}{(83.14)(200)} = 1 - \dfrac{5305}{16628} = 1 - 0.3191 = 0.6809$$

**Step 5: Compute the fugacity coefficient.**

For a gas described by the truncated virial EOS (pressure form):

$$\ln \phi = \int_{0}^{P} \dfrac{Z - 1}{P} dP = \int_{0}^{P} \dfrac{B}{R T} dP = \dfrac{B P}{R T}$$

$$\ln \phi = \dfrac{(-106.1)(50)}{(83.14)(200)} = -0.3191$$

$$\phi = e^{-0.3191} = 0.7268$$

**Step 6: Compute the fugacity.**

$$f = \phi \cdot P = 0.7268 \times 50 = 36.34 \;\text{bar}$$

$$\boxed{\, f \approx 36.3 \;\text{bar} \,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units inconsistency:** Always keep $R$, $P$, and $V$ in a single consistent system. The use of $R = 83.14$ cm³·bar·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ requires $V$ in cm³/mol and $P$ in bar. Mixing SI and CGS causes massive errors.
> 2. **Sign of B:** A negative B is *expected* below the Boyle temperature (here $T_r = 1.049 < 2.5$, the typical Boyle $T_r$ for simple fluids). Don't panic; the sign is physically meaningful.
> 3. **Pitzer B range:** The correlation is valid for $0.3 \le T_r \le 5.0$. For $T_r$ outside this range, the correlation extrapolates poorly and the truncated virial itself may not be adequate.
> 4. **Truncation error:** Truncating at B is *only* valid up to $P \approx 10$ bar or $P_r \approx 0.5 T_r$ (rough rule). At $P_r = 1.087$, the truncated virial begins to lose accuracy — include the warning in your answer.
> 5. **Use of $\ln$ not $\log_{10}$:** When computing $\phi$ from $\ln \phi$, use $e^{(\ln \phi)}$, not $10^{(\ln \phi)}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- **Cubic EOS (must memorize for KTU):** vdW, RK, SRK, PR. Know the **exact forms**, the **constants a and b in terms of $T_c$ and $P_c$**, and the **$\alpha(T_r, \omega)$ for SRK and PR**.
- **Universal $Z_c$ values to remember:** vdW = 0.375, RK = 0.333, SRK = 0.333, PR = 0.307. **Real gas $Z_c$**: 0.27–0.31.
- **Virial equation:** Density form $Z = 1 + B/V + C/V^{2} + \dots$. Pressure form $Z = 1 + B'P + \dots$. Statistical meaning: B = 2-body, C = 3-body, D = 4-body.
- **Pitzer correlation:** $B P_c / R T_c = B^{(0)} + \omega B^{(1)}$ with $B^{(0)} = 0.083 - 0.422 / T_r^{1.6}$ and $B^{(1)} = 0.139 - 0.172 / T_r^{4.2}$. **Valid $0.3 \le T_r \le 5.0$**.
- **Boyle temperature:** Temperature at which $B = 0$. For most substances, $T_B / T_c \approx 2.5$.
- **PCS (Two-parameter):** $Z = Z(T_r, P_r)$. Two-parameter PCS works for Ar, Kr, Xe, N₂, O₂, CO, CH₄. Fails for H₂O, NH₃, and large hydrocarbons.
- **PCS (Three-parameter with $\omega$):** $Z = Z(T_r, P_r, \omega)$. $\omega = -\log_{10} P_r^{\text{sat}}\big\vert_{T_r = 0.7} - 1$. By definition, $\omega = 0$ for Ar, Kr, Xe.
- **Generalized Compressibility Chart (Lee-Kesler):** $Z = Z^{(0)} + (\omega / 0.3978) [Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)}]$. Tabulated at $T_r$ and $P_r$. Use **natural logarithms** for fugacity coefficient interpolation.
- **Fugacity from generalized chart:** $\ln \phi = \ln \phi^{(0)} + (\omega / 0.3978) [\ln \phi^{(1)} - \ln \phi^{(0)}]$.
- **Root selection from cubic EOS:** For $V_{vapor}$, pick the **largest** positive Z. For $V_{liquid}$, pick the **smallest** positive Z. The middle root (if positive) is **meta-stable** and discarded.
- **Acentric factor reference values (KTU favorites):** CH₄ (0.008), N₂ (0.040), CO₂ (0.225), NH₃ (0.256), H₂O (0.344), n-C₁₀H₂₂ (0.490).
- **Engineering use:** PR EOS is the *de facto* standard in commercial process simulators (Aspen, HYSYS). SRK is preferred for vapor-side VLE.
- **Solver approach for cubic in Z:** Use **Newton-Raphson** with $Z_0 = 1$ (vapor) or $Z_0 = b P / R T$ (liquid) as initial guess. Tolerate $|f(Z)| < 10^{-6}$ for convergence.
- **Cross-checks:** Compare $Z$ from cubic EOS with $Z$ from generalized chart — they should agree within ~3% for non-polar substances at $T_r > 1.0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Residual Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Residual Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **residual property** of a fluid is defined as the difference between the actual thermodynamic property of a real fluid and the property of the fluid in the **ideal-gas state at the same temperature, pressure, and composition**.

Mathematically, for any extensive molar property $M$:

$$M^R(T,P) \equiv M(T,P) - M^{ig}(T,P)$$

where the superscript $R$ denotes the *residual* value, and $M^{ig}$ is the value the property would have if the fluid behaved as an ideal gas under identical conditions of temperature, pressure, and composition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Departure Functions:**
> - A *residual property* compares the real fluid state to the **ideal-gas state** at the same T and P.
> - A *departure function* compares the real fluid state to the **ideal-gas state at the same T and a reference pressure** (typically zero or 1 bar).
> - Residual properties are the more commonly used formulation in modern chemical engineering thermodynamics (Smith, Van Ness, Abbott framework).

### Physical Meaning and Intuitive Analogy

Think of a real gas molecule as a "rushing commuter" in a crowded metro train (compressed fluid). Now imagine the same number of commuters spread out in an empty football stadium (ideal gas at the same T, P, composition). The residual property measures **"how much more/less energetic" the molecules are in the compressed crowd compared to the spacious stadium**, holding temperature and pressure constant.

**Intuitive Examples:**
- **Residual Enthalpy $H^R$:** The extra (or missing) heat content of a real gas due to intermolecular forces, *beyond* what an ideal gas would have.
- **Residual Entropy $S^R$:** The change in molecular disorder caused by intermolecular interactions, beyond pure temperature-driven disorder.
- **Residual Volume $V^R$:** The "squish factor" — how much smaller (or larger) the real molar volume is compared to the ideal molar volume $RT/P$.

### Why Residual Properties Are Engineering Gold

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we love residual properties in Chemical Engineering:**
> 1. They **eliminate reference-state ambiguity** (no need to choose a standard state).
> 2. They are **directly computable from a single PVT equation of state** (e.g., vdW, RK, SRK, PR).
> 3. They provide a **unified framework** for both pure fluids and mixtures.
> 4. They naturally lead to the **fugacity coefficient** $\phi$, which is essential for VLE calculations.

### Standard Metrics and Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Units |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Residual Enthalpy | $H^R$ | **J/mol** |
| Residual Entropy | $S^R$ | **J/(mol·K)** |
| Residual Volume | $V^R$ | **m³/mol** |
| Fugacity Coefficient | $\phi$ | **dimensionless** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Thermodynamic Foundation

The residual property framework is built upon a single fundamental identity: the **residual Gibbs energy equals $RT \ln \phi$**.

Starting from the defining equation:

$$M^R(T,P) = M(T,P) - M^{ig}(T,P)$$

We can apply this to every thermodynamic potential. The key relationships are derived from the fundamental property relations:

$$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$$
$$dG^{ig} = -S^{ig}\,dT + V^{ig}\,dP$$

Subtracting:

$$dG^R = -S^R\,dT + V^R\,dP$$

This gives us the Maxwell-type relations:

$$\left(\frac{\partial G^R}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S^R$$
$$\left(\frac{\partial G^R}{\partial P}\right)_T = V^R$$

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the heart of Module 2 exam questions.**

| # | Residual Property | Master Formula | Derivation Basis |
|---|-------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Residual Gibbs Energy** | $\dfrac{G^R}{RT} = \ln \phi$ | Definition: $G^R = \mu^R = RT\ln\phi$ |
| 2 | **Residual Volume** | $V^R = RT\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | $V^R = (\partial G^R/\partial P)_T$ |
| 3 | **Residual Enthalpy** | $\dfrac{H^R}{RT} = -T\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | $H^R = -T^2[\partial(G^R/T)/\partial T]_P$ |
| 4 | **Residual Entropy** | $\dfrac{S^R}{R} = -\ln\phi - T\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | $S^R = (H^R - G^R)/T$ |
| 5 | **Residual Internal Energy** | $U^R = H^R - RT(Z-1)$ | $U = H - PV$ identity |
| 6 | **Residual Helmholtz Energy** | $A^R = RT\ln\phi - RT(Z-1)$ | $A^R = G^R - PV^R$ |
| 7 | **Fugacity Coefficient Integral** | $\ln \phi = \displaystyle\int_0^P \dfrac{Z-1}{P}\,dP$ | Key integral from PVT data |
| 8 | **General EOS-Based Form** | $\ln \phi = \displaystyle\int_0^P \left[\dfrac{PV}{RT}-1\right]\dfrac{dP}{P}$ | Direct integration |

Where $Z = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$ is the compressibility factor and $Z^R = Z - 1$ is the residual compressibility.

### The Three Pillars of the Theory

**Pillar 1: The Fugacity Coefficient Link**
The residual Gibbs energy is **exactly equal** to $RT \ln \phi$. This is not a coincidence — it is the *defining* equation of $\phi$:

$$\phi = \exp\!\left(\frac{G^R}{RT}\right)$$

This single identity bridges residual properties with **vapor-liquid equilibrium calculations**.

**Pillar 2: Residual Properties from PVT Equations of State**
For any explicit PVT EOS like van der Waals, Redlich-Kwong, SRK, or Peng-Robinson, the residual properties are obtained by:
- Step 1: Calculate $Z$ from the cubic equation at given T, P.
- Step 2: Compute $V^R = V - RT/P = RT(Z-1)/P$.
- Step 3: Integrate to get $\ln \phi$, then differentiate to get $H^R, S^R$.

**Pillar 3: Ideal-Gas Anchor at Zero Pressure**
The integral $\int_0^P$ form is justified because **at zero pressure, all gases behave ideally** ($Z \to 1$ as $P \to 0$). Therefore:

$$M(T,P) - M(T,0) = M^{ig}(T,P) - M^{ig}(T,0) + M^R(T,P)$$

Since at $P = 0$, real = ideal, and $M^{ig}(T,P) - M^{ig}(T,0)$ is computable from ideal-gas heat capacity data, we cleanly isolate $M^R$.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Role of Residual Properties |
|---------------------|------------------------------|
| **Refrigeration (LNG, LPG)** | Compute $H^R$ for accurate Joule-Thomson cooling |
| **Petroleum Refining** | Calculate fugacity coefficients for hydrocarbon VLE |
| **Compressor Design** | Predict non-ideal work via $U^R$ and $H^R$ |
| **Supercritical Extraction** | $\phi$ governs solubility in SC-CO₂ processes |
| **Chemical Reactor Design** | Non-ideal reaction equilibria via $\hat{\phi}_i$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Master Integral for $\ln \phi$ from PVT Data

**Starting Point:** The general relationship between residual Gibbs energy and fugacity:

$$G^R(T,P) = RT \ln \phi$$

**Step 1 —** Take the partial derivative with respect to P at constant T:

$$\left(\frac{\partial G^R}{\partial P}\right)_T = RT \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

**Step 2 —** By the fundamental relation, the left side equals $V^R$:

$$V^R = RT \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

**Step 3 —** Recall that $V^R = V - V^{ig} = V - \dfrac{RT}{P}$. Substitute:

$$V - \frac{RT}{P} = RT \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

**Step 4 —** Isolate the derivative:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial P}\right)_T = \frac{V}{RT} - \frac{1}{P} = \frac{PV}{RT \cdot P} - \frac{1}{P} = \frac{Z - 1}{P}$$

**Step 5 —** Integrate both sides from the ideal-gas limit ($P = 0$, where $\phi = 1$, so $\ln \phi = 0$) to the state of interest $P$:

$$\int_0^{\ln \phi} d(\ln \phi) = \int_0^P \frac{Z - 1}{P}\,dP$$

**Final Result —**

$$\boxed{\;\ln \phi = \int_0^P \frac{Z - 1}{P}\,dP\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important residual property equation** in Module 2. Every other residual property flows from here.

---

### Derivation 2: Residual Enthalpy Formula

**Starting Point:** The Gibbs-Helmholtz-like relation:

$$\frac{G^R}{RT} = \ln \phi \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{G^R}{T} = R \ln \phi$$

**Step 1 —** Apply the Gibbs-Helmholtz identity:

$$H^R = -T^2 \left[\frac{\partial (G^R / T)}{\partial T}\right]_P$$

This identity is obtained by writing $G = H - TS$, then differentiating $G/T$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$.

**Step 2 —** Substitute $G^R/T = R \ln \phi$:

$$H^R = -T^2 \cdot R \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 3 —** Divide both sides by $RT$:

$$\frac{H^R}{RT} = -T \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Final Result —**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{H^R}{RT} = -T \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P\;}$$

---

### Derivation 3: Residual Entropy Formula

**Starting Point:** From the definition of Gibbs energy:

$$G^R = H^R - T S^R$$

**Step 1 —** Solve for $S^R$:

$$S^R = \frac{H^R - G^R}{T} = \frac{H^R}{T} - \frac{G^R}{T}$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute the previously derived relations:

- $H^R / T = -RT (\partial \ln \phi / \partial T)_P$
- $G^R / T = R \ln \phi$

$$S^R = -RT \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P - R \ln \phi$$

**Step 3 —** Divide both sides by $R$:

$$\frac{S^R}{R} = -T \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P - \ln \phi$$

**Final Result —**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{S^R}{R} = -\ln \phi - T \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Equivalently**, since $H^R / RT = -T(\partial \ln \phi / \partial T)_P$, you can write:
> $$\frac{S^R}{R} = \frac{H^R}{RT} - \ln \phi$$
> This is a handy shortcut during derivations.

---

### Derivation 4: Residual Volume from Compressibility Factor

**Starting Point:** The definition of residual volume:

$$V^R = V - V^{ig} = V - \frac{RT}{P}$$

**Step 1 —** Use $Z = PV/RT$, which gives $V = ZRT/P$. Substitute:

$$V^R = \frac{ZRT}{P} - \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{RT}{P}(Z - 1)$$

**Final Result —**

$$\boxed{\;V^R = \frac{RT}{P}(Z - 1)\;}$$

This compact form is invaluable for direct numerical computation from a PVT equation of state.

---

### Derivation 5: Residual Internal Energy Formula

**Starting Point:** The fundamental identity $U = H - PV$. Apply to both real and ideal-gas states:

$$U = H - PV \quad\Rightarrow\quad U^{ig} = H^{ig} - PV^{ig}$$

**Step 1 —** Subtract to get the residual:

$$U^R = U - U^{ig} = (H - H^{ig}) - P(V - V^{ig}) = H^R - PV^R$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute the residual volume result:

$$U^R = H^R - P \cdot \frac{RT}{P}(Z - 1) = H^R - RT(Z - 1)$$

**Final Result —**

$$\boxed{\;U^R = H^R - RT(Z - 1)\;}$$

---

### Derivation 6: Residual Helmholtz Energy Formula

**Starting Point:** The relation $A = G - PV$. Apply to both states:

$$A^R = G^R - PV^R$$

**Step 1 —** Substitute $G^R = RT \ln \phi$ and $V^R = RT(Z-1)/P$:

$$A^R = RT \ln \phi - P \cdot \frac{RT}{P}(Z - 1)$$

**Step 2 —** Simplify:

$$A^R = RT \ln \phi - RT(Z - 1)$$

**Final Result —**

$$\boxed{\;A^R = RT[\ln \phi - (Z - 1)]\;}$$

---

### Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Path to Obtain | Primary Equation | Secondary Equation |
|----------|---------------|------------------|-------------------|
| $G^R / RT$ | Definition | $\ln \phi$ | — |
| $V^R$ | $V - V^{ig}$ | $RT(Z-1)/P$ | $RT(\partial \ln\phi/\partial P)_T$ |
| $H^R / RT$ | Gibbs-Helmholtz | $-T(\partial \ln\phi/\partial T)_P$ | $\int_0^P (V - T\partial V/\partial T)dP$ |
| $S^R / R$ | $H^R - G^R$ over $T$ | $-\ln\phi - T(\partial \ln\phi/\partial T)_P$ | $-\int_0^P (\partial V/\partial T - R/P)dP$ |
| $U^R$ | $H^R - PV^R$ | $H^R - RT(Z-1)$ | — |
| $A^R$ | $G^R - PV^R$ | $RT[\ln\phi - (Z-1)]$ | $-\int_\infty^V[(RT/V) - P]dV$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Conceptual Framework of Residual Properties

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates how a real-fluid property is decomposed into its ideal-gas part and its residual part, and how all residual properties cascade from the central $\ln \phi$ integral.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Real Fluid Property M(T,P)"] --> B["Subtract Ideal-Gas State at Same T, P"]
    C["Ideal-Gas Property M^ig(T,P)"] --> B
    B --> D["Residual Property M^R = M - M^ig"]
    D --> E["Computed via Equation of State (EOS)"]
    E --> F["Compute Z = PV/RT from Cubic EOS"]
    F --> G["Master Integral: ln phi = Integral of Z-1 over P dP"]
    G --> H["Fugacity Coefficient phi"]
    H --> I1["G^R / RT = ln phi"]
    H --> I2["V^R = RT d ln phi / dP"]
    H --> I3["H^R / RT = -T d ln phi / dT"]
    H --> I4["S^R / R = -ln phi - T d ln phi / dT"]
    H --> I5["U^R = H^R - RT(Z-1)"]
    H --> I6["A^R = RT[ln phi - (Z-1)]"]
    I3 --> J1["Heat Exchanger Design"]
    I3 --> J2["Joule-Thomson Coefficient"]
    I4 --> J3["Entropy Balance in Turbines"]
    H --> J4["VLE via phi_i = y_i P / x_i f_i"]
```

### Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture for Residual Property Computation

This diagram shows the data flow when using a cubic equation of state (e.g., Peng-Robinson) to compute all six residual properties at a given (T, P) state point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["Input: T, P, Composition, Critical Constants ac, Pc, omega"] --> EOS["Cubic EOS Module: SRK or PR"]
    EOS --> ZCOMP["Solve for Z: Z^3 + c2 Z^2 + c1 Z + c0 = 0"]
    ZCOMP --> ZSEL["Select Correct Root: Vapor or Liquid Z"]
    ZSEL --> VRES["Compute V^R = RT(Z-1)/P"]
    ZSEL --> LPHI["Compute ln phi = Integral of Z-1 over P / P dP"]
    LPHI --> GRES["G^R = RT ln phi"]
    LPHI --> HRES["H^R via Temperature Derivative of ln phi"]
    LPHI --> SRES["S^R via Combined Formula"]
    HRES --> URES["U^R = H^R - RT(Z-1)"]
    GRES --> ARES["A^R = G^R - RT(Z-1)"]
    VRES --> ARES
    HRES --> SRES
    OUTPUT["Output Vector: V^R, H^R, S^R, U^R, G^R, A^R, phi"] <-.- VRES
    OUTPUT <-.- HRES
    OUTPUT <-.- SRES
    OUTPUT <-.- URES
    OUTPUT <-.- ARES
    OUTPUT <-.- GRES
```

### Diagram 3: The Three-State Decomposition (Ideal-Gas Anchor Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["State: Real Fluid at T, P"]
    S1["Hypothetical Ideal-Gas State at T, P"]
    S0 -->|"Difference = M^R"| S1
    S2["Ideal-Gas State at T, P_ref = 0 (Zero Pressure)"]
    S1 -->|"Ideal-Gas Heat Capacity Integration"| S2
    S0 -->|"Real-Fluid Path Integral from P_ref to P"| S2
    NOTE["At P = 0: Real Gas = Ideal Gas (Residual = 0)"] -.-> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The key insight: the **residual property** $M^R$ isolates the effect of **intermolecular forces** from the effect of **thermal motion**, because at $P \to 0$, molecules are so far apart that forces vanish and the gas is ideal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define residual property. How is it distinguished from a departure function?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **residual property** is the difference between the actual thermodynamic property of a real fluid and the property of the fluid in the hypothetical ideal-gas state at the **same temperature, pressure, and composition**:

$$M^R(T,P) = M(T,P) - M^{ig}(T,P)$$

**Distinction from Departure Function:**
- A *residual property* compares the real fluid to an ideal-gas reference at the **same T and P** (no integration along a path required if the equation of state is known).
- A *departure function* compares the real fluid to the ideal-gas state at the **same T but a reference pressure** (typically $P \to 0$ or 1 bar), requiring integration of ideal-gas heat capacities along the temperature path.

In practice, both yield identical numerical values for $H$, $S$, $U$, and $G$, but the **residual formulation is computationally more direct from a single EOS**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the master integral expression for $\ln \phi$ in terms of the compressibility factor. What is its physical significance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The master integral expression is:

$$\ln \phi = \int_0^P \frac{Z - 1}{P}\,dP \quad \text{(at constant T)}$$

where $Z = PV/RT$ is the compressibility factor.

**Physical Significance:**
This equation expresses the **fugacity coefficient** purely in terms of PVT data (or a PVT equation of state). The integrand $(Z-1)/P$ is a measure of the **deviation from ideal-gas behavior**:
- When $Z = 1$ (ideal gas), the integrand vanishes and $\phi = 1$.
- When $Z > 1$ (repulsive forces dominate, high T or low P), $\phi > 1$.
- When $Z < 1$ (attractive forces dominate, near condensation), $\phi < 1$.

It is the **gateway equation** for computing all other residual properties and forms the backbone of VLE calculations.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)**

*Q3(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]*
*Derive the following residual property relations starting from the fundamental definition $M^R = M - M^{ig}$:*
*(i) $\ln \phi = \displaystyle\int_0^P (Z-1)/P\,dP$*
*(ii) $H^R/RT = -T(\partial \ln \phi/\partial T)_P$*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Derivation of $\ln \phi$ master integral:**

Step 1: By definition, the residual Gibbs energy is $G^R(T,P) = RT \ln \phi$. Taking the partial derivative with respect to $P$ at constant $T$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial G^R}{\partial P}\right)_T = RT \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

Step 2: From the fundamental relation $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$, we have $(\partial G/\partial P)_T = V$. The same applies to $G^{ig}$: $(\partial G^{ig}/\partial P)_T = V^{ig}$. Subtracting:

$$\left(\frac{\partial G^R}{\partial P}\right)_T = V - V^{ig} = V^R$$

Step 3: Combining, $V^R = RT(\partial \ln \phi/\partial P)_T$. Now, $V^R = V - RT/P$, so:

$$V - \frac{RT}{P} = RT \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

Step 4: Rearranging:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial P}\right)_T = \frac{V}{RT} - \frac{1}{P} = \frac{PV}{RT \cdot P} - \frac{1}{P} = \frac{Z-1}{P}$$

Step 5: Integrating from $P = 0$ (where $\phi = 1$, so $\ln \phi = 0$) to the state of interest:

$$\boxed{\ln \phi = \int_0^P \frac{Z-1}{P}\,dP \quad \text{(at constant T)}}$$

**Part (ii) — Derivation of $H^R/RT$ formula:**

Step 1: From the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation, for any property:

$$H^R = -T^2 \left[\frac{\partial (G^R/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P$$

Step 2: Substitute $G^R/T = R \ln \phi$:

$$H^R = -T^2 \cdot R \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

Step 3: Divide both sides by $RT$:

$$\boxed{\frac{H^R}{RT} = -T \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P}$$

*Q3(b). [7 Marks]*
*Using the residual property relations, derive expressions for:*
*(i) $S^R/R$ in terms of $\phi$ and its temperature derivative*
*(ii) $V^R$ in terms of $Z$*
*(iii) $A^R$ in terms of $\phi$ and $Z$*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Residual Entropy:**

Step 1: From $G^R = H^R - T S^R$, solve for $S^R$:

$$S^R = \frac{H^R - G^R}{T} = \frac{H^R}{T} - \frac{G^R}{T}$$

Step 2: Substitute the known relations $H^R/T = -RT(\partial \ln \phi/\partial T)_P$ and $G^R/T = R \ln \phi$:

$$S^R = -RT \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P - R \ln \phi$$

Step 3: Divide by $R$:

$$\boxed{\frac{S^R}{R} = -\ln \phi - T \left(\frac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P}$$

**Part (ii) — Residual Volume:**

Step 1: By definition $V^R = V - V^{ig}$. For an ideal gas, $V^{ig} = RT/P$.

Step 2: Substitute $V = ZRT/P$:

$$V^R = \frac{ZRT}{P} - \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{RT}{P}(Z - 1)$$

$$\boxed{V^R = \frac{RT}{P}(Z - 1)}$$

**Part (iii) — Residual Helmholtz Energy:**

Step 1: From $A = G - PV$, the residual form is $A^R = G^R - PV^R$.

Step 2: Substitute $G^R = RT \ln \phi$ and $V^R = RT(Z-1)/P$:

$$A^R = RT \ln \phi - P \cdot \frac{RT}{P}(Z - 1) = RT \ln \phi - RT(Z - 1)$$

$$\boxed{A^R = RT[\ln \phi - (Z - 1)]}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

*Q4(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*
*For steam at 500 K and 10 bar, the experimental compressibility factor is $Z = 0.95$. Estimate the fugacity coefficient using a virial-type approximation where $(Z-1)/P$ is approximately constant over the integration range.*

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Apply the master integral:

$$\ln \phi = \int_0^P \frac{Z-1}{P}\,dP$$

Step 2: Under the assumption that $(Z-1)/P$ is constant, the integral simplifies to:

$$\ln \phi = \frac{Z-1}{P} \cdot P = Z - 1$$

Step 3: Substitute $Z = 0.95$:

$$\ln \phi = 0.95 - 1 = -0.05$$

Step 4: Calculate $\phi$:

$$\phi = e^{-0.05} = 0.9512$$

**Answer:** $\phi \approx 0.9512$ (slightly less than 1, indicating mild attractive intermolecular forces dominate at this state).

*Q4(b). [7 Marks]*
*Starting from the master integral, derive an expression for $\ln \phi$ in terms of the second virial coefficient $B(T)$ and explain its limitations.*

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: The virial equation of state truncated after the second term is:

$$\frac{PV}{RT} = Z = 1 + \frac{BP}{RT}$$

Step 2: Therefore, $Z - 1 = BP/RT$.

Step 3: Substitute into the master integral:

$$\ln \phi = \int_0^P \frac{BP/RT}{P}\,dP = \int_0^P \frac{B}{RT}\,dP$$

Step 4: Since $B = B(T)$ depends only on T at given T, it is a constant inside the integral:

$$\ln \phi = \frac{B(T)}{RT} \int_0^P dP = \frac{BP}{RT}$$

Step 5: This equals $Z - 1$ exactly, consistent with the two-term virial truncation.

$$\boxed{\ln \phi = \frac{BP}{RT} = Z - 1}$$

**Limitations:**
1. Valid only at **low to moderate pressures** (typically $P < 10-15$ bar) where higher virial coefficients ($C, D, \ldots$) are negligible.
2. Requires accurate values of $B(T)$, often obtained from Pitzer correlations.
3. Cannot represent liquid-phase behavior where the virial expansion diverges from real PVT data.
4. Fails near the critical point or in the dense-liquid region.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones**
> 1. **Forgetting the integration limits:** The integral $\int_0^P$ starts at zero pressure (ideal-gas limit), not at atmospheric pressure. *Loss: 1-2 marks.*
> 2. **Sign error in $H^R$ formula:** The negative sign in $H^R/RT = -T(\partial \ln \phi/\partial T)_P$ is frequently dropped. *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 3. **Confusing residual with departure:** Many students write $H^R = H(T,P) - H^{ig}(T,P_{ref})$ instead of using the same P. *Loss: 1-2 marks.*
> 4. **Forgetting the $(Z-1)$ factor in $U^R$ and $A^R$:** The $RT(Z-1)$ correction term is the most-skipped step. *Loss: 1-2 marks.*
> 5. **Not stating that integration is at constant T:** This is a verbal mark sometimes given in derivations. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 6. **In numerical problems, not converting P to Pa or R to 8.314 J/(mol·K)** consistently. *Loss: 1-2 marks.*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Concept of Residual Properties**

- **Definition (Core):** $M^R(T,P) \equiv M(T,P) - M^{ig}(T,P)$ at the **same T, P, and composition**.
- **Master Equation:** $\ln \phi = \displaystyle\int_0^P \dfrac{Z-1}{P}\,dP$ — derived by integrating $(\partial \ln \phi/\partial P)_T = (Z-1)/P$ from $P=0$ to $P$.
- **Residual Gibbs Energy:** $G^R = RT \ln \phi$ — this is the **defining** link between residual properties and fugacity.
- **Residual Volume:** $V^R = \dfrac{RT}{P}(Z-1)$ — direct, no integration needed.
- **Residual Enthalpy:** $\dfrac{H^R}{RT} = -T\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P$ — comes from Gibbs-Helmholtz.
- **Residual Entropy:** $\dfrac{S^R}{R} = -\ln\phi - T\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \phi}{\partial T}\right)_P$ — comes from $S^R = (H^R - G^R)/T$.
- **Residual Internal Energy:** $U^R = H^R - RT(Z-1)$ — comes from $U^R = H^R - PV^R$.
- **Residual Helmholtz Energy:** $A^R = RT[\ln\phi - (Z-1)]$ — comes from $A^R = G^R - PV^R$.
- **Why ideal-gas anchor at $P=0$?** Because at zero pressure, real gas = ideal gas, so the lower limit is well-defined.
- **Physical meaning:** $M^R$ measures the **effect of intermolecular forces** alone, with thermal effects separated into the ideal-gas contribution.
- **Distinction from departure function:** Residual = same T, P; Departure = same T, but a reference P.
- **Key integral trick:** Any $\partial \ln \phi / \partial T$ at constant P gives $H^R$ (with sign); any $\partial \ln \phi / \partial P$ at constant T gives $V^R$ (scaled by $RT$).
- **Engineering use:** Fugacity coefficient $\phi$ is the gateway to **vapor-liquid equilibrium** via $\hat{\phi}_i^V y_i P = \hat{\phi}_i^L x_i f_i$.
- **Computation recipe:** Cubic EOS (SRK/PR) $\to$ $Z$ $\to$ $\ln \phi$ by integration $\to$ differentiate to get all other residual properties.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Fugacity and Activity of Pure Fluids – Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Fugacity and Activity – Definition of Partial Molar Properties and Chemical Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Fugacity and Activity of Pure Fluids – Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Fugacity and Activity – Definition of Partial Molar Properties and Chemical Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fugacity (f) — The "Corrected Pressure"

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Fugacity, denoted by the symbol **f** (with units of **pressure, bar or Pa**), is a thermodynamic property introduced by **Gilbert N. Lewis (1901)** that acts as a *thermodynamically effective pressure*. It replaces the true mechanical pressure (P) in the chemical potential equations of real (non-ideal) gases so that equations valid for ideal gases can still be applied to real systems. Mathematically, fugacity is defined through the fundamental relation:

$$d\bar{G} = RT \, d(\ln f) \quad \text{at constant T}$$

The exact mechanical pressure P is recovered in the limit of ideal gas behavior, where **f = P**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCHT303, Module 2) explicitly requires students to estimate **fugacity of pure fluids** using generalized charts (Newton's or Lee-Kesler), equations of state (vdW, RK, SRK, PR), and the **Principle of Corresponding States**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Discounted Salary":**
Imagine you are promised a salary of **₹50,000** (the *true pressure* P). However, due to taxes, PF deductions, and inflation, the *real amount you can actually spend* is only **₹38,000** (the *fugacity* f). The real spending power (f) is always *less* than the promised salary (P) for any non-ideal agent (real gas). For an ideal gas — a perfect economic agent with no deductions — the spending power equals the salary, so **f = P**.

### 1.2 Fugacity Coefficient (φ)

The dimensionless ratio relating fugacity to pressure:

$$\phi = \frac{f}{P}$$

- For an **ideal gas**: φ = 1 exactly.
- For a **real gas**: φ deviates from unity. φ < 1 (attractive forces dominate, low-to-moderate P), φ > 1 (repulsive forces dominate, very high P).
- At **zero pressure**, all real gases approach ideality, hence **φ → 1 as P → 0**.

### 1.3 Activity (a) — The Normalized Fugacity

**Formal Definition:**
Activity is the fugacity expressed *relative* to a chosen **standard-state fugacity** (f°). It is a pure number (dimensionless).

$$a = \frac{f}{f^{\circ}}$$

- For pure solid or pure liquid → standard state is the pure substance at **1 bar and the temperature of the system (T)**, so a → 1.
- For gases → standard state is the pure ideal gas at **1 bar and system T** (Lewis-Randall standard state), so a → f/P as P → 0.

> [!NOTE]
> The activity is essentially a *thermodynamic concentration*. It tells you "how active" or "how effective" a substance is, compared to its reference state.

### 1.4 Partial Molar Properties

**Formal Definition:**
A partial molar property $\bar{M}_i$ of component *i* in a mixture is defined as the change in the total extensive property **M** of the solution per mole of *i* added, at constant T, P, and moles of all other components.

$$\bar{M}_i = \left( \frac{\partial (nM)}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$

It carries the same units as the original property *per mole* (e.g., J/mol, m³/mol, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most important partial molar property in KTU thermodynamics is the **partial molar Gibbs energy**, which is the **chemical potential (μ)**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Roommate Problem":**
Imagine 3 roommates share a 90 m² apartment. If the apartment had only 1 person, each square meter costs more to maintain; with 3 people, the cost per person is lower. The *average* property per person is not constant — it depends on who else is in the mixture. The **partial molar property** is exactly this "marginal contribution" of one specific roommate (component i) to the total apartment value (total property), while keeping the other roommates' presence (composition) fixed.

### 1.5 Chemical Potential (μ)

**Formal Definition:**
The chemical potential of component *i* is defined as the partial molar Gibbs free energy:

$$\mu_i = \left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} = \bar{G}_i$$

It is the **driving force** for mass transfer, chemical reaction, and phase equilibrium. Equilibrium is reached when μ_i is uniform across all coexisting phases.

$$\mu_i^{I} = \mu_i^{II} = \mu_i^{III} = \ldots$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **fundamental property relation** for a multi-component, multi-phase system is:
> $$dG = -S \, dT + V \, dP + \sum_i \mu_i \, dn_i$$
> This is the most general differential form of G.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermodynamic Origin of Fugacity

The relationship between Gibbs energy and fugacity comes directly from the chemical potential definition. For a *pure* ideal gas, chemical potential depends on pressure as:

$$\mu^{ig}(T, P) = \mu^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln \frac{P}{P^{\circ}}$$

For a *real* gas, we cannot write such a simple equation. Lewis proposed replacing P with f in the ideal-gas expression to recover thermodynamic consistency:

$$\mu(T, P) = \mu^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln \frac{f}{f^{\circ}}$$

Since μ = G_m (molar Gibbs energy), the differential at constant T gives:

$$dG_m = RT \, d(\ln f) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left( \frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial P} \right)_{T} = \frac{V}{RT}$$

This is the **master equation** for computing fugacity from PVT data.

### 2.2 Calculation Pathways for Pure-Fluid Fugacity

**Path A — From PVT data (departure function method):**
Integrate from ideal-gas state (P* → 0, where f = P) to the actual state P:

$$\ln \frac{f}{P} = \int_0^P \left( \frac{V}{RT} - \frac{1}{P} \right) dP$$

**Path B — From residual Gibbs energy (G^R):**
Since G_m^R = G_m(real) − G_m(ideal) at same T,P:

$$\ln \phi = \frac{G_m^{R}}{RT}$$

**Path C — From equations of state (vdW, RK, SRK, PR):**
Substitute V from the EoS into Path A or use G^R expression derived from the EoS.

**Path D — From the Principle of Corresponding States (PCS):**
Read φ directly from the **Newton's generalized fugacity coefficient chart** (using reduced T_r and P_r) or the more accurate **Lee-Kesler tables** (separately for simple fluids and reference fluid n-octane).

> [!TIP]
> For **KTU 2024 board exams**, the most common 7-mark sub-question is: *"Estimate the fugacity of propane at T = 350 K and P = 9 bar using Newton's chart or Lee-Kesler tables."* Always compute T_r = T/T_c and P_r = P/P_c first.

### 2.3 Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Fugacity (Pure Fluid)

The complete differential of ln f for a pure real fluid is:

$$d(\ln f) = \underbrace{\left(\frac{V}{RT}\right) dP}_{\text{pressure effect}} - \underbrace{\frac{H - H^{ig}}{RT^2} dT}_{\text{temperature effect}}$$

Where (H − H^ig) is the **enthalpy departure function**.

**Special case — pure condensed phase (liquid or solid) at constant T:**
Fugacity of a pure liquid or solid equals the fugacity of its saturated vapor at the same T (since at equilibrium f^liquid = f^vapor). Hence:

$$\left( \frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial P} \right)_{T,\text{condensed}} = \frac{V_l}{RT} \approx \text{constant (since V_l is weakly T,P dependent)}$$

The effect of T on condensed-phase fugacity comes from the **Clausius-Clapeyron-type** equation:

$$\frac{d \ln f}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{RT^2} \quad \text{(or } \Delta H_{sub}\text{ for solids)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure effect is dominant for gases** (V is large). **Temperature effect is critical for liquids/solids** (governed by latent heat of vaporization/sublimation).

### 2.4 Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Activity

Starting from a = f / f° at standard state (T, P° = 1 bar for liquids/solids):

$$d(\ln a) = \frac{V}{RT} dP - \frac{H - H^{\circ}}{RT^2} dT$$

For an **ideal solution**, a_i = x_i (Raoult's law basis). For a **real solution**, define activity coefficient γ_i = a_i / x_i.

### 2.5 Gibbs-Duhem Equation (the constraint of partial molar properties)

Since the partial molar property of component i depends on the composition, not all of them can vary independently. The fundamental differential:

$$n \, dM = \sum_i \bar{M}_i \, dn_i + \left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_P dn + \left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial P}\right)_T dn$$

Combining with the total differential of M(T, P, n_1, n_2, ...), at constant T and P, the **Gibbs-Duhem equation** emerges:

$$\sum_i n_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0 \quad \text{at constant T, P}$$

This is the *bookkeeping equation* of solution thermodynamics — if you know the partial molar property of one component, you can compute the other.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Equation** | **Name / Use** | **When Applied** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = f / P$ | Fugacity coefficient | Always (defines non-ideality) |
| 2 | $\ln \phi = \displaystyle\int_0^P \left(\frac{V}{RT} - \frac{1}{P}\right) dP$ | PVT path | When V(P,T) data is given |
| 3 | $\ln \phi = G_m^R / RT$ | Residual property | When G^R from EoS is known |
| 4 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial P}\right)_T = \dfrac{V}{RT}$ | Pressure derivative | Pure fluids |
| 5 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln f}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\dfrac{(H - H^{ig})}{RT^2}$ | Temperature derivative | Pure real fluids |
| 6 | $a = f / f^{\circ}$ | Activity | Pure or mixture |
| 7 | $d(\ln a) = \dfrac{V}{RT} dP - \dfrac{(H - H^{\circ})}{RT^2} dT$ | Activity derivatives | Pure condensed phases |
| 8 | $\dfrac{d \ln f^{sat}}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{RT^2}$ | Clausius-Clapeyron form | Liquid/solid fugacity vs T |
| 9 | $\bar{M}_i = \left(\dfrac{\partial(nM)}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T,P,n_{j\neq i}}$ | Partial molar property | Mixtures |
| 10 | $\mu_i = \bar{G}_i = \left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T,P,n_{j\neq i}}$ | Chemical potential | All multi-component equilibria |
| 11 | $\sum_i n_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0$ (T, P const) | Gibbs-Duhem | Mixture consistency check |
| 12 | $\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln a_i$ | Practical μ | Real fluid / solution |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refrigeration & LNG plants**: Fugacity of methane/ethane/CO₂ at cryogenic conditions dictates phase boundaries and compressor work.
- **Distillation column design**: VLE calculations require fugacity equality μ_i^L = μ_i^V; activity coefficients in the liquid phase (γ-φ approach) are critical.
- **Chemical reactors**: Chemical potential gradients drive reaction direction (Le Chatelier's principle, formalized).
- **Polymer & electrolyte solutions**: Partial molar volumes give information on solute-solvent interactions.
- **Geothermal/petroleum engineering**: Fugacity of water and CO₂ at high T,P determines mineral deposition and reservoir behavior.
