topics.title: Ideal Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ideal Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Ideal Solution

An **ideal solution** is a solution that obeys **Raoult's Law** over the entire composition range at a given temperature, and for which the **partial molar volume** of each component is equal to the molar volume of the pure component. In thermodynamics terms, the intermolecular forces between unlike molecules (A–B) are equal to those between like molecules (A–A and B–B), so no net enthalpy or volume change occurs upon mixing.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Board Definition (Smith, Van Ness & Abbott, Smith & Van Ness Framework):**  
> *An ideal solution is defined as a solution for which the fugacity of each component is proportional to its mole fraction over the entire composition range:*
> $$\hat{f}_i = x_i f_i$$
> *where $f_i$ is the pure-component fugacity at the same T and P.*

---

## 1.2 Conditions for Ideality (The "Why" Behind the "What")

A solution approaches ideal behavior when the following three conditions are simultaneously satisfied:

| Condition | Physical Interpretation | Effect on Mixing |
|---|---|---|
| **Equal Intermolecular Forces** | A–A, B–B, and A–B forces are identical | $\Delta H_{mix} = 0$ |
| **Equal Molecular Sizes** | Pure component molar volumes are nearly equal | $\Delta V_{mix} = 0$ |
| **No Specific Interactions** | No hydrogen bonding, no complex formation, no dissociation | Random mixing at molecular level |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Mixing of Similar Balls" Analogy

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you have a bag containing **red rubber balls** (Species A) and **white rubber balls** (Species B), where every ball is **identical in size, weight, and surface texture**.  
>  
> When you shake the bag and mix them:  
> 1. The **total volume of the bag** does **not change** ($\Delta V_{mix} = 0$).  
> 2. The **temperature of the bag** does **not change**, meaning **no heat is absorbed or released** ($\Delta H_{mix} = 0$).  
> 3. The mixing process is driven **purely by entropy** (randomness increases).  
>  
> This is the **ideal solution** — a benchmark where molecular interactions are perfectly symmetric. Real solutions (e.g., ethanol + water) deviate because A–B interactions differ from A–A and B–B interactions (analogous to mixing rubber balls with steel balls).

---

## 1.4 Practical Examples

**Near-Ideal Systems (KTU High-Yield Examples):**
- **Benzene + Toluene**
- **n-Hexane + n-Heptane**
- **Methanol + Ethanol**
- **Dichloromethane + Chloroform**

**Highly Non-Ideal Systems (for contrast):**
- Acetone + Water (hydrogen bonding)
- Ethanol + Water (strong H-bonding)
- Acetone + Chloroform (negative deviation)

The standard metrics used throughout this module are:
- **Standard Pressure:** $P^{\circ} = 1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Composition Variable:** $x_i$ (mole fraction, $0 \le x_i \le 1$, $\sum x_i = 1$)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Thermodynamic Framework

### 2.1.1 Chemical Potential in an Ideal Solution

The **chemical potential** $\mu_i$ of component $i$ in an ideal solution is the cornerstone of the entire module. Starting from the defining relation for an ideal solution $\hat{f}_i = x_i f_i$, we substitute into the general fugacity-chemical potential relationship:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln \frac{\hat{f}_i}{f_i^{\circ}}$$

For an ideal solution at constant T and P, this reduces to:

$$\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln x_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** This single equation — $\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln x_i$ — is the **defining thermodynamic equation** of an ideal solution. Every property-change-on-mixing equation in this module is derived from it.

---

### 2.1.2 Partial Molar Properties in Ideal Solutions

The **partial molar property** $\bar{M}_i$ of component $i$ in an ideal solution relates to the **pure component property** $M_i$ by:

$$\bar{M}_i^{id} = M_i + RT \ln x_i \quad \text{(property basis)}$$

For specific properties:

$$\bar{V}_i^{id} = V_i \quad ; \quad \bar{H}_i^{id} = H_i \quad ; \quad \bar{S}_i^{id} = S_i - R \ln x_i \quad ; \quad \bar{G}_i^{id} = G_i + RT \ln x_i$$

---

## 2.2 Property Changes on Mixing (The "Delta" Family)

When two or more pure liquids are mixed to form an ideal solution, the **change** in any extensive property is calculated from:

$$\Delta M_{mix} = M_{solution} - \sum x_i M_i$$

Using the partial molar property framework and the defining equation of an ideal solution, the four fundamental mixing properties are:

| Property | Symbol | Ideal-Solution Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volume change of mixing | $\Delta V_{mix}$ | $\boldsymbol{0}$ | No expansion/contraction |
| Enthalpy change of mixing | $\Delta H_{mix}$ | $\boldsymbol{0}$ | No heat effect |
| Entropy change of mixing | $\Delta S_{mix}$ | $-R \sum x_i \ln x_i$ | Increases ($\ge 0$) |
| Gibbs energy change of mixing | $\Delta G_{mix}$ | $RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$ | Decreases ($\le 0$) |

---

## 2.3 Raoult's Law — The Empirical Foundation

> [!NOTE]  
> **Raoult's Law (1887):** *The partial pressure of component $i$ in the vapor phase is equal to its mole fraction in the liquid phase multiplied by the pure-component vapor pressure at the same temperature.*
> $$p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$$

For a binary ideal solution, the **total pressure** is:

$$P = x_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 P_2^{sat} = P_2^{sat} + (P_1^{sat} - P_2^{sat}) x_1$$

Combined with Dalton's law for the vapor phase:

$$y_i = \frac{p_i}{P} = \frac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{P}$$

---

## 2.4 Henry's Law — The Dilute Limit Counterpart

While Raoult's law applies to the **solvent** (major component), **Henry's Law** applies to the **solute** (minor component) in a dilute solution:

$$p_i = x_i \mathcal{H}_i \quad \text{or} \quad \hat{f}_i = x_i \mathcal{H}_i$$

where $\mathcal{H}_i$ is the **Henry's law constant** (with units of pressure). Note that $\mathcal{H}_i \ne P_i^{sat}$ for the same species; it is a fitted constant that depends on the specific solvent.

---

## 2.5 Activity, Activity Coefficient, and the Ideal-Solution Reference

To extend ideal-solution thermodynamics to **non-ideal** systems, we define the **activity** $a_i$ and **activity coefficient** $\gamma_i$:

$$\hat{f}_i = x_i \gamma_i f_i = a_i f_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_i = x_i \gamma_i$$

| Reference State | Activity Expression | Range of Validity |
|---|---|---|
| **Lewis-Randall (Raoult's law ref.)** | $a_i = x_i \gamma_i$, $\gamma_i \to 1$ as $x_i \to 1$ | Solvent-rich region |
| **Henry's Law Reference** | $a_i = \frac{x_i \mathcal{H}_i}{f_i}$, $\gamma_i^* \to 1$ as $x_i \to 0$ | Solute-dilute region |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Pitfall:** An ideal solution has **$\gamma_i = 1$ for ALL compositions**. A solution can be ideal in one reference frame and non-ideal in another — always specify the reference state in KTU answers.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

$$\boxed{\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln x_i}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{mix}^{id} = 0} \quad \boxed{\Delta H_{mix}^{id} = 0}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{mix}^{id} = -R \sum_i x_i \ln x_i}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G_{mix}^{id} = RT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i}$$

$$\boxed{p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)} \quad \text{(Raoult's Law)}$$

$$\boxed{y_i = \frac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{P} = \frac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{x_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 P_2^{sat}}}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{f}_i^{id} = x_i f_i} \quad ; \quad \boxed{a_i^{id} = x_i} \quad ; \quad \boxed{\gamma_i^{id} = 1}$$

$$\boxed{P = \sum_i x_i P_i^{sat}(T)} \quad \text{(Total pressure, ideal)}$$

$$\boxed{K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P}}$$

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Ideal-Solution Thermodynamics |
|---|---|
| **Distillation Column Design** | Raoult's law gives VLE for preliminary design of benzene-toluene, hexane-heptane, etc. |
| **Petroleum Refining** | Hydrocarbon mixtures closely obey Raoult's law; used to compute bubble/dew points |
| **Cryogenic Air Separation** | N₂ + O₂ + Ar ideal-solution VLE used in liquefaction cycles |
| **Solvent Selection** | Henry's law constants dictate gas solubility in absorption columns |
| **Polymer-Solvent Systems** | Flory-Huggins theory extends ideal-solution concepts to polymer solutions |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Property Changes on Mixing for an Ideal Solution

### 3.1.1 General Framework

The change in any extensive molar property $M$ upon mixing is given by:

$$\Delta M_{mix} = M - \sum_i x_i M_i$$

where $M$ is the solution property and $M_i$ is the pure-component property. Using the **summability relation**:

$$M = \sum_i x_i \bar{M}_i$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta M_{mix} = \sum_i x_i \bar{M}_i - \sum_i x_i M_i = \sum_i x_i \left(\bar{M}_i - M_i\right)$$

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of $\Delta V_{mix}^{id} = 0$

The partial molar volume in an ideal solution equals the pure-component molar volume:

$$\bar{V}_i^{id} = V_i \quad \text{(condition of ideal solution)}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta V_{mix}^{id} = \sum_i x_i \left(\bar{V}_i^{id} - V_i\right) = \sum_i x_i \left(V_i - V_i\right) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{mix}^{id} = 0}$$

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of $\Delta H_{mix}^{id} = 0$

The partial molar enthalpy in an ideal solution is derived from the chemical potential via the Gibbs-Helmholtz relation. Alternatively, for an ideal solution, the partial molar enthalpy equals the pure-component molar enthalpy:

$$\bar{H}_i^{id} = H_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\Delta H_{mix}^{id} = 0}$$

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation of $\Delta S_{mix}^{id} = -R \sum x_i \ln x_i$

Start from the chemical potential:

$$\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln x_i$$

Using the relation $\mu_i = \bar{G}_i = \bar{H}_i - T \bar{S}_i$, the partial molar entropy in an ideal solution is:

$$\bar{S}_i^{id} = -\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i^{id}}{\partial T}\right)_{P,x} = -\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i^{\circ}}{\partial T}\right)_P - R \ln x_i = S_i - R \ln x_i$$

Now compute the change:

$$\Delta S_{mix}^{id} = \sum_i x_i \left(\bar{S}_i^{id} - S_i\right) = \sum_i x_i \left(S_i - R \ln x_i - S_i\right) = -R \sum_i x_i \ln x_i$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{mix}^{id} = -R \sum_i x_i \ln x_i}$$

**Sign check:** Since $0 < x_i < 1$, $\ln x_i < 0$, so $\Delta S_{mix} > 0$ — consistent with the second law.

---

### 3.1.5 Derivation of $\Delta G_{mix}^{id} = RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$

Using $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$:

$$\Delta G_{mix}^{id} = \Delta H_{mix}^{id} - T \Delta S_{mix}^{id} = 0 - T \left(-R \sum_i x_i \ln x_i\right) = RT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G_{mix}^{id} = RT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i}$$

**Sign check:** Since $\ln x_i < 0$, we have $\Delta G_{mix} < 0$ — confirming that mixing is **spontaneous** for ideal solutions at constant T and P.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Raoult's Law from Phase Equilibrium

Consider component $i$ distributed between liquid (L) and vapor (V) phases. At equilibrium:

$$\hat{f}_i^L = \hat{f}_i^V$$

For the liquid phase (ideal solution):

$$\hat{f}_i^L = x_i f_i^L$$

For the vapor phase (assuming ideal gas behavior, $\hat{\phi}_i^V = 1$):

$$\hat{f}_i^V = y_i P$$

Equating:

$$x_i f_i^L = y_i P$$

For the pure component liquid at saturation:

$$f_i^{sat} = P_i^{sat}$$

At low to moderate pressures, $f_i^L \approx f_i^{sat} = P_i^{sat}$ (the Poynting correction is negligible). Therefore:

$$x_i P_i^{sat} = y_i P$$

$$\boxed{P = \sum_i x_i P_i^{sat} \quad ; \quad y_i = \frac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{P}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem: VLE for Benzene(1) + Toluene(2)

> **Given:** Benzene-toluene system at **T = 105 °C = 378.15 K**. Antoine constants:  
> For benzene: $\ln P_1^{sat} = A_1 - \frac{B_1}{T + C_1}$, with values that give $P_1^{sat}(378.15) = 1.575\ \text{bar}$.  
> For toluene: $P_2^{sat}(378.15) = 0.630\ \text{bar}$.  
> Compute bubble pressure, vapor composition, and K-values for $x_1 = 0.4$.

### Step 1: Total Pressure (Raoult's Law)

$$P = x_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 P_2^{sat}$$

$$P = (0.4)(1.575) + (0.6)(0.630)$$

$$P = 0.630 + 0.378 = 1.008\ \text{bar}$$

### Step 2: Vapor Phase Composition (Dalton's Law)

$$y_1 = \frac{x_1 P_1^{sat}}{P} = \frac{(0.4)(1.575)}{1.008} = 0.625$$

$$y_2 = 1 - y_1 = 0.375$$

### Step 3: K-Values

$$K_1 = \frac{y_1}{x_1} = \frac{0.625}{0.4} = 1.5625$$

$$K_2 = \frac{y_2}{x_2} = \frac{0.375}{0.6} = 0.6250$$

### Step 4: Verify Raoult's Law Consistency

$$K_1 \cdot K_2 = (1.5625)(0.6250) = 0.977 \approx 1.0 \quad \text{(within rounding)}$$

For an ideal binary solution at the same T and P, $K_1 K_2 = \frac{P_1^{sat} P_2^{sat}}{P^2} = \frac{(1.575)(0.630)}{(1.008)^2} = 0.977$. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Gibbs-Duhem Equation (Verifying Internal Consistency)

The **Gibbs-Duhem equation** at constant T and P is:

$$\sum_i x_i\, d\bar{M}_i = 0$$

For an ideal solution with $\bar{G}_i^{id} = G_i + RT \ln x_i$:

$$\sum_i x_i\, d(\bar{G}_i^{id}) = \sum_i x_i\, d(G_i) + RT \sum_i x_i\, d(\ln x_i) = 0 + 0 = 0$$

This confirms **internal thermodynamic consistency** of the ideal-solution model.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Pure Components to Ideal Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pure Component A at T, P] --> C[Mixing Process]
    B[Pure Component B at T, P] --> C
    C --> D{Is the Solution Ideal?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Ideal Solution Behavior]
    D -- No --> F[Non-Ideal Solution: Use Activity Coefficients]
    E --> G1[Raoult's Law Holds Entire Range]
    E --> G2[Delta V mix equals 0]
    E --> G3[Delta H mix equals 0]
    E --> G4[Delta S mix equals negative R times sum xi ln xi]
    E --> G5[Delta G mix equals RT times sum xi ln xi]
    F --> H1[Modified Raoult's Law]
    F --> H2[Margules or van Laar or Wilson or NRTL Model]
    H1 --> I[Use gamma i from VLE data]
    H2 --> I
```

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Thermodynamic Property Chain for Ideal Solutions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Pure Component Property Mi] --> PM[Partial Molar Property bar Mi equals Mi plus RT ln xi]
    PM --> SM[Summability: M equals sum xi bar Mi]
    SM --> DM[Delta M mix equals sum xi times bar Mi minus Mi]
    DM --> DV[Delta V mix equals 0]
    DM --> DH[Delta H mix equals 0]
    DM --> DS[Delta S mix equals negative R sum xi ln xi]
    DM --> DG[Delta G mix equals RT sum xi ln xi]
    DG --> VLE[VLE Calculation using Raoult's Law]
    VLE --> BP[Bubble Point: P equals sum xi Pisat]
    VLE --> DP[Dew Point: P equals 1 over sum yi divided by Pisat]
    VLE --> KC[K-Value: Ki equals yi divided by xi equals Pisat divided by P]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Binary P-x-y Diagram at Constant T (Mimic of Phase Envelope)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PhaseDiagram[Constant T VLE for Ideal Binary]
        A0[Pure B: x1 equals 0, P equals P2sat] --> B0[Linear P-x Line: P equals P2sat plus x1 times P1sat minus P2sat]
        B0 --> C0[Pure A: x1 equals 1, P equals P1sat]
        C0 --> D0[Vapor Line Lies BELOW Liquid Line for P1sat greater than P2sat]
        D0 --> A0
    end
    note1[Higher P1sat: Component 1 is the MORE VOLATILE Component]
    note2[y1 greater than x1 at all compositions for Component 1]
    note3[Raoult's Law makes the P-x-y diagram a perfect smooth curve]
    PhaseDiagram --> note1
    PhaseDiagram --> note2
    PhaseDiagram --> note3
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Raoult's vs. Henry's Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Determine Component i Fugacity] --> Q{Is Component i the SOLVENT or SOLUTE?}
    Q -- Solvent major x near 1 --> RL[Apply Raoult's Law: pi equals xi times Pisat]
    Q -- Solute minor x near 0 --> HL[Apply Henry's Law: pi equals xi times Hi]
    RL --> RID[Ideal Reference: gamma i equals 1, ai equals xi]
    HL --> HLR[Henry Reference: gamma i-star equals 1 at xi equals 0]
    RID --> USE1[Use in VLE for distillation design]
    HLR --> USE2[Use in gas absorption and solubility calculations]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]  
**State and explain Raoult's Law. Mention two systems that obey Raoult's Law.**

**Model Answer:**

> Raoult's Law states that the partial pressure of a component in a liquid solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid phase, with the proportionality constant being the pure-component vapor pressure at the same temperature.
> $$p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$$
> **Systems that obey Raoult's Law:** Benzene-Toluene, n-Hexane-n-Heptane, Methanol-Ethanol.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]  
**Define an ideal solution. What are the conditions for a solution to be ideal?**

**Model Answer:**

> An ideal solution is one in which the fugacity of each component is proportional to its mole fraction over the entire composition range: $\hat{f}_i = x_i f_i$.  
> **Conditions:**  
> (i) A–A, B–B, and A–B intermolecular forces are equal → $\Delta H_{mix} = 0$  
> (ii) Molar volumes of pure components are equal → $\Delta V_{mix} = 0$  
> (iii) No specific chemical interactions such as H-bonding, association, or dissociation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Derive expressions for the change in Gibbs energy, enthalpy, and entropy of mixing for an ideal solution. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid mixture of benzene(1) and toluene(2) at 378.15 K has $x_1 = 0.3$. Given $P_1^{sat} = 1.575\ \text{bar}$ and $P_2^{sat} = 0.630\ \text{bar}$, calculate the bubble pressure and the vapor-phase composition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Starting Point:** Use the defining chemical potential relation for an ideal solution:

$$\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln x_i$$

**Step 2 — Partial Molar Gibbs Energy:**

$$\bar{G}_i^{id} = G_i + RT \ln x_i$$

**Step 3 — Change of Mixing (General):**

$$\Delta G_{mix} = \sum_i x_i \left(\bar{G}_i^{id} - G_i\right) = RT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i$$

**Step 4 — Partial Molar Enthalpy (from $\bar{H}_i = -T^2 \frac{\partial(\bar{G}_i/T)}{\partial T}$):** For an ideal solution, the T-derivative of the $\ln x_i$ term vanishes (since $x_i$ is T-independent at constant composition), so $\bar{H}_i^{id} = H_i$:

$$\Delta H_{mix}^{id} = \sum_i x_i \left(\bar{H}_i^{id} - H_i\right) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Entropy from $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$:**

$$\Delta S_{mix}^{id} = \frac{\Delta H_{mix} - \Delta G_{mix}}{T} = \frac{0 - RT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i}{T} = -R \sum_i x_i \ln x_i$$

**Assumptions:** (i) Solution obeys Raoult's law, (ii) Temperature and pressure are constant, (iii) Vapor phase behaves as an ideal gas.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Given:** $x_1 = 0.3$, $x_2 = 0.7$, $T = 378.15$ K, $P_1^{sat} = 1.575$ bar, $P_2^{sat} = 0.630$ bar.

**Step 1 — Bubble Pressure (Raoult's Law):**

$$P = x_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 P_2^{sat}$$

$$P = (0.3)(1.575) + (0.7)(0.630)$$

$$P = 0.4725 + 0.4410 = 0.9135\ \text{bar}$$

**Step 2 — Vapor Phase Composition:**

$$y_1 = \frac{x_1 P_1^{sat}}{P} = \frac{(0.3)(1.575)}{0.9135} = 0.5172$$

$$y_2 = 1 - y_1 = 0.4828$$

**Step 3 — K-Values:**

$$K_1 = \frac{y_1}{x_1} = \frac{0.5172}{0.3} = 1.724 \quad ; \quad K_2 = \frac{y_2}{x_2} = \frac{0.4828}{0.7} = 0.690$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Define chemical potential. Derive the expression for the chemical potential of a component in an ideal solution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two liters of pure ethanol (density 789 kg/m³, M = 46 g/mol) is mixed with one liter of pure water (density 1000 kg/m³, M = 18 g/mol) at 298 K. Assuming ideal solution behavior, calculate $\Delta G_{mix}$, $\Delta S_{mix}$, and $\Delta H_{mix}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition:** Chemical potential $\mu_i$ is the partial molar Gibbs energy, representing the change in total Gibbs energy when one mole of component $i$ is added at constant T, P, and $n_{j \ne i}$:

$$\mu_i = \left(\frac{\partial (nG)}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T,P,n_{j\ne i}} = \bar{G}_i$$

**Step 2 — General Relation:** For a component in any phase:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln \frac{\hat{f}_i}{f_i^{\circ}}$$

**Step 3 — For an Ideal Solution:** Substitute $\hat{f}_i^{id} = x_i f_i$ (where $f_i$ is the pure-component fugacity at the same T and P):

$$\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln \frac{x_i f_i}{f_i^{\circ}}$$

If the reference state is the pure component ($f_i^{\circ} = f_i$):

$$\boxed{\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln x_i}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Moles of Each Component:**

$$n_{\text{ethanol}} = \frac{\rho V}{M} = \frac{(789\ \text{kg/m}^3)(2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3)}{0.046\ \text{kg/mol}} = \frac{1.578}{0.046} = 34.30\ \text{mol}$$

$$n_{\text{water}} = \frac{(1000)(1 \times 10^{-3})}{0.018} = \frac{1.000}{0.018} = 55.56\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Total Moles and Mole Fractions:**

$$n_{total} = 34.30 + 55.56 = 89.86\ \text{mol}$$

$$x_{\text{ethanol}} = \frac{34.30}{89.86} = 0.3818 \quad ; \quad x_{\text{water}} = 0.6182$$

**Step 3 — Property Changes (per mole of mixture):**

$$\Delta H_{mix}^{id} = 0\ \text{J/mol (by definition)}$$

$$\Delta S_{mix}^{id} = -R (x_1 \ln x_1 + x_2 \ln x_2)$$

$$= -8.314 \cdot \big[(0.3818)(\ln 0.3818) + (0.6182)(\ln 0.6182)\big]$$

$$= -8.314 \cdot \big[(0.3818)(-0.9625) + (0.6182)(-0.4808)\big]$$

$$= -8.314 \cdot \big[-0.3674 - 0.2972\big] = -8.314 \cdot (-0.6646)$$

$$\Delta S_{mix}^{id} = +5.525\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 4 — Gibbs Energy of Mixing:**

$$\Delta G_{mix}^{id} = RT \sum x_i \ln x_i = (8.314)(298)(-0.6646)$$

$$\Delta G_{mix}^{id} = -1647\ \text{J/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Total (Extensive) Values:**

$$\Delta G_{mix,total} = (-1647)(89.86) = -1.48 \times 10^5\ \text{J} = -148\ \text{kJ}$$

$$\Delta S_{mix,total} = (5.525)(89.86) = 496.5\ \text{J/K}$$

$$\Delta H_{mix,total} = 0\ \text{J}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Board Exams:**  
> 1. **Sign Convention:** Many students write $\Delta S_{mix} = R \sum x_i \ln x_i$ (positive sign) and $\Delta G_{mix} = -RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$ (negative sign). The **correct** KTU-accepted convention is: $\Delta S_{mix} = \boldsymbol{-}R \sum x_i \ln x_i$ (because $\ln x_i < 0$, the result is positive) and $\Delta G_{mix} = \boldsymbol{+}RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$ (negative, because $\ln x_i < 0$).  
> 2. **Raoult's vs. Modified Raoult's Law:** If a problem says "ideal solution," always use **Raoult's Law directly** — do not introduce activity coefficients.  
> 3. **Units of Henry's Constant:** $\mathcal{H}_i$ has units of **pressure (Pa or bar)**, not dimensionless. Forgetting units loses 1 mark.  
> 4. **Benzene-Toluene VLE:** Many students forget the Antoine equation and use approximate vapor pressures. KTU expects **at least 3 significant figures** in $P_i^{sat}$.  
> 5. **Mole Fraction Sum:** Always verify $\sum x_i = 1$ and $\sum y_i = 1$ before writing the final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Defining Equation:** $\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln x_i$ is the master equation of an ideal solution.
- **Fugacity Relation:** $\hat{f}_i^{id} = x_i f_i$ (linear in composition).
- **Activity:** $a_i^{id} = x_i$ and activity coefficient $\gamma_i^{id} = 1$ for all $x_i$.
- **Volume & Enthalpy:** $\Delta V_{mix}^{id} = 0$ and $\Delta H_{mix}^{id} = 0$ — these are the two **thermodynamic fingerprints** of ideality.
- **Entropy & Gibbs Energy:** $\Delta S_{mix}^{id} = -R \sum_i x_i \ln x_i > 0$ and $\Delta G_{mix}^{id} = RT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i < 0$.
- **Raoult's Law:** $p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ — linear partial pressure behavior.
- **Total Pressure:** $P = \sum_i x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ — bubble-point pressure for ideal solutions.
- **Vapor Composition:** $y_i = \dfrac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{P} = \dfrac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{\sum_j x_j P_j^{sat}}$.
- **K-Value:** $K_i = \dfrac{y_i}{x_i} = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P}$ for an ideal solution.
- **Henry's Law:** $p_i = x_i \mathcal{H}_i$ — applies to solutes at infinite dilution; complementary to Raoult's law.
- **Reference States:** Lewis-Randall (Raoult's law) for solvents; Henry's law reference for solutes at infinite dilution.
- **Examples:** Benzene-Toluene, n-Hexane-n-Heptane, Methanol-Ethanol are the **KTU-favorite** near-ideal systems.
- **Non-Ideal Counterparts:** Acetone-Water (positive deviation), Acetone-Chloroform (negative deviation) — for these, $\gamma_i \ne 1$.
- **Gibbs-Duhem Check:** An ideal solution automatically satisfies the Gibbs-Duhem equation: $\sum x_i d\bar{M}_i = 0$.
- **Engineering Use:** Ideal-solution VLE drives the design of distillation columns for hydrocarbon separations.
- **Key Examination Skill:** Always state the **assumptions** (Raoult's law valid, ideal gas vapor) before starting derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lewis-Randall Rule and Raoult's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lewis-Randall Rule and Raoult's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Lewis-Randall Rule (Standard State Convention)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Lewis-Randall Rule** states that the **fugacity of a component in a mixture is defined as the product of its mole fraction in the mixture and the pure component fugacity at the same temperature and pressure of the mixture.**
> Mathematically: $\hat{f}_i = x_i f_i$

where:
- $\hat{f}_i$ = fugacity of component $i$ **in the mixture**
- $x_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ in the liquid mixture
- $f_i$ = fugacity of **pure** component $i$ at the same $T$ and $P$ as the mixture

> [!NOTE]
> The Lewis-Randall Rule serves as the **standard state for component fugacity** in non-ideal solution thermodynamics. It assumes each component in the mixture behaves as if it were diluted into a "hypothetical pure component sea" at the mixture's $T$ and $P$.

### 1.2 Raoult's Law (Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Raoult's Law** states that the **partial pressure of a component in the vapor phase is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid phase**, with the proportionality constant being the vapor pressure of the pure component at the same temperature.
> Mathematically: $P_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$

where:
- $P_i$ = partial pressure of component $i$ in the vapor phase
- $x_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ in the liquid phase
- $P_i^{sat}$ = saturated (pure component) vapor pressure of $i$ at temperature $T$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Cup" Intuition

Imagine a **coffee cup containing water and sugar (solutes)**:

- **Lewis-Randall Analogy**: Think of each molecule in a liquid mixture as a "person at a party." The Lewis-Randall rule imagines that each component "feels" the temperature and pressure of the **whole crowd (pure component standard state)**, but is present only in proportion to its mole fraction. It is like saying "the loudness contribution of one person is their share of the group in a quiet room" — a baseline reference.
- **Raoult's Law Analogy**: Raoult's law is like saying "the number of molecules escaping from a liquid surface (vapor pressure) is simply proportional to how many of those molecules are sitting at the surface." If half the molecules at the surface are water (mole fraction = 0.5), then half the pure-water vapor pressure escapes into the vapor phase.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Units (SI) |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| $T$ | Absolute Temperature | **K (Kelvin)** |
| $P$ | Total System Pressure | **Pa (Pascals)** or **bar** |
| $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| $x_i$ | Mole fraction (liquid) | **dimensionless** |
| $y_i$ | Mole fraction (vapor) | **dimensionless** |
| $\hat{f}_i$ | Component fugacity in mixture | **Pa** or **bar** |
| $f_i$ | Pure component fugacity | **Pa** or **bar** |
| $\hat{\phi}_i$ | Fugacity coefficient (mixture) | **dimensionless** |
| $\gamma_i$ | Activity coefficient | **dimensionless** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Henry's Law vs. Raoult's Law Distinction (frequently confused in exams!):**
> - **Raoult's Law** applies to the **solvent** (the major component, $x_i \rightarrow 1$).
> - **Henry's Law** applies to the **solute** (the minor component, $x_i \rightarrow 0$).
> - **Lewis-Randall Rule** is the **reference standard state** used for Raoult's Law side.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — Why Do We Need These Rules?

In real (non-ideal) mixtures, intermolecular forces between unlike molecules differ from those between like molecules. This creates deviations from ideal behavior. Chemical engineers need:

1. A **reference standard state** (Lewis-Randall Rule) to define "what fugacity would be if the mixture were ideal-like at this T and P."
2. A **correction factor** (activity coefficient $\gamma_i$ or fugacity coefficient $\hat{\phi}_i$) to quantify how far reality deviates from this ideal reference.
3. A **practical VLE relationship** (Raoult's Law, or its modified form) to compute vapor-liquid equilibria — essential for distillation column design.

### 2.2 The Lewis-Randall Rule — Detailed Logic Steps

- **Step 1**: For an **ideal solution**, the fugacity of component $i$ in the liquid mixture is given by:
  $$\hat{f}_i^{id} = x_i f_i$$
  This is the **Lewis-Randall Rule**.

- **Step 2**: For **real (non-ideal) solutions**, we introduce the **activity coefficient** $\gamma_i$:
  $$\hat{f}_i = x_i \gamma_i f_i$$

- **Step 3**: The **rational activity coefficient** is defined as:
  $$\gamma_i \equiv \frac{\hat{f}_i}{x_i f_i}$$

- **Step 4**: As $x_i \rightarrow 1$, $\gamma_i \rightarrow 1$ (the pure component is the reference).

### 2.3 Raoult's Law — Detailed Logic Steps

- **Step 1**: At vapor-liquid equilibrium, the **fugacity of component $i$ in the liquid equals its fugacity in the vapor**:
  $$\hat{f}_i^L = \hat{f}_i^V$$

- **Step 2**: For the vapor phase, using the Lewis-Randall standard state:
  $$\hat{f}_i^V = y_i \hat{\phi}_i P$$

- **Step 3**: For the liquid phase (assuming Raoult's Law behavior):
  $$\hat{f}_i^L = x_i P_i^{sat}$$

- **Step 4**: Equating both sides gives the **Raoult's Law VLE expression**:
  $$y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$$

- **Step 5**: For **modified Raoult's Law** (with activity coefficient):
  $$y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Formula | When to Use | Key Assumption |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Lewis-Randall Rule | $\hat{f}_i^{id} = x_i f_i$ | Ideal solution reference | Standard state at mixture T, P |
| Real Solution Fugacity | $\hat{f}_i = x_i \gamma_i f_i$ | Non-ideal liquid mixture | Activity coefficient correction |
| Activity Coefficient Definition | $\gamma_i = \dfrac{\hat{f}_i}{x_i f_i}$ | Any solution | Reference: pure component |
| Pure Component Fugacity | $f_i = \phi_i P_i^{sat}$ | Pure component at saturation | Vapor as ideal gas: $f_i = P_i^{sat}$ |
| Raoult's Law (Vapor) | $P_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$ | Ideal solution VLE | Solvent-rich region |
| Raoult's Law (VLE form) | $y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$ | Ideal solution VLE | Equilibrium condition |
| Modified Raoult's Law | $y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$ | Non-ideal VLE | Activity coefficient model needed |
| Total Pressure (Raoult) | $P = \sum_{i} x_i P_i^{sat}$ | Binary/ternary ideal systems | Dalton's law applied |
| Bubble Point Equation | $\sum_i x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat} = P$ | Bubble T calculation | Specified P, find T |
| Dew Point Equation | $\sum_i \dfrac{y_i P}{P_i^{sat}} = 1$ | Dew T calculation | Specified P, find T |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Memorize for KTU:**
> - $\lim_{x_i \to 1} \gamma_i = 1$ (pure component reference)
> - $\lim_{x_i \to 0} \gamma_i = \gamma_i^{\infty}$ (infinite dilution)
> - At low pressures, $\phi_i \approx 1$, so $f_i = P_i^{sat}$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation Column Design**: Raoult's law provides the equilibrium curve ($y$ vs. $x$) for binary systems — the foundation of McCabe-Thiele analysis.
- **Evaporator Design**: Total vapor pressure predictions from Raoult's law help size condensers and reboilers.
- **Petroleum Refining**: Hydrocarbon mixtures often follow Raoult's law closely due to similar molecular sizes and forces.
- **Azeotrope Detection**: Deviations from Raoult's law ($\gamma_i \neq 1$) signal potential azeotrope formation — critical for separation feasibility.
- **Refrigeration**: Low-pressure refrigerant mixtures use Raoult's law as a starting point.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis-Randall Rule from Fundamental Thermodynamics

**Starting Point:** For an ideal solution, the chemical potential of component $i$ is given by:
$$\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^* + RT \ln x_i$$

where $\mu_i^*$ is the chemical potential of pure component $i$ at the system $T$ and $P$.

**Step 1:** Convert chemical potential to fugacity using the defining relation:
$$\mu_i - \mu_i^{ref} = RT \ln \frac{\hat{f}_i}{f_i^{ref}}$$

**Step 2:** Apply this to both the mixture and the pure component, choosing the pure component at the same $T, P$ as the reference:
$$\mu_i^{id} - \mu_i^* = RT \ln \frac{\hat{f}_i^{id}}{f_i}$$

**Step 3:** From Step 1 of the original chemical potential equation:
$$RT \ln x_i = RT \ln \frac{\hat{f}_i^{id}}{f_i}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $RT$ on both sides:
$$\ln x_i = \ln \frac{\hat{f}_i^{id}}{f_i}$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides:
$$x_i = \frac{\hat{f}_i^{id}}{f_i}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to obtain the **Lewis-Randall Rule**:
$$\hat{f}_i^{id} = x_i f_i \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Raoult's Law from Phase Equilibrium

**Starting Point:** At vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE), the **fugacities of component $i$ in both phases are equal**:
$$\hat{f}_i^V = \hat{f}_i^L$$

**Step 1 — Vapor Phase Fugacity:** Assuming the vapor behaves as an ideal gas (low to moderate pressure):
$$\hat{f}_i^V = y_i P$$

This follows from the definition $f_i^V = \phi_i P$ with $\phi_i = 1$ for ideal gas.

**Step 2 — Liquid Phase Fugacity (Lewis-Randall + Pure Component):** 
The fugacity of pure component $i$ as a liquid at temperature $T$ equals its saturation pressure (pure component VLE):
$$f_i^L = P_i^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Lewis-Randall to the liquid mixture:**
$$\hat{f}_i^L = x_i f_i^L = x_i P_i^{sat}$$

This step uses Lewis-Randall because the pure liquid at saturation is our standard state reference.

**Step 4 — Equate Vapor and Liquid Fugacities:**
$$y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Partial Pressure:**
$$P_i = y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$$

This is the famous **Raoult's Law**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation of Modified Raoult's Law (Non-Ideal Case)

**Step 1:** For non-ideal solutions, the Lewis-Randall rule must be corrected with the activity coefficient $\gamma_i$:
$$\hat{f}_i^L = x_i \gamma_i f_i^L$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $f_i^L = P_i^{sat}$:
$$\hat{f}_i^L = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$$

**Step 3:** Equating to vapor fugacity (ideal gas vapor):
$$y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$$

**Step 4:** This is the **Modified Raoult's Law**, valid for non-ideal solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Note:** Always check whether your problem requires **Raoult's Law** ($\gamma_i = 1$) or **Modified Raoult's Law** ($\gamma_i \neq 1$). Marks are commonly lost here.

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — Bubble Pressure of a Benzene-Toluene System

**Problem:** A liquid mixture contains 40 mol% benzene and 60 mol% toluene at $T = 105°C$. Using Raoult's Law, find the total pressure and vapor composition. Given:
- $P_{benzene}^{sat}(105°C) = 1.668$ bar
- $P_{toluene}^{sat}(105°C) = 0.693$ bar

**Step 1 — Apply Raoult's Law for Each Component:**
$$P_{benzene} = x_{benzene} \cdot P_{benzene}^{sat} = 0.40 \times 1.668$$
$$P_{benzene} = 0.6672 \text{ bar}$$

$$P_{toluene} = x_{toluene} \cdot P_{toluene}^{sat} = 0.60 \times 0.693$$
$$P_{toluene} = 0.4158 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dalton's Law for Total Pressure:**
$$P_{total} = P_{benzene} + P_{toluene} = 0.6672 + 0.4158$$
$$P_{total} = 1.083 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Vapor Mole Fractions:**
$$y_{benzene} = \frac{P_{benzene}}{P_{total}} = \frac{0.6672}{1.083}$$
$$y_{benzene} = 0.616$$

$$y_{toluene} = \frac{P_{toluene}}{P_{total}} = \frac{0.4158}{1.083}$$
$$y_{toluene} = 0.384$$

**Verification:** $y_{benzene} + y_{toluene} = 0.616 + 0.384 = 1.000$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Benzene, being more volatile (higher $P^{sat}$), concentrates preferentially in the vapor phase ($y_{benzene} = 0.616 > x_{benzene} = 0.40$). This enrichment principle is the basis of fractional distillation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Hierarchy Diagram — Lewis-Randall, Raoult's, and Activity Coefficient

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FUGACITY OF COMPONENT i IN MIXURE fhat-i"]
    A --> B["Apply Lewis Randall Standard State"]
    B --> C["Standard Reference: Pure Component at T P"]
    C --> D["Divide into two cases"]
    D --> E["IDEAL SOLUTION gamma-i equals 1"]
    D --> F["REAL SOLUTION gamma-i not equal 1"]
    E --> G["Lewis Randall Equation fhat-i equals x-i times f-i"]
    F --> H["Modified Equation fhat-i equals x-i gamma-i f-i"]
    G --> I["Apply VLE Condition"]
    H --> I["Apply VLE Condition"]
    I --> J["Liquid fugacity equals Vapor fugacity"]
    J --> K["IDEAL CASE: Raoult Law yP equals x-i P-i sat"]
    J --> L["REAL CASE: Modified Raoult Law yP equals x-i gamma-i P-i sat"]
    K --> M["Distillation Column Design McCabe Thiele"]
    L --> N["Use models NRTL UNIQUAC Margules"]
    N --> O["Azeotrope Prediction and Separation Feasibility"]
```

### 4.2 VLE Calculation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["INPUT: T, x1, x2, vapor pressure data"]
    P1 --> P2["Calculate Partial Pressures using Raoult: Pi equals xi Pisat"]
    P2 --> P3["Sum partial pressures to get Ptotal"]
    P3 --> P4{"Is pressure realistic?"}
    P4 -- "No" --> P5["Check Antoine equation accuracy"]
    P4 -- "Yes" --> P6["Calculate yi equals Pi divided by Ptotal"]
    P6 --> P7["OUTPUT: Ptotal and vapor composition yi"]
```

### 4.3 Solution Behavior Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["MIXTURE TYPE IDENTIFICATION"]
    S1 --> S2["Are components chemically similar?"]
    S2 -- "Yes Similar Forces" --> S3["IDEAL SOLUTION Raoult Law Valid gamma equals 1"]
    S2 -- "No Different Forces" --> S4["NON IDEAL SOLUTION"]
    S4 --> S5{"Type of deviation?"}
    S5 -- "Both P greater than ideal" --> S6["POSITIVE DEVIATION: gamma greater than 1: Minimum Boiling Azeotrope"]
    S5 -- "Both P less than ideal" --> S7["NEGATIVE DEVIATION: gamma less than 1: Maximum Boiling Azeotrope"]
    S5 -- "Mixed" --> S8["SADDLE POINT: Use Modified Raoult Law with gamma model"]
```

### 4.4 Fugacity Reference State Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["FUGACITY REFERENCE STATES"]
    R1 --> R2["LEWIS RANDALL STANDARD STATE"]
    R1 --> R3["HENRYS LAW REFERENCE STATE"]
    R2 --> R4["Pure component at mixture T and P"]
    R2 --> R5["Used for SOLVENT region x close to 1"]
    R2 --> R6["Equation: fhat-i equals x-i f-i"]
    R3 --> R7["Hypothetical pure component obeying Henry constant"]
    R3 --> R8["Used for SOLUTE region x close to 0"]
    R3 --> R9["Equation: fhat-i equals x-i kH-i"]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — VLE Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BL1["USER INPUT MODULE: T P x1 x2 Antoine Coefficients"]
    BL1 --> BL2["SATURATION PRESSURE CALCULATOR: logP equals A minus B over C plus T"]
    BL2 --> BL3["ACTIVITY COEFFICIENT MODULE: select NRTL or UNIQUAC"]
    BL3 --> BL4["EQUATION OF STATE MODULE: vapor phase correction if P is high"]
    BL4 --> BL5["EQUILIBRIUM SOLVER: Newton Raphson iteration"]
    BL5 --> BL6{"Convergence achieved?"}
    BL6 -- "No" --> BL7["Adjust T or P and iterate"]
    BL6 -- "Yes" --> BL8["OUTPUT MODULE: y1 y2 Ptotal and K values"]
    BL8 --> BL9["DATA VALIDATION: check K1 K2 ratio consistency"]
```
