## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 2: Activity Coefficient & Gibbs–Duhem**

### Core Definitions
- **Activity coefficient** $\gamma_i$ — measures non-ideality; $\hat{a}_i = \gamma_i x_i$.
- **Excess Gibbs energy** $G^E$ — the "parent function" for all $\gamma_i$ via partial differentiation.
- **Gibbs–Duhem equation** — the thermodynamic constraint that ties intensive properties together.

### Master Formulas
- $G^E = RT \sum_i x_i \ln \gamma_i$
- $\ln \gamma_i = \partial(nG^E/RT)/\partial n_i \big|_{T,P,n_{j\ne i}}$
- **G–D (general):** $V dP - S dT = \sum_i x_i d\mu_i$
- **G–D (VLE form):** $\sum_i x_i d\ln \gamma_i = 0$ at const $T, P$
- **Binary G–D:** $d\ln \gamma_2 = -(x_1/x_2) d\ln \gamma_1$

### Models of $G^E$ (in order of increasing sophistication)
1. **1-suffix Margules** — symmetric, $G^E/RT = A x_1 x_2$, $\ln \gamma_i = A x_j^2$
2. **2-suffix Margules** — asymmetric, $G^E/RT = x_1 x_2 (A_{21} x_1 + A_{12} x_2)$
3. **3-suffix Margules** — adds $D x_1^2 x_2^2$ term
4. **van Laar** — rational-function form, regular-solution basis
5. **Wilson** — local composition, best for VLE (not LLE)
6. **NRTL** — Renon–Prausnitz, handles VLE + LLE
7. **UNIQUAC** — most general, VLE + LLE + polymers, two parameters

### Engineering Rules of Thumb
- **Wilson / NRTL / UNIQUAC** are the three workhorse models in industrial simulators (Aspen, HYSYS).
- For **multicomponent VLE**, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC predict from *binary* parameters only — no ternary data needed.
- For **LLE / partially miscible systems**, use NRTL or UNIQUAC (Wilson fails).
- The **Herington consistency test** is the practical application of G–D in validating experimental VLE data.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU Exams
- ✗ Writing $d\ln(\gamma_i x_i) = d\ln \gamma_i$ (WRONG — the $x_i$ part integrates to $d\ln x_i$, which doesn't vanish in the sum).
- ✗ Confusing G–D (closed-system constraint) with the Gibbs adsorption equation (interface).
- ✗ Forgetting to set $dT = 0$ AND $dP = 0$ when applying the VLE form of G–D.
- ✗ Using $\gamma_i = 1$ as the "ideal reference" without checking Henry's law for highly dilute solutes.
- ✗ Mixing up "1-suffix" and "2-suffix" Margules notation ($A$ vs. $A_{12}, A_{21}$).

### Quick Mnemonic
- **"M-V-W-N-U"** — Margules, van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC — the $G^E$ model family in order.
- **"Sum to zero"** — G–D always says $\sum x_i d(\text{something}) = 0$ at equilibrium.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Property Changes of Mixing and Excess Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Property Changes of Mixing and Excess Properties # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Property Change of Mixing (ΔM) In solution thermodynamics, the **property change of mixing** is the difference between the value of an extensive thermodynamic property **M** of a mixture (at given T, P, and composition) and the sum of the values of that property for the **pure components** (each at the same T and P). $$ \Delta M_{\text{mix}} = M_{\text{mixture}} - \sum_{i} n_i M_i $$ On a **molar basis**, this is written as: $$ \Delta m_{\text{mix}} = m - \sum_{i} x_i m_i $$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Notation (KTU Board Standard):** > - $M$ → extensive property (J, m³, etc.) for $n$ moles of mixture > - $m = M/n$ → molar property (J/mol, m³/mol) > - $x_i$ → mole fraction of component $i$ > - $M_i$ or $m_i$ → property of pure component $i$ at the same T and P ### Physical Meaning When we mix pure substances, three things can happen to a property: 1. **It may not change** (e.g., for an **ideal gas** or **ideal solution**, $\Delta V_{\text{mix}} = 0$ and $\Delta H_{\text{mix}} = 0$) 2. **It may increase or decrease** (real mixtures often show $\Delta V_{\text{mix}} \neq 0$) 3. **Entropy always increases** on mixing ($\Delta S_{\text{mix}} > 0$, Second Law) > [!IMPORTANT] > Property changes of mixing are **experimentally measurable**. Calorimeters measure $\Delta H_{\text{mix}}$, dilatometers measure $\Delta V_{\text{mix}}$, and E.m.f. cells probe $\Delta G_{\text{mix}}$. --- ## 1.2 Excess Property ($M^E$) The **excess property** is defined as the difference between the actual property of a real mixture and the property the mixture would have if it behaved as an **ideal solution** at the same T, P, and composition. $$ M^E = M - M^{\text{id}} $$ On a molar basis: $$ m^E = m - m^{\text{id}} $$ where $m^{\text{id}}$ is the value of the property for an **ideal solution** at the same T, P, and $x_i$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Excess properties vanish by definition for an ideal solution:** > For an ideal solution, $V^E = 0$, $H^E = 0$, $S^E = 0$, and $G^E = 0$. > Therefore, $M^E$ is a direct quantitative measure of **non-ideality**. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detour" Picture Imagine you are walking from Town A to Town B: - **Town A** = Pure components (unmixed) - **Town B** = Real mixture - **Town C** = Hypothetical ideal solution at the same T, P, $x_i$ The **property change of mixing** is the journey from **A → B**. The **excess property** is the *detour* taken via **Town C**, i.e., the deviation from the ideal path: $$ M^E = (M - M^{\text{id}}) = \Delta M_{\text{mix}} - \Delta M^{\text{id}}_{\text{mix}} $$ > A positive $H^E$ (endothermic mixing) is like climbing a hill — the molecules of different kinds "repel" weakly. > A negative $H^E$ (exothermic mixing) is like going downhill — strong attractive cross-interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding in acetone–chloroform). --- ## 1.4 Why Are Excess Properties Important in KTU Chemical Engineering? | Engineering Application | Excess Property Used | |---|---| | Distillation column design | $G^E$ (via activity coefficients $\gamma_i$) | | Liquid–liquid extraction | $\gamma_i$ from $G^E$ models | | Heat of mixing data | $H^E$ (Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC) | | Reactor design with non-ideal mixtures | $f_i = \gamma_i x_i f_i^{\text{sat}}$ | | Cryogenic gas processing | $H^E$, $V^E$ for equation of state tuning | > [!NOTE] > Every KTU problem on VLE, LLE, or chemical reaction equilibrium in non-ideal mixtures eventually traces back to **excess Gibbs energy** and **activity coefficients**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Property Changes of Mixing for an Ideal Gas Mixture For a mixture of ideal gases at T, P: $$ \Delta V^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = 0, \quad \Delta H^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = 0, \quad \Delta U^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = 0 $$ The non-zero property changes of mixing are: $$ \Delta S^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = -nR \sum_i x_i \ln y_i $$ $$ \Delta G^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = nRT \sum_i x_i \ln y_i $$ Here $y_i$ is the mole fraction in the gas phase (identical to mole fraction of the mixture of ideal gases). --- ## 2.2 Property Changes of Mixing for an Ideal Solution An **ideal solution** (typically liquid) is defined by the Lewis–Randall rule: $$ \bar{f}_i^{\,id} = x_i f_i $$ For an ideal solution at T, P, $x_i$: $$ \Delta V^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0, \quad \Delta H^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0, \quad \Delta U^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0 $$ The non-ideal-in-origin entropy and Gibbs energy of mixing are: $$ \Delta S^{id}_{\text{mix}} = -nR \sum_i x_i \ln x_i $$ $$ \Delta G^{id}_{\text{mix}} = nRT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i $$ > [!NOTE] > The only difference between ideal gas mixing and ideal solution mixing is that the **mole fraction in the log term** is taken with respect to the **mixture of the same phase** ($y_i$ for gas, $x_i$ for liquid). --- ## 2.3 Excess Properties — Key Relations ### Partial Molar Excess Property $$ \bar{m}_i^{\,E} = \left[\frac{\partial (n m^E)}{\partial n_i}\right]_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} $$ ### Total Excess Property (Summability) $$ m^E = \sum_i x_i \, \bar{m}_i^{\,E} $$ ### Connection to Activity Coefficient $$ \bar{m}_i^{\,E} = RT \ln \gamma_i $$ $$ g^E = RT \sum_i x_i \ln \gamma_i $$ ### Excess Property = Property Change of Mixing minus Ideal Mixing $$ m^E = \Delta m_{\text{mix}} - \Delta m^{id}_{\text{mix}} $$ For $V$, $H$, $U$: $\Delta m^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0$, hence $m^E = \Delta m_{\text{mix}}$. For $G$: $\Delta g^{id}_{\text{mix}} = RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$, so $g^E = \Delta g_{\text{mix}} - RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$. --- ## 2.4 Partial Molar Property Relations The **partial molar property** is defined as: $$ \bar{m}_i = \left[\frac{\partial (n m)}{\partial n_i}\right]_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} $$ The corresponding extensive form is: $$ \bar{M}_i = \left[\frac{\partial M}{\partial n_i}\right]_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} $$ The **summability** relation: $$ m = \sum_i x_i \, \bar{m}_i $$ The **Gibbs–Duhem equation** (intensive, at constant T and P): $$ \sum_i x_i \, d\bar{m}_i = 0 $$ For excess properties: $$ \sum_i x_i \, d\bar{m}_i^{\,E} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sum_i x_i \, d\ln \gamma_i = 0 $$ --- ## 2.5 Fundamental Relations for Excess Properties $$ g^E = h^E - T s^E = u^E + P v^E - T s^E $$ Temperature and pressure derivatives: $$ \left(\frac{\partial g^E}{\partial T}\right)_{P, x} = -s^E $$ $$ \left(\frac{\partial (g^E / T)}{\partial T}\right)_{P, x} = -\frac{h^E}{T^2} $$ $$ \left(\frac{\partial g^E}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x} = v^E $$ --- ## 2.6 Fugacity and Activity Coefficient The **fugacity** of component $i$ in a real liquid mixture (Lewis–Randall reference state): $$ \bar{f}_i = \gamma_i \, x_i \, f_i $$ For an ideal solution ($\gamma_i = 1$): $$ \bar{f}_i^{\,id} = x_i \, f_i $$ Hence: $$ \gamma_i = \frac{\bar{f}_i}{x_i f_i} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Standard-state conventions** (frequently tested in KTU): > - **Pure component reference**: $\gamma_i \to 1$ as $x_i \to 1$ (symmetric convention) > - **Infinite-dilution reference**: $\gamma_i^* \to 1$ as $x_i \to 0$ (unsymmetric convention) --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Expression | Units | Valid For | |---|---|---|---| | Property change of mixing | $\Delta m_{\text{mix}} = m - \sum_i x_i m_i$ | varies | All mixtures | | Ideal gas $\Delta G$ mixing | $\Delta g^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = RT \sum_i x_i \ln y_i$ | J/mol | Ideal gases | | Ideal solution $\Delta G$ mixing | $\Delta g^{id}_{\text{mix}} = RT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i$ | J/mol | Ideal solutions | | Excess property | $m^E = m - m^{id}$ | varies | Real mixtures | | Partial molar excess | $\bar{m}_i^{\,E} = RT \ln \gamma_i$ | J/mol | Real mixtures | | Molar excess Gibbs energy | $g^E = RT \sum_i x_i \ln \gamma_i$ | J/mol | Real mixtures | | Fugacity in real solution | $\bar{f}_i = \gamma_i x_i f_i$ | Pa | Real solutions | | Gibbs–Duhem (excess) | $\sum_i x_i d\ln \gamma_i = 0$ at T,P | – | All mixtures | | $\partial g^E / \partial T$ | $-s^E$ | J/(mol·K) | Any mixture | | $\partial g^E / \partial P$ | $v^E$ | m³/mol | Any mixture | > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Hack:** Whenever asked "Why is $H^E$ a measure of non-ideality?" — answer: *"Because for an ideal solution, $\Delta H^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0$ by Raoult's-law definition, so any non-zero $\Delta H_{\text{mix}}$ is purely an excess contribution."* --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **$G^E$** → directly gives $\gamma_i$ → controls VLE in distillation (Margules, van Laar, Wilson equations). - **$H^E$** → determines heat effects in mixer/heat-exchanger design; can be measured by flow calorimeters. - **$V^E$** → controls density-driven flow in pipelines; used in custody transfer of hydrocarbon liquids. - **$S^E$** → determines configurational entropy change on mixing for polymer solutions. - **$C_p^E$** → controls temperature rise in reactive mixing; key in safety analysis. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Property Change of Mixing from Pure Components Starting from the definition for an extensive property $M$ of a mixture of $n$ moles: $$ M = M(T, P, n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_C) $$ For **pure components**, each at the **same T and P** as the mixture: $$ M_{\text{pure, tot}} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, m_i(T, P) $$ The property change of mixing is therefore: $$ \Delta M_{\text{mix}} = M(T, P, n_1, \ldots, n_C) - \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \, m_i(T, P) $$ Dividing throughout by $n = \sum_i n_i$: $$ \Delta m_{\text{mix}} = m - \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i \, m_i $$ This identity is the **starting point** for all mixing computations. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — $\Delta S^{ig}_{\text{mix}}$ and $\Delta G^{ig}_{\text{mix}}$ for an Ideal Gas The entropy of an ideal gas (pure $i$) is: $$ dS_i = \frac{C_{p,i}}{T} dT - \frac{R}{P} dP $$ Integrating from the pure component state (where $P_i = y_i P$ if the partial pressure equals its mole-fraction share) to the mixture state, **at constant T**: $$ \bar{S}_i^{ig} - S_i = -R \ln\left(\frac{y_i P}{P}\right) = -R \ln y_i $$ Multiplying by $n_i$ and summing: $$ S^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = \sum_i n_i S_i - R \sum_i n_i \ln y_i $$ Subtracting the pure-component sum $\sum_i n_i S_i$: $$ \Delta S^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = -R \sum_i n_i \ln y_i = -n R \sum_i x_i \ln y_i $$ (per mole: $\Delta s^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = -R \sum_i x_i \ln y_i$) Then, since $\Delta H^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = 0$ and $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$: $$ \Delta G^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = 0 - T \cdot \left(-nR \sum_i x_i \ln y_i\right) = n R T \sum_i x_i \ln y_i $$ Per mole: $$ \Delta g^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = R T \sum_i x_i \ln y_i $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — $g^E = \Delta g_{\text{mix}} - RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$ For a real liquid mixture (molar): $$ \Delta g_{\text{mix}} = g - \sum_i x_i g_i $$ The corresponding **ideal solution** mixing Gibbs energy (at the same T, P, $x_i$) is: $$ \Delta g^{id}_{\text{mix}} = R T \sum_i x_i \ln x_i $$ By definition of excess property: $$ g^E \equiv g - g^{id} $$ Now, $g^{id} = \sum_i x_i g_i + \Delta g^{id}_{\text{mix}}$ by the ideal-solution summability (since $g^{id} = \sum_i x_i \bar{g}_i^{id} = \sum_i x_i g_i + RT\sum x_i \ln x_i$). Therefore: $$ g^E = g - \left(\sum_i x_i g_i + RT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i\right) = \left(g - \sum_i x_i g_i\right) - RT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i $$ The first bracket is exactly $\Delta g_{\text{mix}}$: $$ \boxed{\,g^E = \Delta g_{\text{mix}} - R T \sum_i x_i \ln x_i\,} $$ By the same logic, for entropy: $$ \boxed{\,s^E = \Delta s_{\text{mix}} + R \sum_i x_i \ln x_i\,} $$ For volume, enthalpy, and internal energy, $\Delta m^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0$, so: $$ v^E = \Delta v_{\text{mix}}, \quad h^E = \Delta h_{\text{mix}}, \quad u^E = \Delta u_{\text{mix}} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — $\bar{g}_i^{\,E} = RT \ln \gamma_i$ (Activity Coefficient) Starting from the chemical potential equality: $$ \mu_i \equiv \bar{g}_i = \bar{g}_i^{\,id} + \bar{g}_i^{\,E} $$ For the **ideal-solution** part, from $\bar{g}_i^{\,id} = g_i + RT \ln x_i$ (Lewis–Randall reference state): $$ \bar{g}_i^{\,id} = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln x_i $$ where $\mu_i^{\circ} = g_i(T, P)$ is the chemical potential of pure $i$. The **real** chemical potential in terms of fugacity: $$ \mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln \frac{\bar{f}_i}{f_i} $$ Subtracting the ideal-solution expression: $$ \bar{g}_i^{\,E} = \mu_i - \bar{g}_i^{\,id} = RT \ln \frac{\bar{f}_i}{f_i} - RT \ln x_i = RT \ln \left(\frac{\bar{f}_i}{x_i f_i}\right) $$ By definition of the activity coefficient $\gamma_i$: $$ \boxed{\,\gamma_i \equiv \frac{\bar{f}_i}{x_i f_i} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \bar{g}_i^{\,E} = R T \ln \gamma_i\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > This single boxed identity is the **most important equation in solution thermodynamics** for KTU exam purposes — it links excess Gibbs energy to fugacity, VLE, and chemical reaction equilibrium in one step. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Activity Coefficients from a Binary $g^E$ Model (Margules 2-Suffix) Given a binary molar excess Gibbs energy model: $$ \frac{g^E}{RT} = A \, x_1 x_2 $$ where $A$ is a constant (often tabulated at a reference T). We need $\ln \gamma_1$ and $\ln \gamma_2$. For a binary system with $n$ total moles, the **extensive** excess is: $$ n \frac{g^E}{RT} = \frac{A \, n_1 n_2}{n_1 + n_2} $$ To find $\ln \gamma_1$, take the partial derivative with respect to $n_1$ at constant T, P, $n_2$: $$ \ln \gamma_1 = \left[\frac{\partial (n g^E / RT)}{\partial n_1}\right]_{T, P, n_2} $$ Computing the partial: $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial n_1}\left[\frac{A n_1 n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right] = A n_2 \cdot \frac{(n_1 + n_2) - n_1}{(n_1 + n_2)^2} = \frac{A n_2^2}{(n_1 + n_2)^2} $$ Converting to mole fractions: $n_2/(n_1 + n_2) = x_2$. Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,\ln \gamma_1 = A\, x_2^2\,} $$ By symmetry, swapping indices 1 and 2: $$ \boxed{\,\ln \gamma_2 = A\, x_1^2\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The Margules 2-suffix (one-constant) model is **symmetric**: $A_{12} = A_{21} = A$. For asymmetric systems, the 3-suffix Margules or Wohl expansion is used in advanced problems. --- ## 3.6 Worked Example — Numerical Computation (KTU 2024 Pattern) > **Problem:** A binary liquid mixture of ethanol (1) and water (2) at 350 K has the following one-constant Margules model: > > $$ > \frac{g^E}{RT} = 0.5 \, x_1 x_2 > $$ > > Calculate $\gamma_1$, $\gamma_2$, and the excess Gibbs energy of mixing for $x_1 = 0.4$. **Step 1 — Compute mole fractions.** $$ x_1 = 0.4, \quad x_2 = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6 $$ **Step 2 — Compute activity coefficients using Margules formulas.** $$ \ln \gamma_1 = A x_2^2 = 0.5 \times (0.6)^2 = 0.5 \times 0.36 = 0.1800 $$ $$ \gamma_1 = e^{0.1800} = 1.1972 $$ $$ \ln \gamma_2 = A x_1^2 = 0.5 \times (0.4)^2 = 0.5 \times 0.16 = 0.0800 $$ $$ \gamma_2 = e^{0.0800} = 1.0833 $$ **Step 3 — Compute $g^E$ (per mole).** $$ \frac{g^E}{RT} = 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.6 = 0.12 $$ $$ g^E = 0.12 \times R T = 0.12 \times 8.314 \times 350 = 349.2 \,\text{J/mol} $$ **Step 4 — Cross-check using $\sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$.** $$ \sum_i x_i \ln \gamma_i = 0.4 \times 0.1800 + 0.6 \times 0.0800 = 0.072 + 0.048 = 0.120 $$ $$ g^E = RT \times 0.120 = 8.314 \times 350 \times 0.120 = 349.2 \,\text{J/mol} \quad \checkmark $$ > [!TIP] > The two methods give **identical** results — this is exactly the consistency check examiners expect in KTU valuation. --- ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU) | Property | Ideal Gas | Ideal Solution | Real Mixture | Excess | |---|---|---|---|---| | $\Delta V_{\text{mix}}$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\Delta V_{\text{mix}}$ | $v^E = \Delta v_{\text{mix}}$ | | $\Delta H_{\text{mix}}$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\Delta H_{\text{mix}}$ | $h^E = \Delta h_{\text{mix}}$ | | $\Delta S_{\text{mix}}$ | $-nR\sum x_i \ln y_i$ | $-nR\sum x_i \ln x_i$ | $\Delta S_{\text{mix}}$ | $s^E = \Delta s_{\text{mix}} + R\sum x_i \ln x_i$ | | $\Delta G_{\text{mix}}$ | $nRT\sum x_i \ln y_i$ | $nRT\sum x_i \ln x_i$ | $\Delta G_{\text{mix}}$ | $g^E = \Delta g_{\text{mix}} - RT\sum x_i \ln x_i$ | | Fugacity $\bar{f}_i$ | $y_i P$ | $x_i f_i$ | $\gamma_i x_i f_i$ | $\gamma_i = \bar{f}_i/(x_i f_i)$ | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Architecture — Hierarchy of Solution Thermodynamic Quantities The following Mermaid block diagram maps the conceptual flow from a real mixture down to individual component activity coefficients, which is the **engineer's mental model** when solving KTU problems. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Real Mixture at T, P, xi"]:::real --> B["Extensive Property M"] A --> C["Fugacity of each component f-bar-i"] B --> D["Property Change of Mixing delta-M-mix"] B --> E["Excess Property M to power E equals M minus M-id"] E --> F["Molar Excess g to power E"] F --> G["Partial Molar Excess g-bar-i to power E"] G --> H["Activity Coefficient gamma-i"] C --> I["gamma-i equals f-bar-i over xi times f-i"] I --> H H --> J["VLE or Reaction Equilibrium Calculations"] classDef real fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000 classDef id fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 class A,B,C real ``` > [!NOTE] > The red blocks are **observable / measurable** quantities (real mixture data, calorimetry, VLE experiments). The blue blocks are the **derived** quantities (excess properties, activity coefficients) that engineers use in design. --- ## 4.2 Decision Flow — When to Use Ideal vs. Real Solution ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Start: Given a mixture at T, P, xi"] --> Q1{"Is the mixture
a gas at low P?"} Q1 -- "Yes" --> IG["Use Ideal Gas Equations
delta-H = 0, delta-V = 0
delta-G = nRT Sum xi ln yi"] Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Does it follow
Raoult's Law?"} Q2 -- "Yes" --> IS["Use Ideal Solution Equations
delta-H = 0, delta-V = 0
delta-G = nRT Sum xi ln xi"] Q2 -- "No" --> RM["Use Real Mixture Framework
1. Define gamma-i
2. Choose g-E model Margules Wilson NRTL
3. Compute activity coefficients
4. Apply VLE or reaction equilibrium"] classDef yes fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 classDef no fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 class IG,IS,RM proc ``` > [!TIP] > In KTU board problems, the phrase *"the mixture follows Raoult's Law"* is a **direct trigger** to use the ideal-solution expressions without computing $\gamma_i$. Otherwise, always proceed through the real-mixture block. --- ## 4.3 Interaction Map — Excess Property Thermodynamic Web ```mermaid flowchart LR GE["g to power E"] --> HE["h to power E = minus T squared d g to power E over T divided by dT"] GE --> SE["s to power E = minus d g to power E over dT"] GE --> VE["v to power E = d g to power E over dP"] GE --> GE1["g to power E = RT Sum xi ln gamma-i"] GE1 --> G1["ln gamma-1 from partial derivative"] GE1 --> G2["ln gamma-2 from partial derivative"] HE --> CP["c-p to power E = d h to power E over dT"] SE --> Sconf["Configurational entropy"] classDef root fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef branch fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#283593,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000 class GE root class HE,SE,VE,GE1 branch class CP,Sconf,G1,G2 leaf ``` > [!NOTE] > This **thermodynamic web** is the examiner's favorite question. A typical 14-mark KTU problem asks you to start from a $g^E$ model and derive three of the leaves: $\ln \gamma_1$, $h^E$, and $v^E$. --- ## 4.4 Reference-State Schematic for Fugacity & Activity ```mermaid flowchart TD PC1["Pure component 1 at T, P
f-1"] --> LS["Lewis-Randall Reference State
f-bar-1 to power id equals x1 f1"] PC2["Pure component 2 at T, P
f-2"] --> LS LS --> RM1["Real mixture
f-bar-1 equals gamma-1 x1 f1"] LS --> RM2["Real mixture
f-bar-2 equals gamma-2 x2 f2"] RM1 --> G1["gamma-1 = 1 at x1 = 1"] RM2 --> G2["gamma-2 = 1 at x2 = 1"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Lewis–Randall reference state** anchors the activity coefficient to unity at the pure-component limit: $\gamma_i \to 1$ as $x_i \to 1$. This is the symmetric convention. Always state this in KTU answers before using $\gamma_i$. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] > **Define the term "excess property" in solution thermodynamics. How is it related to the property change of mixing?** **Model Answer:** > The **excess property** $M^E$ of a mixture is defined as the difference between the value of an extensive property of the real mixture and the value it would have if the mixture behaved as an **ideal solution** at the same temperature, pressure, and composition: > > $$M^E = M - M^{id}$$ > > The **property change of mixing** is the difference between the property of the mixture and the sum of the properties of the pure components at the same T and P: > > $$\Delta M_{\text{mix}} = M - \sum_i n_i M_i$$ > > The two are linked by: > > $$M^E = \Delta M_{\text{mix}} - \Delta M^{id}_{\text{mix}}$$ > > For an ideal solution, $\Delta V^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0$ and $\Delta H^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0$, so $V^E = \Delta V_{\text{mix}}$ and $H^E = \Delta H_{\text{mix}}$. > For entropy and Gibbs energy, the ideal mixing contribution is non-zero: $\Delta S^{id}_{\text{mix}} = -nR\sum x_i \ln x_i$ and $\Delta G^{id}_{\text{mix}} = nRT\sum x_i \ln x_i$, so $G^E = \Delta G_{\text{mix}} - nRT \sum x_i \ln x_i$. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] > **What is an activity coefficient? Write down the equation relating it to excess Gibbs energy and state its significance.** **Model Answer:** > The **activity coefficient** $\gamma_i$ of component $i$ in a non-ideal liquid mixture is defined as the ratio of its fugacity in the real mixture to the fugacity it would have in an ideal solution at the same T, P, and composition: > > $$\gamma_i = \frac{\bar{f}_i}{x_i f_i}$$ > > The link to excess Gibbs energy is: > > $$\bar{g}_i^{\,E} = RT \ln \gamma_i \quad \text{(partial molar form)}$$ > > $$g^E = RT \sum_i x_i \ln \gamma_i \quad \text{(molar form)}$$ > > **Significance:** $\gamma_i$ quantifies the **deviation from ideal-solution (Raoult's Law) behaviour** for component $i$. It is essential for the design of distillation columns, extractors, and reactors handling non-ideal liquid mixtures, because it enters directly into the phase equilibrium condition $\bar{f}_i^L = \bar{f}_i^V$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model] > **(a)** Discuss the property changes of mixing for **(i)** an ideal gas mixture and **(ii)** an ideal solution. Tabulate the expressions for $\Delta V_{\text{mix}}$, $\Delta H_{\text{mix}}$, $\Delta S_{\text{mix}}$, and $\Delta G_{\text{mix}}$ in both cases. **[$7$ marks]** > > **(b)** For a binary liquid mixture at 300 K, the molar excess Gibbs energy is given by: > > $$\frac{g^E}{RT} = 0.8 \, x_1 x_2$$ > > Determine $\ln \gamma_1$, $\ln \gamma_2$, $\gamma_1$, $\gamma_2$, and $g^E$ (in J/mol) at $x_1 = 0.3$. **[$7$ marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Ideal Gas Mixture (T, P constant):** - Since $V_i = RT/P$ for each pure ideal gas and $\bar{V}_i = RT/P$ in the mixture (partial molar volume of ideal gas $i$ depends only on T and P), the total mixture volume is $V = \sum n_i (RT/P) = nRT/P$. The pure-component sum is the same. Hence: $$ \Delta V^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = 0 $$ - Enthalpy of an ideal gas is a function of T only: $\bar{H}_i^{ig} = H_i(T)$. Therefore: $$ \Delta H^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = 0 $$ - For entropy, integrating $dS$ at constant T from pure $i$ at P to component $i$ in mixture at partial pressure $y_i P$: $$ \bar{S}_i^{ig} = S_i - R \ln y_i $$ Therefore: $$ \Delta S^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = -nR \sum_i x_i \ln y_i $$ - Gibbs energy change: $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$: $$ \Delta G^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = 0 - T \times (-nR \sum x_i \ln y_i) = nRT \sum_i x_i \ln y_i $$ **Ideal Solution (T, P constant):** The Lewis–Randall rule $\bar{f}_i^{id} = x_i f_i$ combined with $\Delta H^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0$ and $\Delta V^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0$ gives: $$ \Delta V^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0, \quad \Delta H^{id}_{\text{mix}} = 0 $$ $$ \Delta S^{id}_{\text{mix}} = -nR \sum_i x_i \ln x_i $$ $$ \Delta G^{id}_{\text{mix}} = nRT \sum_i x_i \ln x_i $$ **Tabulation:** | Property of Mixing | Ideal Gas | Ideal Solution | |---|---|---| | $\Delta V_{\text{mix}}$ | $0$ | $0$ | | $\Delta H_{\text{mix}}$ | $0$ | $0$ | | $\Delta S_{\text{mix}}$ | $-nR\sum x_i \ln y_i$ | $-nR\sum x_i \ln x_i$ | | $\Delta G_{\text{mix}}$ | $nRT\sum x_i \ln y_i$ | $nRT\sum x_i \ln x_i$ | > The **only** difference lies in the mole fraction inside the logarithm: $y_i$ for gases, $x_i$ for liquids. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $\dfrac{g^E}{RT} = A x_1 x_2$ with $A = 0.8$, $T = 300$ K, $x_1 = 0.3$, $x_2 = 0.7$. **Step 1 — Activity coefficient formulas (Margules 2-suffix):** For a binary Margules model $g^E/(RT) = A x_1 x_2$: $$ \ln \gamma_1 = A x_2^2, \quad \ln \gamma_2 = A x_1^2 $$ **Step 2 — Plug in numbers:** $$ \ln \gamma_1 = 0.8 \times (0.7)^2 = 0.8 \times 0.49 = 0.392 $$ $$ \ln \gamma_2 = 0.8 \times (0.3)^2 = 0.8 \times 0.09 = 0.072 $$ **Step 3 — Exponentiate:** $$ \gamma_1 = e^{0.392} = 1.4800 $$ $$ \gamma_2 = e^{0.072} = 1.0747 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $g^E$:** $$ \frac{g^E}{RT} = 0.8 \times 0.3 \times 0.7 = 0.168 $$ $$ g^E = 0.168 \times 8.314 \times 300 = 419.0 \,\text{J/mol} $$ **Step 5 — Cross-check:** $$ \sum_i x_i \ln \gamma_i = 0.3 \times 0.392 + 0.7 \times 0.072 = 0.1176 + 0.0504 = 0.1680 \quad \checkmark $$ $$ g^E = RT \times 0.168 = 8.314 \times 300 \times 0.168 = 419.0 \,\text{J/mol} \quad \checkmark $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Forgetting the $RT$ factor** when computing $g^E$ from $g^E/(RT)$ — results in a dimensionless answer marked **wrong**. > 2. **Using $y_i$ instead of $x_i$** for liquid-phase mixing — read the problem carefully. > 3. **Mixing up extensive and molar** forms — when the problem gives $G^E$ in J, do not multiply by $n$ again. > 4. **Skipping the cross-check** $g^E = RT \sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$ — this is a 1-mark freebie that examiners specifically look for. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) > **(a)** Define the **partial molar excess property**. Starting from the definition of excess Gibbs energy, derive the relation $\bar{g}_i^{\,E} = RT \ln \gamma_i$. **[$7$ marks]** > > **(b)** For a binary system the molar excess Gibbs energy is given by the Wohl (two-constant Margules) model: > > $$\frac{g^E}{RT} = (A_{21} x_1 + A_{12} x_2) x_1 x_2$$ > > with $A_{12} = 0.4$ and $A_{21} = 0.7$. Compute $\ln \gamma_1$ and $\ln \gamma_2$ at $x_1 = 0.5$. **[$7$ marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Definition:** The partial molar excess property $\bar{m}_i^{\,E}$ of component $i$ in a mixture is defined as: $$ \bar{m}_i^{\,E} = \left[\frac{\partial (n m^E)}{\partial n_i}\right]_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} $$ **Derivation:** Step 1 — Start with the chemical potential (partial molar Gibbs energy): $$ \mu_i \equiv \bar{g}_i $$ Step 2 — Express $\bar{g}_i$ in terms of the standard chemical potential $\mu_i^{\circ}$ and fugacity: $$ \mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ}(T, P) + RT \ln \frac{\bar{f}_i}{f_i^{\circ}} $$ For the **Lewis–Randall standard state** ($f_i^{\circ} = f_i$, fugacity of pure liquid $i$ at T, P): $$ \mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln \frac{\bar{f}_i}{f_i} $$ Step 3 — For an **ideal solution** at the same T, P, $x_i$: $$ \bar{f}_i^{\,id} = x_i f_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln x_i $$ Step 4 — Subtract to obtain the excess chemical potential: $$ \bar{g}_i^{\,E} = \mu_i - \mu_i^{id} = RT \ln \frac{\bar{f}_i}{f_i} - RT \ln x_i = RT \ln \left(\frac{\bar{f}_i}{x_i f_i}\right) $$ Step 5 — Recognise the activity coefficient by definition: $$ \gamma_i \equiv \frac{\bar{f}_i}{x_i f_i} $$ Hence: $$ \boxed{\,\bar{g}_i^{\,E} = R T \ln \gamma_i\,} $$ The molar form follows by summation: $$ g^E = \sum_i x_i \bar{g}_i^{\,E} = RT \sum_i x_i \ln \gamma_i $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** Wohl expansion with $A_{12} = 0.4$, $A_{21} = 0.7$, $x_1 = x_2 = 0.5$. **Step 1 — Standard Wohl results (derivation similar to §3.5):** $$ \ln \gamma_1 = x_2^2 \left[A_{12} + 2 (A_{21} - A_{12}) x_1\right] $$ $$ \ln \gamma_2 = x_1^2 \left[A_{21} + 2 (A_{12} - A_{21}) x_2\right] $$ **Step 2 — Plug in values:** For $\ln \gamma_1$: $$ \ln \gamma_1 = (0.5)^2 \left[0.4 + 2(0.7 - 0.4)(0.5)\right] $$ $$ = 0.25 \times \left[0.4 + 2(0.3)(0.5)\right] $$ $$ = 0.25 \times \left[0.4 + 0.3\right] $$ $$ = 0.25 \times 0.7 = 0.175 $$ For $\ln \gamma_2$: $$ \ln \gamma_2 = (0.5)^2 \left[0.7 + 2(0.4 - 0.7)(0.5)\right] $$ $$ = 0.25 \times \left[0.7 + 2(-0.3)(0.5)\right] $$ $$ = 0.25 \times \left[0.7 - 0.3\right] $$ $$ = 0.25 \times 0.4 = 0.100 $$ **Step 3 — Activity coefficients:** $$ \gamma_1 = e^{0.175} = 1.1912 $$ $$ \gamma_2 = e^{0.100} = 1.1052 $$ **Step 4 — Cross-check using $g^E = RT\sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$:** $$ \frac{g^E}{RT} = 0.5 \times 0.175 + 0.5 \times 0.100 = 0.0875 + 0.0500 = 0.1375 $$ Direct from the model: $$ \frac{g^E}{RT} = (0.7 \times 0.5 + 0.4 \times 0.5)(0.5)(0.5) = (0.35 + 0.20)(0.25) = 0.55 \times 0.25 = 0.1375 \quad \checkmark $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Specific to Wohl/Two-Constant Margules:** > 1. **Swapping $A_{12}$ and $A_{21}$** — by KTU convention, $A_{12}$ is the constant paired with $x_2$ inside the bracket. Do not interchange. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the cross-term $2(A_{21} - A_{12}) x_1$ — this is the **most common** mark-loser on Wohl-type problems. > 3. **Misreading the Wohl form** as Margules 2-suffix: Wohl has **two** constants; if you set $A_{12} = A_{21} = A$, you recover the simpler 1-constant Margules. > 4. **Forgetting the unit conversion** for $g^E$ (multiply by $RT$ to get J/mol). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** ### Definitions to Memorise - [ ] **Property change of mixing:** $\Delta m_{\text{mix}} = m - \sum x_i m_i$ - [ ] **Excess property:** $m^E = m - m^{id}$ (vs. ideal solution) - [ ] **Partial molar property:** $\bar{m}_i = [\partial(nm)/\partial n_i]_{T,P,n_j}$ - [ ] **Activity coefficient:** $\gamma_i = \bar{f}_i / (x_i f_i)$ - [ ] **Excess chemical potential:** $\bar{g}_i^{\,E} = RT \ln \gamma_i$ ### Core Equations - [ ] $\Delta G^{ig}_{\text{mix}} = nRT \sum x_i \ln y_i$ (ideal gas) - [ ] $\Delta G^{id}_{\text{mix}} = nRT \sum x_i \ln x_i$ (ideal solution) - [ ] $g^E = RT \sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$ - [ ] $g^E = h^E - T s^E$ - [ ] $(\partial g^E / \partial T)_{P,x} = -s^E$ - [ ] $(\partial g^E / \partial P)_{T,x} = v^E$ - [ ] Gibbs–Duhem (excess): $\sum x_i d\ln \gamma_i = 0$ at constant T, P ### Numerical Triggers - [ ] If $g^E/(RT) = A x_1 x_2$ (Margules 1-constant) → $\ln \gamma_1 = A x_2^2$ and $\ln \gamma_2 = A x_1^2$ - [ ] If $g^E/(RT) = (A_{21} x_1 + A_{12} x_2) x_1 x_2$ (Wohl) → use the full Wohl equations in §3.5 - [ ] Always cross-check via $g^E = RT \sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$ ### Common Pitfalls - [ ] Don't confuse $y_i$ (gas) with $x_i$ (liquid) inside logarithms. - [ ] Don't forget the $RT$ factor when reporting $g^E$ in J/mol. - [ ] Don't claim $G^E = 0$ for an ideal solution — only $V^E$, $H^E$, $U^E$, $S^E$ vanish. - [ ] For binary problems, always compute **both** $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ to ensure consistency. ### Key Insight for KTU > A **positive** $g^E$ implies $\gamma_i > 1$ (positive deviation from Raoult's Law) → **minimum-boiling azeotrope** possible. > A **negative** $g^E$ implies $\gamma_i < 1$ → **maximum-boiling azeotrope** possible. > This single sign-determines-the-distillation-type observation is worth **2–3 marks** in most KTU 14-mark questions.

topics.title: Problem Solving in Equations of State and Properties of Ideal Solutions Using EXCEL/MATLAB/SCILAB/PYTHON
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Problem Solving in Equations of State and Properties of Ideal Solutions Using EXCEL/MATLAB/SCILAB/PYTHON

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computational Problem Solving in Thermodynamics** refers to the systematic application of numerical methods, root-finding algorithms, and iterative solvers implemented in high-level computational environments (EXCEL, MATLAB, SCILAB, PYTHON) to determine the thermodynamic state properties of pure fluids and mixtures governed by **Equations of State (EOS)** and **Ideal Solution Models** (Raoult's Law, Dalton's Law, Amagat's Law, Kay's Mixing Rules).

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 2, this topic covers the use of these tools to solve:

1. **Cubic Equations of State (CEOS):** Van der Waals (vdW), Redlich-Kwong (RK), Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK), and Peng-Robinson (PR) for finding compressibility factor **Z**, molar volume **V**, and reduced properties.
2. **Ideal Solution VLE:** Bubble pressure, bubble temperature, dew pressure, dew temperature, and isothermal flash calculations for binary and multi-component systems obeying Raoult's Law.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to formulate thermodynamic problems as numerical root-finding problems (non-linear algebraic equations) and solve them using spreadsheet/iterative tools. The emphasis is on **formulation**, **convergence behavior**, and **interpretation of results**, NOT on coding syntax memorization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are an **engineer at a refinery** designing a distillation column. You need to know the exact pressure and temperature at which a liquid mixture of benzene and toluene will start boiling (bubble point). The math involved is a transcendental equation that cannot be inverted by hand — you must guess an answer, plug it in, and refine your guess until the equation is satisfied. This process of **systematic guessing and refining** is precisely what computers excel at.

**The Real-World Analogy:** Think of finding the root of an equation like **finding the exact spot on a hiking map** where a river crosses a trail. You can estimate, walk a little, check if you are above or below the river, and adjust direction. Newton-Raphson, Bisection, and Secant methods are the mathematical equivalents of this "walk and check" strategy, but performed millions of times faster.

**Why Use Computational Tools?**

| Manual Calculation Limitation | Computational Tool Advantage |
|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Cubic equations need iterative guessing | `fsolve`, `fzero`, `roots` find roots automatically |
| Mixture VLE involves 5–10 simultaneous equations | Matrix operations handle systems efficiently |
| Sensitivity analysis (changing T, P) requires recomputation | Loops and arrays allow parametric sweeps |
| Activity coefficient models (Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC) are highly non-linear | Built-in solvers converge robustly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constants (KTU Reference):**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 0.08314 L·bar/(mol·K) = 82.06 cm³·atm/(mol·K)**
> - Critical properties of common substances (Tabulated): **Pc, Tc, ω (acentric factor)**
> - Antoine constants: **A, B, C** for vapor pressure correlations: **ln(P^sat) = A − B/(T + C)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of Cubic Equations of State (CEOS)

All cubic EOS can be written in the canonical **compressibility factor form**:

$$Z^3 + \alpha_2 Z^2 + \alpha_1 Z + \alpha_0 = 0$$

where Z = PV/(RT). The coefficients α₂, α₁, α₀ differ for each EOS. Computational tools solve this cubic by:

1. **Analytical method:** Cardano's formula or trigonometric method (for real roots only).
2. **Numerical method:** Newton-Raphson, Bisection, or built-in polynomial root finders.

### 2.2 The Four Major CEOS — Coefficients Summary

| EOS | α₂ | α₁ | α₀ |
|------|------|------|------|
| **Van der Waals (vdW)** | −(1 + B) | A | −AB |
| **Redlich-Kwong (RK)** | −1 | A − B − B² | −AB |
| **Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK)** | −1 | A − B − B² | −AB |
| **Peng-Robinson (PR)** | −(1 − B) | A − 3B² − 2B | −AB + B² + B³ |

> Where:
> $$A = \frac{aP}{R^2 T^2}, \qquad B = \frac{bP}{RT}$$

### 2.3 EOS Parameter Formulas (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| EOS | a Parameter | b Parameter | α(T) for SRK/PR |
|-----|-------------|-------------|------------------|
| **vdW** | $a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}$ | $b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c}$ | — |
| **RK** | $a = \frac{0.42748 R^2 T_c^{2.5}}{P_c \sqrt{T}}$ | $b = \frac{0.08664 R T_c}{P_c}$ | — |
| **SRK** | $a = \frac{0.42748 R^2 T_c^2}{P_c}$ | $b = \frac{0.08664 R T_c}{P_c}$ | $\alpha(T) = \left[1 + m\left(1 - \sqrt{T_r}\right)\right]^2$ |
| **PR** | $a = \frac{0.45724 R^2 T_c^2}{P_c}$ | $b = \frac{0.07780 R T_c}{P_c}$ | Same as SRK with different m |

$$m = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^2 \quad \text{(SRK)}$$

$$m = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^2 \quad \text{(PR — same form)}$$

**Acentric factor ω** quantifies the non-sphericity of a molecule.

### 2.4 Ideal Solution VLE — KTU Formula Sheet

For an **ideal solution**, the vapor-liquid equilibrium relations reduce to **Raoult's Law** and **Dalton's Law**:

$$y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}(T) \quad \text{(Raoult's Law — component form)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i = \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P} = 1 \quad \text{(Bubble Point Equation)}$$

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i P_i^{sat}(T) \quad \text{(Bubble Pressure — closed form)}$$

$$1 = \sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \frac{P}{P_i^{sat}(T)} \quad \text{(Dew Point Equation — implicit in T)}$$

$$x_i = \frac{y_i P}{P_i^{sat}(T)} \quad \text{(Dew Pressure — closed form)}$$

**Vapor Pressure (Antoine Equation):**

$$\log_{10}(P_i^{sat}) = A_i - \frac{B_i}{T + C_i} \quad \text{[P in mmHg, T in °C]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raoult's Law is valid only for IDEAL solutions** (e.g., benzene-toluene, hexane-heptane). For non-ideal systems, activity coefficients γᵢ or fugacity coefficients φᵢ must be introduced. This is covered in Module 3 of KTU syllabus.

### 2.5 Iterative Numerical Methods Used in Tools

| Method | Formula | Convergence | Use Case |
|--------|---------|-------------|----------|
| **Bisection** | Halves interval each step | Linear (slow but guaranteed) | Bracketed roots |
| **Newton-Raphson** | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | Quadratic (fast) | Smooth functions, needs derivative |
| **Secant** | $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)\frac{x_n - x_{n-1}}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$ | Superlinear | Derivative-free |
| **Müller** | Quadratic interpolation through 3 points | Quadratic | Complex or real roots of cubic |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Simulation Software (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, ChemCAD):** Internally use exactly these numerical routines — the KTU problem-solving exercise teaches the "engine under the hood".
- **Refinery Operations:** Bubble/dew point computations determine distillation column operating windows.
- **Cryogenic Storage:** Vapor pressure calculations predict boil-off rates for LNG and LPG.
- **Refrigeration:** Z-factor corrections are essential for compressor work calculations (Module 1 link).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From EOS to Compressibility Factor Cubic

Starting with the **general cubic EOS** in pressure form:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a(T)}{V(V + b) + b(V - b)}$$

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides by V/(RT):

$$\frac{PV}{RT} = \frac{V}{V - b} - \frac{a(T)V}{RT[V(V + b) + b(V - b)]}$$

**Step 2:** Introduce Z = PV/(RT), so V = ZRT/P. Substitute V:

$$Z = \frac{1}{1 - B/Z} - \frac{A/Z}{1 + B/Z - (B/Z)^2} \quad \text{where } A = \frac{aP}{R^2T^2}, \, B = \frac{bP}{RT}$$

Wait — let me re-derive more carefully using the standard textbook approach. For **Van der Waals** as a clean example:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$

Multiply both sides by V/RT:

$$Z = \frac{V}{V - b} - \frac{a}{RTV}$$

Substitute V = ZRT/P:

$$Z = \frac{ZRT/P}{ZRT/P - b} - \frac{aP}{R^2T^2} \cdot \frac{1}{Z}$$

$$Z = \frac{Z}{Z - BP} \cdot \frac{P}{P} \quad \text{(rearranging)} $$

Let's use a cleaner standard form. Multiply original equation by V:

$$PV = \frac{RTV}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V}$$

Introduce A = aP/(R²T²) and B = bP/(RT). Then:

$$\frac{a}{V} = \frac{aP}{ZRT \cdot P} = \frac{A}{Z} \cdot RT \quad \text{(substituting } V = ZRT/P\text{)}$$

$$\frac{RTV}{V - b} = \frac{RT \cdot (ZRT/P)}{(ZRT/P) - b} = \frac{ZRT}{Z - bP/(RT)} = \frac{ZRT}{Z - B} \cdot \frac{1}{1} $$

Hmm, let me use the cleanest derivation. Multiply vdW by V:

$$PV = \frac{RTV}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V}$$

Rearrange to single fraction:

$$PV = \frac{RTV^2 - a(V - b)}{V(V - b)} = \frac{RTV^2 - aV + ab}{V^2 - bV}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$P(V^2 - bV) = RTV^2 - aV + ab$$

$$PV^2 - PbV = RTV^2 - aV + ab$$

$$(P)V^2 - (Pb + RT)V + ab - RTV^2 + aV = ab \quad \text{(rearranging)} $$

Let me restart cleanly:

$$PV^2 - PbV - RTV^2 + aV - ab = 0$$

$$(P - RT)V^2 + (a - Pb)V - ab = 0$$

Divide by P:

$$\left(1 - \frac{RT}{P}\right)V^2 + \left(\frac{a}{P} - b\right)V - \frac{ab}{P} = 0$$

$$\left(1 - \frac{1}{Z} \cdot \frac{RT}{P} \cdot \frac{P}{RT}\right)V^2 + \ldots$$

This is getting messy. The **cleanest** derivation uses Z directly. Substitute V = ZRT/P into vdW:

$$P = \frac{RT}{\frac{ZRT}{P} - b} - \frac{a}{\left(\frac{ZRT}{P}\right)^2}$$

$$P = \frac{RT \cdot P}{ZRT - bP} - \frac{aP^2}{Z^2 R^2 T^2}$$

$$P = \frac{PR}{ZR - bP/RT \cdot R/1} \quad \text{(incorrect — let me redo)} $$

$$P = \frac{PR}{ZR - bP/R} \cdot \frac{1}{RT} \quad \text{(no, this is wrong)} $$

**Clean Standard Derivation:** The textbook (Smith, Van Ness, Abbott) does it like this. Multiply vdW by V/RT:

$$Z = \frac{V}{V - b} - \frac{a}{RTV}$$

Let B = bP/(RT) and A = aP/(R²T²). Then V = ZRT/P, so:

$$\frac{V}{V - b} = \frac{ZRT/P}{ZRT/P - b} = \frac{Z}{Z - bP/(RT)} = \frac{Z}{Z - B}$$

$$\frac{a}{RTV} = \frac{aP}{RT \cdot ZRT} = \frac{aP}{Z R^2 T^2} = \frac{A}{Z}$$

Therefore:

$$Z = \frac{Z}{Z - B} - \frac{A}{Z}$$

Multiply both sides by Z(Z − B):

$$Z^2(Z - B) = Z^2 - A(Z - B)$$

$$Z^3 - BZ^2 = Z^2 - AZ + AB$$

$$Z^3 - BZ^2 - Z^2 + AZ - AB = 0$$

$$Z^3 - (1 + B)Z^2 + AZ - AB = 0$$

$$\boxed{Z^3 - (1 + B)Z^2 + AZ - AB = 0 \quad \text{(Van der Waals)}}$$

This matches the table: α₂ = −(1+B), α₁ = A, α₀ = −AB. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Newton-Raphson for Solving f(Z) = 0

Given a function f(Z) = Z³ + α₂Z² + α₁Z + α₀, Newton-Raphson iteration:

$$Z_{k+1} = Z_k - \frac{f(Z_k)}{f'(Z_k)}$$

$$f'(Z) = 3Z^2 + 2\alpha_2 Z + \alpha_1$$

**Convergence Criterion (KTU standard):**

$$\vert Z_{k+1} - Z_k \vert < 10^{-6} \quad \text{or} \quad \vert f(Z_k) \vert < 10^{-8}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 1: Van der Waals Root Finding (Hand + Tool)

**Problem:** Find Z for CO₂ at T = 350 K, P = 100 bar. Use Van der Waals EOS. Given: Tc = 304.2 K, Pc = 73.8 bar, R = 83.14 cm³·bar/(mol·K).

**Step 1: Compute EOS parameters**

$$a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c} = \frac{27 \times (83.14)^2 \times (304.2)^2}{64 \times 73.8}$$

$$= \frac{27 \times 6912.26 \times 92544.64}{4723.2} = \frac{1.7283 \times 10^{10}}{4723.2} = 3.659 \times 10^6 \text{ cm}^6 \text{ bar/mol}^2$$

$$b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c} = \frac{83.14 \times 304.2}{8 \times 73.8} = \frac{25291.2}{590.4} = 42.84 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}$$

**Step 2: Compute reduced A and B**

$$A = \frac{aP}{R^2 T^2} = \frac{3.659 \times 10^6 \times 100}{(83.14)^2 \times (350)^2} = \frac{3.659 \times 10^8}{6912.26 \times 122500}$$

$$= \frac{3.659 \times 10^8}{8.4675 \times 10^8} = 0.4321$$

$$B = \frac{bP}{RT} = \frac{42.84 \times 100}{83.14 \times 350} = \frac{4284}{29099} = 0.1472$$

**Step 3: Form the cubic**

$$Z^3 - (1 + 0.1472)Z^2 + 0.4321 Z - 0.4321 \times 0.1472 = 0$$

$$Z^3 - 1.1472 Z^2 + 0.4321 Z - 0.0636 = 0$$

**Step 4: Newton-Raphson (manual iterations starting Z₀ = 0.5)**

f(Z) = Z³ − 1.1472Z² + 0.4321Z − 0.0636
f'(Z) = 3Z² − 2.2944Z + 0.4321

| Iter k | Z_k | f(Z_k) | f'(Z_k) | Z_{k+1} |
|--------|------|---------|---------|---------|
| 0 | 0.5000 | −0.1187 | 0.0349 | 3.9022 |
| 1 | 3.9022 | 29.9464 | 36.9291 | 3.0913 |
| 2 | 3.0913 | 12.0413 | 23.6408 | 2.5820 |
| 3 | 2.5820 | 4.7257 | 16.9103 | 2.3025 |
| 4 | 2.3025 | 1.6770 | 14.0052 | 2.1828 |
| 5 | 2.1828 | 0.5238 | 12.7358 | 2.1417 |
| 6 | 2.1417 | 0.1486 | 12.2465 | 2.1296 |
| 7 | 2.1296 | 0.0406 | 11.9599 | 2.1262 |
| 8 | 2.1262 | 0.0109 | 11.8880 | 2.1253 |
| 9 | 2.1253 | 0.0029 | 11.8687 | 2.1250 |
| 10 | 2.1250 | 0.0008 | 11.8637 | 2.1250 ✓ |

**Converged Result: Z = 2.1250** (indicates supercritical/supersaturated vapor-like region — physically the larger of the three real roots is the vapor Z).

### 3.4 Computational Implementation in Python (Numerical Tool)

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import fsolve, brentq

# ---- INPUT DATA ----
T = 350.0          # K
P = 100.0          # bar
Tc = 304.2         # K
Pc = 73.8          # bar
R = 83.14          # cm^3·bar/(mol·K)
omega = 0.225      # acentric factor for CO2

# ---- VAN DER WAALS EOS ----
a = 27.0 * R**2 * Tc**2 / (64.0 * Pc)
b = R * Tc / (8.0 * Pc)
A = a * P / (R**2 * T**2)
B = b * P / (R * T)

# ---- CUBIC IN Z ----
coeffs = [1.0, -(1.0 + B), A, -A*B]
all_roots = np.roots(coeffs)
real_roots = all_roots[np.isreal(all_roots)].real
print("All real roots of vdW cubic:", real_roots)

# ---- VAPOR ROOT (largest real root) ----
Z_vap = np.max(real_roots)
V_vap = Z_vap * R * T / P
print(f"Vapor Z = {Z_vap:.4f},  V = {V_vap:.2f} cm^3/mol")

# ---- NEWTON-RAPHSON VERIFICATION ----
def f(Z):  return Z**3 - (1+B)*Z**2 + A*Z - A*B
def fp(Z): return 3*Z**2 - 2*(1+B)*Z + A

Z = 0.5
for k in range(20):
    dZ = f(Z)/fp(Z)
    Z = Z - dZ
    if abs(dZ) < 1e-10: break
print(f"Newton-Raphson converged Z = {Z:.6f} in {k+1} iterations")
```

### 3.5 Computational Implementation in MATLAB / SCILAB

```matlab
% MATLAB / SCILAB code
T = 350; P = 100; Tc = 304.2; Pc = 73.8; R = 83.14;

% Van der Waals parameters
a = 27*R^2*Tc^2/(64*Pc);
b = R*Tc/(8*Pc);
A = a*P/(R^2*T^2);
B = b*P/(R*T);

% Cubic coefficients [1, -alpha2, alpha1, alpha0]
c = [1, -(1+B), A, -A*B];
Z_all = roots(c);
Z_real = Z_all(imag(Z_all)==0);   % keep real roots
Z_vap = max(Z_real);

fprintf('Real roots: %s\n', mat2str(Z_real'));
fprintf('Vapor root Z = %.4f\n', Z_vap);

% Built-in solver as alternative
fhandle = @(Z) Z^3 - (1+B)*Z^2 + A*Z - A*B;
Z_solve = fzero(fhandle, 2.0);
fprintf('fzero solution Z = %.6f\n', Z_solve);
```

### 3.7 Worked Example 2: Bubble Pressure of Binary Ideal Solution

**Problem:** A liquid mixture contains 40 mol% n-butane (1) and 60 mol% n-pentane (2) at T = 60 °C = 333.15 K. Compute bubble pressure P and vapor composition yᵢ using Raoult's Law. Antoine constants (P^sat in mmHg, T in °C):

| Component | A | B | C |
|-----------|---|---|---|
| n-Butane | 6.80288 | 961.79 | 240.00 |
| n-Pentane | 6.85296 | 1064.63 | 232.00 |

**Step 1: Compute vapor pressures at T = 60 °C**

$$P_1^{sat} = 10^{6.80288 - 961.79/(60+240)} = 10^{6.80288 - 3.20797} = 10^{3.5949} = 3933.6 \text{ mmHg}$$

$$P_2^{sat} = 10^{6.85296 - 1064.63/(60+232)} = 10^{6.85296 - 3.6424} = 10^{3.2106} = 1623.5 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 2: Bubble pressure (closed-form for ideal solution)**

$$P = x_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 P_2^{sat} = 0.40 \times 3933.6 + 0.60 \times 1623.5$$

$$P = 1573.44 + 974.10 = 2547.54 \text{ mmHg} = 2547.54/750.06 = 3.397 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3: Vapor composition (Raoult's Law)**

$$y_1 = \frac{x_1 P_1^{sat}}{P} = \frac{0.40 \times 3933.6}{2547.54} = 0.6177$$

$$y_2 = 1 - y_1 = 0.3823$$

**Check:** y₁ + y₂ = 0.6177 + 0.3823 = 1.0000 ✓

### 3.8 Python Implementation of Bubble Pressure

```python
import numpy as np

# Antoine (mmHg, deg C)
A1, B1, C1 = 6.80288, 961.79, 240.00     # n-butane
A2, B2, C2 = 6.85296, 1064.63, 232.00    # n-pentane
T_C = 60.0
x1, x2 = 0.40, 0.60

# Vapor pressures (mmHg)
P1sat = 10**(A1 - B1/(T_C + C1))
P2sat = 10**(A2 - B2/(T_C + C2))

# Bubble pressure
P_bubble_mmHg = x1*P1sat + x2*P2sat
P_bubble_bar = P_bubble_mmHg * 1.01325/760
print(f"P1sat = {P1sat:.1f} mmHg,  P2sat = {P2sat:.1f} mmHg")
print(f"Bubble pressure = {P_bubble_mmHg:.1f} mmHg = {P_bubble_bar:.3f} bar")

# Vapor composition
y1 = x1*P1sat / P_bubble_mmHg
y2 = x2*P2sat / P_bubble_mmHg
print(f"y1 = {y1:.4f},  y2 = {y2:.4f},  sum = {y1+y2:.4f}")
```

### 3.9 Worked Example 3: Dew Point Temperature (Iterative)

**Problem:** Find dew temperature of a vapor mixture with y₁ = y₂ = 0.50 (benzene-toluene) at P = 1 atm = 760 mmHg.

**Antoine (mmHg, °C):** Benzene: A=6.90565, B=1211.033, C=220.790; Toluene: A=6.95464, B=1344.800, C=219.482.

**Dew Point Equation (implicit in T):**

$$f(T) = \sum_{i=1}^{2} y_i \frac{P}{P_i^{sat}(T)} - 1 = 0$$

**Newton-Raphson (initial guess T₀ = 100 °C):**

| Iter k | T_k (°C) | P_ben^sat | P_tol^sat | f(T) | T_{k+1} |
|--------|----------|-----------|-----------|------|---------|
| 0 | 100.0 | 1347.0 | 557.3 | 0.0719 | 96.39 |
| 1 | 96.39 | 1242.1 | 510.5 | −0.0091 | 96.86 |
| 2 | 96.86 | 1261.4 | 519.3 | −0.0009 | 96.92 |
| 3 | 96.92 | 1264.2 | 521.0 | −0.0001 | 96.93 |

**Converged: T_dew ≈ 96.93 °C**, x₁ = y₁P/P₁^sat = 0.5·760/1264 = 0.3007, x₂ = 0.6993.

### 3.10 Python Implementation of Dew Point (fsolve)

```python
from scipy.optimize import fsolve

A1, B1, C1 = 6.90565, 1211.033, 220.790   # benzene
A2, B2, C2 = 6.95464, 1344.800, 219.482   # toluene
y1, y2 = 0.5, 0.5
P = 760.0   # mmHg

def dew_func(T):
    P1sat = 10**(A1 - B1/(T + C1))
    P2sat = 10**(A2 - B2/(T + C2))
    return y1*P/P1sat + y2*P/P2sat - 1.0

T_dew = fsolve(dew_func, 100.0)[0]
print(f"Dew temperature = {T_dew:.2f} °C")
```

### 3.11 Convergence Diagnostics for KTU

| Issue | Symptom | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| Divergence in Newton-Raphson | Z values oscillate or shoot to infinity | Use Bisection or damped NR (α = 0.5) |
| Three real roots | Z = 0.05, 0.4, 0.9 | Liquid = smallest, vapor = largest (check Gibbs criteria) |
| No real roots (T > Tc at low P) | All complex | Z = 1 (ideal gas limit) — use Z = 0.99 as start |
| Antoine gives P^sat < 0 | T outside valid range | Use Clausius-Clapeyron fallback or check constants |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Computational Thermodynamics Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Thermodynamic Data T, P, Tc, Pc, omega, xi, yi] --> B[Choose EOS or Ideal Solution Model]
    B --> C{Is it EOS problem?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Compute a, b, A, B]
    D --> E[Form Cubic in Z]
    E --> F[Apply Root-Finding Method]
    F --> G[Select Physical Root smallest=liquid, largest=vapor]
    G --> H[Compute V, Z, departure functions]
    C -- No --> I[Ideal Solution VLE]
    I --> J{Bubble P / Dew T / Flash?}
    J -- Bubble P --> K[Direct formula: P = sum xi Pisat]
    J -- Dew T / Bubble T --> L[Iterative f T = 0 with Newton Raphson]
    J -- Flash --> M[Rachford Rice Equation + Iterative]
    K --> N[Output: yi, P, or T]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    H --> N
    N[Output: thermodynamic state properties and composition]
    N --> O[Validation: Compare with known data, check physical limits]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Root of the Cubic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Cubic yields 3 real roots Z1 less than Z2 less than Z3] --> Check1{Is the system<br/>in VLE region?}
    Check1 -- Yes --> LiqRoot[Liquid root = Z1 smallest]
    Check1 -- Yes --> VapRoot[Vapor root = Z3 largest]
    Check1 -- No --> CondCheck{Conditions}
    CondCheck -- P less than Psat --> SV[Superheated vapor: Z = Z3]
    CondCheck -- P greater than Psat --> SL[Subcooled liquid: Z = Z1]
    CondCheck -- Supercritical --> SC[Single real root: take that root]
    LiqRoot --> ComputeV[V = ZRT/P]
    VapRoot --> ComputeV
    SV --> ComputeV
    SL --> ComputeV
    SC --> ComputeV
    ComputeV --> Use[Compute phi, U_dep, H_dep, S_dep]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: From Spreadsheet to Solution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1[Spreadsheet Cell T] --> Calc1
    Input2[Spreadsheet Cell P] --> Calc1
    Input3[Spreadsheet Cell Tc, Pc, omega] --> Calc1
    Calc1[Compute a, b, A, B] --> Calc2[Form Cubic Coefficients]
    Calc2 --> Calc3[Build f of Z and f prime of Z columns]
    Calc3 --> Calc4[Enable Excel Iterative Calculation]
    Calc4 --> Calc5[Self Convergence]
    Calc5 --> Output[Cell shows Z, V, phi]
```

### 4.4 Algorithm Pseudocode: Newton-Raphson in Mermaid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Input T, P, EOS type, Z0, epsilon] --> A1[Compute A, B, a, b]
    A1 --> A2[Set k = 0, Z = Z0]
    A2 --> A3[Evaluate fZ = Z cubed + a2 Z squared + a1 Z + a0]
    A3 --> A4[Evaluate fpZ = 3 Z squared + 2 a2 Z + a1]
    A4 --> A5[Delta = fZ / fpZ]
    A5 --> A6[Z new = Z - Delta]
    A6 --> A7{Abs Delta less than epsilon?}
    A7 -- No --> A8[k = k + 1, Z = Z new]
    A8 --> A3
    A7 -- Yes --> A9[Output Z final]
```

### 4.5 Multi-Component VLE Computational Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Input: zi, T, P, Antoine constants] --> M2{Type of calculation}
    M2 -- Bubble P --> MP1[Initialize P low]
    MP1 --> MP2[Compute Pi_sat for all i]
    MP2 --> MP3[Compute yi = xi Pi_sat divided by P]
    MP3 --> MP4{Sum yi = 1?}
    MP4 -- No --> MP5[Update P and iterate]
    MP5 --> MP2
    MP4 -- Yes --> MP6[Output: P, yi]
    M2 -- Dew T --> MT1[Initialize T]
    MT1 --> MT2[Compute Pi_sat for all i]
    MT2 --> MT3[Compute xi = yi P divided by Pi_sat]
    MT3 --> MT4{Sum xi = 1?}
    MT4 -- No --> MT5[Update T and iterate]
    MT5 --> MT2
    MT4 -- Yes --> MT6[Output: T, xi]
```
