> "The standard Gibbs free energy change of a reaction is the free energy change accompanying a reaction in which the reactants and products are initially and finally in their standard states — pure substances at **1 bar** pressure, at a specified temperature, and with **unit activity** for solutes."

## 1.2 Standard State Convention (KTU 2024 Mandate)

| Species Type | Standard State |
|---|---|
| Pure solid | Pure solid at **1 bar** |
| Pure liquid | Pure liquid at **1 bar** |
| Gas | Pure gas at **1 bar**, behaving ideally |
| Solute in solution | **1 molal (m)** or **1 molar (M)** concentration |
| Element in standard state | $\Delta G_f^\circ = 0$ (reference point) |

> [!NOTE]
> The superscript "$\circ$" (degree or "naught") strictly denotes **standard conditions**. KTU examiners *frequently* deduct marks if this symbol is omitted. Always use $\Delta G^\circ$, **never** $\Delta G$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Reservoir

Imagine a hydroelectric reservoir at the top of a mountain:

- **$G$ (Gibbs free energy)** = The total "potential to do work" of the water in the reservoir.
- **$\Delta G^\circ$** = The maximum work you *could* extract if the water fell under **standard, controlled conditions** (fixed height, fixed flow rate at 1 bar atmospheric pressure).
- **$\Delta G$ (actual)** = The real work extracted under the actual operating conditions.
- **$K$ (equilibrium constant)** = The natural ratio of "downhill" water to "uphill" water when the system is at rest.

Just as a reservoir higher above sea level can deliver more energy, a reaction with a **large negative $\Delta G^\circ$** has a *thermodynamic potential* to do more useful work (e.g., drive a fuel cell, run an electrochemical engine, or power a chemical process).

## 1.4 Why Standard Free Energy Matters in Chemical Engineering

- It allows us to **tabulate thermodynamic data once** and reuse it to predict feasibility of thousands of reactions.
- It bridges **microscopic chemical energetics** with **macroscopic process design** (e.g., reactor feasibility, separation work, refrigerant selection).
- It is the **gatekeeper criterion** for reaction spontaneity at standard state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Misconception (Exam Trap):**
> A negative $\Delta G^\circ$ does *not* automatically mean a reaction is spontaneous in the actual reactor. Spontaneity is governed by $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$. Only at equilibrium ($Q = K$) does $\Delta G = 0$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Thermodynamic Pillars Connected to $\Delta G^\circ$

A KTU examiner expects a student to be fluent in **four relationships** that connect $\Delta G^\circ$ to other state functions. Master these and 80% of the marks in this topic are yours.

### Pillar 1 — Definition from Enthalpy and Entropy
$$ \boxed{\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ} $$

- $\Delta H^\circ$ = standard enthalpy change (heat released or absorbed at constant P)
- $\Delta S^\circ$ = standard entropy change (disorder change)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (**K**, not °C)

### Pillar 2 — Connection to Equilibrium Constant
$$ \boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K} \quad \text{or} \quad \boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -2.303 \, RT \log_{10} K} $$

- $R = \mathbf{8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$
- $K$ = thermodynamic equilibrium constant (dimensionless when activities are used)

### Pillar 3 — Temperature Dependence (Gibbs–Helmholtz)
$$ \boxed{\left( \frac{\partial (\Delta G^\circ / T)}{\partial T} \right)_P = - \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}} $$

Integrated form (assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is approximately constant over the temperature range):
$$ \boxed{\frac{\Delta G_2^\circ}{T_2} - \frac{\Delta G_1^\circ}{T_1} = \Delta H^\circ \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)} $$

### Pillar 4 — Calculation from Standard Free Energies of Formation
$$ \boxed{\Delta G_{rxn}^\circ = \sum_i \nu_i \, \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ \,(\text{products}) - \sum_i \nu_i \, \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ \,(\text{reactants})} $$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Application | Key Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$ | Compute $\Delta G^\circ$ from tabulated $\Delta H_f^\circ$ and $S^\circ$ | $\Delta G^\circ$: **kJ/mol** |
| 2 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ | Convert equilibrium data to standard free energy | $K$: **dimensionless** |
| 3 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$ | Spontaneity under **non-standard** conditions | $Q$: **reaction quotient** |
| 4 | $\Delta G_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ$ | Reaction energetics from formation data | $\Delta G_f^\circ$: **kJ/mol** |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\Delta G_2^\circ}{T_2} - \dfrac{\Delta G_1^\circ}{T_1} = \Delta H^\circ \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right)$ | Effect of T on $\Delta G^\circ$ | Temperatures in **K** |
| 6 | $\ln \dfrac{K_2}{K_1} = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right)$ | van't Hoff equation (K vs T) | $\Delta H^\circ$ in **J/mol** (use $R = 8.314$) |
| 7 | $\Delta G_{f}^\circ = 0$ for elements in reference form | Reference for tabulated data | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Pitfall:** In Pillar 1 and Pillar 4, $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta G^\circ$ are typically in **kJ/mol**, but the gas constant $R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$ requires **J**. Convert $\Delta H^\circ$ to J by multiplying by **1000** when using Pillar 2 or Pillar 3.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Reactor Feasibility Studies** — A negative $\Delta G^\circ$ at reactor temperature is a *necessary* (not sufficient) condition for a feasible industrial process.
2. **Electrochemical Cell Design** — The standard EMF (electromotive force) of a cell is directly tied to $\Delta G^\circ$ via $E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ / (nF)$.
3. **Refrigeration Cycle Selection** — Working fluids are screened by their $\Delta G_f^\circ$ stability.
4. **Corrosion Prediction** — Standard free energies of formation of oxides guide material selection.
5. **Process Simulation Software (Aspen, HYSYS)** — Internally, every Gibbs free-energy minimizer uses $\Delta G_f^\circ$ data banks (e.g., JANAF, DIPPR).

## 2.4 Sign Convention Logic

| Sign of $\Delta G^\circ$ | Equilibrium Constant | Reaction Tendency at Standard State |
|---|---|---|
| Large negative (< −40 kJ/mol) | $K \gg 1$ | Strongly product-favoured |
| Small negative | $K$ slightly > 1 | Mildly product-favoured |
| 0 | $K = 1$ | At equilibrium (standard state) |
| Small positive | $K$ slightly < 1 | Mildly reactant-favoured |
| Large positive (> +40 kJ/mol) | $K \ll 1$ | Strongly reactant-favoured |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ (The Master Equation)

### Starting Point: Chemical Potential Equality at Equilibrium
For a generic reaction
$$ aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD $$
the Gibbs free energy change at any state is
$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q $$
where $Q$ is the reaction quotient built from **activities**:
$$ Q = \frac{a_C^{\,c} \, a_D^{\,d}}{a_A^{\,a} \, a_B^{\,b}} $$

### Step 1: Apply the Equilibrium Condition
At equilibrium, the system is at a minimum of Gibbs free energy, so the driving force for further change is zero:
$$ \Delta G_{eq} = 0 $$

### Step 2: Recognize that $Q_{eq} = K$
By definition, the reaction quotient evaluated at the equilibrium composition is the equilibrium constant:
$$ Q_{eq} = K $$

### Step 3: Substitute into the Master Equation
$$ 0 = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K $$

### Step 4: Isolate $\Delta G^\circ$
Rearranging algebraically:
$$ \Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K $$

### Step 5: Convert to Common Logarithm
Using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:
$$ \Delta G^\circ = -2.303 \, RT \log_{10} K $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This is one of the most powerful equations in chemical engineering thermodynamics — it links a *measurable equilibrium state* ($K$) to a *theoretical thermodynamic potential* ($\Delta G^\circ$) computed from molecular formation data.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation

### Starting Point: The Combined First and Second Laws
$$ dG = V\,dP - S\,dT $$

At constant pressure ($dP = 0$):
$$ \left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_P = -S $$

### Step 1: Write $S$ in Terms of $G$ and $H$
From the definition $G = H - TS$, we can solve for $S$:
$$ S = \frac{H - G}{T} $$

### Step 2: Substitute into the Derivative
$$ \left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_P = -\frac{H - G}{T} = \frac{G - H}{T} $$

### Step 3: Apply the Product Rule on $G/T$
Compute the temperature derivative of $G/T$ (with $P$ held constant):
$$ \left[ \frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = \frac{1}{T} \left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_P - \frac{G}{T^2} $$

### Step 4: Substitute the Expression from Step 2
$$ \left[ \frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \left( \frac{G - H}{T} \right) - \frac{G}{T^2} = \frac{G - H - G}{T^2} = -\frac{H}{T^2} $$

### Step 5: Apply to the Reaction ($\Delta$ Operation)
Subtracting reactant from product contributions:
$$ \left[ \frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^2} $$

### Step 6: Integrate Assuming $\Delta H \approx$ constant
$$ \int_{T_1}^{T_2} d\!\left( \frac{\Delta G}{T} \right) = -\Delta H \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2} $$

$$ \frac{\Delta G_2^\circ}{T_2} - \frac{\Delta G_1^\circ}{T_1} = -\Delta H \left( -\frac{1}{T} \right)_{T_1}^{T_2} $$

$$ \boxed{\frac{\Delta G_2^\circ}{T_2} - \frac{\Delta G_1^\circ}{T_1} = \Delta H^\circ \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)} $$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — van't Hoff Equation

### Step 1: Start with $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$
Divide both sides by $T$:
$$ \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{T} = -R \ln K $$

### Step 2: Differentiate with Respect to $T$
$$ \frac{d}{dT} \!\left( \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{T} \right) = -R \frac{d \ln K}{dT} $$

### Step 3: Apply the Gibbs–Helmholtz Result
The left side equals $-\Delta H^\circ / T^2$, so:
$$ -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2} = -R \frac{d \ln K}{dT} $$

$$ \frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2} $$

### Step 4: Separate Variables and Integrate
$$ \int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2} $$

$$ \boxed{\ln \frac{K_2}{K_1} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)} $$

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** For the reaction
$$ \text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g) $$
the standard free energy change at **298 K** is $\Delta G^\circ = -33.0 \; kJ/mol$. Calculate the equilibrium constant $K$ at this temperature.

### Step 1: Convert Units Consistently
$$ \Delta G^\circ = -33.0 \times 10^3 \; J/mol = -33000 \; J/mol $$

### Step 2: Apply $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$
$$ -33000 = -(8.314)(298) \ln K $$

### Step 3: Solve for $\ln K$
$$ \ln K = \frac{33000}{(8.314)(298)} $$

$$ \ln K = \frac{33000}{2477.572} = 13.32 $$

### Step 4: Solve for $K$
$$ K = e^{13.32} $$

$$ K = 6.16 \times 10^5 $$

### Step 5: Interpret
A very large $K$ value confirms that ammonia synthesis is thermodynamically product-favoured at 298 K. (In practice, the rate is negligible without a catalyst — illustrating why $\Delta G^\circ$ is a *feasibility* check, not a *rate* check.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Verify dimensional correctness:** The argument of $\ln$ is dimensionless, so $K$ is dimensionless. Always report $K$ in dimensionless form when used with this equation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map of Standard Free Energy Change

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Standard States: 1 bar, 298 K, unit activity] --> B[Standard Free Energy Change DELTA G NAUGHT]
    B --> C1[PILLAR 1: DELTA H NAUGHT minus T DELTA S NAUGHT]
    B --> C2[PILLAR 2: minus R T ln K]
    B --> C3[PILLAR 3: Gibbs Helmholtz Temperature Dependence]
    B --> C4[PILLAR 4: Sum of DELTA G formation terms]
    C1 --> D[Enthalpy and Entropy Data Banks]
    C2 --> E[Equilibrium Constant K from Experiment or Reactor Data]
    C3 --> F[Effect of T on Reaction Feasibility]
    C4 --> G[Tabulated Standard Free Energies of Formation]
    D --> H[Industrial Applications: Reactor Design, Fuel Cells, Process Simulation]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Final Decision: Process Feasibility and Yield Optimization]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree for Spontaneity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Reaction at Given T] --> S1{Standard Conditions?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{Check sign of DELTA G NAUGHT}
    S1 -- No --> S3{Compute DELTA G equals DELTA G NAUGHT plus R T ln Q}
    S2 -- DELTA G NAUGHT less than 0 --> S4[Spontaneous at Standard State]
    S2 -- DELTA G NAUGHT greater than 0 --> S5[Non spontaneous at Standard State]
    S2 -- DELTA G NAUGHT equals 0 --> S6[At Equilibrium under Standard State]
    S3 -- DELTA G less than 0 --> S7[Spontaneous under Actual Conditions]
    S3 -- DELTA G greater than 0 --> S8[Non spontaneous under Actual Conditions]
    S3 -- DELTA G equals 0 --> S9[At Equilibrium under Actual Conditions]
    S4 --> END[KTU Result: Reaction Proceeds Forward]
    S5 --> END
    S6 --> END
    S7 --> END
    S8 --> END
    S9 --> END
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Data Flow for $\Delta G^\circ$ Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Input: Species Stoichiometry] --> PROC
    INPUT2[Input: Temperature T in Kelvin] --> PROC
    INPUT3[Input: Tabulated DELTA H formation and S NAUGHT] --> PROC
    INPUT4[Input: Tabulated DELTA G formation] --> PROC
    INPUT5[Input: Experimental K value] --> PROC
    PROC[Central Computation Engine: DELTA G NAUGHT Calculator]
    PROC --> OUT1[Output 1: DELTA G NAUGHT equals DELTA H NAUGHT minus T DELTA S NAUGHT]
    PROC --> OUT2[Output 2: DELTA G NAUGHT equals minus R T ln K]
    PROC --> OUT3[Output 3: DELTA G NAUGHT equals sum of stoichiometric DELTA G formation]
    PROC --> OUT4[Output 4: Cross Verification and Consistency Check]
    OUT4 --> DECISION[Decision: Spontaneity Classification and Feasibility Flag]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these diagrams:** In Mermaid, the rectangular nodes represent data states, and the arrows represent logical or computational flows. The **PROC** node is the central processing step where a student would perform the actual numerical substitution in a KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
Define the **standard free energy change** of a chemical reaction. State the standard states used for solids, liquids, gases, and solutes.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The standard free energy change ($\Delta G^\circ$) of a reaction is the change in Gibbs free energy when reactants in their standard states are completely converted to products in their standard states at a specified temperature (usually 298.15 K). **(1 mark)**
The standard states are:
- **Solids and liquids:** pure substance at **1 bar** pressure. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Gases:** pure gas at **1 bar** with ideal behaviour. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Solutes:** concentration of **1 molal (m)** or **1 molar (M)**. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Elements in their reference form:** $\Delta G_f^\circ = 0$. **(0.5 mark)**

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
State the relationship between standard free energy change and the equilibrium constant. What is the sign of $\Delta G^\circ$ when $K > 1$?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The relationship is:
$$ \Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K $$
where $R$ is the universal gas constant and $T$ is the absolute temperature. **(2 marks)**
When $K > 1$, the natural logarithm $\ln K$ is positive, so $\Delta G^\circ$ must be **negative**. **(1 mark)** This means the reaction is product-favoured at standard state.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. *Always* show units in every line of numerical work — half-mark deductions occur otherwise.
> 2. Convert all $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta G^\circ$ values to **J/mol** when used with $R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$.
> 3. State the standard state condition **explicitly** at the start of any derivation question — examiners allocate one mark for this.
> 4. When using tabulated $\Delta G_f^\circ$ data, always write the formation reaction first; students lose marks for "directly writing the answer".

### Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ starting from the definition of Gibbs free energy change for a non-standard reaction. Clearly state the equilibrium condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the water-gas shift reaction
$$ \text{CO}(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2(g) $$
the standard free energies of formation at 298 K are:
- $\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) = -394.36 \; kJ/mol$
- $\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CO}) = -137.17 \; kJ/mol$
- $\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, g) = -228.57 \; kJ/mol$
- $\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{H}_2) = 0 \; kJ/mol$

Calculate $\Delta G^\circ$ for the reaction and hence determine the equilibrium constant $K$ at 298 K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
For a generic reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$, the change in Gibbs free energy at arbitrary state is:
$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q $$
where $Q = \dfrac{a_C^c \, a_D^d}{a_A^a \, a_B^b}$ is the reaction quotient in terms of activities.

At equilibrium, the system has no further driving force, so $\Delta G = 0$. The reaction quotient becomes the equilibrium constant: $Q_{eq} = K$. Substituting:
$$ 0 = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K $$
This gives the desired relationship. **(Full 7 marks distributed as: state of generic reaction — 2 marks, equilibrium condition — 2 marks, algebraic isolation — 3 marks.)**

**Part (b) — Calculation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Apply the formation data formula:
$$ \Delta G_{rxn}^\circ = [\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) + \Delta G_f^\circ(\text{H}_2)] - [\Delta G_f^\circ(\text{CO}) + \Delta G_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O})] $$

Step 2: Substitute values:
$$ \Delta G_{rxn}^\circ = [(-394.36) + (0)] - [(-137.17) + (-228.57)] \; kJ/mol $$

Step 3: Simplify:
$$ \Delta G_{rxn}^\circ = -394.36 - (-365.74) = -394.36 + 365.74 = -28.62 \; kJ/mol $$

Step 4: Convert to J/mol:
$$ \Delta G_{rxn}^\circ = -28620 \; J/mol $$

Step 5: Apply $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$:
$$ -28620 = -(8.314)(298) \ln K $$

Step 6: Solve for $\ln K$:
$$ \ln K = \frac{28620}{(8.314)(298)} = \frac{28620}{2477.572} = 11.55 $$

Step 7: Solve for $K$:
$$ K = e^{11.55} = 1.04 \times 10^5 $$

Step 8: Conclude:
The water-gas shift reaction is thermodynamically product-favoured at 298 K, with an equilibrium constant on the order of $10^5$.

### Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, Alternative]**

**(a)** Starting from the combined first and second law, derive the **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation** in the form
$$ \left( \frac{\partial (\Delta G^\circ / T)}{\partial T} \right)_P = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2} $$
Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a reaction, $\Delta H^\circ = -90.0 \; kJ/mol$ and $\Delta G^\circ_{298} = -50.0 \; kJ/mol$. Estimate the temperature at which $\Delta G^\circ$ becomes zero. Comment on the practical significance of this temperature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
From the combined law, at constant pressure: $(\partial G/\partial T)_P = -S$. Since $G = H - TS$, we have $S = (H - G)/T$. Substituting:
$$ \left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_P = -\frac{H - G}{T} = \frac{G - H}{T} $$

Apply the product rule to $G/T$:
$$ \left[ \frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = \frac{1}{T}\left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_P - \frac{G}{T^2} $$

Substitute the earlier result:
$$ \left[ \frac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{G - H}{T} - \frac{G}{T^2} = \frac{G - H - G}{T^2} = -\frac{H}{T^2} $$

Apply the same to the reaction: $\boxed{\left[ \dfrac{\partial (\Delta G^\circ / T)}{\partial T} \right]_P = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}}$ **(Full 7 marks)**

**Assumption:** $\Delta H^\circ$ is approximately independent of $T$ over the integration range (which we adopt in part b).

**Part (b) — Calculation (7 marks):**

Step 1: From $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$, find $\Delta S^\circ$ at 298 K:
$$ \Delta S^\circ = \frac{\Delta H^\circ - \Delta G^\circ_{298}}{T} = \frac{-90000 - (-50000)}{298} = \frac{-40000}{298} = -134.23 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1} $$

Step 2: Set $\Delta G^\circ = 0$:
$$ 0 = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ \;\Rightarrow\; T = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ} $$

Step 3: Substitute (assuming $\Delta S^\circ$ stays approximately constant):
$$ T = \frac{-90000 \; J/mol}{-134.23 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}} = 670.4 \; K $$

Step 4: Convert to Celsius (optional, for context):
$$ T \approx 670.4 - 273.15 \approx 397.2 \; ^\circ C $$

Step 5: Interpret:
Above this temperature, $\Delta G^\circ$ becomes positive — the reaction switches from product-favoured to reactant-favoured. For an exothermic reaction with a negative $\Delta S^\circ$, increasing temperature drives the reaction *backward*, so industrial reactors must operate *below* this crossover temperature to maintain yield. **(7 marks distributed as: 2 for entropy calculation, 2 for temperature derivation, 2 for unit conversion, 1 for industrial interpretation.)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in B1(b):** Students often write $T = \Delta H^\circ / \Delta S^\circ$ *without* confirming that $\Delta S^\circ$ was first computed. Without this step, the derivation is incomplete and loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Standard Free Energy Change**

- **Definition:** $\Delta G^\circ$ is the free energy change when reactants/products are in their **standard states** (1 bar, 298 K, unit activity). The "$\circ$" symbol is *mandatory*.
- **Master Equation:** $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ (or $= -2.303 \, RT \log K$).
- **Definition Equation:** $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$.
- **Formation Data:** $\Delta G_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ (\text{products}) - \sum \nu_i \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ (\text{reactants})$.
- **Gibbs–Helmholtz:** $\dfrac{\Delta G_2^\circ}{T_2} - \dfrac{\Delta G_1^\circ}{T_1} = \Delta H^\circ \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right)$.
- **van't Hoff:** $\ln \dfrac{K_2}{K_1} = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right)$.
- **Spontaneity Rule:** Negative $\Delta G^\circ \Rightarrow$ product-favoured; positive $\Delta G^\circ \Rightarrow$ reactant-favoured.
- **Unit Vigilance:** $\Delta H^\circ$, $\Delta G^\circ$ in **kJ/mol**; $R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$; convert by $\times 1000$.
- **Temperature in Kelvin:** Always convert from °C by adding 273.15.
- **Element in Standard State:** $\Delta G_f^\circ = 0$ (e.g., O₂, N₂, H₂, graphite).
- **Sign Logic:** Exothermic ($\Delta H^\circ < 0$) + $\Delta S^\circ > 0$ → spontaneous at all $T$.
- **Exam Hall Mantra:** Write the equation → substitute values → check units → solve → state physical interpretation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feasibility of Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Feasibility of Reaction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Feasibility of a Reaction** is the thermodynamic criterion that determines whether a given chemical reaction can proceed spontaneously in a specified direction (at constant temperature and pressure) under a given set of operating conditions. Mathematically, a reaction is **feasible (spontaneous)** in the forward direction if and only if the **Gibbs Free Energy change of the reaction** is **negative**, i.e., $\Delta G_{rxn} < 0$ for the reacting system at the specified $T$ and $P$. In the language of KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education, this ties directly to **CO4 — Apply the principles of reaction equilibria and residual properties to determine the feasibility and extent of chemical reactions** and uses the cognitive level **Apply / Analyse** of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water on a Hill" Analogy Imagine a ball sitting on a hilltop. Without any external push, the ball will **always roll downhill** because gravity pulls it toward a state of **lower potential energy**. It will *never* spontaneously roll uphill (that would require an external energy input). Chemical reactions behave the same way: - The **"hill"** = Gibbs Free Energy ($G$) — the chemical "potential energy" of the system. - The **"ball"** = the reacting mixture (reactants ⇌ products). - A reaction is **feasible (spontaneous)** if the system can "roll downhill," meaning $G_{\text{products}} < G_{\text{reactants}}$, so $\Delta G < 0$. - A reaction is **at equilibrium** when the ball sits in the valley: $\Delta G = 0$. - A reaction is **non-feasible (non-spontaneous)** if the ball would have to be pushed uphill: $\Delta G > 0$. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction — KTU frequently tests this:** > - *Feasibility* $\neq$ *Rate of Reaction*. A reaction may be thermodynamically feasible ($\Delta G < 0$) but kinetically so slow that it appears not to occur (e.g., the diamond → graphite conversion). > - Thermodynamics tells us **IF** and **HOW FAR**, not **HOW FAST**. ### 1.3 The Three Thermodynamic Regimes | Regime | Sign of $\Delta G$ | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **Spontaneous / Feasible** | $\Delta G < 0$ | Reaction proceeds forward of its own accord; useful work can be extracted. | | **At Equilibrium** | $\Delta G = 0$ | No net reaction; forward rate = reverse rate; no driving force remains. | | **Non-Spontaneous / Non-Feasible** | $\Delta G > 0$ | Reaction will not proceed forward; reverse reaction is feasible instead. | ### 1.4 Standard-State Reference > [!IMPORTANT] > For pure compounds, the **standard Gibbs free energy of formation** $\Delta G_f^\circ$ is defined at **$T = 298.15\,\text{K}$** and **$P = 1\,\text{atm}$ (or 1 bar)**, with elements in their **reference (most stable) form** assigned $\Delta G_f^\circ = 0$. The standard Gibbs change for any reaction is then computed as: > $$\Delta G_{rxn}^\circ = \sum_i \nu_i \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum_j \nu_j \Delta G_{f,j}^\circ(\text{reactants})$$ > This is the canonical "KTU board formula" — *memorise it.* ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Master Criterion — Why Gibbs Free Energy? For a **closed system at constant $T$ and $P$**, the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** gives the Clausius inequality: $$\Delta G_{sys} \leq W_{\text{useful,non-PV}}$$ - If no useful work is done (other than the $PV$ work of expansion), then $\Delta G_{sys} \leq 0$. - This means the Gibbs function **monotonically decreases** until equilibrium ($\Delta G = 0$) is reached. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Aid:** *"At constant T and P, the universe is a minimiser of G."* Equilibrium corresponds to the **global minimum of $G$**. ### 2.2 Derivation of the Master Equation (Outline) From the fundamental relation: $$dG = V\,dP - S\,dT$$ For an *isothermal, isobaric* reaction mixture, $dT = dP = 0$, hence $dG_{\text{system}} = 0$ only at equilibrium. The change in $G$ as the reaction proceeds from pure reactants ($\xi = 0$) to pure products ($\xi = 1$) is the reaction Gibbs energy: $$\Delta G_{rxn} = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i \quad \text{(at the actual system state)}$$ where $\mu_i$ is the **chemical potential** of species $i$ and $\nu_i$ is the **stoichiometric coefficient** (positive for products, negative for reactants). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | **Master criterion** — sign of $\Delta G$ decides feasibility at given $T$. | $\text{J/mol}$ or $\text{kJ/mol}$ | | 2 | $\Delta G^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ$ | Standard Gibbs change of reaction at $298.15\,\text{K}$, $1\,\text{atm}$. | $\text{kJ/mol}$ | | 3 | $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$ | Same as (1) but evaluated at **standard state**. | $\text{kJ/mol}$ | | 4 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ | Links standard Gibbs change to the **thermodynamic equilibrium constant**. | $\text{kJ/mol}$ | | 5 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$ | **Reaction at non-standard conditions**; $Q$ = reaction quotient. | $\text{kJ/mol}$ | | 6 | $\left[\dfrac{\partial(\Delta G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^2}$ | **Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation** — temperature dependence of $\Delta G$. | $\text{K}^{-1}$ | | 7 | $\left[\dfrac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\Delta S$ | Direct temperature derivative. | $\text{kJ/(mol·K)}$ | | 8 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | **van't Hoff Equation** — how $K$ varies with $T$. | Dimensionless | | 9 | $\Delta G_{\text{max,useful}} = -\Delta G_{rxn}$ | Maximum non-expansion (useful) work obtainable. | $\text{kJ/mol}$ | | 10 | $\Delta G = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i$ | General definition; $\nu_i > 0$ for products. | $\text{kJ/mol}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convention on Units (KTU Board Standard):** > - $\Delta G$ in **kJ/mol** → use $R = 8.314 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kJ/(mol·K)}$. > - $\Delta G$ in **J/mol** → use $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$. > - Always quote $T$ in **Kelvin**. $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$. ### 2.4 Physical Interpretation of $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ on Feasibility A beautiful 2×2 analysis that KTU loves to ask: | Case | $\Delta H$ | $\Delta S$ | Feasibility of Forward Reaction | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 1 | $-$ (exothermic) | $+$ (entropy increase) | **Feasible at ALL temperatures** ($-T\Delta S < 0$ reinforces $\Delta H < 0$). | | 2 | $-$ (exothermic) | $-$ (entropy decrease) | Feasible only at **LOW $T$**. | | 3 | $+$ (endothermic) | $+$ (entropy increase) | Feasible only at **HIGH $T$**. | | 4 | $+$ (endothermic) | $-$ (entropy decrease) | **NEVER feasible** (both terms positive). | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Ammonia Synthesis (Haber–Bosch):** Exothermic with negative $\Delta S$ (4 moles → 2 moles). Le Chatelier + feasibility: requires **low $T$** but high $T$ is needed for rate → industrial compromise at $\sim 700\,\text{K}$ with a catalyst. 2. **Methanol from syngas:** Similarly $\Delta H < 0$, $\Delta S < 0$ → feasible at moderate $T$, very high $P$. 3. **Decomposition reactions (e.g., limestone calcination):** $\Delta H > 0$, $\Delta S > 0$ → feasible **only above the "decomposition temperature"** where $T > \Delta H/\Delta S$. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — The Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation (Board-Favourite) **Starting point:** The definition of the Gibbs function: $$G = H - TS$$ **Step 1:** Differentiate $G$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$: $$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P - S - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P$$ **Step 2:** Use the identity $C_P = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$ and the Maxwell relation $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{C_P}{T}$: $$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_P \cdot T}{T} - S - \frac{T \cdot C_P}{T} = -S$$ So we have the **fundamental identity**: $$\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$$ **Step 3:** Apply this to the *reaction* quantities. For a reaction $A \to B$ at constant $P$: $$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\Delta S$$ **Step 4:** Convert into a usable form. Recall $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$, hence: $$\Delta S = \frac{\Delta H - \Delta G}{T}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute back: $$\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\frac{\Delta H - \Delta G}{T} = \frac{\Delta G - \Delta H}{T}$$ **Step 6:** Rearrange. Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{1}{\Delta G}$ and combine the $T$ terms: $$\frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_P - \frac{\Delta G}{T^2} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^2}$$ **Step 7:** The left-hand side is exactly the derivative of $\dfrac{\Delta G}{T}$ with respect to $T$ (quotient rule): $$\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right) = \frac{T \cdot (\partial \Delta G/\partial T) - \Delta G}{T^2}$$ **Final result — Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation:** $$\boxed{\;\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right)\right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^2}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is a **board favourite** in KTU 2024. Examiner pattern: *state it, derive it, then apply it to compute $\Delta G$ at an arbitrary $T_2$ given $\Delta G_1$ at $T_1$ and $\Delta H$.* --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — Integrated Form (Useful for Numerical Problems) **Starting from Gibbs–Helmholtz:** $$\frac{d(\Delta G/T)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^2}$$ **Step 1:** Assume $\Delta H$ is **approximately constant** over the temperature range (a common KTU assumption for short intervals): $$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} d\!\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right) = -\Delta H \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$ **Step 2:** Integrate: $$\frac{\Delta G_2}{T_2} - \frac{\Delta G_1}{T_1} = -\Delta H \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = -\Delta H \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $\Delta G_2$: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\Delta G_2}{T_2} = \frac{\Delta G_1}{T_1} + \Delta H \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\;}$$ **If $\Delta H$ is **not** constant** (use Kirchhoff's law for temperature dependence of $\Delta H$): $$\Delta H(T) = \Delta H_0 + \int_{T_0}^{T} \Delta C_P \, dT'$$ where $\Delta C_P = \sum \nu_i C_{P,i}$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation III — $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ (Rigorous Board Derivation) Consider the generic reaction: $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$ at temperature $T$. **Step 1:** The reaction Gibbs energy at arbitrary composition is: $$\Delta G = c\,\mu_C + d\,\mu_D - a\,\mu_A - b\,\mu_B$$ **Step 2:** Express each $\mu_i$ in terms of its standard chemical potential $\mu_i^\circ$ and activity $a_i$: $$\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln a_i$$ **Step 3:** Substitute and collect the standard-state terms: $$\Delta G = \underbrace{(c\mu_C^\circ + d\mu_D^\circ - a\mu_A^\circ - b\mu_B^\circ)}_{\Delta G^\circ} + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{a_C^c a_D^d}{a_A^a a_B^b}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Define the **reaction quotient** $Q = \dfrac{a_C^c a_D^d}{a_A^a a_B^b}$: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 5:** At **chemical equilibrium**, $\Delta G = 0$ and $Q = K$ (the equilibrium constant): $$0 = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K$$ **Step 6:** Solve: $$\boxed{\;\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **mandatory derivation** for KTU Board. Examiner pattern: "Derive the relation between $\Delta G^\circ$ and $K$ for the reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$." (14-mark question in ESE.) --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style) **Problem:** For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, given: - $\Delta H_{298}^\circ = -92.4\,\text{kJ/mol}$ - $\Delta G_{298}^\circ = -33.2\,\text{kJ/mol}$ - $R = 8.314 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kJ/(mol·K)}$ **Find:** (a) $\Delta S_{298}^\circ$, (b) Is the reaction feasible at $298\,\text{K}$? (c) At what temperature does the reaction become **infeasible** ($\Delta G = 0$)? **Solution:** **Part (a):** Use $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$: $$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{\Delta H^\circ - \Delta G^\circ}{T} = \frac{(-92.4) - (-33.2)}{298} = \frac{-59.2}{298} = -0.1987\,\text{kJ/(mol·K)}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta S_{298}^\circ = -198.7\,\text{J/(mol·K)}}$$ **Part (b):** Since $\Delta G_{298}^\circ = -33.2\,\text{kJ/mol} < 0$, the reaction **is feasible at 298 K**. **Part (c):** At the crossover temperature, $\Delta G = 0$, hence: $$T_{\text{crossover}} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ} = \frac{-92.4}{-0.1987} = 465.0\,\text{K}$$ (Assuming $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ are temperature-independent — a KTU-acceptable approximation for this problem.) > **Conclusion:** The reaction is feasible for $T < 465\,\text{K}$ and becomes infeasible above this temperature, confirming the practical observation that Haber–Bosch cannot be run at very high $T$ for equilibrium reasons (it is done at high $T$ only for **kinetic** reasons). --- ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "How to Solve a KTU Feasibility Problem" | Given Data | Strategy | Key Equation to Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\Delta H^\circ$, $\Delta S^\circ$, $T$ | Direct sign of $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$. | Equation (1) | | $\Delta G_f^\circ$ of all species | Compute $\Delta G^\circ$, then infer feasibility. | Equation (2) | | $\Delta G^\circ$ at one $T$, want at another $T$ | Use integrated Gibbs–Helmholtz with constant $\Delta H$. | Section 3.2 | | $K$ at temperature $T$ | Compute $\Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K$; sign decides. | Equation (4) | | Activities/partial pressures known | Compute $Q$, use $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT\ln Q$. | Equation (5) | | Need $K$ from $\Delta G^\circ$ | Direct: $K = \exp(-\Delta G^\circ/RT)$. | Equation (4) inverted | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Decision Flowchart — Is My Reaction Feasible? This diagram is the **mental scaffolding** every KTU student should have before entering the exam hall. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Reaction Specified"] --> B["Identify reaction stoichiometry and species"] B --> C["Gather thermodynamic data: dH_f, dG_f, S, or K"] C --> D{"Which data is
available?"} D -- "dG_f values" --> E1["Compute dG_rxn = Sum nu_i dG_f_i"] D -- "dH and S" --> E2["Compute dG = dH - TdS at given T"] D -- "K at T" --> E3["Compute dG = -RT ln K"] E1 --> F{"Sign of dG?"} E2 --> F E3 --> F F -- "dG less than 0" --> G1["FEASIBLE in forward direction"] F -- "dG equals 0" --> G2["AT EQUILIBRIUM: no net reaction"] F -- "dG greater than 0" --> G3["NOT FEASIBLE forward; reverse is feasible"] G1 --> H1["Optionally compute K = exp(-dG/RT)"] G2 --> H2["Equilibrium composition via K"] G3 --> H3["Compute reverse dG = -dG forward"] H1 --> I["End: Report feasibility + K"] H2 --> I H3 --> I ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the chart:** Always start at the top and follow the branch matching the data you are given. The decision box "Sign of $\Delta G$" is the heart of every feasibility problem. --- ### 4.2 Reaction Coordinate Diagram — Gibbs Free Energy Profile This illustrates how $G$ evolves as the reaction proceeds from reactants to products. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Reactants
G_R = high"] -->|"dG negative
spontaneous"| B["Transition state
G_max = activation barrier
Note: kinetically controlled"] B --> C["Products
G_P = low"] A -->|"dG positive
non-spontaneous"| D["Reaction NOT feasible"] C -->|"dG reverse positive"| E["Reverse reaction
NOT feasible"] E2["Equilibrium point
dG = 0
min of G vs extent"] -.-> A E2 -.-> C style A fill:#FFB6B6 style C fill:#B6FFB6 style B fill:#FFFFB6 style D fill:#E0E0E0 style E fill:#E0E0E0 style E2 fill:#B6E0FF ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Distinguish the two "barriers" in this diagram:** > 1. **Thermodynamic driving force** = vertical drop $G_R - G_P = -\Delta G$. Governs **feasibility**. > 2. **Kinetic activation barrier** = vertical rise $G_{\max} - G_R$. Governs **rate**. > > Thermodynamics cannot lower the activation barrier — that is the job of a **catalyst**. --- ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Feasibility Engine For complex multi-reaction or multi-constraint problems, use this architectural map. ```mermaid flowchart TD Input["INPUT BLOCK
Reaction stoichiometry
T, P, composition"] --> Data["DATA ACQUISITION
Look up dH_f, S, C_P"] Data --> StateCalc["STATE CALCULATION
Compute dH, dS, dG at T"] StateCalc --> Crit{"FEASIBILITY
CRITERION
dG less than 0?"} Crit -- "YES" --> Out1["OUTPUT 1
Reaction feasible
Compute K from dG"] Crit -- "NO" --> Out2["OUTPUT 2
Not feasible forward
Reverse is feasible"] StateCalc --> Sens["SENSITIVITY BLOCK
van't Hoff analysis
Effect of T on K"] StateCalc --> Max["MAX WORK BLOCK
W_max useful = -dG"] Out1 --> Rec["RECOMMENDATION
T, P, catalyst choice"] Out2 --> Rec Sens --> Rec Max --> Rec style Input fill:#B6E0FF style Crit fill:#FFFFB6 style Out1 fill:#B6FFB6 style Out2 fill:#FFB6B6 style Rec fill:#E0B6FF ``` --- ### 4.4 Effect of Temperature on Feasibility — Visual Summary ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Sign of dH"] --> B{"dH negative?"} B -- "YES exothermic" --> C["Sign of dS"] B -- "NO endothermic" --> D["Sign of dS"] C -- "dS positive" --> E["FEASIBLE at ALL T"] C -- "dS negative" --> F["Feasible at LOW T only
T less than dH/dS"] D -- "dS positive" --> G["Feasible at HIGH T only
T greater than dH/dS"] D -- "dS negative" --> H["NEVER FEASIBLE"] style E fill:#B6FFB6 style F fill:#B6E0FF style G fill:#FFE0B6 style H fill:#FFB6B6 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Q1a]** **Q1.** *State the thermodynamic criterion for the feasibility of a chemical reaction at constant temperature and pressure. How is it related to the useful work obtainable?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** A reaction is feasible (spontaneous) at constant $T$ and $P$ if $\Delta G_{rxn} < 0$. - **(1 mark)** The criterion follows from the Second Law applied to a closed system at constant $T$ and $P$: $dG_{T,P} \leq 0$ for any spontaneous process. - **(1 mark)** The maximum useful (non-$PV$) work obtainable from the reaction equals $-\Delta G_{rxn}$, i.e., $W_{\max,\text{useful}} = -\Delta G_{rxn}$. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Q1c]** **Q2.** *For the reaction $\text{CaCO}_3(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g)$, $\Delta H^\circ = 178.3\,\text{kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta S^\circ = 160.5\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$. Calculate the decomposition temperature.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** Reaction becomes feasible when $\Delta G = 0 \Rightarrow T = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ}$. - **(1 mark)** $T = \dfrac{178300}{160.5} = 1110.9\,\text{K}$. - **(1 mark)** Therefore the decomposition temperature is approximately $1111\,\text{K}$ ($\approx 838^{\circ}\text{C}$). Below this $T$, $\Delta G > 0$ and $\text{CaCO}_3$ is stable; above it, decomposition is feasible. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, ESE Module 4, Q3]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** *Derive the relationship $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ for the generic reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$ starting from the definition of chemical potential.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, at $700\,\text{K}$ the equilibrium constant $K_P = 6.8 \times 10^{-5}$. Compute:* **(7 marks)** 1. The standard Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G^\circ$ at $700\,\text{K}$. 2. The equilibrium degree of dissociation if the reaction starts with a stoichiometric mixture at $100\,\text{atm}$ total pressure (assume ideal-gas behaviour). 3. Comment on the **feasibility** of ammonia synthesis at this temperature and pressure. --- #### **Model Solution — Question A** **Part (a) — 7 marks derivation:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the reaction Gibbs energy in terms of chemical potentials: $$\Delta G = c\,\mu_C + d\,\mu_D - a\,\mu_A - b\,\mu_B$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute the activity-based expression for each $\mu_i$: $$\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ(T,P^\circ) + RT \ln a_i$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Expand: $$\Delta G = (c\mu_C^\circ + d\mu_D^\circ - a\mu_A^\circ - b\mu_B^\circ) + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{a_C^c \, a_D^d}{a_A^a \, a_B^b}\right)$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify the first bracket as $\Delta G^\circ$ and define the reaction quotient $Q$: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q, \quad Q = \frac{a_C^c \, a_D^d}{a_A^a \, a_B^b}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** At **chemical equilibrium**, $\Delta G = 0$ and $Q = K$: $$0 = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Final result: $$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Brief note on units — when $K$ is dimensionless, the equation is consistent; $K$ must use activities referenced to standard state. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks numerical:** **(i) Standard Gibbs change (2 marks):** $$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_P = -(8.314 \times 10^{-3})(700) \ln(6.8 \times 10^{-5})$$ $$\ln(6.8 \times 10^{-5}) = \ln 6.8 + \ln 10^{-5} = 1.917 - 11.513 = -9.596$$ $$\Delta G^\circ = -(5.8198)(-9.596) = +55.85\,\text{kJ/mol}$$ > **Interpretation (½ mark):** $\Delta G^\circ > 0$ at standard state means the reaction is **not feasible** at $700\,\text{K}$ under standard conditions. This is why industrial synthesis uses **high pressure** to shift the actual $\Delta G$ negative. **(ii) Equilibrium composition (4 marks):** Let the initial moles be: $1\,\text{mol N}_2$, $3\,\text{mol H}_2$, $0\,\text{mol NH}_3$ (stoichiometric basis). Let $x$ = moles of $\text{N}_2$ reacted at equilibrium. | Species | Initial | Change | Equilibrium | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $\text{N}_2$ | $1$ | $-x$ | $1-x$ | | $\text{H}_2$ | $3$ | $-3x$ | $3-3x$ | | $\text{NH}_3$ | $0$ | $+2x$ | $2x$ | | **Total** | $4$ | $-2x$ | $4-2x$ | Partial pressures (with $P = 100\,\text{atm}$): $$p_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{(1-x)}{4-2x} \cdot 100, \quad p_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{3(1-x)}{4-2x} \cdot 100, \quad p_{\text{NH}_3} = \frac{2x}{4-2x} \cdot 100$$ The equilibrium constant in terms of partial pressure is: $$K_P = \frac{p_{\text{NH}_3}^2}{p_{\text{N}_2} \, p_{\text{H}_2}^3} = 6.8 \times 10^{-5}$$ Substituting (with $K_P$ in $\text{atm}^{-2}$): $$6.8 \times 10^{-5} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{2x}{4-2x} \cdot 100\right)^2}{\left(\dfrac{1-x}{4-2x}\cdot 100\right)\left(\dfrac{3(1-x)}{4-2x}\cdot 100\right)^3}$$ Simplify (the $100$ terms give a $100^{-2}$ factor): $$6.8 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \frac{1}{100^2} = \frac{4x^2 (4-2x)^2}{27(1-x)^4 (4-2x)^4} \cdot \frac{(4-2x)^2}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{100^2}$$ Letting $y = \dfrac{x}{1-x}$ and simplifying gives (after algebraic reduction): $$K_P \cdot (100)^{-2} \cdot 27 \cdot (1-x)^2 = \frac{4 x^2}{(1-x)^2}$$ Let $\alpha = \dfrac{x}{1-x}$, then: $$\alpha^2 = \frac{27 \cdot 6.8 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 100^2} \cdot (1-x)^2$$ Solving iteratively with $x \ll 1$ (since $K_P$ is very small): $$x \approx 0.118 \text{ (per mole of } \text{N}_2\text{)}$$ So the **mole fraction of $\text{NH}_3$ at equilibrium** $\approx \dfrac{2(0.118)}{4 - 2(0.118)} = \dfrac{0.236}{3.764} \approx 0.0627$, or about **6.3 mol% NH₃**. **Examination note (½ mark):** Board answers often accept the linearised approximation when $K$ is very small, yielding $x \approx \sqrt{27 K_P / 4} \cdot 100$ and then iterating once. **(iii) Feasibility comment (1 mark):** - $\Delta G^\circ(700\,\text{K}) = +55.85\,\text{kJ/mol} > 0$: **infeasible at standard state**. - However, with $P = 100\,\text{atm}$ the actual $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT\ln Q$ becomes negative (Q becomes small as reactants dominate), making the forward reaction feasible. The Haber–Bosch process exploits this by operating at **150–300 atm**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** *Starting from the definition $G = H - TS$, derive the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation:* $$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\Delta G}{T}\right)\right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^2}$$ *Show clearly each mathematical step.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *For a hypothetical reaction, $\Delta G^\circ_{298\,\text{K}} = -25.0\,\text{kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta H^\circ = -110.0\,\text{kJ/mol}$ (assumed constant with $T$).* **(7 marks)** 1. Compute $\Delta G^\circ$ at $T = 500\,\text{K}$ using the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation. 2. Determine the temperature at which the reaction ceases to be feasible ($\Delta G = 0$). 3. Is the reaction endothermic or exothermic? Exothermic or endothermic entropy change? Justify. --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** **Part (a) — 7 marks derivation (already shown in Section 3.1):** Refer to the **exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1** above. The board expects the following sequence: 1. Differentiate $G = H - TS$ at constant $P$ to get $(\partial G/\partial T)_P = -S$ (using $C_P$ identity and Maxwell relation). 2. Apply to reaction: $(\partial \Delta G/\partial T)_P = -\Delta S$. 3. Express $\Delta S$ from $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ as $\Delta S = (\Delta H - \Delta G)/T$. 4. Combine to get $(\partial \Delta G/\partial T)_P = (\Delta G - \Delta H)/T$. 5. Apply the quotient rule for $\partial(\Delta G/T)/\partial T$. 6. **Final result:** $\partial(\Delta G/T)/\partial T = -\Delta H/T^2$. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks numerical:** **(i) Compute $\Delta G^\circ$ at $500\,\text{K}$ (3 marks):** Use the integrated Gibbs–Helmholtz (constant $\Delta H$): $$\frac{\Delta G_2}{T_2} = \frac{\Delta G_1}{T_1} + \Delta H \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ $$\frac{\Delta G_2}{500} = \frac{-25.0}{298} + (-110.0)\left(\frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{298}\right)$$ $$\frac{\Delta G_2}{500} = -0.0839 + (-110.0)(-0.001356) = -0.0839 + 0.1492 = 0.0653$$ $$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ_{500\,\text{K}} = +32.65\,\text{kJ/mol}}$$ **(ii) Crossover temperature (2 marks):** Set $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S = 0 \Rightarrow T_{\text{crit}} = \Delta H / \Delta S$. We need $\Delta S$ first; use data at $298\,\text{K}$: $$\Delta S = \frac{\Delta H - \Delta G}{T} = \frac{-110.0 - (-25.0)}{298} = \frac{-85.0}{298} = -0.2852\,\text{kJ/(mol·K)}$$ $$T_{\text{crit}} = \frac{-110.0}{-0.2852} = 385.6\,\text{K}$$ $$\boxed{T_{\text{crit}} \approx 386\,\text{K}}$$ **(iii) Sign analysis (2 marks):** - $\Delta H = -110\,\text{kJ/mol} < 0 \Rightarrow$ **exothermic**. - $\Delta S = -285.2\,\text{J/(mol·K)} < 0 \Rightarrow$ **entropy decreases** (system becomes more ordered, e.g., fewer gas molecules on the product side). - Feasibility window: $T < 386\,\text{K}$ only. Above this $T$, $\Delta G > 0$ and the reaction is **not feasible** in the forward direction. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers** > > 1. **Unit conversion of $R$** — Mixing $R = 8.314$ (J units) with $\Delta G$ in kJ. Examiner deducts **1 full mark**. > 2. **Forgetting to convert $T$ to Kelvin** — A temperature in ${}^{\circ}\text{C}$ used in $RT$ will be marked wrong. > 3. **Sign error in $\Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K$** — A positive $\ln K$ with a missing negative sign flips the entire conclusion. > 4. **Confusing $\Delta G_f^\circ$ (formation) with $\Delta G_{rxn}^\circ$ (reaction)** — Always compute $\Delta G_{rxn}^\circ$ as products minus reactants. > 5. **Equilibrium constant units** — $K$ in $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ must be **dimensionless**; if $K_P$ has units, the formula is invalid. Always convert to $K$ (activities) or note the standard state. > 6. **Mistaking "feasibility" for "rate"** — KTU question: *"Is the reaction feasible at 1000 K?"* Answer: *Thermodynamically — check $\Delta G$.* Kinetically — depends on catalyst. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — print this for the night before the exam.** - [x] **Master Criterion:** A reaction is feasible (spontaneous) at constant $T$ and $P$ iff $\Delta G_{rxn} < 0$. - [x] **Master Equation:** $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$. - [x] **Standard state:** $T = 298.15\,\text{K}$, $P = 1\,\text{atm}$, pure substances in their reference form ($\Delta G_f^\circ = 0$ for elements). - [x] **Reaction Gibbs change:** $\Delta G_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ (\text{products}) - \sum \nu_j \Delta G_{f,j}^\circ (\text{reactants})$. - [x] **Gibbs–Helmholtz:** $\partial(\Delta G/T)/\partial T = -\Delta H/T^2$. - [x] **Equilibrium link:** $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$. - [x] **Non-standard link:** $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$. - [x] **van't Hoff:** $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -(\Delta H^\circ/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$. - [x] **Max useful work:** $W_{\max} = -\Delta G$. - [x] **Case 1** ($\Delta H < 0$, $\Delta S > 0$): feasible at **all $T$**. - [x] **Case 2** ($\Delta H < 0$, $\Delta S < 0$): feasible at **low $T$**; $T_{\text{crit}} = \Delta H/\Delta S$. - [x] **Case 3** ($\Delta H > 0$, $\Delta S > 0$): feasible at **high $T$**; $T_{\text{crit}} = \Delta H/\Delta S$. - [x] **Case 4** ($\Delta H > 0$, $\Delta S < 0$): **never feasible**. - [x] **Feasibility $\neq$ Rate** — thermodynamics tells **IF**, not **HOW FAST**. - [x] **Catalysts** do not change $\Delta G$ or $K$ — they only lower the activation barrier. - [x] **Pressure effect** — increasing $P$ shifts $\Delta G$ for reactions with $\Delta n_{gas} < 0$ in the **feasible** direction. - [x] **$K$ depends on $T$** — but **not on $P$** (for ideal systems) or on initial concentrations. - [x] **Always quote $T$ in Kelvin** and use a consistent value of $R$ in the matching unit. - [x] **Reversibility:** $\Delta G_{\text{reverse}} = -\Delta G_{\text{forward}}$. - [x] **Coupled reactions** — a non-feasible reaction can be driven by coupling with a strongly feasible one if total $\Delta G_{\text{net}} < 0$.

topics.title: Effect of Temperature on Equilibrium Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of Temperature on Equilibrium Constant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Equilibrium Constant ($K$)** of a chemical reaction is a dimensionless (or dimensionally normalized via fugacity/activity) quantity that expresses, at a given temperature, the ratio of the activities (or partial pressures, fugacities, or concentrations) of the products to those of the reactants, each raised to the power of their respective stoichiometric coefficients, when the system has reached thermodynamic equilibrium.

For a generic reaction:
$$\sum_{i} \nu_{i} A_{i} = 0$$

The equilibrium constant is defined as:
$$K = \prod_{i} a_{i}^{\nu_{i}}$$

where $a_{i}$ is the activity of species $i$ and $\nu_{i}$ is its stoichiometric coefficient (negative for reactants, positive for products).

The **Effect of Temperature on Equilibrium Constant** is rigorously described by the **Van 't Hoff Equation**, which quantitatively relates the variation of $K$ with absolute temperature $T$ to the standard enthalpy of reaction $\Delta H^{\circ}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Van 't Hoff Equation, its differential form, integrated forms (both for constant and variable $\Delta H$), and physical interpretation are high-yield topics frequently tested for 7 to 14 marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **two-pan balance scale** representing a reversible chemical reaction. One pan holds the reactants, the other holds the products. The **equilibrium constant $K$** tells you how heavily one pan is loaded compared to the other at equilibrium.

Now, think of **temperature as a "thermostat dial"** that you can turn up or down. In this analogy:
- For an **endothermic reaction** ($\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$), increasing temperature acts like adding invisible heat-warmth to the *reactants' pan*, helping them overcome the activation barrier and convert into products. The product pan becomes heavier, so $K$ **increases** with $T$.
- For an **exothermic reaction** ($\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$), heat is a "product". Increasing temperature means adding more of the product, which (by Le Chatelier's Principle) pushes the reaction *backwards* toward the reactants. Thus $K$ **decreases** with $T$.

This qualitative intuition is precisely what the **Van 't Hoff Equation** captures mathematically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used:**
> - Universal Gas Constant: $R = 8.314 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
> - Standard State Pressure: $P^{\circ} = 1 \; \text{bar}$ (or $10^{5} \; \text{Pa}$)
> - All thermodynamic properties are evaluated at the standard state unless otherwise specified.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Starting from First Principles

The entire derivation of temperature dependence rests on **two foundational pillars**:

1. **The Fundamental Thermodynamic Identity linking $K$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}$:**
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -R T \ln K$$

2. **The Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation**, which expresses how $\Delta G^{\circ}/T$ varies with $T$ at constant pressure:
$$\left[ \frac{\partial \left( \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T} \right)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

By combining these two pillars, one obtains the **Van 't Hoff Equation**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Reasoning

**Step 1 — Identify the thermodynamic potential of interest.**
The chemical reaction equilibrium is governed by the minimum of the Gibbs free energy. The standard change $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is the master variable linking $K$ to $T$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Gibbs-Helmholtz relationship.**
The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation tells us *how fast* the quantity $\Delta G^{\circ}/T$ changes with temperature, and that rate of change is controlled entirely by $\Delta H^{\circ}$ (the heat absorbed or released at constant pressure).

**Step 3 — Substitute the identity $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$.**
This converts the abstract Gibbs-Helmholtz equation into a *measurable* equation involving $K$ and $T$.

**Step 4 — Isolate the derivative of $\ln K$.**
This gives the differential form of the Van 't Hoff Equation.

**Step 5 — Integrate the differential form.**
Depending on whether $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is treated as constant or temperature-dependent, we obtain different integrated forms:
- **Constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$** → Linear $\ln K$ vs $1/T$ plot (Arrhenius-type).
- **Variable $\Delta H^{\circ}$** (via Kirchhoff's Law) → Non-linear form.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial chemical reactors, the equilibrium constant dictates the **maximum achievable conversion** at any given operating temperature. Engineers use the Van 't Hoff Equation to:
- **Optimize reactor temperature** for ammonia synthesis (Haber-Bosch process).
- Design **adiabatic reactors** by computing $K(T)$ along the reactor length.
- Perform **economic trade-off studies** (higher $T$ may increase rate but lower equilibrium conversion).
- Estimate **heat of reaction** from experimental $K$ vs $T$ data using linear regression.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation** | **Mathematical Form** | **Applicable Condition** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equilibrium Identity | $\Delta G^{\circ} = - R T \ln K$ | Always (at standard state) | $\Delta G^{\circ}$ in J/mol |
| Van 't Hoff (Differential) | $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$ | Always | K dimensionless |
| Van 't Hoff (Integrated, const. $\Delta H$) | $\ln \left( \dfrac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = - \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$ | $\Delta H^{\circ}$ independent of $T$ | $T$ in K, $\Delta H^{\circ}$ in J/mol |
| Van 't Hoff (Integrated, linear in $T$) | $\ln K = - \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}$ | Constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}$ | K dimensionless |
| Kirchhoff's Law | $\Delta H^{\circ}(T_{2}) = \Delta H^{\circ}(T_{1}) + \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \Delta C_{P} \, dT$ | Variable $\Delta H^{\circ}$ with $T$ | J/mol |
| Heat Capacity Change | $\Delta C_{P} = \sum_{i} \nu_{i} C_{P,i}$ | All reactions | J/(mol·K) |
| Polynomial $\Delta H^{\circ}(T)$ form | $\Delta H^{\circ}(T) = \Delta H_{0} + \Delta a \, T + \dfrac{\Delta b}{2} T^{2} + \dfrac{\Delta c}{3} T^{3}$ | When $C_{P} = a + bT + cT^{2}$ | J/mol |
| Integrated form with variable $\Delta H^{\circ}$ | $\ln K = - \dfrac{\Delta H_{0}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta a}{R} \ln T + \dfrac{\Delta b}{2R} T + \dfrac{\Delta c}{6R} T^{2} + I$ | Polynomial $C_{P}$ | K dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule (KTU Examiner's Tip):** In the integrated Van 't Hoff Equation
> $\ln \left( \dfrac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = - \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$, the order of subscripts in the temperature bracket $\left( \dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$ must **match the order of $K$** in the $\ln$ argument. A common student error is writing $\left( \dfrac{1}{T_{1}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{2}} \right)$, which produces the wrong sign.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Differential Van 't Hoff Equation

We begin from the fundamental thermodynamic identity:

$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = - R T \ln K
$$

**Step 1 — Divide both sides by $T$:**
$$
\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = - R \ln K
$$

This expresses a *logarithmic function* of $K$ in terms of $\Delta G^{\circ}/T$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation.**
The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation for any state function $X$ at constant pressure states:
$$
\left[ \frac{\partial \left( \dfrac{X}{T} \right)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = - \frac{1}{T^{2}} \left( \frac{\partial X}{\partial T} \right)_{P}
$$

For the standard Gibbs energy change of reaction, $X = \Delta G^{\circ}$, and using the identity $\left( \dfrac{\partial \Delta G^{\circ}}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = - \Delta S^{\circ}$:
$$
\left[ \frac{\partial \left( \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T} \right)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}
$$

(We substituted $\Delta H^{\circ} = \Delta G^{\circ} + T \Delta S^{\circ}$ to obtain the RHS.)

**Step 3 — Substitute $\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = -R \ln K$ into the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation:**
$$
\left[ \frac{\partial (- R \ln K)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Pull out the constant $-R$ from the partial derivative:**
$$
- R \left( \frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $-R$ on both sides:**
$$
\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}
$$

> This is the **Differential Van 't Hoff Equation**. The pressure subscript is dropped because $K$ depends *only* on temperature for an ideal system.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Integrated Van 't Hoff Equation (Constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$)

We now **integrate** the differential form. Rearranging:
$$
d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}} \, dT
$$

**Step 1 — Separate the variables.**
Since $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is assumed constant and $R$ is a universal constant:
$$
d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot T^{-2} \, dT
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from state $(T_{1}, K_{1})$ to state $(T_{2}, K_{2})$:**
$$
\int_{K_{1}}^{K_{2}} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} T^{-2} \, dT
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the LHS:**
$$
\ln K \Big\vert_{K_{1}}^{K_{2}} = \ln K_{2} - \ln K_{1} = \ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the RHS:**
$$
\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \left[ -T^{-1} \right]_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( -\frac{1}{T_{2}} + \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Equate LHS and RHS:**
$$
\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{1}} - \frac{1}{T_{2}} \right) = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right)
$$

> This is the **Integrated Van 't Hoff Equation** for constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Linear Form $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T} + \dfrac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}$

Starting from the differential Van 't Hoff equation under constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$:
$$
\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}
$$

**Step 1 — Multiply both sides by $dT$ and integrate from a reference state to an arbitrary state:**
$$
\int d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \int \frac{dT}{T^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Perform indefinite integration:**
$$
\ln K = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T} + C
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the integration constant $C$** by using the equilibrium identity at a reference temperature:
$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}
$$
$$
- R T \ln K = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\ln K$:**
$$
\ln K = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}
$$

Comparing with Step 2, we identify:
$$
C = \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Plot Interpretation:** A plot of $\ln K$ (y-axis) vs $1/T$ (x-axis) gives a **straight line** with:
> - **Slope** $= - \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}$
> - **Y-intercept** $= \dfrac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}$
>
> This is a powerful experimental technique used in KTU numerical problems to extract $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}$ from $K$ vs $T$ data.

---

### 3.4 Derivation with Temperature-Dependent $\Delta H^{\circ}$ (Kirchhoff's Law Integration)

When the temperature change is large, $\Delta H^{\circ}$ cannot be assumed constant. Using Kirchhoff's Law:
$$
\Delta H^{\circ}(T) = \Delta H_{0} + \int_{0}^{T} \Delta C_{P} \, dT
$$

Assume a polynomial heat capacity form:
$$
C_{P,i} = a_{i} + b_{i} T + c_{i} T^{2}
$$

Then:
$$
\Delta C_{P} = \Delta a + \Delta b \, T + \Delta c \, T^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Express $\Delta H^{\circ}(T)$ explicitly:**
$$
\Delta H^{\circ}(T) = \Delta H_{0} + \Delta a \, T + \frac{\Delta b}{2} T^{2} + \frac{\Delta c}{3} T^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the differential Van 't Hoff equation:**
$$
\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{1}{R T^{2}} \left[ \Delta H_{0} + \Delta a \, T + \frac{\Delta b}{2} T^{2} + \frac{\Delta c}{3} T^{3} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Divide through by $T^{2}$ and split:**
$$
\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{0}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T^{2}} + \frac{\Delta a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta b}{2R} + \frac{\Delta c}{3R} \, T
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate term-by-term with respect to $T$:**
$$
\ln K = - \frac{\Delta H_{0}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta a}{R} \ln T + \frac{\Delta b}{2R} \, T + \frac{\Delta c}{6R} \, T^{2} + I
$$

> where $I$ is the integration constant determined from a known reference point $(T_{\text{ref}}, K_{\text{ref}})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$ version is by far the most commonly tested. The variable $\Delta H^{\circ}$ version is reserved for higher-order problems and carries full 14-mark weightage when it appears.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Temperature Effect on $K$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Reaction at Equilibrium] --> B{Is ΔH° Known?}
    B -- Yes, Constant --> C[Apply Differential Van t Hoff Equation]
    B -- No, Variable --> D[Apply Kirchhoff Law for ΔH° of T]
    C --> E[Integrate assuming constant ΔH°]
    D --> F[Substitute ΔH° of T Polynomial]
    E --> G[Obtain ln K2 by K1 equals minus ΔH° by R times 1 by T2 minus 1 by T1]
    F --> H[Integrate term by term in T]
    G --> I{Sign of ΔH°}
    H --> J[General ln K of T Expression with I Constant]
    I -- Positive Endothermic --> K[K Increases with T]
    I -- Negative Exothermic --> L[K Decreases with T]
    I -- Zero --> M[K Independent of T]
    J --> N[Plot ln K vs 1 slash T for Parameter Extraction]
    K --> O[Conclusion: Select Optimal Reactor T]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree: Selection of Van 't Hoff Form

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Given K at Two Temperatures?] -- Yes --> Q2[ΔH° Assumed Constant?]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use Plot Method or Polynomial Form]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Use Integrated Constant ΔH° Form]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Check if Cp Data is Given]
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Kirchhoff Law Integration]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Use Mean ΔH° Approximation]
    Q4 --> QEND[Solve for Unknown K or T or ΔH°]
    Q6 --> QEND
    Q7 --> QEND
    Q3 --> QEND
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Effect of Temperature on $K$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REACT[Reactants + Heat] -- Endothermic ΔH positive --> PROD1[Products Favored]
    REACT2[Reactants] -- Exothermic ΔH negative --> PROD2[Products + Heat Released]
    PROD2 -- Temperature Increase --> BACK[Reverse Reaction Favored K Decreases]
    PROD1 -- Temperature Increase --> FORW[Forward Reaction Favored K Increases]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State and explain the Van 't Hoff Equation describing the effect of temperature on the equilibrium constant.**

**Model Answer:**
The Van 't Hoff Equation in differential form is:
$$
\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}
$$

It states that the rate of change of the natural logarithm of the equilibrium constant with absolute temperature is directly proportional to the standard enthalpy of reaction and inversely proportional to the square of the temperature. For an **endothermic reaction** ($\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$), $K$ increases with temperature. For an **exothermic reaction** ($\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$), $K$ decreases with temperature. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Why does the equilibrium constant become independent of temperature when $\Delta H^{\circ} = 0$?**

**Model Answer:**
From the differential Van 't Hoff Equation:
$$
\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}
$$
If $\Delta H^{\circ} = 0$, then $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = 0$, meaning $\ln K$ is constant with respect to $T$. Therefore $K$ is independent of temperature. This corresponds to an athermal reaction (e.g., certain mixing processes), where no heat is absorbed or released. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 4]

**(a)** Starting from the relation $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ and the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation, derive the differential form of the Van 't Hoff Equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the gaseous reaction $\text{N}_{2}\text{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_{2}(g)$, the equilibrium constant $K_{p} = 0.144$ at $298 \; \text{K}$ and $K_{p} = 0.717$ at $350 \; \text{K}$. Assuming $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is constant in this temperature range, calculate the standard enthalpy of reaction $\Delta H^{\circ}$ in kJ/mol. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The derivation follows the standard steps shown in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key steps to be written in the answer script:
1. Start with $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$, hence $\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = -R \ln K$. **(1 mark)**
2. State the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation: $\left[ \dfrac{\partial (\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = - \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$. **(2 marks)**
3. Differentiate $\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$ to obtain $\left( \dfrac{\partial \Delta G^{\circ}}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = - \Delta S^{\circ}$, leading to Gibbs-Helmholtz form above. **(1 mark)**
4. Substitute $\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = -R \ln K$ into the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation:
$$
\left[ \frac{\partial (-R \ln K)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}
$$
**(1 mark)**
5. Pull the constant $-R$ outside and cancel to obtain:
$$
\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

We use the integrated Van 't Hoff Equation with constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$:
$$
\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right)
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the given values:**
- $K_{1} = 0.144$ at $T_{1} = 298 \; \text{K}$
- $K_{2} = 0.717$ at $T_{2} = 350 \; \text{K}$
- $R = 8.314 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{0.717}{0.144} \right) = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{298} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the LHS:**
$$
\frac{0.717}{0.144} = 4.9792
$$
$$
\ln(4.9792) = 1.6054
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracket on the RHS:**
$$
\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{298} = 0.002857 - 0.003356 = -0.0004986 \; \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**
$$
1.6054 = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{8.314} \times (-0.0004986)
$$
$$
1.6054 = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ} \times 0.0004986}{8.314}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\Delta H^{\circ}$:**
$$
\Delta H^{\circ} = \frac{1.6054 \times 8.314}{0.0004986}
$$
$$
\Delta H^{\circ} = \frac{13.347}{0.0004986} = 26768 \; \text{J/mol} \approx 26.77 \; \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**Answer:** $\Delta H^{\circ} \approx +26.77 \; \text{kJ/mol}$ (positive sign confirms the reaction is endothermic, consistent with $K$ increasing as $T$ rises). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 4 Alternative]

**(a)** Derive the integrated form of the Van 't Hoff Equation for the case when $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is constant. Discuss its graphical interpretation as a plot of $\ln K$ vs $1/T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a certain reaction, $\ln K$ is observed to be $4.20$ at $500 \; \text{K}$ and $1.80$ at $700 \; \text{K}$. Calculate the standard enthalpy of reaction $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and the standard entropy of reaction $\Delta S^{\circ}$ assuming both are constant over this temperature range. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Start from the differential Van 't Hoff Equation:**
$$
\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}
$$

Assuming $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $R$ are constants: **(1 mark)**
$$
d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate between two arbitrary states:**
$$
\int_{\ln K_{1}}^{\ln K_{2}} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \frac{dT}{T^{2}}
$$
$$
\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left[ -\frac{1}{T} \right]_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}}
$$
**(2 marks)**
$$
\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right)
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Alternatively, derive the linear form via indefinite integration:**
$$
\ln K = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Graphical interpretation:**
A plot of $\ln K$ (y-axis) against $1/T$ (x-axis) yields a straight line with:
- Slope $= -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}$
- Intercept $= \dfrac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}$

Experimental data plotted this way allows extraction of both $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}$ from a single linear regression. The slope is negative for exothermic reactions and positive for endothermic reactions. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

We use the linear form:
$$
\ln K = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the two-point equations:**

At $T_{1} = 500 \; \text{K}$, $\ln K_{1} = 4.20$:
$$
4.20 = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{8.314} \cdot \frac{1}{500} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{8.314} \quad \text{...(I)}
$$

At $T_{2} = 700 \; \text{K}$, $\ln K_{2} = 1.80$:
$$
1.80 = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{8.314} \cdot \frac{1}{700} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{8.314} \quad \text{...(II)}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract (II) from (I):**
$$
4.20 - 1.80 = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{700} \right)
$$
$$
2.40 = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{8.314} \left( \frac{7 - 5}{3500} \right)
$$
$$
2.40 = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{8.314} \times \frac{2}{3500}
$$
$$
2.40 = - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{8.314} \times 5.714 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta H^{\circ}$:**
$$
\Delta H^{\circ} = - \frac{2.40 \times 8.314}{5.714 \times 10^{-4}}
$$
$$
\Delta H^{\circ} = - \frac{19.954}{5.714 \times 10^{-4}} = -34925 \; \text{J/mol} \approx -34.93 \; \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into Equation (I) to find $\Delta S^{\circ}$:**
$$
4.20 = - \frac{(-34925)}{8.314} \cdot \frac{1}{500} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{8.314}
$$
$$
4.20 = \frac{34925}{4157} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{8.314}
$$
$$
4.20 = 8.40 + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{8.314}
$$
$$
\frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{8.314} = 4.20 - 8.40 = -4.20
$$
$$
\Delta S^{\circ} = -4.20 \times 8.314 = -34.92 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Answers:**
- $\Delta H^{\circ} \approx -34.93 \; \text{kJ/mol}$ (exothermic reaction)
- $\Delta S^{\circ} \approx -34.92 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$ **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Pitfall:** The integrated Van 't Hoff Equation contains a minus sign on the RHS: $\ln(K_{2}/K_{1}) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$. Many students **drop this minus sign**, leading to wrong sign for $\Delta H^{\circ}$. Always carry the minus sign explicitly through your algebra. **(Loss of 2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** $\Delta H^{\circ}$ must be in **J/mol** (not kJ/mol) when used with $R = 8.314 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$. Convert at the start. **(Loss of 1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Natural vs. Common Logarithm:** Use $\ln$ (natural log) for Van 't Hoff. Using $\log_{10}$ requires multiplying by $2.303$. **(Loss of 2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$ Assumption:** Always **state** this assumption explicitly when using the integrated form. Examiners award 1 mark for the assumption statement.
>
> 5. **Interpretation of Slope:** When asked to extract $\Delta H^{\circ}$ from a $\ln K$ vs $1/T$ plot, students frequently report the wrong sign. Remember: slope $= -\Delta H^{\circ}/R$, so $\Delta H^{\circ} = -(\text{slope}) \times R$. **(Loss of 1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- **Master Identity:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ — the gateway between $K$ and thermodynamics.
- **Differential Form:** $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$ — base equation for all derivations.
- **Integrated Form (Constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$):** $\ln \left( \dfrac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = - \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$.
- **Linear Form:** $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}$ — yields straight-line plot.
- **Endothermic** ($\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$): $K$ **increases** with $T$.
- **Exothermic** ($\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$): $K$ **decreases** with $T$.
- **Athermal** ($\Delta H^{\circ} = 0$): $K$ is **independent** of $T$.
- **Slope of $\ln K$ vs $1/T$:** $= -\Delta H^{\circ}/R$.
- **Intercept of $\ln K$ vs $1/T$:** $= \Delta S^{\circ}/R$.
- **Kirchhoff's Law:** Required when $\Delta H^{\circ}$ varies significantly with $T$.
- **Assumption Statement:** Always declare $\Delta H^{\circ}$ = constant when using the standard integrated form.
- **Unit Conversion:** $\Delta H^{\circ}$ in J/mol (with $R = 8.314$) or in kJ/mol (with $R = 8.314 \times 10^{-3}$).
- **Le Chatelier Coupling:** Van 't Hoff Equation is the **quantitative** counterpart of Le Chatelier's Principle.
- **Haber-Bosch Context:** Industrial ammonia synthesis uses Van 't Hoff to optimize temperature-pressure trade-off.
