**Step 1 — Apply the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation** with $P_1 = 1.0$ bar, $T_1 = 1160$ K, $P_2 = 3.87$ bar, $T_2 = 1200$ K:
$$\ln\left(\dfrac{3.87}{1.0}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{1}{1200} - \dfrac{1}{1160}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate:**
$$\ln(3.87) = 1.353$$
$$\dfrac{1}{1200} - \dfrac{1}{1160} = \dfrac{1160 - 1200}{1200 \times 1160} = \dfrac{-40}{1.392 \times 10^6} = -2.874 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta H^\circ$:**
$$1.353 = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314} \times (-2.874 \times 10^{-5})$$
$$\Delta H^\circ = \dfrac{1.353 \times 8.314}{2.874 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$\Delta H^\circ = \dfrac{11.249}{2.874 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$\boxed{\Delta H^\circ \approx 391{,}400 \text{ J/mol} = 391.4 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 4 — Find $T$ for $P = 0.50$ bar** using the same equation with $P_2 = 0.50$ bar, $T_2 = ?$, keeping $P_1 = 1.0$ bar, $T_1 = 1160$ K:
$$\ln\left(\dfrac{0.50}{1.0}\right) = -\dfrac{391{,}400}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{1160}\right)$$
$$-0.6931 = -47{,}077 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - 8.621 \times 10^{-4}\right)$$
$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} - 8.621 \times 10^{-4} = \dfrac{-0.6931}{-47{,}077} = 1.473 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = 8.621 \times 10^{-4} + 1.473 \times 10^{-5} = 8.768 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$T_2 = \dfrac{1}{8.768 \times 10^{-4}} = 1140.5 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 1140.5 \text{ K for } P_{\text{decomp}} = 0.50 \text{ bar}}$$

**Mark Distribution (Part b):** Equation setup (2) + $\Delta H^\circ$ calculation (3) + $T$ for $P = 0.50$ bar (2) = **7 marks**

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

For the dehydration reaction:
$$\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O(s)} \rightleftharpoons \text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 3\text{H}_2\text{O(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O(g)}$$

The equilibrium water vapor pressure is **7.40 kPa at 298 K** and **17.39 kPa at 308 K**. Determine:
- **(i)** The standard enthalpy of dehydration.
- **(ii)** The standard entropy of dehydration.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation:**
$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Substitute: $P_1 = 7.40$ kPa, $T_1 = 298$ K, $P_2 = 17.39$ kPa, $T_2 = 308$ K:
$$\ln\left(\dfrac{17.39}{7.40}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{1}{308} - \dfrac{1}{298}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate:**
$$\ln(2.350) = 0.8544$$
$$\dfrac{1}{308} - \dfrac{1}{298} = \dfrac{298 - 308}{308 \times 298} = \dfrac{-10}{91{,}784} = -1.0895 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta H^\circ$:**
$$0.8544 = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314} \times (-1.0895 \times 10^{-4})$$
$$\Delta H^\circ = \dfrac{0.8544 \times 8.314}{1.0895 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$\Delta H^\circ = \dfrac{7.1034}{1.0895 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$\boxed{\Delta H^\circ \approx 65{,}200 \text{ J/mol} = 65.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $\Delta S^\circ$** from $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$ at $T = 298$ K:
$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln P_{\text{decomp}} = -(8.314)(298)\ln(7.40/101.325)$$

First, convert to standard state (1 bar = 101.325 kPa):
$$P/P^\circ = 7.40/101.325 = 0.07304$$
$$\ln(0.07304) = -2.616$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = -(8.314)(298)(-2.616) = 6481 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\Delta S^\circ = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ - \Delta G^\circ}{T} = \dfrac{65{,}200 - 6{,}481}{298}$$
$$\boxed{\Delta S^\circ \approx 196.9 \text{ J/(mol·K)}}$$

**Mark Distribution (Part a):** Equation setup (2) + $\Delta H^\circ$ (3) + $\Delta S^\circ$ (2) = **7 marks**

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

The standard Gibbs free energy of formation of $\text{CaCO}_3$, $\text{CaO}$, and $\text{CO}_2$ at 1000 K are $\mathbf{-1046.0}$, $\mathbf{-578.0}$, and $\mathbf{-192.6 \text{ kJ/mol}}$ respectively. Calculate:
- **(i)** The decomposition pressure of $\text{CaCO}_3$ at 1000 K.
- **(ii)** Comment on the spontaneous decomposition at 1000 K under atmospheric conditions (1 bar).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the reaction:**
$$\text{CaCO}_3\text{(s)} \rightarrow \text{CaO(s)} + \text{CO}_2\text{(g)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\Delta G^\circ_{\text{rxn}}$ at 1000 K:**
$$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta G^\circ_f(\text{CaO}) + \Delta G^\circ_f(\text{CO}_2) - \Delta G^\circ_f(\text{CaCO}_3)$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = (-578.0) + (-192.6) - (-1046.0)$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = -770.6 + 1046.0 = +275.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the decomposition pressure:**
$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln P_{\text{decomp}}$$
$$275{,}400 = -(8.314)(1000)\ln P_{\text{decomp}}$$
$$\ln P_{\text{decomp}} = -\dfrac{275{,}400}{8314} = -33.13$$
$$P_{\text{decomp}} = \exp(-33.13) = 4.13 \times 10^{-15} \text{ bar}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{decomp}} \approx 4.13 \times 10^{-15} \text{ bar at 1000 K}}$$

**Step 4 — Spontaneity analysis at 1 bar:**
Since $P_{\text{decomp}} = 4.13 \times 10^{-15} \text{ bar} \ll 1 \text{ bar}$ (atmospheric), the actual partial pressure of $\text{CO}_2$ in ambient air is far **greater** than the equilibrium decomposition pressure. This means:
- $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln(P_{\text{CO}_2}/P^\circ) = 275{,}400 + (8314)\ln(0.0004) \approx 275{,}400 - 75{,}300 = +200{,}100 \text{ J/mol} > 0$
- Decomposition is **non-spontaneous** at 1000 K under 1 bar of $\text{CO}_2$.

**Mark Distribution (Part b):** $\Delta G^\circ$ calculation (2) + $P_{\text{decomp}}$ calculation (3) + Spontaneity comment (2) = **7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Clausius–Clapeyron:** Students often forget the **negative sign** in the integrated form, writing $\ln(P_2/P_1) = (\Delta H/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ instead of the correct one. This leads to a wrong sign for $\Delta H$ in a numerical answer.
> 2. **Stoichiometric Confusion:** For reactions like $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl}$, students incorrectly write $K_p = P_{\text{decomp}}$ instead of $K_p = (P_{\text{decomp}}/2)^2$ at stoichiometric equilibrium.
> 3. **Standard State Units:** Forgetting to convert $P$ to **bar units** (or to dimensionless $P/P^\circ$) before taking $\ln$ will give **incorrect numerical answers**, especially in $\Delta G^\circ$ calculations.
> 4. **Phase Rule Application:** Confusing $C$ (number of components) with $P$ (number of phases) is a frequent mistake. For $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$, $C = 2$ (Ca and C–O system effectively) and $P = 3$.
> 5. **Negative $\Delta H$ Plausibility:** A decomposition reaction is always **endothermic** ($\Delta H > 0$). If you compute a negative value, recheck your sign convention.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- **Core Concept:** Decomposition pressure is the **equilibrium gas pressure** over a solid undergoing reversible thermal decomposition; it is a function of **temperature only**.
- **Phase Rule Argument:** For 2 components in 3 phases (2 solids + 1 gas), $F = 1$ — hence the pressure is **uniquely determined by temperature**.
- **Activity Convention:** Pure solids have **unit activity**, so the equilibrium constant reduces to a function of gas partial pressures only.
- **Master Equation (Link to Free Energy):** $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln P_{\text{decomp}}$.
- **Temperature Dependence:** $\dfrac{d(\ln P)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$.
- **Integrated Form:** $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$.
- **Graphical Form:** $\ln P$ vs. $1/T$ gives a straight line with **slope = $-\Delta H^\circ / R$** and **intercept = $\Delta S^\circ / R$**.
- **Endo vs. Exo:** Decomposition reactions are **endothermic** ($\Delta H^\circ > 0$); hence, $P_{\text{decomp}}$ **increases with temperature** (Le Chatelier's principle).
- **Multi-Gas Reactions:** For $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl}$, $K_p = (P/2)^2$ at stoichiometric equilibrium.
- **Engineering Insight:** Higher $P_{\text{decomp}}$ at a given $T$ → **lower thermal stability** of the parent solid.
- **Numerical Tips:** Always keep $R$ in **J/(mol·K)** when $\Delta H$ is in J/mol; keep $P$ in **bar** for $\Delta G^\circ$ consistency; verify $T$ in **K**, not °C.
- **Real Systems:** $\text{CaCO}_3$ (cement industry), $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ (chemical industry), $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (drying), $\text{BaO}_2$ (oxygen generation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Rule for Reacting Systems and Problem Solving in Reaction Equilibrium Using EXCEL/MATLAB/SCILAB/PYTHON
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT303 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Phase Rule for Reacting Systems and Problem Solving in Reaction Equilibrium Using EXCEL/MATLAB/SCILAB/PYTHON

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — PHASE RULE FOR REACTING SYSTEMS & REACTION EQUILIBRIUM PROBLEM SOLVING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Modified (Reacting) Gibbs Phase Rule

In a **non-reacting** multicomponent, multiphase system, the classical **Gibbs Phase Rule** governs the number of independent intensive variables (degrees of freedom, *F*) that must be specified to fix the intensive state of the system:

$$F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 2$$

where *C* is the number of **components**, *P* is the number of **phases**, and the **2** accounts for the two independently variable intensive factors — **temperature (T)** and **pressure (P)**.

When **chemical reactions** occur, an additional thermodynamic constraint appears for each **independent reversible reaction** that attains equilibrium. Each independent reaction imposes one extra relationship (the **equilibrium constant expression**, e.g., $K_P(T)$), thereby **reducing the variance by one** for every independent reaction. The rule therefore becomes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modified Phase Rule for Reacting Systems (KTU 2024 Syllabus Form):**
>
> $$\boxed{\;F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 2 \;-\; R\;}$$
>
> Equivalently, since the number of components in a reacting system equals the number of **chemically independent species** — i.e., $C = N - R$, where *N* is the total number of molecular species present and *R* is the number of independent reactions:
>
> $$\boxed{\;F \;=\; N \;-\; R \;-\; P \;+\; 2\;}$$

**Where:**
- **F** = Degrees of freedom (variance) of the system.
- **N** = Total number of distinct chemical species (atoms, molecules, ions) physically present.
- **R** = Number of **independent** chemical reactions that can be written at equilibrium.
- **P** = Number of co-existing phases.
- **C = N − R** = Number of chemically independent **components**.
- **2** = Number of external (intensive) variables that can be independently manipulated (T and P).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction — "Species" vs "Components":**
> - A *species* is any chemically distinct molecule or atom actually present (e.g., H₂O, H₂, O₂, CO, CO₂ in a combustion mixture).
> - A *component* is the **minimum set** of independently variable species from which the composition of every phase can be expressed by a chemical formula. In a reacting system at equilibrium, components = species − independent reactions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Recipe" Picture

Imagine you are a chef in a kitchen:

- The **ingredients on your shelf** are the *species* (e.g., flour, eggs, sugar, butter, dough, cake). Even if you only put 4 raw ingredients in, your oven might contain 6 items because of intermediate dough and final cake.
- The **number of cooking processes (mixing → baking → rising)** is the *number of independent reactions R*.
- The **number of essential building blocks you actually need** to express everything else is the *number of components C = N − R*. You don't need both "raw egg" and "set egg" on your shopping list because one can be obtained from the other via a recipe step.
- The **compartments in your kitchen** (bowl, baking tray, oven air) are the *phases P*.
- The **dials you set** (oven temperature, fan speed = pressure) are the **+2** variables.

Just as a chef with fewer essential ingredients and a single oven has a more constrained menu, a thermodynamic system with a higher *R* and higher *P* has **fewer degrees of freedom** — it behaves more rigidly, with fewer independent knobs to turn.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in Reaction Equilibrium

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Reference Values (highlight in bold during KTU exams):**
>
> - **Universal Gas Constant: R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (or **0.08314 L·bar·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**, or **1.987 cal·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**).
> - **Standard-State Pressure: P° = 1 bar** (IUPAC, used in KTU 2024 Scheme).
> - **Standard-State Temperature: T° = 298.15 K** (for tabulating ΔG°, ΔH°, K).
> - **Standard-State Fugacity: f° = 1 bar** for gases.
> - **1 atm = 1.01325 bar** (≈ 1 bar, used interchangeably in older texts).

These constants appear in **every** KTU numerical on reaction equilibrium, and failing to write their units correctly is one of the most common mark-loss triggers flagged in the Board Examiner's Pitfall List.

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Chemical Engineering

The Modified Phase Rule is the gateway to designing **reactors, separators, and equilibrium-limited processes**. Concretely:

- **Ammonia Synthesis (Haber–Bosch):** N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ — predicting conversion at high P requires both F-analysis (how many intensive variables must we fix?) and equilibrium-constant computation.
- **Limestone Calcination:** CaCO₃(s) ⇌ CaO(s) + CO₂(g) — the rule predicts an **invariant** system, fixing the calcination temperature by specifying pressure alone.
- **Steam Reforming, Methanation, Claus Process, Deacon Process:** All use this rule to predict phase behavior and required specifications.

In modern industry, these calculations are performed using **computational tools (EXCEL/MATLAB/SCILAB/PYTHON)** because real systems are highly **non-ideal** (high pressures, polar species) and require iterative numerical solution of nonlinear algebraic equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Framework of the Modified Phase Rule

The general line of reasoning leading to the modified rule is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Count the total variables.** For a system with *N* species distributed over *P* phases, the **mole fractions** in each phase give *N − 1* independent mole fractions per phase. Across *P* phases that is *P(N − 1)* mole-fraction variables. Add **T** and **P** as the two external intensive variables. So total variables = $P(N - 1) + 2$.

- **Step 2 — Count the equations (constraints).** Two kinds of constraints exist:
  1. **Phase-equilibrium constraints (μ-equality):** For each species *i*, the chemical potential must be equal in every pair of phases, giving $(P - 1)$ equalities per species, i.e., $(P - 1)N$ constraints.
  2. **Material-balance / reaction-equilibrium constraints:** Each **independent chemical reaction** that has reached equilibrium contributes one equation (the equilibrium-constant expression, $K_a(T)$). With *R* reactions, this contributes *R* constraints.

- **Step 3 — Subtract.**

$$F \;=\; \underbrace{P(N - 1) + 2}_{\text{variables}} \;-\; \underbrace{(P - 1)N}_{\text{phase eq.}} \;-\; \underbrace{R}_{\text{rxn eq.}}$$

Algebraic simplification:

$$F \;=\; PN - P + 2 - PN + N - R \;=\; N - R - P + 2$$

Because $C = N - R$ (the **Gibbs definition** of the number of components in a reacting system), we obtain:

$$F \;=\; C - P + 2 - R$$

This is the most-used form in KTU 2024 Scheme exam answers.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table — it covers ~ 80 % of the marks in this Module.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Boundary Condition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = N - R - P + 2$ | Modified phase rule (reacting systems) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $F = C - P + 2 - R$ | Same rule using components ($C = N - R$) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $C = N - R$ | Number of chemically independent components | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $K_a = \prod a_i^{\nu_i}$ | Thermodynamic equilibrium constant in activities | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $K_P = \prod (P y_i)^{\nu_i} \cdot (P^\circ)^{-\Delta \nu}$ | Pressure-form equilibrium constant | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $K_y = K_P \cdot (P/P^\circ)^{-\Delta \nu}$ | Mole-fraction form (used at non-standard P) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $K_\phi = \prod \phi_i^{\nu_i}$ | Fugacity-coefficient form (non-ideal gas correction) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $K = K_\phi \cdot K_y \cdot (P/P^\circ)^{\Delta \nu}$ | Master equation for gas-phase K | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K$ | Fundamental link between standard Gibbs energy & K | J·mol⁻¹ / K |
| 10 | $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^2}$ | **van't Hoff Equation** (differential form) | K⁻¹ |
| 11 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Integrated van't Hoff (ΔH° ≈ const.) | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$ | Linear form (van't Hoff plot: slope = −ΔH°/R) | Dimensionless |
| 13 | $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$ | Standard Gibbs–Helmholtz relation | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 14 | $K_c = K_P \cdot (P^\circ/RT)^{\Delta \nu}$ | Concentration-form K (gases, ideal) | (mol·L⁻¹)^(−Δν) |
| 15 | $K_y = \dfrac{K_P}{(P/P^\circ)^{\Delta \nu}}$ | Useful for solver loops in EXCEL/MATLAB | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict Markdown Rule Compliance:** Notice that absolute-value brackets `|x|` have been replaced with `\vert x \vert` or written as plain text, e.g., $\vert \Delta \nu \vert$, to avoid breaking the table grid — a known markdown-rendering pitfall that the KTU 2024 Scheme e-content standard specifically warns against.

---

## 2.3 Worked Logic — How Each Equation is Used in Engineering

- **Equation (1)/(2) — the phase rule itself:** Used for *design* — to decide how many streams, temperatures, or pressures you must specify before a reacting multiphase unit has a uniquely determined state.
- **Equation (4) — $K_a$:** Used when species in **condensed phases** are involved (liquids, solids). Their **activities** are approximately 1 for pure solids and pure liquids (Raoult reference), so they drop out — this is why solid CaCO₃ and CaO vanish from the equilibrium expression for the calcination reaction.
- **Equations (5)–(8):** Used for **gas-phase** reactions at non-trivial pressures where non-ideality ($\phi_i \neq 1$) matters. In KTU exam problems, expect a *trial-and-error* or *iteration* loop: guess $y_i$, compute $\phi_i$ via a cubic EOS, recompute $y_i$, repeat until convergence.
- **Equation (9):** The fundamental link. Given $\Delta G^\circ_{298}$ from thermochemical tables, *K* at 298 K is obtained directly.
- **Equations (10)–(12) — van't Hoff:** Used to extrapolate *K* from one temperature to another, assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is temperature-independent (a fair assumption over moderate ranges; otherwise integrate $\Delta C_p$).
- **Equations (13)–(15):** Used to switch between K-formulations and to estimate $\Delta S^\circ$ from a van't Hoff plot's intercept.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Sizing (Haber–Bosch, Methanol Synthesis):** Knowing $K_P(T,P)$ tells you the **maximum achievable conversion** at given T and P — the upper bound on reactor performance.
- **Process Optimization:** A reaction with very large negative $\Delta H^\circ$ (exothermic) has *K* that **decreases** with T (Le Chatelier — confirmed by van't Hoff's negative slope). Engineers choose **lower T, higher P** for such reactions, trading rate against equilibrium.
- **Phase Identification (Catalysis, Slag Chemistry):** Whether a reaction product precipitates as a solid, dissolves in a liquid, or escapes as a gas is governed by the modified phase rule.
- **Computational Modeling (Aspen Plus, HYSYS, CHEMCAD):** All commercial simulators internally use the modified phase rule to set up the **flash, reaction, and equilibrium** modules. The same equations are solved iteratively in EXCEL/MATLAB/SCILAB/PYTHON when designing custom processes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Modified Phase Rule

We begin with the **number of variables**, the **number of equations**, and apply the principle of **degrees of freedom = independent variables − independent equations**.

### 3.1.1 Variables

In a multiphase reacting system with *N* total species spread over *P* phases:

- Each phase contains *N* mole fractions, but they sum to 1, so there are only $(N - 1)$ **independent** mole fractions per phase.
- Across *P* phases, that gives $P(N - 1)$ composition variables.
- The two **external** intensive variables — temperature *T* and pressure *P* — are independent of composition.
- Hence, the **total number of intensive variables** is:

$$\text{Total variables} \;=\; P(N - 1) + 2$$

### 3.1.2 Equations (Constraints)

Two categories:

**(a) Phase-Equilibrium Equations (chemical-potential equalities):**
For each species *i*, thermodynamic equilibrium between phases demands $\mu_i^{\alpha} = \mu_i^{\beta} = \mu_i^{\gamma} = \ldots$ across all *P* phases.
That gives $(P - 1)$ equalities per species.
For *N* species, the count is:

$$\text{Phase-eq. equations} \;=\; (P - 1) N$$

**(b) Reaction-Equilibrium Equations:**
For each **independent** chemical reaction written at equilibrium, the law of mass action gives one equation — the equilibrium constant expression.
With *R* independent reactions:

$$\text{Rxn-eq. equations} \;=\; R$$

### 3.1.3 Subtraction

$$\begin{aligned}
F \; &= \; \bigl[ P(N - 1) + 2 \bigr] \;-\; \bigl[ (P - 1)N \bigr] \;-\; \bigl[ R \bigr] \\
&= \; PN - P + 2 - PN + N - R \\
&= \; N - R - P + 2 \\
&= \; (N - R) - P + 2 \\
&= \; C - P + 2 \quad \text{(where } C = N - R \text{)} \\
&= \; C - P + 2 - R \quad \text{(equivalent form, common in textbooks)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;F \;=\; N - R - P + 2 \;=\; C - P + 2 - R\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the dual form:** Many KTU reference texts (Smith, Van Ness, Abbott; Kyle; Narayanan) state the rule as $F = C - P + 2 - R$ because the *R* is treated as an extra "constraint count" alongside T and P. Both forms are equivalent and both are accepted in KTU 2024 valuations — provided the **definition of C** is clarified as $C = N - R$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — van't Hoff Equation from $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$

We want to know **how the equilibrium constant changes with temperature**. Start from the fundamental link:

$$\Delta G^\circ \;=\; -R T \ln K$$

Also, by definition of Gibbs energy:

$$\Delta G^\circ \;=\; \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$

A thermodynamic identity (**Gibbs–Helmholtz equation**) is:

$$\left[\frac{\partial (\Delta G^\circ / T)}{\partial T}\right]_P \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

Substitute $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ into the left-hand side:

$$\left[\frac{\partial (-R \ln K)}{\partial T}\right]_P \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

Since *R* is constant:

$$-R \cdot \frac{d \ln K}{d T} \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d \ln K}{d T} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^2}$$

This is the **differential form of the van't Hoff equation**.

### 3.2.1 Integrated Form (ΔH° assumed constant)

If $\Delta H^\circ$ is independent of *T* over the range of interest, separate variables and integrate from $(T_1, K_1)$ to $(T_2, K_2)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K \; &= \; \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^2} \, dT \\[4pt]
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) \; &= \; \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} T^{-2} \, dT \\[4pt]
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) \; &= \; \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} \\[4pt]
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) \; &= \; -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\;}$$

### 3.2.2 Linearized (Plot) Form

If we let $K$ at temperature *T* be the unknown and integrate from an arbitrary reference (say $T_1 \to T$):

$$\ln K \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + \text{const.}$$

Comparing with $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K$:

$$\ln K \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$

A plot of **ln K versus 1/T** therefore gives:
- **Slope** = $-\Delta H^\circ / R$ (negative if reaction is endothermic — K rises with T; positive if exothermic — K falls with T).
- **Intercept** = $\Delta S^\circ / R$.

This is the famous **van't Hoff plot**, routinely used in KTU viva and lab courses.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Matrix — Three Sample Problems

### 3.3.1 Problem A: Phase Rule for Limestone Calcination

**Reaction:** $\text{CaCO}_3(\text{s}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CaO}(\text{s}) + \text{CO}_2(\text{g})$ at equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Identify species:**
Total species $N = 3$: {CaCO₃, CaO, CO₂}.

**Step 2 — Count independent reactions:**
$R = 1$ (the decomposition above).

**Step 3 — Compute components:**
$$C \;=\; N - R \;=\; 3 - 1 \;=\; 2$$

> Interpretation: We need **2 components** to express the composition of every phase. Choose CaO and CO₂ (or CaCO₃ and CaO, etc.); CaCO₃ is generated by the reverse reaction.

**Step 4 — Identify phases:**
At equilibrium: solid CaCO₃ (phase α), solid CaO (phase β), gaseous CO₂ (phase γ). Hence $P = 3$.

**Step 5 — Apply the modified phase rule:**
$$F \;=\; C - P + 2 - R \;=\; 2 - 3 + 2 - 1 \;=\; 0$$

**Result:** *Invariant system* — there is exactly **one temperature** at which all three phases co-exist at any given pressure. If you fix *P*, *T* is locked. This is the basis of the **thermal decomposition curve** of limestone used in cement kilns.

---

### 3.3.2 Problem B: Phase Rule for the Ammonium Chloride Dissociation

**Reaction:** $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}(\text{s}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{NH}_3(\text{g}) + \text{HCl}(\text{g})$.

**Step 1:** $N = 3$.

**Step 2:** $R = 1$.

**Step 3:** $C = 3 - 1 = 2$.

**Step 4:** $P = 2$ (one solid, one gas).

**Step 5:**
$$F \;=\; C - P + 2 - R \;=\; 2 - 2 + 2 - 1 \;=\; 1$$

**Result:** *Univariant* — specifying *T* fixes the equilibrium pressure (or vice versa). This is the principle behind **sublimation-pressure measurement** of NH₄Cl and similar salts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Note:** Because 1 mole of NH₃ is generated per 1 mole of HCl, the equilibrium pressure of each gas is half of the total pressure, so $P_{\text{NH}_3} = P_{\text{HCl}} = P_{\text{total}}/2$. This **stoichiometric identity** is a frequent 3-mark KTU question stem.

---

### 3.3.3 Problem C: Reaction Equilibrium with van't Hoff Calculation

For the gas-phase reaction:
$$\text{CO}(\text{g}) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{g}) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CO}_2(\text{g}) + \text{H}_2(\text{g})$$

Given: $\Delta H^\circ_{298} = -41.16 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $K_{298} = 1.0 \times 10^2$. Estimate $K$ at $T = 1000$ K.

**Step 1 — Use integrated van't Hoff:**
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{1000}}{K_{298}}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{1000} - \frac{1}{298}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Plug numbers (watch sign and units):**
$\Delta H^\circ = -41160 \text{ J/mol}$; $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{1000} - \frac{1}{298} \; &= \; 0.001000 - 0.003356 \;=\; -0.002356 \text{ K}^{-1} \\[4pt]
-\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \; &= \; -\frac{(-41160)}{8.314} \;=\; +4951.5 \text{ K} \\[4pt]
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{1000}}{100}\right) \; &= \; 4951.5 \times (-0.002356) \;=\; -11.66 \\[4pt]
\frac{K_{1000}}{100} \; &= \; e^{-11.66} \;=\; 8.55 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
K_{1000} \; &= \; 100 \times 8.55 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 8.55 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:**
The strongly exothermic reaction has $K$ that **drops by ~ 5 orders of magnitude** on heating from 298 K to 1000 K — confirming **Le Chatelier's principle** (heat shifts equilibrium backward for exothermic reactions). This is why the **water-gas shift reaction** is operated at *low* temperature for maximum conversion.

---

## 3.4 Problem-Solving Strategy Using Computational Tools (EXCEL / MATLAB / SCILAB / PYTHON)

Although the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists computational tools, the *engineering mathematics* itself is what examiners reward. Below is the **algorithmic reasoning** (described in plain mathematical form) used by these tools to solve reaction-equilibrium problems with **non-ideal** gases or **multiple simultaneous reactions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict Compliance Note (Non-CS Engineering Constraint):** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, no source code (Python, MATLAB script, etc.) is reproduced here. Instead, the **mathematical algorithm** is presented step-by-step — this is the **engineering content** examiners actually test in Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics.

### 3.4.1 The "Guess-and-Iterate" Algorithm for Gas-Phase K with Fugacity Coefficients

Given a single gas-phase reaction with known stoichiometry, *T*, *P*, and pure-component critical properties, the steps are:

1. **Initialize** the equilibrium mole fractions $y_i^{(0)}$ (e.g., start from the stoichiometric feed, or from the *ideal-gas* solution).
2. **Compute** fugacity coefficients $\phi_i$ at $(T, P, y_i^{(k)})$ using a cubic Equation of State (Peng–Robinson or Soave–Redlich–Kwong):
   $$\ln \phi_i \;=\; \int_0^P \!\left(\frac{\bar{V}_i}{RT} - \frac{1}{P}\right) dP$$
3. **Form** $K_\phi^{(k)} = \prod \phi_i^{\nu_i}$ and **substitute** into the master equation:
   $$K \;=\; K_\phi^{(k)} \cdot K_y^{(k)} \cdot (P/P^\circ)^{\Delta \nu}$$
   Solve for the updated $K_y^{(k+1)} = \prod y_i^{\nu_i}$.
4. **Apply** element balances and the constraint $\sum y_i = 1$ to obtain a new set of $y_i^{(k+1)}$.
5. **Check convergence:** $\vert y_i^{(k+1)} - y_i^{(k)} \vert < 10^{-6}$ for all *i*. If not, return to step 2 with $y_i^{(k+1)}$.

This is precisely the **Newton–Raphson / successive-substitution** algorithm coded into the "RGibbs" reactor of Aspen Plus and the solver routines of MATLAB's `fsolve` / SCILAB's `fsolve` / Python's `scipy.optimize.fsolve`.

### 3.4.2 Multiple-Reaction Equilibrium — Minimization of Total Gibbs Energy

For *R* independent simultaneous reactions, the algorithm shifts to a **single Gibbs-energy minimization** over a single vector of species mole numbers $\mathbf{n}$ subject to element-abundance constraints:

$$\min_{\mathbf{n}} \; G_{\text{total}}(\mathbf{n}) \;=\; \sum_i n_i \mu_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{A}\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{b}, \quad n_i \geq 0$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is the atom-matrix and $\mathbf{b}$ is the feed atom vector. The Lagrangian is set up:

$$\mathcal{L} \;=\; \sum_i n_i \mu_i \;-\; \sum_k \lambda_k\!\left(\sum_i a_{ki} n_i - b_k\right)$$

Setting $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial n_i = 0$ yields the **mass-action law** for each reaction:

$$\prod_i \hat{a}_i^{\nu_{i,r}} \;=\; K_r(T) \quad \text{for each reaction } r = 1, 2, \ldots, R$$

The standard numerical approach is to:

1. **Guess** the equilibrium mole vector $\mathbf{n}^{(0)}$.
2. **Compute** $G_{\text{total}}$ and its gradient $\nabla G$.
3. **Apply** a constrained optimizer (e.g., `fmincon` in MATLAB; `scipy.optimize.minimize` with method `'SLSQP'` in Python; `fsolve` in SCILAB; or Excel's built-in Solver add-in via GRG nonlinear algorithm).
4. **Iterate** until $\vert \nabla G \vert < \epsilon$ and all constraints are satisfied.

### 3.4.3 Validation Tips (what a KTU examiner rewards)

- Check that **Σ yᵢ = 1** to at least 6 decimal places.
- Check **mass-balance closure**: total kg in = total kg out.
- Check that **K(298 K) computed from ΔG° matches tabulated value** to within 1 % (sanity check on ΔG° data).
- For multiple reactions, verify that **the second-law condition ΔG_total ≤ 0** is satisfied — the equilibrium state must lie on or below the Gibbs-energy surface.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Map of the Modified Phase Rule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reactive Multiphase System<br/>(N species, P phases)"] --> B["Identify N<br/>Total distinct species"]
    A --> C["Identify R<br/>Independent reactions"]
    A --> D["Identify P<br/>Co-existing phases"]
    B --> E["Compute Components<br/>C = N - R"]
    C --> E
    E --> F["Apply Modified Phase Rule<br/>F = C - P + 2 - R"]
    D --> F
    F --> G{"Variance F?"}
    G -->|F = 0| H["Invariant<br/>T and P are fixed<br/>(e.g. CaCO3 -> CaO + CO2)"]
    G -->|F = 1| I["Univariant<br/>One variable free<br/>(e.g. NH4Cl dissociation)"]
    G -->|F = 2| J["Bivariant<br/>Two variables free<br/>(e.g. single gas-phase rxn)"]
    G -->|F >= 3| K["Polyvariant<br/>Common for multi-rxn mixtures"]
    H --> L["Engineering Use<br/>Calcination curve of limestone"]
    I --> M["Engineering Use<br/>Sublimation-pressure data"]
    J --> N["Engineering Use<br/>Reactor design at chosen T,P"]
    K --> O["Engineering Use<br/>Multi-rxn fuel cells, crackers"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input:<br/>T, P, Feed n_i^0,<br/>Thermo Data (DeltaH, DeltaG)<br/>EOS (PR/SRK)"] --> B["Initialize<br/>Equilibrium Guess<br/>y_i = 0, K = 0"]
    B --> C["Compute phi_i<br/>via Cubic EOS<br/>at T, P, y_i"]
    C --> D["Form K_phi<br/>and K_y"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State the **modified phase rule** for a reacting system and define each term. How does it differ from the **Gibbs phase rule** for non-reacting systems?

**Model Answer:**

The modified phase rule for a reacting system is:

$$F \;=\; C - P + 2 - R \;=\; N - R - P + 2$$

where:
- **F** = degrees of freedom (number of independent intensive variables).
- **N** = total number of species present.
- **R** = number of independent chemical reactions at equilibrium.
- **C = N − R** = number of chemically independent components.
- **P** = number of co-existing phases.
- **2** = number of external intensive variables (T and P).

It differs from the classical Gibbs phase rule ($F = C - P + 2$) by the **subtraction of R**, because each independent reaction that reaches equilibrium introduces one extra thermodynamic constraint (the equilibrium-constant expression). Hence the variance of a reacting system is **lower** than that of the same species in a non-reacting system. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** For the reaction $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}(\text{s}) \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_3(\text{g}) + \text{HCl}(\text{g})$, find the number of degrees of freedom using the modified phase rule and comment on its variance.

**Model Answer:**

| Quantity | Value | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Total species N | **3** | NH₄Cl, NH₃, HCl |
| Independent reactions R | **1** | The single decomposition |
| Components C | **2** | $C = N - R = 3 - 1$ |
| Phases P | **2** | One solid, one gas |
| Degrees of freedom F | **1** | $F = 2 - 2 + 2 - 1$ |

**Comment:** The system is **univariant** — fixing the temperature fixes the equilibrium pressure of NH₃ and HCl (which are equal due to stoichiometry, each = P/2). The dissociation-pressure curve is a single line on a P–T diagram. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Every Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice (Module-level). Two alternatives are presented below; in a live exam you would answer **one** of the two.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **modified phase rule for reacting systems** from first principles. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the reaction $\text{CaCO}_3(\text{s}) \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO}(\text{s}) + \text{CO}_2(\text{g})$:
   (i) Apply the modified phase rule and interpret the result. **[3 Marks]**
   (ii) Explain why the equilibrium constant expression contains only $P_{\text{CO}_2}$, despite three species being involved. **[4 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of the modified phase rule — 7 Marks:**

- **Variables:** With N species in P phases, each phase has $(N - 1)$ independent mole fractions, giving $P(N - 1)$ composition variables. Add T and P → total = $P(N - 1) + 2$. **[1 Mark]**

- **Phase-equilibrium equations:** Chemical potential of each species is equal across all P phases. For each of the N species there are $(P - 1)$ independent equalities → total = $(P - 1)N$. **[2 Marks]**

- **Reaction-equilibrium equations:** Each of the R independent reactions contributes one constraint via the equilibrium-constant relation $K_a(T)$ → total = $R$. **[2 Marks]**

- **Subtraction and simplification:**
$$F = [P(N - 1) + 2] - (P - 1)N - R = N - R - P + 2$$
Since $C = N - R$, $F = C - P + 2 - R$. **[2 Marks]**

**(b) Application to limestone calcination:**

(i) N = 3, R = 1, C = 2, P = 3 (two solids + one gas).
$$F = C - P + 2 - R = 2 - 3 + 2 - 1 = 0$$
The system is **invariant** — T and P are uniquely linked by the equilibrium constant at the calcination curve. **[3 Marks]**

(ii) The activity of a pure solid is unity (Raoult reference state, $\gamma_i x_i \to 1$). Therefore $a_{\text{CaCO}_3} = a_{\text{CaO}} = 1$, and only $P_{\text{CO}_2}$ remains in the expression:

$$K_P \;=\; \frac{a_{\text{CaO}} \cdot (P_{\text{CO}_2}/P^\circ)}{a_{\text{CaCO}_3}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{CO}_2}}{P^\circ}$$

Hence the decomposition pressure $P_{\text{CO}_2}$ at any temperature is fixed — explaining why limestone decomposes sharply at ~ 900 °C in air. **[4 Marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** State and derive the **van't Hoff equation**. Show that a plot of $\ln K$ versus $1/T$ is linear, and identify the slope and intercept. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the gas-phase reaction $\text{CO(g)} + \text{H}_2\text{O(g)} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2\text{(g)} + \text{H}_2\text{(g)}$, given:
   - $K_{298} = 1.0 \times 10^2$
   - $\Delta H^\circ_{298} = -41.16 \text{ kJ/mol}$
   - $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$

   Calculate the equilibrium constant $K$ at **1000 K** and at **500 K**, and comment on the trend. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) van't Hoff equation — 7 Marks:**

Starting from $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ and the Gibbs–Helmholtz identity $\partial(\Delta G^\circ/T)/\partial T = -\Delta H^\circ/T^2$, substitute:

$$-R \frac{d \ln K}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{d\ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^2}$$

Integrate (ΔH° assumed constant) between $(T_1, K_1)$ and $(T_2, K_2)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Reformulate as a function of a single T (taking reference $T_1$):

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$

Therefore the **ln K vs 1/T plot is linear**, with:
- **Slope = $-\Delta H^\circ / R$** (negative of standard enthalpy change divided by R).
- **Intercept = $\Delta S^\circ / R$** (standard entropy change divided by R). **[7 Marks]**

**(b) Equilibrium constant at 1000 K and 500 K — 7 Marks:**

Common factor:

$$-\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} = -\frac{-41160}{8.314} = +4951.5 \text{ K}$$

**At T₂ = 1000 K** (from $T_1 = 298$ K, $K_1 = 100$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{1000}}{100}\right) &= 4951.5 \times \!\left(\frac{1}{1000} - \frac{1}{298}\right) \\
&= 4951.5 \times (-0.002356) = -11.66 \\
K_{1000} &= 100 \times e^{-11.66} \approx 8.55 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

**At T₂ = 500 K**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{500}}{100}\right) &= 4951.5 \times \!\left(\frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{298}\right) \\
&= 4951.5 \times (-0.001357) = -6.72 \\
K_{500} &= 100 \times e^{-6.72} \approx 1.20
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (Trend):** As T increases (298 K → 500 K → 1000 K), $K$ falls steeply from $100$ → $1.20$ → $8.55 \times 10^{-4}$. This is consistent with the **exothermic** nature of the reaction (negative $\Delta H^\circ$): the **van't Hoff slope is positive** (because $-\Delta H^\circ/R > 0$), so $\ln K$ decreases as $1/T$ decreases (i.e., as T rises). The system obeys Le Chatelier's principle: heating drives the exothermic reaction backward. Engineering implication — **operate at low T** for higher equilibrium conversion in the water-gas shift reaction. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU 2024 Board Examiner Pitfall List — Common Mark-Loss Triggers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract R:** Many students write $F = C - P + 2$ even for reacting systems — this gives a value **too large by R**, leading to a wrong variance. *Always explicitly state* that R = number of independent reactions.
>
> 2. **Confusing species (N) with components (C):** In a reacting system, C = N − R, not C = N. Examiners explicitly look for this distinction.
>
> 3. **Sign error in van't Hoff:** $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -\Delta H^\circ / R \cdot (1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$. The **double negative** when $\Delta H^\circ < 0$ (exothermic) makes students drop a sign and report K rising with T instead of falling.
>
> 4. **Forgetting standard-state pressure P° = 1 bar:** When computing $K_P$ or $K_y$ at non-standard P, dropping $P/P^\circ$ gives dimensionless error.
>
> 5. **Wrong phase count:** For CaCO₃(s) ⇌ CaO(s) + CO₂(g), students often write P = 2 (counting only solids, ignoring gas). Correct P = 3.
>
> 6. **Treating pure-solid activity as ≠ 1:** The activities of pure solids and pure liquids (Raoult convention) are unity at standard state. Marking them into K_equations is incorrect.
>
> 7. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing ΔH° in kJ with R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ without converting (× 1000). This single error costs 1–2 marks per question.
>
> 8. **Skipping the convergence argument for computational tools:** When asked about EXCEL/MATLAB/SCILAB/PYTHON methods, students must mention **iteration, convergence criterion (≤ 10⁻⁶), and use of EOS for non-ideal gases**. A bare mention of "use software" scores 0.
>
> 9. **Writing code instead of explaining the algorithm:** This is a **chemical engineering thermodynamics** paper — describing the algorithm mathematically is what earns marks; pasting code may even be penalized as out-of-syllabus.
>
> 10. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state the assumptions (ideal gas, ΔH° ≈ constant, single phase, etc.) explicitly. Examiners allocate 1 mark per major assumption in 14-mark problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- ✅ **Modified Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2 - R$ — *always* subtract the number of independent reactions.
- ✅ **Components in reacting systems:** $C = N - R$, *not* $C = N$.
- ✅ **Degrees of Freedom meaning:** Number of intensive variables (T, P, composition ratios) you must fix to uniquely determine the system state.
- ✅ **Equilibrium constant master equation:** $K = K_\phi \cdot K_y \cdot (P/P^\circ)^{\Delta \nu}$ — for ideal gases $K_\phi = 1$.
- ✅ **Fundamental link:** $\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K$ (R in consistent units!).
- ✅ **van't Hoff (differential):** $d\ln K/dT = \Delta H^\circ / R T^2$.
- ✅ **van't Hoff (integrated, ΔH° constant):** $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -(\Delta H^\circ / R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$.
- ✅ **van't Hoff Plot:** Slope $= -\Delta H^\circ / R$, Intercept $= \Delta S^\circ / R$.
- ✅ **Exothermic reactions (ΔH° < 0):** Slope > 0 → K decreases with T.
- ✅ **Endothermic reactions (ΔH° > 0):** Slope < 0 → K increases with T.
- ✅ **Standard-state values (always write in bold):** $P^\circ = 1$ bar, $T^\circ = 298.15$ K, $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.
- ✅ **Pure-solid / pure-liquid activity = 1** — drop from K expression.
- ✅ **For multiple simultaneous reactions:** use Gibbs-energy minimization with element-balance constraints (Lagrangian approach).
- ✅ **Computational methodology (EXCEL/MATLAB/SCILAB/PYTHON):** describe the *algorithm* (initial guess → fugacity calc → mass-action solve → mass balance → convergence check), not the code.
- ✅ **Sanity checks:** Σ yᵢ = 1, mass balance closure, ΔG_total ≤ 0 at the answer.
- ✅ **KTU favorite industrial examples:** Haber–Bosch (NH₃), Methanol synthesis, Water-gas shift, Limestone calcination, NH₄Cl dissociation, Claus process (H₂S + SO₂ → S + H₂O).

> 🏁 **End of Module 4 Notes — Phase Rule for Reacting Systems & Computational Reaction Equilibrium Problem Solving.**
